The four layers of software engineering technology (Pressman model) are: 1. **Quality Focus** — Total Quality Management and Six Sigma applied throughout the process. 2. **Process** — The framework defining what to do, when, by whom, and how. 3. **Methods** — Technical "how-to" techniques for analysis, design, coding, testing. 4. **Tools** — CASE (Computer-Aided Software Engineering) tools that automate and support the methods and process. > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Set B2]` > **Explain the concept of "Umbrella Activities" in a generic software process framework with two examples.** **Model Answer:** Umbrella activities are software engineering activities that are **not associated with any single framework stage** but rather **span the entire process**, executing in parallel across all five core activities (Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, Deployment). **Examples:** 1. **Software Quality Assurance (SQA):** Defines, monitors, and enforces process and product standards across every activity — from requirements validation during communication to defect prevention during construction. 2. **Risk Management:** Identifies, analyzes, and mitigates risks continuously — schedule risks during planning, technical risks during modeling, integration risks during construction, and support risks during deployment. > *[Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if the student explicitly mentions "parallel/overlapping across all 5 activities" — this is a frequent omission.]* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Paper - Set 3]`** --- ### 🟦 Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Generic Process Framework** of software engineering. List and briefly describe its five core activities and four umbrella activities. Illustrate with a labeled diagram. **(b) [7 Marks]** A software project has the following Function Point characteristics: - 25 External Inputs (Average complexity) - 20 External Outputs (High complexity) - 15 External Inquiries (Low complexity) - 10 Internal Logical Files (Average complexity) - 8 External Interface Files (High complexity) - TDI = 30 - Project type: **Organic** - Language: **C++ (55 LOC/FP)** Compute the **Adjusted Function Points, KLOC, Effort, Development Time, and Average Staff** using the Basic COCOMO model. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **Generic Process Framework** is a unified, abstract structure that underlies *every* software process — from a student project to enterprise-scale development. It is not a specific methodology; rather, it is a *skeleton* that can be instantiated into different process models (Waterfall, Agile, Spiral, etc.). **Five Core (Sequential) Activities:** | # | Activity | Description | Key Output | |---|----------|-------------|------------| | 1 | **Communication** | Customer-stakeholder interaction to understand business needs, constraints, and acceptance criteria | Statement of Work, Requirements Document | | 2 | **Planning** | Defines the technical tasks, resources, schedule, and risks; produces the SPMP | Software Project Management Plan | | 3 | **Modeling** | Creates analysis model (data, function, behavior) and design model (architecture, interface, components) | UML Diagrams, ER Models, Design Specs | | 4 | **Construction** | Code generation, unit testing, integration; produces the executable software | Source Code, Test Reports | | 5 | **Deployment** | Delivery, installation, training, and ongoing maintenance | Operational Software, Patch Releases | **Four Umbrella (Parallel) Activities:** 1. **Software Project Tracking and Control** — Compares actual progress with the plan; triggers corrective action. 2. **Risk Management** — Identifies high-impact threats and devises mitigation strategies continuously. 3. **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** — Verifies process and product compliance with standards (e.g., ISO, CMMI). 4. **Formal Technical Reviews (FTRs)** — Structured peer reviews at each milestone to find defects before testing. **Diagram (Reproduce in Exam):** ``` Core (Sequential) Umbrella (Parallel) ------------------ ------------------- Communication ──→ Planning ──→ Modeling ──→ Construction ──→ Deployment \ \ \ \ / ──→─── [ Tracking & Control ] ──→───[ Risk Mgmt ] ──→──[ SQA ] ──→ [ FTRs ] ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Compute UFP using standard weight table.** | Component | Count | Weight | Product | |-----------|-------|--------|---------| | EI (Avg) | 25 | 4 | 100 | | EO (High) | 20 | 7 | 140 | | EQ (Low) | 15 | 3 | 45 | | ILF (Avg) | 10 | 10 | 100 | | EIF (High) | 8 | 12 | 96 | | **UFP** | | | **481** | **Step 2: Compute CAF.** $$ \begin{aligned} CAF &= 0.65 + (0.01 \times 30) \\ &= 0.65 + 0.30 \\ &= 0.95 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Adjusted FP.** $$ \begin{aligned} FP &= 481 \times 0.95 \\ &= 456.95 \text{ FP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Convert to KLOC (C++: 55 LOC/FP).** $$ \begin{aligned} LOC &= 456.95 \times 55 \\ &= 25{,}132.25 \\ &\approx 25{,}132 \\ KLOC &= 25.13 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Effort (Organic: a = 2.4, b = 1.05).** $$ \begin{aligned} Effort &= 2.4 \times (25.13)^{1.05} \end{aligned} $$ Intermediate: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(25.13) &\approx 3.2245 \\ 1.05 \times 3.2245 &\approx 3.3857 \\ e^{3.3857} &\approx 29.55 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} Effort &= 2.4 \times 29.55 \\ &\approx 70.92 \text{ Person-Months} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Development Time (c = 2.5, d = 0.38).** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{dev} &= 2.5 \times (70.92)^{0.38} \end{aligned} $$ Intermediate: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(70.92) &\approx 4.2615 \\ 0.38 \times 4.2615 &\approx 1.6194 \\ e^{1.6194} &\approx 5.05 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{dev} &= 2.5 \times 5.05 \\ &\approx 12.63 \text{ Months} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Average Staffing.** $$ \begin{aligned} Staff &= \frac{70.92}{12.63} \\ &\approx 5.6 \approx 6 \text{ Persons} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer Table:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | UFP | 481 | | FP | 456.95 | | LOC | 25,132 | | KLOC | 25.13 | | Effort | 70.92 Person-Months | | Dev Time | 12.63 Months | | Avg Staff | ~6 Persons | --- ### 🟥 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered technology** of Software Engineering. Discuss the role of **Process** and **Methods** in detail. **(b) [7 Marks]** Define the term **"Software Engineering Practice"**. State and explain the **five practice principles** that form the foundation of all software engineering work. Use a real-world analogy to support your answer. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **The Layered Technology of Software Engineering** (Pressman, KTU Module 1): ``` ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Quality Focus │ ← Customer Satisfaction ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Process │ ← The "Glue" ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Methods │ ← Technical "How-To" ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Tools │ ← Automated Support └─────────────────────────────┘ ▲ Foundation Engineering Principles + Computer Science ``` **Role of Process:** A process is a *framework* that establishes the activities, sequencing, deliverables, and quality gates for the team. Without a defined process, projects become chaotic ("heroin-driven development"). The process provides: - **Visibility** — managers can see what is happening. - **Reusability** — proven work products can be reused. - **Predictability** — past experience can guide future estimation. **Role of Methods:** Methods provide the **technical "how-to"** for each framework activity. They encompass: - **Communication methods:** JAD, interviews, use-case writing. - **Planning methods:** COCOMO, PERT, Gantt, WBS. - **Analysis methods:** Data Flow Diagrams, ER modeling, OO analysis. - **Design methods:** Structured design, OO design with UML, architectural patterns. - **Testing methods:** Black-box, white-box, regression, unit testing frameworks. - **Maintenance methods:** Re-engineering, reverse engineering. The key insight: **Process defines *when* and *who*; Methods define *how*.** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Definition:** Software Engineering Practice refers to the set of organized, technical activities that a software engineer applies to develop, verify, and deploy software in a predictable, repeatable, and quantifiable manner. **The Five Practice Principles (Roger Pressman, Module 1 — KTU 2024):** | # | Principle | What it Means | House-Building Analogy | |---|-----------|---------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Understand the Problem** | Listen to the customer; analyze requirements, constraints, and acceptance criteria | Interview the family; understand rooms, budget, plot | | 2 | **Plan a Solution** | Translate requirements into a design — architecture, data, interface, components | Architect draws the blueprint | | 3 | **Carry Out the Plan** | Convert design into code; implement modules; perform unit tests | Masons, electricians, plumbers execute the construction | | 4 | **Examine the Result** | Test thoroughly; verify against requirements; fix defects | Building inspector checks structural integrity | | 5 | **Deploy and Support** | Deliver, install, train, and maintain the software over its lifecycle | Hand over keys; provide plumbing/electrical support for decades | **Analogy Summary:** A software engineer is to a *software product* what a *civil engineer + architect + contractor + maintenance team* combined is to a building. Each practice principle is essential — skipping "Understand the Problem" leads to misbuilt software; skipping "Examine the Result" leads to buggy software; skipping "Deploy and Support" leads to orphaned software. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Pitfall 1:** Many students write the five practice principles but *omit the "Why"*. For full marks, you must connect each principle to a *concrete software-engineering activity* (e.g., "Examine the Result" = Unit + Integration + System testing, not just "testing"). > - **Pitfall 2:** In numerical problems, students often **forget the CAF step** or apply LOC/FP conversion with the wrong language. Always double-check the language from the **standard KTU LOC/FP table** (C=128, C++=55, Java=53, Python=32). > - **Pitfall 3:** Distinguish **Core Activities (sequential)** from **Umbrella Activities (parallel)** — examiners ask this as a 3-mark question and *will* deduct marks if you mix them up. > - **Pitfall 4:** In COCOMO problems, **transcribe the constants $a, b, c, d$ correctly** from the table — a single wrong digit leads to cascading wrong answers with **zero partial credit** in Part (b). > - **Pitfall 5:** Avoid writing "Software Engineering = just coding" — KTU explicitly tests for the *systematic, process-driven* understanding (Bloom's Level 2/3). --- ## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Software Engineering** = Engineering principles + Computer Science + Project Management + Quality Focus applied to software. - ✅ **Layered Technology =** Quality Focus → Process → Methods → Tools (top-down). Foundation is **Computer Science + Engineering Principles**. - ✅ **Generic Process Framework =** 5 Core Activities: **Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, Deployment** (in order). - ✅ **Umbrella Activities =** 4 Parallel Activities: **Tracking & Control, Risk Management, SQA, Formal Technical Reviews**. - ✅ **Practice Principles =** Understand → Plan → Execute → Examine → Deploy. - ✅ **COCOMO Basic Model:** $Effort = a \cdot KLOC^b$, $T = c \cdot Effort^d$. - ✅ **FP Conversion:** $UFP \rightarrow CAF \rightarrow FP \rightarrow LOC \rightarrow KLOC \rightarrow Effort \rightarrow T_{dev} \rightarrow Staff$. - ✅ **COCOMO Constants (must memorize):** Organic (2.4, 1.05, 2.5, 0.38); Semi-Detached (3.0, 1.12, 2.5, 0.35); Embedded (3.6, 1.20, 2.5, 0.32). - ✅ **14 GSCs of FP:** Data communications, distributed processing, performance, heavily used configuration, transaction rate, online data entry, end-user efficiency, online update, complex processing, reusability, installation ease, operational ease, multiple sites, facilitate change. - ✅ **CMMI Levels:** Initial → Repeatable → Defined → Managed → Optimizing (Levels 1–5). - ✅ **Mantra for Viva:** *"Process is the glue; Methods are the technical know-how; Tools are the automation; Quality is the goal."* ---

topics.title: Phases of Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC): Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment and Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phases of Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC): Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment and Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)?

The **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** is a structured, process-oriented framework that defines the systematic sequence of phases involved in the creation, deployment, operation, and retirement of an information system, from the initial feasibility study to the final maintenance phase. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCITT503, the SDLC provides a disciplined, repeatable, and measurable approach that ensures software quality, predictable delivery timelines, controlled cost, and alignment with stakeholder requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> SDLC is a process that produces software with the highest quality and lowest cost in the shortest time possible. It provides a well-structured flow of phases that help an organization to quickly produce high-quality software which is well-tested and ready for production use.

## 1.2 The Six Canonical Phases of SDLC

The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies **six (6) sequential phases** of the SDLC. Each phase has its own deliverables, stakeholders, entry criteria, and exit criteria.

| # | Phase | Core Purpose |
|---|-------|--------------|
| 1 | **Requirements** | Gather, analyze, and document what the system must do |
| 2 | **Design** | Translate requirements into system architecture and module structure |
| 3 | **Implementation** | Code the software components based on design specifications |
| 4 | **Testing** | Verify and validate the system against requirements |
| 5 | **Deployment** | Install and release the software to the production environment |
| 6 | **Maintenance** | Evolve, fix, and enhance the deployed software |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: Building Your Own House

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Intuition: Constructing a House**

Think of building a house instead of software. You would never start laying bricks without first deciding how many rooms, what style, and what your budget is. The same logic applies to software:

- **Requirements Phase** = *Architectural Consultation*. You discuss with the architect what rooms you need, your family size, and budget. (This is your "Software Requirements Specification".)
- **Design Phase** = *Blueprint Creation*. The architect drafts 2D/3D plans showing wall placement, electrical wiring, plumbing routes. (This is your "System Design Document".)
- **Implementation Phase** = *Construction*. Masons, electricians, and plumbers start building exactly as per the blueprint. (This is your "Coding" activity.)
- **Testing Phase** = *Inspection*. The municipal inspector checks that wiring is safe, walls are plumb, and no leaks exist. (This is your "QA & Testing".)
- **Deployment Phase** = *Move-In Day*. Furniture is moved in, utilities are connected, and the house is ready. (This is your "Go-Live / Release".)
- **Maintenance Phase** = *Annual Upkeep*. Painting walls every 3 years, fixing leaks, and extending a new room when needed. (This is your "Software Maintenance".)

Just as a house needs all six steps, software also progresses through the same logical flow to ensure quality and client satisfaction.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked in the exam to "List the phases of SDLC", always list them in **chronological order** and add a *one-line purpose* for each. Examiners award marks for sequencing, not just naming.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Phase 1: Requirements (Engineering) Phase

The Requirements Phase is the foundation of the entire SDLC. Errors introduced here propagate downstream and become **10x to 100x more expensive** to fix during later phases (a concept known as the *1:10:100 Rule*).

### 2.1.1 Sub-Activities
- **Feasibility Study** (Technical, Economic, Operational, Legal, Schedule)
- **Requirements Elicitation** (Interviews, Questionnaires, Observation, Workshops, Prototyping, Brainstorming)
- **Requirements Analysis & Negotiation** (Resolving conflicts between stakeholders)
- **Requirements Specification** (Documenting using SRS)
- **Requirements Validation & Management** (Reviews, walkthroughs, traceability matrices)

### 2.1.2 Key Deliverable
- **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** document conforming to the **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** standard.

### 2.1.3 Stakeholders
Customers, End Users, Business Analysts, System Architects, Project Managers, Domain Experts.

## 2.2 Phase 2: Design Phase

The Design Phase transforms the "what" (requirements) into the "how" (architecture). It is typically divided into two levels of abstraction.

### 2.2.1 Two Tiers of Design

| Design Level | Also Called | Focus | Common Notation |
|--------------|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| **High-Level Design (HLD)** | Architectural Design | System decomposition into modules, sub-systems, data flow, technology stack | Architecture diagrams, DFDs (Context + Level 0/1) |
| **Low-Level Design (LLD)** | Detailed Design | Internal logic of each module — algorithms, data structures, database schema, interface contracts | UML Class Diagrams, Sequence Diagrams, ER Diagrams, Pseudocode |

### 2.2.2 Key Design Principles (KTU High-Yield)
- **Modularity** — Divide system into independent modules.
- **Coupling** — Measure of *inter-dependence* between modules (Low Coupling is desirable).
- **Cohesion** — Measure of *intra-dependence* within a module (High Cohesion is desirable).
- **Abstraction & Decomposition** — Hide complexity, expose essentials.
- **Information Hiding / Encapsulation** — Reveal only interfaces.

### 2.2.3 Key Deliverables
- **Software Design Description (SDD)** document (IEEE 1016 standard)
- HLD Document + LLD Document
- ER Diagram, DFD (Data Flow Diagram), UML Diagrams

## 2.3 Phase 3: Implementation (Coding) Phase

The Implementation Phase is where the abstract design is converted into executable code. It is the most resource-intensive phase in terms of developer effort.

### 2.3.1 Core Activities
- Writing source code in the chosen programming language (Java, Python, C++, etc.)
- Following defined **coding standards** and **style guides**
- Performing **unit testing** at the developer level
- Conducting **peer code reviews**
- Continuous integration of code modules
- Using version control systems (Git, SVN)

### 2.3.2 Key Deliverables
- Source code files
- Executable programs
- Unit test cases & test results
- Build artifacts

## 2.4 Phase 4: Testing Phase

Testing is a systematic activity to discover defects, verify functionality, and validate that the system meets user needs.

### 2.4.1 Levels of Testing (Verifiable in KTU 2024)

| Test Level | Performed By | Scope |
|------------|--------------|-------|
| **Unit Testing** | Developers | Individual functions/methods |
| **Integration Testing** | Testers | Interaction between modules |
| **System Testing** | Independent QA Team | The complete integrated system |
| **Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | End Users / Client | Validates against business needs |

### 2.4.2 Types of Testing
- **Functional** (Black-Box): Equivalence Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, Decision Tables
- **Structural** (White-Box): Statement, Branch, Path coverage
- **Non-Functional**: Performance, Load, Stress, Security, Usability, Compatibility

### 2.4.3 Key Deliverables
- Test Plan
- Test Cases & Test Scripts
- Test Data
- Defect / Bug Reports
- Test Summary Report

## 2.5 Phase 5: Deployment Phase

The Deployment Phase moves the tested, approved software from the staging environment to the live production environment where real users will use it.

### 2.5.1 Deployment Strategies
- **Big-Bang Deployment** — Entire system goes live at once (high risk)
- **Phased / Pilot Deployment** — Released to a small user group first
- **Parallel Deployment** — Old and new systems run simultaneously
- **Rolling Deployment** — Incremental replacement of instances

### 2.5.2 Key Activities
- Installation and configuration
- Data migration from legacy systems
- User training and documentation handover
- Production environment setup
- Smoke testing in production

## 2.6 Phase 6: Maintenance Phase

The Maintenance Phase is the longest phase of the SDLC, often consuming **60% to 80%** of the total software life-cycle cost. It begins immediately after deployment and continues until the system is retired.

### 2.6.1 Four Types of Maintenance (IEEE Standard 1219)

| Maintenance Type | Purpose | Approx. Share |
|------------------|---------|---------------|
| **Corrective** | Fixing reported defects/bugs | 20% |
| **Adaptive** | Adapting to environment changes (OS, hardware, regulations) | 25% |
| **Perfective** | Adding new features / improving performance | 50% |
| **Preventive** | Refactoring to prevent future problems | 5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (Examiner's Favorite):**
> **Software Maintenance** is defined by **IEEE Standard 1219** as the modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, to improve performance or other attributes, or to adapt the product to a changed environment.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Since PCITT503 also includes cost estimation concepts, the following table maps the SDLC to standard metrics, formulas, and boundary conditions frequently tested in KTU 2024.

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Meaning / Unit |
|------------------|------------|-----------------|
| Basic COCOMO Effort | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^{b}$ | Effort in Person-Months |
| Basic COCOMO Duration | $D = c \cdot (E)^{d}$ | Development time in Months |
| Average Staff Size | $SS = \frac{E}{D}$ | Persons |
| Maintenance Effort Ratio | $M_{effort} = \frac{M_{p}}{KLOC \cdot \Delta t}$ | Where $M_{p}$ is maintenance person-months |
| Defect Density | $DD = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}}$ | Defects per KLOC |
| Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours (Reliability metric) |
| Test Coverage | $TC = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Independent paths in a program |
| Coupling (conceptual) | $C = \frac{\text{Inter-Module Dependencies}}{\text{Total Possible Connections}}$ | Normalized [0,1] |
| Availability | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Uptime ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rendering Note:** All `|`, `\vert`, or `\mid` symbols inside math equations have been escaped (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) so the markdown table does NOT break. Do not type `|x|` directly inside a table cell.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the industry, the SDLC phases form the operational backbone of:
- **IT Service Management** (aligned with **ITIL v4** framework)
- **Project Management** (aligned with **PMBOK 7th Edition** by PMI)
- **DevOps Pipelines** (where the SDLC maps directly to CI/CD stages: Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor)
- **Safety-Critical Domains** (Avionics DO-178C, Medical FDA IEC 62304) where each phase requires formal certification sign-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Process Matrix

Because PCITT503 (Software Engineering) is process-driven rather than calculus-driven, this section replaces algebraic derivations with **detailed, step-by-step analytical process flows** for each SDLC phase, alongside the COCOMO derivation since it is a KTU-favorite quantitative topic.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Phase-by-Phase Process Walkthrough

### Process A — Requirements Phase Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Inception:** The client articulates a high-level business need (e.g., "We need an online exam portal").

**Step 2 — Feasibility Study:** Four feasibility dimensions are evaluated:
- *Technical*: Can we build it with current technology?
- *Economic*: Is the ROI positive?
- *Operational*: Will the user's organization adopt it?
- *Schedule*: Can we deliver within deadlines?

**Step 3 — Elicitation:** Business analysts conduct interviews, surveys, and joint application development (JAD) sessions to extract requirements from stakeholders.

**Step 4 — Analysis & Negotiation:** Conflicts (e.g., "fast and cheap" vs. "high quality") are prioritized using techniques like MoSCoW (Must, Should, Could, Won't).

**Step 5 — Specification:** All agreed requirements are documented in a formal **SRS** containing: functional requirements, non-functional requirements, external interfaces, constraints, and use-cases.

**Step 6 — Validation:** Reviews, walkthroughs, and inspections are conducted. A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is created to map each requirement to its test case later.

### Process B — Design Phase Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Architectural Design (HLD):** The system is decomposed into top-level modules, sub-systems, and their interactions. Architectural patterns (Layered, MVC, Microservices) are chosen.

**Step 2 — Data Design:** Conceptual (ER Diagram) → Logical (Relational Schema) → Physical (Tables, Indexes) data design is completed.

**Step 3 — Interface Design:** User interfaces (UI wireframes) and external system APIs (REST, GraphQL) are specified.

**Step 4 — Component-Level Design (LLD):** Algorithms, data structures, control flow, and database queries for each module are designed using pseudocode or UML activity diagrams.

**Step 5 — Design Review:** A formal **Design Review** is held; defects are logged and fixed before coding begins.

### Process C — Implementation Phase Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Environment Setup:** IDE, version control, and CI pipeline are configured.

**Step 2 — Coding:** Developers write code following the LLD, adhering to coding standards (e.g., MISRA C for embedded, PEP 8 for Python).

**Step 3 — Unit Testing:** Each function is unit-tested using frameworks like JUnit, pytest, or Google Test.

**Step 4 — Code Review:** Peer reviews via pull requests catch defects early.

**Step 5 — Integration into Build:** Code is merged into a shared repository and built by an automated CI server (Jenkins, GitHub Actions).

### Process D — Testing Phase Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Test Planning:** The **Test Plan** defines scope, resources, schedule, and exit criteria.

**Step 2 — Test Case Design:** Test cases are derived using techniques such as **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** and **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)**.

**Step 3 — Test Environment Setup:** Hardware, software, and test data are prepared in a separate environment.

**Step 4 — Test Execution:** Test cases are run; results are logged.

**Step 5 — Defect Reporting & Tracking:** Defects are logged in tools like JIRA, Bugzilla, and tracked through the lifecycle: New → Assigned → Fixed → Verified → Closed.

**Step 6 — Test Closure:** A **Test Summary Report** is produced; exit criteria are evaluated.

### Process E — Deployment Phase Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Pre-Deployment Audit:** Final user acceptance sign-off is obtained.

**Step 2 — Production Environment Preparation:** Servers, networks, and DBs are provisioned (often via Infrastructure as Code — Terraform, Ansible).

**Step 3 — Data Migration:** Legacy data is extracted, transformed, and loaded (ETL) into the new system.

**Step 4 — Go-Live:** The system is released. Smoke tests are executed on production.

**Step 5 — Post-Deployment Verification:** Monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana) are checked for anomalies.

### Process F — Maintenance Phase Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Issue Tracking:** User-reported issues are logged in a helpdesk system.

**Step 2 — Impact Analysis:** The change is analyzed for ripple effects using the RTM and design documents.

**Step 3 — Change Implementation:** The code is updated, tested, and re-deployed.

**Step 4 — Regression Testing:** All existing test cases are re-run to ensure no new defects.

**Step 5 — Release of Patch / Version:** Updated software is rolled out, often as a service pack or minor version.

## 3.2 Quantitative Derivation — Basic COCOMO Model

The **Constructive Cost Model (COCOMO)**, proposed by **Barry Boehm (1981)**, is the most frequently tested quantitative concept alongside SDLC in KTU exams. It estimates effort and duration as a function of program size in **KLOC (Thousand Lines of Code)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Project Mode**

COCOMO defines three modes based on project complexity:

| Mode | Description | a | b | c | d |
|------|-------------|---|---|---|---|
| **Organic** | Small team, familiar environment | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| **Semi-Detached** | Medium team, mixed experience | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| **Embedded** | Tight constraints, complex hardware/software | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

**Step 2 — Compute Effort using the Basic Formula**

$$
E = a \cdot (KLOC)^{b}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Effort in *Person-Months* (PM)
- $KLOC$ = Estimated size in *Thousand Lines of Code*
- $a, b$ = Coefficients from the mode table above

**Step 3 — Compute Development Duration**

$$
D = c \cdot (E)^{d}
$$

Where $D$ is the development time in *calendar months*.

**Step 4 — Compute Average Staff Size**

$$
SS = \frac{E}{D}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** An organic-mode project is estimated at 8 KLOC. Compute the effort, duration, and average staff size.

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients for Organic Mode**

$$
a = 2.4, \quad b = 1.05, \quad c = 2.5, \quad d = 0.38
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Effort Formula**

$$
E = 2.4 \cdot (8)^{1.05}
$$

Intermediate computation:

$$
8^{1.05} = 8^{1.00} \cdot 8^{0.05} = 8 \cdot 1.0456 \approx 8.3648
$$

Therefore:

$$
E \approx 2.4 \cdot 8.3648 \approx 20.08 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Duration Formula**

$$
D = 2.5 \cdot (20.08)^{0.38}
$$

Intermediate computation:

$$
(20.08)^{0.38} \approx e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(20.08)} \approx e^{0.38 \cdot 3.0004} \approx e^{1.1402} \approx 3.127
$$

Therefore:

$$
D \approx 2.5 \cdot 3.127 \approx 7.82 \text{ Months}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Staff Size**

$$
SS = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{20.08}{7.82} \approx 2.57 \approx 3 \text{ Persons}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Result:** Effort = **20.08 PM**, Duration = **7.82 months**, Average Team Size = **3 persons**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inputs / Process / Outputs (IPO) per Phase

| Phase | Input | Process | Output |
|-------|-------|---------|--------|
| Requirements | Business need, stakeholder interviews | Feasibility study, Elicitation, Negotiation | SRS Document, RTM |
| Design | SRS Document | Architectural & Detailed Design, Prototyping | SDD, DFD, ER, UML |
| Implementation | SDD, Coding standards | Coding, Unit Testing, Code Review | Source Code, Build Artifacts |
| Testing | Build Artifacts, Test Plan | Test Execution, Defect Tracking | Test Reports, Bug List, Fixed Build |
| Deployment | Approved Build | Installation, Data Migration, Smoke Test | Live System, User Manuals |
| Maintenance | User Feedback, Change Requests | Impact Analysis, Change Implementation, Regression | Updated Software Versions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master SDLC Phases Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the chronological flow of all six SDLC phases with their key deliverables. Note that the maintenance phase loops back iteratively because the system evolves continuously.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Business Need]) --> reqP[Phase 1: Requirements Engineering]
    reqP --> reqD[Deliverable: SRS + RTM]
    reqD --> desP[Phase 2: Design]
    desP --> desD[Deliverable: HLD + LLD + SDD]
    desD --> impP[Phase 3: Implementation]
    impP --> impD[Deliverable: Source Code + Unit Tests]
    impD --> testP[Phase 4: Testing]
    testP --> testD[Deliverable: Test Reports + Fixed Build]
    testD --> depP[Phase 5: Deployment]
    depP --> depD[Deliverable: Live Production System]
    depD --> mntP[Phase 6: Maintenance]
    mntP --> mntD[Deliverable: Updated Versions / Patches]
    mntD --> retire{System Retired?}
    retire -- No --> mntP
    retire -- Yes --> end1([End of Life])

    classDef phaseStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef delivStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000
    classDef termStyle fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    class reqP,desP,impP,testP,depP,mntP phaseStyle
    class reqD,desD,impD,testD,depD,mntD delivStyle
    class start1,end1 termStyle
```

## 4.2 V-Model Mapping (Verification and Validation)

The **V-Model** is a popular variant of SDLC where each development phase on the left has a corresponding testing phase on the right. The diagram below is KTU-frequently tested.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reqL[Requirements] -- Verification --> sysTestR[System Testing]
    sysTestR -- Validation --> acceptR[Acceptance Testing]
    hlL[High-Level Design] -- Verification --> integTestL[Integration Testing]
    llL[Low-Level Design] -- Verification --> unitTestL[Unit Testing]
    codeL[Implementation] -.-> unitTestL

    subgraph LeftWing[Development Side]
        reqL
        hlL
        llL
        codeL
    end

    subgraph RightWing[Testing Side]
        unitTestL
        integTestL
        sysTestR
        acceptR
    end
```

## 4.3 SDLC Phase vs Stakeholder Interaction Diagram

This block-level functional architecture shows who participates at each phase. Useful for KTU questions on "Roles and Responsibilities".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph stakeholderBlock[Stakeholders]
        custA[Customer]
        userA[End User]
        baA[Business Analyst]
        saA[System Architect]
        devA[Developer]
        qaA[QA Tester]
        pmA[Project Manager]
        opsA[DevOps / SysAdmin]
        suppA[Support Engineer]
    end

    subgraph sdlcBlock[SDLC Phases]
        p1A((Phase 1: Requirements))
        p2A((Phase 2: Design))
        p3A((Phase 3: Implementation))
        p4A((Phase 4: Testing))
        p5A((Phase 5: Deployment))
        p6A((Phase 6: Maintenance))
    end

    custA --> p1A
    userA --> p1A
    baA --> p1A
    pmA --> p1A

    baA --> p2A
    saA --> p2A
    pmA --> p2A

    saA --> p3A
    devA --> p3A

    devA --> p4A
    qaA --> p4A

    qaA --> p5A
    opsA --> p5A
    pmA --> p5A

    suppA --> p6A
    userA --> p6A
    opsA --> p6A

    classDef ph fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef st fill:#475569,stroke:#475569,color:#ffffff
    class p1A,p2A,p3A,p4A,p5A,p6A ph
    class custA,userA,baA,saA,devA,qaA,pmA,opsA,suppA st
```

## 4.4 Maintenance Type Classification Diagram

This diagram visualizes the four IEEE 1219 maintenance categories and their typical triggers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mStart([Maintenance Trigger]) --> mCat{Type of Maintenance?}
    mCat -- Bug Report --> corM[Corrective: Fix Defects]
    mCat -- Environment Change --> adaM[Adaptive: New OS / Regulation]
    mCat -- New Requirement --> perM[Perfective: New Features]
    mCat -- Future Risk --> preM[Preventive: Refactoring]

    corM --> rel1[Release Patch v1.0.1]
    adaM --> rel2[Release v1.1.0]
    perM --> rel3[Release v2.0.0]
    preM --> rel4[Internal Refactor]

    classDef trigger fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    classDef type fill:#2563eb,stroke:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    class mStart,mCat trigger
    class corM,adaM,perM,preM type
    class rel1,rel2,rel3,rel4 out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define SDLC and list its phases.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) is a well-defined, structured sequence of phases used by software engineers and developers to plan, design, build, test, and deploy an information system, ensuring that the final product meets customer expectations and is delivered within cost and time constraints.

**Phases in Order (1.5 Marks):**

1. **Requirements Phase** — Gathers and documents user needs in the SRS.
2. **Design Phase** — Produces HLD and LLD documents.
3. **Implementation Phase** — Converts design into executable source code.
4. **Testing Phase** — Verifies and validates the software against requirements.
5. **Deployment Phase** — Installs the system in the live production environment.
6. **Maintenance Phase** — Performs corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive changes.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between HLD and LLD.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form, 3 Marks):**

| Parameter | High-Level Design (HLD) | Low-Level Design (LLD) |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Also Called** | Architectural Design | Detailed Design |
| **Scope** | System-wide structure and modules | Internal logic of each module |
| **Performed By** | System Architect | Module Lead / Senior Developer |
| **Inputs** | SRS Document | HLD Document |
| **Outputs** | HLD Document, DFDs | LLD Document, Pseudocode, Class Diagrams |
| **Focus** | *What* are the major components | *How* does each component work |
| **Notation** | Architecture diagrams, DFDs (Level 0, 1) | UML Sequence, Activity, Class diagrams |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** For each Part B question, KTU provides an internal choice — students must answer **either OR**. The marks are split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. We have created two equivalent alternatives below.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Requirements Phase** of the SDLC. Discuss its sub-activities, deliverables, and the IEEE standard used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Waterfall Model** of SDLC. State **two advantages and two disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Introduction (1 Mark):** The Requirements Phase is the foundation of SDLC where customer needs are systematically identified, analyzed, and documented.

2. **Sub-Activities (3 Marks):**
   - **Feasibility Study** — Evaluates Technical, Economic, Operational, Legal, and Schedule feasibility (TELOS).
   - **Requirements Elicitation** — Interviewing stakeholders, distributing questionnaires, observing workflows, conducting JAD workshops, building prototypes, and brainstorming.
   - **Requirements Analysis** — Prioritization using MoSCoW or Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP); conflict resolution.
   - **Requirements Specification** — Documenting in the SRS.
   - **Requirements Validation** — Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, prototyping, and acceptance testing of the requirements themselves.
   - **Requirements Management** — Establishing a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) to track each requirement through design, code, and test.

3. **Key Deliverable (1 Mark):** Software Requirements Specification (SRS) Document — contains introduction, overall description, specific requirements, external interfaces, functional & non-functional requirements, and use cases.

4. **IEEE Standard (1 Mark):** **IEEE 830** (legacy) / **IEEE 29148:2018** (current) — provides the recommended structure for an SRS.

5. **Importance (1 Mark):** Errors fixed at this stage cost 1x, while the same errors cost 10x at design, 100x at testing, and 1000x at maintenance — the famous *1:10:100:1000 Rule*.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    wStart([Start]) --> wReq[Requirements]
    wReq --> wDes[Design]
    wDes --> wImp[Implementation]
    wImp --> wTest[Testing]
    wTest --> wDep[Deployment]
    wDep --> wMnt[Maintenance]
    wMnt --> wEnd([End])

    classDef wf fill:#0369a1,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    class wReq,wDes,wImp,wTest,wDep,wMnt wf
```

**2. Explanation (2 Marks):** The Waterfall Model, introduced by **Winston W. Royce (1970)**, is a linear, sequential SDLC model where each phase must be completed before the next begins. There is no overlap or iteration between phases (in its pure form). Progress flows steadily downward like a waterfall.

**3. Advantages (1 Mark — any two):**
- Simple, easy to understand and manage.
- Well-suited for small, well-defined projects with stable requirements.
- Clear milestones and deliverables make progress tracking easy.

**4. Disadvantages (1 Mark — any two):**
- High risk due to late discovery of defects.
- Poor fit for projects with evolving requirements.
- No working software is produced until late in the cycle.
- No client feedback during development.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe the **Design Phase** of SDLC. Differentiate between **Coupling and Cohesion** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the four types of **Software Maintenance** as per **IEEE Standard 1219**. Explain why maintenance consumes 60–80% of total lifecycle cost. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Design Phase Overview (3 Marks):** The Design Phase transforms the SRS into a system blueprint. It includes:
- **High-Level Design (HLD) / Architectural Design:** Identifies top-level modules, sub-systems, hardware/software platforms, and data flow. Tools: DFD, Context Diagrams.
- **Low-Level Design (LLD) / Detailed Design:** Designs the internal logic of each module — algorithms, data structures, database schema, and interface contracts. Tools: UML Class, Sequence, State, and Activity diagrams.
- **Deliverables:** Software Design Description (SDD) document per **IEEE 1016** standard.

**2. Coupling vs Cohesion (3 Marks):**

| Attribute | Coupling | Cohesion |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Definition** | Measure of *inter-dependence* between modules | Measure of *intra-dependence* within a module |
| **Goal** | Should be **LOW** | Should be **HIGH** |
| **Types** | Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common, Content (worst to best) | Functional, Sequential, Communicational, Procedural, Temporal, Logical, Coincidental (best to worst) |
| **Example** | Module A directly accesses Module B's internal variables = *Content Coupling* (worst) | A module that performs all operations of a single Payroll calculation = *Functional Cohesion* (best) |

**3. Importance (1 Mark):** Good design maximizes cohesion and minimizes coupling, leading to high-quality, maintainable, and reusable software.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Four Types of Maintenance per IEEE 1219 (4 Marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Corrective** | Fixing reported defects/bugs after delivery | Fixing a login crash reported by users |
| **Adaptive** | Modifying software to keep it usable in a changing environment | Migrating code to a new version of the OS or DB |
| **Perfective** | Adding new features or improving performance to satisfy new user requirements | Adding a "Forgot Password" feature requested by users |
| **Preventive** | Refactoring or re-engineering to improve maintainability and prevent future defects | Restructuring the code to remove unused dependencies |

**2. Why Maintenance Consumes 60–80% of Total Cost (3 Marks):**
- **Longevity of Systems:** Software typically lives 10–20 years, far longer than its development period.
- **Defect Repairs:** Defects emerge over time as edge cases appear in production.
- **Evolving Environment:** Hardware, OS, network, and regulatory changes force adaptive maintenance.
- **New Requirements:** Users continually demand new features (perfective).
- **Technical Debt:** Earlier shortcuts must be paid off (preventive maintenance).
- **User Training and Support:** Documentation updates and user support consume resources.
- **Empirical Evidence:** Lientz & Swanson (1980) study and subsequent IEEE studies confirm this ratio for typical enterprise systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
> 1. **Sequence Errors (–1 to 2 Marks):** Listing phases out of order (e.g., Testing before Implementation). Always use the exact KTU order: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.
> 2. **Missing IEEE Standard Names (–1 Mark):** Failing to mention *IEEE 830* (SRS), *IEEE 1016* (SDD), or *IEEE 1219* (Maintenance) loses easy marks.
> 3. **Conflating Coupling & Cohesion (–2 Marks):** Writing "low coupling, low cohesion" is a fatal error. The correct rule is *low coupling + high cohesion*.
> 4. **No Diagram in Part B (–2 Marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates diagrams for full marks in design/model questions. Always include a labeled Mermaid/hand-drawn diagram.
> 5. **Omitting the 1:10:100 Rule:** When asked about Requirements Phase importance, this is a *must-mention* concept.
> 6. **Confusing Waterfall with Iterative Models:** Waterfall is *purely linear*; mention that it lacks iteration. Do not confuse it with Spiral or V-Model.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save This Checklist for Last-Minute Revision Before KTU ESE.**

- [ ] **SDLC** = 6 phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance.
- [ ] **SRS** is the output of the Requirements Phase → standard is **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148:2018**.
- [ ] **SDD** is the output of the Design Phase → standard is **IEEE 1016**.
- [ ] Design has two levels: **HLD** (architecture) and **LLD** (module-internal).
- [ ] Good design = **High Cohesion** + **Low Coupling**.
- [ ] Implementation = Coding + Unit Testing + Code Reviews.
- [ ] Testing Levels: **Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance**.
- [ ] Testing Types: **Functional (Black-Box) & Structural (White-Box)**; plus non-functional (Performance, Security).
- [ ] Deployment Strategies: **Big-Bang, Phased, Parallel, Rolling**.
- [ ] Maintenance = **60–80% of total lifecycle cost**.
- [ ] Four Maintenance Types (IEEE 1219): **Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive**.
- [ ] Perfective Maintenance is the **largest share** (~50%).
- [ ] **1:10:100:1000 Rule** — cost of fixing defects rises exponentially across phases.
- [ ] **COCOMO** (Boehm, 1981) — basic formula: $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^{b}$.
- [ ] Three COCOMO Modes: **Organic, Semi-Detached, Embedded**.
- [ ] **V-Model** maps each development phase to a corresponding test phase (Verification on left, Validation on right).
- [ ] **Waterfall** = pure linear; **Spiral** = iterative + risk-driven; **Agile** = iterative + incremental.
- [ ] **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — used during testing to design test paths.
- [ ] **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC — a key quality metric.
- [ ] **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix)** — bidirectional mapping of requirements ↔ design ↔ code ↔ test.
- [ ] **Stakeholders per Phase:** Requirements = Customer + BA; Design = Architect; Implementation = Developers; Testing = QA Team; Deployment = DevOps; Maintenance = Support Engineers + End Users.
- [ ] **Exam Mantra:** Always draw a **diagram** for 14-mark questions and quote the **IEEE standard** wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Process Models: Prescriptive and Specialized Process Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Process Models: Prescriptive and Specialized Process Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Software Process Model?

A **Software Process Model** (also called a Software Development Life Cycle / SDLC model) is a **simplified, abstract representation of a software process**. It is a strategic blueprint that defines the **order of activities**, the **flow of information**, the **roles of stakeholders**, and the **transition criteria** (entry/exit conditions) for each phase of software development.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A process model is a representation of the order in which the activities of software development are to be performed. It is **NOT** an actual process — it is a *prescription* of how the process should be structured.

---

## 1.2 The Two Major Families Discussed in Module 1

### 1.2.1 Prescriptive Process Models
These are **traditional, plan-driven models** that prescribe a **defined, systematic, and orderly** approach to software engineering. They are also called "**traditional engineering models**" because they mirror classical engineering discipline (requirements → design → construction → testing → maintenance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prescriptive** literally means *"to lay down a rule or order officially"*. These models officially dictate the sequence of work.

**Core Philosophy:** *Predictability, repeatability, and documentation-first thinking.*

### 1.2.2 Specialized Process Models
These are **focused, targeted models** designed to address **specific application domains**, **engineering problems**, or **technological niches** where generic prescriptive models fall short (e.g., safety-critical systems, reusable component systems, mathematically rigorous systems).

**Core Philosophy:** *Domain-specific optimization and problem-specific rigor.*

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Building a House:**
>
> - A **Prescriptive Model** is like building a house using the traditional architect's blueprint: foundation → walls → roof → interior → handover. It is rigid, predictable, and well-documented.
> - A **Specialized Model** is like a custom builder who specializes in earthquake-resistant high-rises. The general "house-building" plan doesn't fit — they use specialized techniques (base isolators, moment frames) for a *specific problem*.
>
> In software, prescriptive models (Waterfall, Spiral) are the "general house blueprints," while specialized models (Formal Methods for avionics, Component-Based for ERP) are the "specialized techniques for unique problems."

---

## 1.4 KTU Taxonomy Map (Module 1 Scope)

| **Category** | **Models Covered in KTU Module 1** |
|---|---|
| **Prescriptive Models** | Waterfall, Incremental, RAD, Evolutionary (Prototyping, Spiral, WinWin Spiral), Concurrent |
| **Specialized Models** | Component-Based Development (CBD), Formal Methods Model, Aspect-Oriented Software Development (AOSD) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Agile, Scrum, and DevOps models are typically covered in *Module 5* of KTU 2024 PCITT503. Do **not** mix them into Module 1 answers unless specifically asked.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Prescriptive Process Models — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Waterfall Model
The oldest and most rigid prescriptive model. Each phase **must** be completed before the next begins, with **no overlap** and **no iteration** in the classical version.

**The 5–7 Phases:**
1. **System/Information Engineering** → Establish requirements for all system elements (hardware, software, people, database).
2. **Software Requirements Analysis** → Domain requirements, functional/non-functional requirements, SRS document.
3. **Design** → Data structure, software architecture, interface representation, algorithmic detail.
4. **Coding (Implementation)** → Translation of design into machine-readable form.
5. **Testing** → Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing.
6. **Maintenance** → Corrective, adaptive, perfective, preventive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The Waterfall model works best when **requirements are well-understood and stable** (e.g., payroll systems, attendance systems). It fails in projects with high uncertainty (e.g., AI/ML products).

---

### 2.1.2 Incremental Model
Combines elements of the linear Waterfall with the iterative philosophy of prototyping. The product is built **increment by increment**, with each increment delivering a *functional slice* of the system.

**Key Characteristics:**
- First increment is the **core product** (basic requirements).
- Each subsequent increment **adds new functionality**.
- User can start using initial increments while later ones are still under development.
- Total development time can be **reduced** compared to Waterfall.

**KTU Word Memory Trigger:** *"Slice and Deliver"*

---

### 2.1.3 RAD (Rapid Application Development)
A **high-speed** incremental model with extremely short development cycles (typically **60–90 days**). Developed by **James Martin** in the 1980s.

**The 5 Phases of RAD:**
1. **Business Modeling** → Information flow between business functions.
2. **Data Modeling** → Refines data objects identified in business modeling.
3. **Process Modeling** → Data objects transformed to achieve business functions.
4. **Application Generation** → Using 4GL tools, code is auto-generated.
5. **Testing & Turnover** → Reuses components → less testing time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** RAD requires **highly skilled developers** and **strong user commitment**. Do not recommend RAD for projects with poor user availability or low technical maturity.

---

### 2.1.4 Evolutionary Models
Evolutionary models build the system in **successive versions**, where each version **refines and extends** the previous one. Three sub-types:

#### (a) Prototyping Model
- Build a **throwaway prototype** quickly to clarify requirements.
- Prototype becomes a **learning tool**, not the production system.
- Best for projects with **unclear or evolving requirements**.

#### (b) Spiral Model (Boehm, 1988)
- Combines **Waterfall + Prototyping + Risk Analysis**.
- Uses **concentric loops (spirals)**. Each loop = one phase.
- Each loop is divided into **6 sectors**: Customer Communication, Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Construction & Release, Customer Evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The Spiral Model is the **most-asked** evolutionary model. Always draw a spiral with quadrants/sectors and label risk analysis prominently.

#### (c) WinWin Spiral Model
- A **variant of the Spiral** proposed by Boehm (1998).
- Adds an explicit **"Win-Win negotiation"** phase at the start of each cycle.
- Goal: Achieve **mutual win** conditions among customers, developers, and other stakeholders.

---

### 2.1.5 Concurrent Development Model
Also called **Concurrent Engineering Model**.
- Represents all process activities (analysis, design, coding, testing) as **parallel tracks or states**.
- Activities exist **concurrently**, not sequentially.
- A component may be in *“under development”* state while another is in *“under review”*.
- Best for **large, complex systems** requiring real-time coordination.

---

## 2.2 Specialized Process Models — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Component-Based Development (CBD)
- Focuses on **reusability** of pre-built, tested, validated software components.
- Uses a **component repository / marketplace** (e.g., COTS — Commercial Off-The-Shelf components).
- **Process Steps:**
  1. Identify candidate components.
  2. Search and evaluate available components.
  3. Negotiate and acquire components.
  4. Integrate components into the architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Term to Memorize:** *COTS* = Commercial Off-The-Shelf. The CBD model heavily relies on COTS.

### 2.2.2 Formal Methods Model
- Uses **rigorous mathematical notation** (set theory, predicate logic, Z notation, Vienna Development Method / VDM) to specify, design, and verify software.
- **No ambiguity** — mathematical precision eliminates interpretive errors.
- **Best for:** Safety-critical systems (air traffic control, medical devices, nuclear plant control, avionics).
- **Drawback:** Expensive, time-consuming, requires specialized mathematical expertise.

### 2.2.3 Aspect-Oriented Software Development (AOSD)
- Addresses the **"cross-cutting concerns"** problem (concerns that cut across multiple modules, e.g., logging, security, error handling).
- Uses **aspects** to modularize these scattered concerns.
- **Core concepts:** Join points, pointcuts, advice, aspect weaving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AOSD was developed to overcome the limitations of OOP**, where cross-cutting concerns cannot be cleanly encapsulated in objects.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| **Feature** | **Waterfall** | **Incremental** | **RAD** | **Prototyping** | **Spiral** | **CBD** | **Formal Methods** | **AOSD** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Prescriptive | Prescriptive | Prescriptive | Evolutionary | Evolutionary | Specialized | Specialized | Specialized |
| **Iteration** | None | Yes (increments) | Yes (very short) | Yes (until understood) | Yes (loops) | Yes (components) | Limited | Yes (aspects) |
| **Risk Handling** | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Excellent (explicit phase) | High (COTS risks) | High (math verification) | Moderate |
| **User Involvement** | Low (start & end) | Medium (each increment) | Very High | Very High | High (each loop) | Low | Low | Low |
| **Best For** | Clear, stable requirements | Medium-scale projects | Time-critical apps | Unclear requirements | High-risk, large projects | Reusable systems | Safety-critical | Cross-cutting needs |
| **Time to Market** | Long | Medium | Short | Medium | Variable | Short (via reuse) | Very Long | Medium |
| **Documentation** | Very Heavy | Moderate | Light | Low | Heavy (risk doc) | Moderate | Very Heavy (math) | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** For "compare and contrast" questions, always create **a table with at least 5–6 comparison axes**. KTU examiners reward structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Waterfall Model — Step-by-Step Analysis

**Scenario:** You are a KTU student asked to "Explain the Waterfall Model in detail with phases and limitations."

### Step 1: Define the Model
> The Waterfall Model, proposed by **Winston W. Royce (1970)**, is a linear-sequential life cycle model in which each phase is completed and verified before the next begins.

### Step 2: State the Phases with Deliverables
$$
\text{Phases}_{\text{waterfall}} = \{SIE, SRA, D, C, T, M\}
$$
where:
- $SIE$ = System/Information Engineering
- $SRA$ = Software Requirements Analysis
- $D$ = Design
- $C$ = Coding
- $T$ = Testing
- $M$ = Maintenance

### Step 3: Logical Flow
$$
SIE \rightarrow SRA \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow T \rightarrow M
$$
Each arrow represents a **formal review/verification gate**. No backward movement is allowed.

### Step 4: List Limitations (KTU Board Favourite)
1. **Real projects rarely follow a strict linear flow** — iteration is almost always needed.
2. **High risk and uncertainty** is not handled.
3. **Customer sees the product only at the end** → late defect detection.
4. **No working software is produced until late in the cycle.**
5. **Poor model for long, complex, object-oriented projects.**

### Step 5: State Advantages
1. Simple, easy to understand and manage.
2. Strict verification at each stage ensures quality.
3. Works well for small projects with clear requirements.
4. Documentation is produced at every stage.

---

## 3.2 The Spiral Model — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

The Spiral Model divides software development into **$n$ loops** (spirals). Each loop represents one phase of the SDLC. Each loop has **6 sectors** (KTU standard):

$$
\text{Each Spiral Loop} = \{CC, P, RA, E, CR, CE\}
$$
where:
- $CC$ = Customer Communication
- $P$ = Planning
- $RA$ = Risk Analysis
- $E$ = Engineering
- $CR$ = Construction & Release
- $CE$ = Customer Evaluation

**Logical Sequence within Each Loop:**
$$
CC \rightarrow P \rightarrow RA \rightarrow E \rightarrow CR \rightarrow CE \rightarrow (\text{Next Loop})
$$

### Step 3.1 — Risk Analysis Decomposition (Most Important)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern:** *"Explain the role of Risk Analysis in the Spiral Model."*

**Risk Analysis Steps:**
1. **Identify risks** (technical, schedule, cost, performance, schedule slippage).
2. **Assess probability** of each risk occurring: $P(r_i)$.
3. **Assess impact** (severity): $I(r_i)$.
4. **Compute Risk Exposure:** 
$$
RE = P(r_i) \times I(r_i)
$$
5. **Develop mitigation plan / prototype** to resolve the risk.
6. **Re-evaluate** at the start of the next loop.

### Step 3.2 — Win-Win Spiral Variant
In the Win-Win Spiral, an **extra negotiation sector** is added:
$$
\{CC, P, RA, E, CR, CE, WN\}
$$
where $WN$ = Win-Win Negotiation. This ensures all stakeholders agree on a *mutually beneficial* set of "win conditions" before the loop's engineering work begins.

---

## 3.3 Comparison of Prescriptive vs. Specialized — Analytical Matrix

> **Decision Logic for KTU Exam:** *"Which model should you recommend for a given project scenario?"*

**Step-by-Step Recommendation Algorithm:**

$$
\text{Recommended Model} = f(\text{Req. Clarity}, \text{Risk}, \text{Reuse}, \text{Safety})
$$

**Apply the matrix:**

| **Project Profile** | **Best Model** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Stable, clear requirements (e.g., payroll) | **Waterfall** | Sequential, well-defined phases |
| Large, complex, unclear requirements | **Spiral** | Explicit risk analysis loop |
| Need quick delivery in 2–3 months | **RAD** | Short cycles, 4GL tools |
| Unclear user interface or workflow | **Prototyping** | Throwaway prototype to learn |
| Reusable ERP component integration | **CBD** | COTS-based assembly |
| Aviation / nuclear / medical software | **Formal Methods** | Mathematical verification |
| Logging/security repeated in many modules | **AOSD** | Aspect-based separation |

---

## 3.4 When is Each Model Most Effective? (Engineering Judgement)

### Waterfall Effectiveness Function
$$
E_{wf} = \frac{R_{clarity} \times S_{stability}}{C_{complexity}}
$$
where $R_{clarity}$ = requirements clarity (0–10), $S_{stability}$ = stability of requirements (0–10), $C_{complexity}$ = project complexity (0–10).

**Interpretation:** If $E_{wf} > 1$, Waterfall is suitable. If $E_{wf} \leq 1$, prefer Spiral or Prototyping.

### RAD Effectiveness
$$
E_{rad} = \frac{U_{availability} \times T_{urgency} \times D_{skill}}{R_{uncertainty}}
$$
where $U_{availability}$ = user availability, $T_{urgency}$ = time urgency, $D_{skill}$ = developer skill, $R_{uncertainty}$ = requirements uncertainty.

**Interpretation:** RAD is best when $E_{rad}$ is very high (i.e., high user availability, urgent deadlines, skilled developers, low uncertainty).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
