> **Example:** *Traditional* — A formal interview with the bank manager lasting 2 hours with a pre-approved questionnaire. *Modern* — Daily 15-minute stand-up meetings where the customer adds/modifies user stories on a physical or digital board.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📕 Question B-A (14 Marks) — ESE Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Requirements Elicitation Process** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **stakeholders** and the **Power/Interest Grid** in elicitation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast any **four elicitation techniques** in terms of stakeholder involvement, information depth, cost, and best-use scenarios. **(7 Marks)**

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

> **1. Definition (1 mark):** Requirements Elicitation is the systematic process of discovering, capturing, and understanding the needs and constraints of all stakeholders for a software system. It bridges the knowledge gap between the *problem domain* and the *solution domain*.

> **2. The Process — Step-by-Step (4 marks):**

1. **Domain Understanding** — The analyst studies the business context, regulations, and terminology of the application area.
2. **Stakeholder Identification** — All individuals, groups, and organizations affected by or influencing the system are identified.
3. **Selecting Elicitation Techniques** — The analyst chooses from a toolbox (interviews, observation, workshops, etc.) based on context.
4. **Conducting Elicitation Activities** — Interviews, focus groups, and observations are carried out. The analyst uses active listening, 5-Whys, and open-ended questions.
5. **Documenting Requirements** — Discovered requirements are written in natural language, use cases, or user stories with metadata (source, priority, traceability tag).
6. **Negotiation and Conflict Resolution** — Conflicting requirements between stakeholders are identified and resolved through compromise, cost–benefit analysis, or escalation.
7. **Validation/Playback** — Each requirement is read back to the stakeholder for confirmation.

> **3. Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the Mermaid-style flow diagram from Section 4.1 here. The diagram should show the iterative nature with at least one feedback loop.)*

> **4. Role of Stakeholders + Power/Interest Grid (1 mark):** Stakeholders are the *source* of all requirements. The Power/Interest Grid (Mendelow Matrix) classifies them into four quadrants: Manage Closely, Keep Satisfied, Keep Informed, and Monitor. The elicitation strategy and technique frequency depend on the quadrant. (See Section 3.2 for details.)

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

Comparing the following four techniques — **Interviews, Observation, Focus Group, Prototyping** — across the four criteria:

| Criterion | Interviews | Observation | Focus Group | Prototyping |
|-----------|------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Stakeholder Involvement** | One-to-one | One (observed) | 6–12 simultaneous | 1–5 per iteration |
| **Information Depth** | Deep | Deepest (tacit) | Medium | Medium (visual) |
| **Cost** | High (1.5–3 hrs/req) | High (2–4 hrs/req) | Medium (1–2 hrs/req) | Medium-High |
| **Best Use** | Deep expert knowledge | Tacit workflow knowledge | Group consensus, brainstorming | UI/UX clarity, unclear requirements |

> **Key Justification (2 marks for the justification, not just the table):**
> - *Interviews* are best when deep, individual knowledge is needed, but they risk *single-source bias*.
> - *Observation* reveals *what users actually do*, not what they *say* they do — this is unique and cannot be replaced by interviews.
> - *Focus Groups* leverage group synergy but may suffer from *dominant personality bias*; a skilled facilitator is essential.
> - *Prototyping* is invaluable when stakeholders cannot articulate their needs verbally but can react to a tangible artifact. However, users may focus only on the look-and-feel and miss critical backend issues like security or performance.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** No single technique is sufficient for all situations. A mature elicitation process *combines* multiple techniques — typically starting with interviews and observation, validating with focus groups or JAD, and refining with prototyping.

---

### 📕 Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** What are the **major challenges** in Requirements Elicitation? Explain any **four** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Joint Application Development (JAD)** technique and the **Ethnography (Contextual Inquiry)** technique. Discuss their respective advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

> **Definition of Challenges (1 mark):** Requirements Elicitation is considered the most difficult phase of software development because it deals with *human communication, tacit knowledge, and conflicting goals*. The challenges are:

**1. Tacit Knowledge (1.5 marks):**  
Stakeholders often *know* what they need but cannot *say* it. For example, a pilot may say "the cockpit display should feel responsive" — but "responsive" is not measurable. The analyst must translate this into "display update latency ≤ 100 ms."

**2. Stakeholder Conflicts (1.5 marks):**  
Different stakeholders have different goals. Example: In a hospital management system, the *Finance department* wants strict cost control (minimal features), while the *Doctors* want maximum functionality (every diagnostic feature). The analyst must mediate.

**3. Stakeholder Unavailability (1 mark):**  
Busy executives and subject-matter experts have limited time. They may delegate to junior staff who lack the authority or knowledge. Example: A CEO delegates to a secretary who is unaware of strategic business rules.

**4. Changing Requirements / Volatility (1 mark):**  
Business conditions change during the project. Example: A tax-software requirement changes every year when the government revises the tax law. The elicitation process must be *iterative and continuous*.

**5. (Optional 5th, for extra credit) Communication Barriers (1 mark):**  
Domain-specific jargon, cultural differences, and language barriers can lead to misunderstanding. Example: A medical analyst in India working with a US-based client may misinterpret clinical terms.

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

> **JAD (Joint Application Development) (3.5 marks):**
> - **Definition:** JAD is a structured, facilitated group session where stakeholders, developers, and the analyst meet intensively (typically 3–5 days) to define requirements.
> - **Participants:** Facilitator, end users, executives, developers, scribe.
> - **Process:** Agenda is pre-set; sessions are highly time-boxed; outcomes are documented in real-time by a scribe.
> - **Advantages:** Fast consensus, high stakeholder buy-in, reduces rework, encourages creativity.
> - **Limitations:** Requires skilled facilitator; can fail if executive support is weak; expensive (many people in one room for days).

> **Ethnography / Contextual Inquiry (3.5 marks):**
> - **Definition:** Ethnography is a deep, immersive observation technique where the analyst spends days or weeks in the *actual work environment* of users, observing and asking "in-context" questions.
> - **Process:** The analyst shadows the user, watches real work, and asks "why" questions as they occur. The output is a set of *work stories* and *cultural models*.
> - **Advantages:** Captures tacit knowledge that users themselves cannot verbalize; reveals the *real* workflow (not the *ideal* one); excellent for complex socio-technical systems.
> - **Limitations:** Extremely time-consuming (weeks to months); requires special training in social-science methods; results are hard to formalize into SRS; only feasible for high-criticality projects (e.g., air traffic control, military).

> **Comparison (built into the above — 1 mark for the synthesis):** JAD is best for *fast, executive-driven, IT projects* with clear scope. Ethnography is best for *complex, safety-critical, or research-intensive* projects where the real workflow is hidden. The choice depends on project risk and stakeholder accessibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Elicitation Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Elicitation with Gathering.** Saying "we *gather* requirements" instead of "we *elicit* requirements" loses 0.5–1 mark. Use the word **"elicit"** deliberately.
> 2. **Listing techniques without justifying the choice.** For "which technique would you use for X?" — you MUST justify *why* in terms of stakeholder type, information depth, and project constraints.
> 3. **Omitting the iterative nature.** Always draw at least **one feedback loop** in your process diagram. Linear diagrams lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs).** Techniques like *observation* and *interviews* are the *primary* sources for NFRs (performance, security, usability). Mentioning this scores extra credit.
> 5. **Not mentioning Power/Interest Grid.** It is a *direct syllabus topic* in KTU 2024 Module 2. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss in stakeholder-related questions.
> 6. **Confusing "Validation" with "Verification."** Validation = "are we building the *right* system?" (done with stakeholders during elicitation). Verification = "are we building the system *right*?" (done against the SRS during testing).
> 7. **Writing long paragraphs without structure.** Always use **bulleted points + sub-headings + diagrams** in 14-mark answers. Examiners scan for structure first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 🎯 **Pre-Exam Rapid Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam:**

- ✅ Requirements Elicitation is the *first* phase of Requirements Engineering. It **discovers** stakeholder needs.
- ✅ The four fundamental activities of Elicitation are: **Discovery → Understanding → Conflict-Checking → Negotiation**.
- ✅ **Elicitation ≠ Gathering.** Elicitation draws out *tacit* and *unstated* needs.
- ✅ The **Power/Interest Grid** has 4 quadrants: Manage Closely / Keep Satisfied / Keep Informed / Monitor. Use it to choose the *frequency and type* of elicitation.
- ✅ The major elicitation techniques are: **Interviews, Questionnaires, Observation, Focus Group, JAD, Brainstorming, Prototyping, User Stories, Ethnography, Document Analysis.**
- ✅ **Interviews and Observation** are *best for non-functional requirements*. **Prototyping** is *best for unclear UI requirements*. **Questionnaires** are *best for geographically distributed users*. **User Stories** are *best for Agile projects*.
- ✅ Elicitation is **iterative**, not linear. Always include a **feedback loop** in the process diagram.
- ✅ **Tacit knowledge** is the hardest to elicit. Use *observation and 5-Whys* to surface it.
- ✅ **Conflicts** between stakeholders are resolved via negotiation, compromise, cost–benefit, or escalation.
- ✅ **JAD** = structured, executive-supported, intensive group session. **Ethnography** = deep, immersive, long-duration observation in real work environment.
- ✅ Industry data: **~50% of software defects** originate from requirements errors, most from elicitation.
- ✅ Always convert vague requirements into **SMART, measurable** form: "fast" → "responds within 2 seconds, 95% of the time."
- ✅ KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically tests: *technique comparison, Power/Interest Grid, elicitation challenges, and JAD/Ethnography detailed explanation.*

---

> **End of Notes — Requirements Elicitation (PCITT503, Module 2)**  
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Computer Science & Engineering.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Requirements Specification** is the engineering activity of precisely documenting the complete set of functional, non-functional, and constraint-based expectations that a software system must satisfy. The deliverable is canonically known as the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** — a formal, structured document that acts as the contractual bridge between the *problem space* (user needs) and the *solution space* (design and code).

According to the **IEEE 29148:2018** standard (the contemporary successor to IEEE 830-1998, commonly used in KTU syllabi), the SRS is defined as:

> *"A structured collection of requirements — functional, performance, design constraints, attributes, and external interfaces — that completely describes the external behavior of the system under specified conditions."*

The SRS serves **four primary stakeholders**:
- **Customers** – to verify that the system meets their needs.
- **Developers** – as the authoritative source for design and implementation.
- **Testers** – as the basis for deriving test cases and acceptance criteria.
- **Maintenance Engineers** – as a reference for future enhancements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Distinction (High-Weight Point)**
> A *requirement* is a *condition or capability* that the system **must** possess. An *SRS* is the **document** that holds these requirements in a precise, verifiable, and traceable form. Do NOT use these terms interchangeably in your answer scripts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" View

Imagine you are commissioning a custom home. Before a single brick is laid, the architect produces a **detailed blueprint** containing:

| Blueprint Element | SRS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Plot dimensions, location, orientation | **Scope** of the software system |
| Number of rooms, their purpose | **Functional Requirements** |
| Material quality, load-bearing strength, fire-rating | **Non-Functional Requirements** (Quality Attributes) |
| Local municipal building codes | **Constraints** (regulatory, hardware, platform) |
| Plumbing/electrical schematics | **External Interface Requirements** |
| Inspection checkpoints | **Verification & Validation criteria** |

If the blueprint is **vague** ("make it strong, big, and nice"), the contractor will guess — and you will end up with a glass-walled bathroom in a hurricane zone. Similarly, an **ambiguous SRS** is the #1 root cause of cost overruns and project failure in software engineering. The Requirements Specification is the discipline of *making the blueprint un-guessable*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The SRS is the SINGLE most influential document in the entire software lifecycle.** Studies (Standish Group CHAOS reports) consistently attribute ~60% of project failures to incomplete or poorly specified requirements — a fact frequently cited as a 14-mark question trigger in KTU ESE papers.

---

## 1.3 Why Requirements Specification is a *Separate* Engineering Activity

Requirements are NOT merely gathered once and forgotten. They go through a rigorous **engineering process**:

1. **Elicitation** → discovering requirements from stakeholders.
2. **Analysis & Negotiation** → resolving conflicts, prioritizing.
3. **Specification** → documenting them formally (this is OUR module topic).
4. **Validation** → ensuring they are correct, complete, and represent the user's true need.
5. **Management** → handling changes throughout the project lifecycle.

This entire chapter focuses on **Step 3 — Specification** — the act of writing the requirements down in a structured, unambiguous, and machine-/human-verifiable manner.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary (Must-Memorize for KTU)

- **Functional Requirement (FR):** Describes *what* the system does (a feature, action, or service).
- **Non-Functional Requirement (NFR) / Quality Attribute:** Describes *how well* the system does it (performance, security, usability, reliability).
- **Constraint:** A restriction imposed on the design or implementation choices.
- **User Requirement:** High-level, abstract statement from the customer's viewpoint.
- **System Requirement:** Detailed, technical requirement derived from user requirements.
- **Verification:** "Are we building the product **right**?" (Does it meet the spec?)
- **Validation:** "Are we building the **right** product?" (Does it meet the real need?)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love a one-line question: *"Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional requirements with examples."* Memorize the FR/NFR distinction and give **one clean example pair** (e.g., FR: "System shall allow login"; NFR: "Login response shall occur within 2 seconds for 95% of requests").

---

## 1.5 The "Three Amigos" of Software Specification

A specification is judged along **three orthogonal dimensions**:

- **Abstraction Level** → How close to the user vs. the machine?
- **Formality** → Natural language vs. semi-formal vs. formal (Z, VDM, Alloy).
- **Granularity** → High-level user stories vs. detailed system-level requirements.

For a typical KTU 14-mark answer, students are expected to be aware of **Natural Language (Structured)**, **Formal Specification Languages (Z-notation, VDM)**, and **Graphical Notations (Use-Case diagrams, UML)** as the three dominant specification styles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IEEE 29148 / IEEE 830 Structure of the SRS

The KTU syllabus explicitly references the **IEEE standard SRS template** as the canonical answer format. The SRS is divided into **three major sections** and several numbered subsections:

### Section 1 — Introduction
1.1 **Purpose** — Why this SRS exists; intended audience.
1.2 **Scope** — Product name, what it will do, what it will NOT do.
1.3 **Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations** — A mini-glossary.
1.4 **References** — Pointer to higher-level docs, standards, and related specifications.
1.5 **Overview** — How the rest of the document is organized.

### Section 2 — Overall Description
2.1 **Product Perspective** — Is it a brand-new product, a replacement, or a member of a product family? What are the system interfaces?
2.2 **Product Functions** — A high-level summary of the major capabilities.
2.3 **User Characteristics** — Education, experience, technical proficiency of intended users.
2.4 **Constraints** — Regulatory, hardware, platform, performance, language, standards.
2.5 **Assumptions and Dependencies** — External factors the spec assumes to be true.
2.6 **Apportioning of Requirements** — Which requirements are deferred to future versions.

### Section 3 — Specific Requirements (The Heart of the SRS)
3.1 **External Interface Requirements**
- User interfaces
- Hardware interfaces
- Software interfaces
- Communication protocols
3.2 **Functional Requirements** — Detailed description of every function.
3.3 **Performance Requirements** — Speed, throughput, capacity, response time.
3.4 **Design Constraints** — Adherence to standards, language, resource limits.
3.5 **Software System Attributes** (Quality Attributes / Non-Functional):
- **Reliability**
- **Availability**
- **Security**
- **Maintainability**
- **Portability**
- **Usability**
3.6 **Other Requirements** — Database, installation, legal, operations, etc.

---

## 2.2 The Nine Pillars — Characteristics of a GOOD SRS

A high-scoring KTU answer *must* enumerate these **nine quality characteristics** with one-line descriptions. Memorize the table below — it is a perennial 7-mark and 14-mark favorite.

| # | Characteristic | Meaning / Test |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Correct** | Every requirement accurately represents stakeholder need. |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | Each requirement has **one and only one** interpretation. |
| 3 | **Complete** | All functional, non-functional, and interface requirements are present — including responses to invalid inputs. |
| 4 | **Consistent** | No requirement conflicts with any other requirement. |
| 5 | **Ranked / Prioritized** | Each requirement is tagged with priority (e.g., High / Medium / Low or MoSCoW). |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | A finite, repeatable test can be designed to confirm the requirement is met. |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | The structure allows changes to be made easily, completely, and consistently. |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Each requirement can be tracked forward (to design/code) and backward (to origin). |
| 9 | **Design-Independent** | The SRS specifies *what*, not *how* — no premature design decisions. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Examiners frequently ask: *"List the characteristics of a good SRS."* — awarding 7 marks. The mnemonic **"CUC-RVM-T-D"** (Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable, Design-independent) with **Consistency** folded in is a quick-remember cheat.

---

## 2.3 The "Three-Circle" Venn View of Requirements Quality

A requirement is considered *well-formed* if and only if it is:

- **Necessary** (it is genuinely required — not gold-plating)
- **Verifiable** (a test can prove it)
- **Attainable** (it can be implemented within known constraints)
- **Unambiguous** (only one reading exists)
- **Traceable** (has a known source/origin)

If a requirement fails **any** of these five tests, it MUST be revised before being included in the final SRS.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Specification Metrics

Although Requirements Specification is qualitative, KTU papers sometimes include *quantitative questions* on specification effort. The dominant metrics are:

| Metric | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Specification Effort** | $E_{spec} = k \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Function-point-based empirical estimate. |
| **Requirement Volatility** | $RV = \dfrac{N_{changed} + N_{added} + N_{deleted}}{N_{total}}$ | Expressed as a **percentage**. Range: 0 to 1 (or 0%–100%). |
| **Requirement Stability Index** | $RSI = \dfrac{N_{unchanged}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Higher is better. |
| **Defect Injection Rate (Requirements Phase)** | $DIR = \dfrac{D_{spec}}{KLOC \text{ or FP}}$ | Defects per KLOC or per Function Point. |
| **Requirements Completeness Ratio** | $RCR = \dfrac{R_{specified}}{R_{total\ expected}}$ | Closer to 1 is better. |
| **Cost of Fix Ratio (Boehm's Law)** | $\dfrac{C_{post-deployment}}{C_{requirements}} \approx 100$ | Empirical — **highly cited** in KTU answers. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boehm's Cost-of-Fix Ratio** is a *favourite* 2-mark question. Memorize the 1× : 10× : 100× : 1000× pattern (Requirements → Design → Implementation → Post-deployment). It is the cornerstone argument for *why* we invest time in specification.

---

## 2.5 Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements — The Pivotal Distinction

| Dimension | Functional Requirement (FR) | Non-Functional Requirement (NFR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | What the system *does* | How *well* it does it |
| **Also called** | Behavioral / Capability requirement | Quality attribute / Constraint |
| **Origin** | Use-cases, user stories | Architectural drivers, SLAs |
| **Example** | "The system shall allow a user to reset a forgotten password via email." | "The password reset email shall be delivered within 30 seconds in 99.5% of cases." |
| **Test type** | Functional / Black-box | Performance / Load / Security / Usability test |
| **Verification** | Direct test of behavior | Measurement against a quantitative threshold |

### 2.5.1 FURPS+ Model (Industry-Standard NFR Taxonomy)

A widely-used KTU-recommended classification of quality attributes is **FURPS+** (Grady, Hewlett-Packard):

- **F**unctional
- **U**sability
- **R**eliability
- **P**erformance
- **S**upportability
- **+** Design constraints, Implementation requirements, Interface requirements, Physical requirements

> [!TIP]
> A common 7-mark KTU question: *"Explain the FURPS+ model."* Structure the answer as: (a) name the 5 core categories with one-line meaning, (b) explain the "+" extension, (c) give one example for each.

---

## 2.6 Traceability — The Connective Tissue

A traceable requirement has **two directional links** maintained in a *Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)*:

- **Forward Traceability:** User Requirement → System Requirement → Design → Code → Test Case
- **Backward Traceability:** Each requirement traces back to its stakeholder source or use-case origin.

The **RTM** is a tabular artifact (typically a spreadsheet) with columns: *Req ID → Use-Case → Design Element → Code Module → Test Case → Status*.

> [!WARNING]
> If you are asked "What is the purpose of an RTM?" in a KTU paper, do NOT say "to track requirements." Say: *"To establish bidirectional traceability between requirements, design, implementation, and test artifacts — thereby enabling impact analysis, change management, and verification of coverage."*

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Devices (FDA 21 CFR Part 11):** Mandates a formally reviewed SRS before code.
- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Each software requirement must be traced to at least one test case.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Safety requirements are derived, specified, and traced at ASIL-classified levels.
- **Defense / Aerospace:** SRS is a contractual deliverable; ambiguity can void the contract.
- **Agile / DevOps:** Even in Agile, the *Definition of Done* and the *Product Backlog with Acceptance Criteria* serve as lightweight, living specifications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Requirements Specification Process

The complete Requirements Specification process can be decomposed into **six sequential, iterative activities**. KTU papers frequently demand a labelled flow diagram — this matrix is the *content* you would place inside those blocks.

| Stage | Activity | Input | Output | Technique / Tool | Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Elicitation** | Discover raw requirements | Business goals, domain knowledge | Elicitation notes | Interviews, questionnaires, observation, workshops, brainstorming | Customer, end-users, domain experts |
| **2. Analysis** | Refine, classify, prioritize | Elicitation notes | Analyzed requirements | Conflict resolution, negotiation, prioritization (MoSCoW) | Analyst, customer, project manager |
| **3. Specification** | Document formally | Analyzed requirements | **SRS document** | IEEE 830 template, natural language, UML, formal methods | Analyst, technical writer |
| **4. Validation** | Check quality of the SRS | SRS draft | Validated SRS | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, prototyping, acceptance test design | Customer, QA team, end-users |
| **5. Negotiation** | Resolve conflicting or infeasible items | Validated SRS + stakeholder feedback | Reconciled SRS | Win-win negotiation, trade-off analysis | All stakeholders |
| **6. Management** | Track changes over time | Approved SRS | Updated SRS + change log | RTM, change control board, version control | Configuration manager, PM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stages 3, 4, 5, and 6 are cyclic** — the SRS is rarely "done" after one pass. Iteration is normal and expected, especially in incremental or Agile lifecycles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — The Cost-of-Fix Curve (Boehm's Law)

This is a derivation-style question KTU examiners sometimes pose. We derive the *geometric cost growth* observed empirically across decades of industry data.

### 3.2.1 Statement of the Law

If we define the cost of fixing a defect discovered at stage $i$ as $C_i$, then the empirical relationship observed by Barry Boehm (1981) is:

$$
C_{i+1} \approx \alpha \cdot C_i
$$

where $\alpha$ is the *cost-amplification factor* per phase.

For a typical Waterfall lifecycle, the empirical value is $\alpha \approx 10$, with the following known data points:

$$
C_{requirements} : C_{design} : C_{implementation} : C_{post-deployment} \approx 1 : 10 : 100 : 1000
$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $C_1$ be the cost of fixing a defect at the **Requirements** stage (this is our reference cost).

$$
\text{Step 1:} \quad C_1 = x \quad \text{(say, INR 500)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2:} \quad C_2 = \alpha \cdot C_1 = 10 \cdot x
$$

$$
\text{Step 3:} \quad C_3 = \alpha \cdot C_2 = \alpha^2 \cdot C_1
$$

$$
\text{Step 4:} \quad C_4 = \alpha \cdot C_3 = \alpha^3 \cdot C_1
$$

Substituting $\alpha = 10$:

$$
C_1 = 1 \cdot x = x
$$

$$
C_2 = 10 \cdot x
$$

$$
C_3 = 100 \cdot x
$$

$$
C_4 = 1000 \cdot x
$$

### 3.2.3 Generalised Closed-Form Expression

For $n$ lifecycle phases, the cost of fixing a defect at phase $k$ is:

$$
C_k = \alpha^{k-1} \cdot C_1
$$

For $k=4$ and $\alpha=10$:

$$
C_4 = 10^{4-1} \cdot C_1 = 10^3 \cdot C_1 = 1000 \cdot C_1
$$

### 3.2.4 The Specification Investment Argument

Suppose we invest $I$ extra units of effort in the **specification stage** to detect and fix a defect that *would otherwise* be found at post-deployment.

- **Without specification investment:** Cost to fix in production = $C_4 = 1000 \cdot C_1$
- **With specification investment:** Cost to fix in spec stage = $C_1 = 1 \cdot C_1$, plus investment $I$

The **Net Savings Ratio (NSR)** is:

$$
NSR = \frac{C_4 - (C_1 + I)}{C_4} = 1 - \frac{C_1 + I}{1000 \cdot C_1}
$$

For $I = 5 \cdot C_1$:

$$
NSR = 1 - \frac{1 + 5}{1000} = 1 - 0.006 = 0.994
$$

Thus, a *tiny* upfront investment yields a **99.4% cost saving** for each requirement-level defect — justifying the very existence of the specification phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Favourite Line (Memorize):** *"An hour spent in requirements specification saves a hundred hours in testing and a thousand hours in maintenance."* — paraphrased from Boehm (1981).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Writing a Well-Formed Requirement

### 3.3.1 Bad Requirement (Common Student Mistake)

> *"The system should be fast, secure, and user-friendly."*

**Why it fails:**
- "Fast" is **not verifiable** — fast for whom? Measured how?
- "Secure" is **ambiguous** — secure against what threats, to what level?
- "User-friendly" is **subjective** — depends on the user.

### 3.3.2 Well-Formed Requirement (Model Answer)

> *"The web application shall respond to a user search query within **2 seconds** for **95%** of all queries, where the search index contains up to **10 million records**, measured on a server with the minimum specified hardware configuration of 8 GB RAM, quad-core CPU."*

**Why it passes:**
- **Verifiable** — we can design a load test that measures this.
- **Unambiguous** — only one interpretation.
- **Complete** — the operating condition is specified.
- **Attainable** — the hardware context is named.
- **Necessary** — performance is a stated business goal.

### 3.3.3 The "EARS" Template — A Standardised Requirement Form

**EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax)** is a popular 5-pattern template for writing well-formed requirements:

| Pattern | Template | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Ubiquitous | The `<system>` shall `<action>`. | *The ATM shall dispense cash.* |
| Event-driven | When `<trigger>`, the `<system>` shall `<action>`. | *When the user inserts a card, the ATM shall validate the PIN.* |
| State-driven | While `<state>`, the `<system>` shall `<action>`. | *While in maintenance mode, the ATM shall reject all transactions.* |
| Unwanted behaviour | If `<condition>`, then the `<system>` shall `<action>`. | *If the network fails, then the ATM shall store transactions locally.* |
| Optional | Where `<feature>`, the `<system>` shall `<action>`. | *Where biometric login is enabled, the system shall accept fingerprint.* |

> [!TIP]
> For a 14-mark "explain how to write a good requirement" question, pair **EARS** with the **five quality tests** (necessary, verifiable, attainable, unambiguous, traceable). This combination easily earns full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Specification Techniques

| Technique | Formality | Strength | Weakness | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Language (Structured)** | Low | Universally understood, easy to write | Ambiguous, verbose | Most business applications |
| **Use-Case Descriptions (UML)** | Low-Medium | Captures user-system interaction | Less suited for non-functional | Object-oriented systems |
| **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)** | Medium | Shows functional flow | No control flow, no timing | Information systems |
| **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams** | Medium | Models data clearly | Does not model behavior | Database-centric systems |
| **State Transition Diagrams** | Medium | Models dynamic behavior | Verbose for large systems | Real-time, embedded |
| **Decision Tables / Trees** | Medium | Compact logic | Hard to scale | Business rules, policies |
| **Z-notation / VDM** | High (Formal) | Mathematically precise | Steep learning curve | Safety-critical systems |
| **Alloy / TLA+** | High (Formal) | Model-checkable | Specialized expertise | Distributed, concurrent systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Loses ~2 marks):** Students often describe *gathering* requirements when the question asks about *specifying* them. Always separate the two: *Elicitation* discovers; *Specification* documents.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 SRS Document Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the canonical structure of a Software Requirements Specification, as prescribed by the IEEE standards. This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing how each section of the SRS relates to the others.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SRS Document - Software Requirements Specification] --> B[Section 1 - Introduction]
    A --> C[Section 2 - Overall Description]
    A --> D[Section 3 - Specific Requirements]

    B --> B1[1.1 Purpose]
    B --> B2[1.2 Document Conventions]
    B --> B3[1.3 Intended Audience and Reading Suggestions]
    B --> B4[1.4 Scope of the Project]
    B --> B5[1.5 References]

    C --> C1[2.1 Product Perspective]
    C --> C2[2.2 Product Functions]
    C --> C3[2.3 User Class and Characteristics]
    C --> C4[2.4 Operating Environment]
    C --> C5[2.5 Design and Implementation Constraints]
    C --> C6[2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies]

    D --> D1[3.1 External Interface Requirements]
    D --> D2[3.2 Functional Requirements]
    D --> D3[3.3 Performance Requirements]
    D --> D4[3.4 Design Constraints]
    D --> D5[3.5 Software System Attributes - Quality]
    D --> D6[3.6 Other Requirements]

    D1 --> D1a[User Interfaces]
    D1 --> D1b[Hardware Interfaces]
    D1 --> D1c[Software Interfaces]
    D1 --> D1d[Communication Interfaces]

    D5 --> D5a[Reliability]
    D5 --> D5b[Availability]
    D5 --> D5c[Security]
    D5 --> D5d[Maintainability]
    D5 --> D5e[Portability]
    D5 --> D5f[Usability]

    D2 --> E[Use-Case Models - UML]
    D2 --> F[Data Flow Diagrams - DFD]
    D2 --> G[State Transition Diagrams - STD]
    D2 --> H[Decision Tables and Trees]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The SRS document is a *root* node that branches into three top-level sections (Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements). Section 3 — the *Specific Requirements* — is the engineering heart of the document, further decomposed into functional, non-functional (quality), interface, and performance categories. The four boxes `E`, `F`, `G`, `H` are the *modelling notations* used to specify functional requirements.

---

## 4.2 The Requirements Specification Process Flow

The next Mermaid diagram captures the **iterative workflow** through which raw requirements become a *validated, approved SRS*. This is the most common flow-diagram question in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[1. Elicitation<br/>Interviews, Workshops,<br/>Observation] --> S2[2. Analysis and<br/>Prioritization]
    S2 --> S3[3. Specification<br/>SRS Document Draft]
    S3 --> S4[4. Validation<br/>Reviews, Walkthroughs,<br/>Prototyping]
    S4 --> S5{5. Validation<br/>Passed?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[6. Negotiation<br/>with Stakeholders]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[7. Approval and<br/>Baseline SRS]
    S7 --> S8[8. Requirements<br/>Management]
    S8 --> S9[9. Change Request<br/>or New Project Phase]
    S9 --> S1
```

**Explanation of nodes:**

- `S1` — *Elicitation* — discovers what stakeholders want.
- `S2` — *Analysis* — classifies, prioritizes (using MoSCoW or 100-point method), and resolves conflicts.
- `S3` — *Specification* — writes the SRS using IEEE 830 structure.
- `S4` — *Validation* — verifies correctness, completeness, and consistency via formal reviews.
- `S5` — *Decision gate* — does the SRS pass validation?
- `S6` — *Negotiation loop* — if not, go back to analysis and re-negotiate with stakeholders.
- `S7` — *Approval* — formal sign-off; the SRS becomes the *baseline* (a controlled, versioned artifact).
- `S8` — *Management* — controlled changes through change-control board.
- `S9` — *Trigger* — either a change request or the start of a new development iteration restarts the loop.

---

## 4.3 The FR vs NFR Hierarchy

A high-quality KTU diagram answer distinguishes *functional* and *non-functional* requirements. The following Mermaid hierarchy visualizes the FURPS+ taxonomy.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Software Requirements] --> FR[Functional Requirements]
    ROOT --> NFR[Non-Functional Requirements - FURPS Plus]

    FR --> FR1[Use Cases]
    FR --> FR2[User Stories]
    FR --> FR3[Business Rules]
    FR --> FR4[Feature Lists]

    NFR --> NFR_U[Usability]
    NFR --> NFR_R[Reliability]
    NFR --> NFR_P[Performance]
    NFR --> NFR_S[Supportability]
    NFR --> NFR_plus[Plus: Design Constraints,<br/>Interface, Implementation,<br/>Physical Requirements]

    NFR_U --> U1[Learnability]
    NFR_U --> U2[Operability]
    NFR_U --> U3[Accessibility]

    NFR_R --> R1[MTTF - Mean Time To Failure]
    NFR_R --> R2[MTBF - Mean Time Between Failures]
    NFR_R --> R3[Availability Percentage]

    NFR_P --> P1[Response Time]
    NFR_P --> P2[Throughput]
    NFR_P --> P3[Resource Utilization]

    NFR_S --> S1[Maintainability]
    NFR_S --> S2[Configurability]
    NFR_S --> S3[Testability]
    NFR_S --> S4[Installability]
```

---

## 4.4 The Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Logical View

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **bidirectional traceability** between requirements and downstream artifacts.

```mermaid
graph LR
    UC[Use-Case Origin] --> SR[System Requirement ID]
    SR --> DR[Design Element]
    DR --> COD[Code Module]
    COD --> TC[Test Case ID]
    TC --> VR[Verification Result]
    VR -.Feedback.-> SR
    SR -.Forward Trace.-> TC
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU answers that include an RTM diagram with explicit *forward* and *backward* arrows earn bonus visibility. The dotted lines in the diagram above represent the *backward traceability* loop, while the solid lines represent the *forward* derivation chain.

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Specification Languages Stack

For topics requiring a deeper view of specification *styles*, this block-level architecture shows how requirements move from informal to fully formal representations.

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Informal Requirements] --> B[Structured Natural Language]
    B --> C[Graphical Notations - UML, DFD, ER, STD]
    C --> D[Mathematical Specification - Z, VDM, Alloy]
    D --> E[Executable Specifications - TLA Plus, Spec Plus Plus]

    A1[Stakeholder Interviews] --> A
    A2[Use Case Narratives] --> A
    A3[User Stories] --> A

    B1[EARS Templates] --> B
    B2[Volere Templates] --> B

    C1[UML Use-Case Diagrams] --> C
    C2[UML Class Diagrams] --> C
    C3[State Machines] --> C

    D1[Set Theory and Logic] --> D
    D2[Type Systems] --> D

    E1[Model Checkers] --> E
    E2[Proof Assistants] --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** As we move from left to right, **formality increases** and **ambiguity decreases**, but the *cost of authorship* and *skill required* also increase. KTU expects you to choose the appropriate level for the project's safety, scale, and regulatory context.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, S3 B.Tech CSE]`

> **Define the term "Software Requirements Specification (SRS)". List any FOUR essential characteristics that a good SRS should possess.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Software Requirements Specification (SRS)* is a formal, structured document that completely describes the external behaviour of the proposed software system. It serves as the authoritative reference for customers, developers, testers, and maintainers throughout the project lifecycle.

The four essential characteristics of a good SRS are:

1. **Correct** — Every requirement must accurately reflect the true need of the intended user or stakeholder.
2. **Unambiguous** — Each requirement admits one and only one interpretation; no vague adjectives like "fast", "user-friendly", or "efficient" are allowed.
3. **Complete** — All functional, non-functional, and interface requirements are present, including responses to invalid, unexpected, or boundary-condition inputs.
4. **Verifiable** — A finite, repeatable, cost-effective test or measurement can confirm that the system satisfies the requirement.

*(Other valid characteristics: Consistent, Modifiable, Traceable, Ranked/Prioritized, Design-Independent.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for any 4 characteristics. Examiners *do* penalize for vague adjectives in the example.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S3 B.Tech IT]`

> **Differentiate between Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements (FR) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Describe *what* the system must do — the specific features, actions, or services it provides. | Describe *how well* the system performs those features — quality attributes and constraints. |
| **Also Called** | Behavioral or Capability requirements | Quality attributes or Quality of Service requirements |
| **Verifiability** | Verified by functional / black-box tests | Verified by performance, load, security, or usability tests against a quantitative metric |
| **Example** | *The system shall allow registered users to log in using a username and password.* | *The login request shall be authenticated and responded to within 2 seconds for 95% of all users under a load of 1000 concurrent sessions.* |

> [!NOTE]
> The NFR example must contain a **quantifiable threshold** to score full marks. Vague phrasings like "fast login" lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer / Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE Module Internal Choice mandates: **answer ONE full question from each module**, where each full question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, S3 B.Tech]`

> **(a)** Explain the IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 standard structure of a Software Requirements Specification. Enumerate all major sections with a brief description of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the various characteristics that a good SRS document should satisfy. Why is each characteristic important from an engineering and managerial perspective? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IEEE 830 / 29148 Standard Structure (7 marks):**

The IEEE 830 standard (and its successor IEEE 29148:2018) prescribes a **three-section structure** for the SRS document:

- **Section 1 — Introduction**
  - **1.1 Purpose** — Specifies the *intent* of the SRS, the intended audience, and the expected usage.
  - **1.2 Document Conventions** — Defines typographical or stylistic rules used throughout the document.
  - **1.3 Intended Audience and Reading Suggestions** — Identifies primary vs secondary readers and the recommended reading order.
  - **1.4 Scope of the Project** — Names the product, its objectives, and explicit *non-goals*.
  - **1.5 References** — Lists all referenced documents including higher-level specifications, standards, and related SRS docs.

- **Section 2 — Overall Description**
  - **2.1 Product Perspective** — States whether the product is *new*, an *enhancement*, a *replacement*, or a *member of a product family*. Identifies external system interfaces.
  - **2.2 Product Functions** — High-level summary of major features; the *what* in plain language.
  - **2.3 User Class and Characteristics** — Profiles of the users: education, skill, experience.
  - **2.4 Operating Environment** — Hardware, OS, network, and any other platform constraints.
  - **2.5 Design and Implementation Constraints** — Mandated languages, frameworks, policies, regulations.
  - **2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies** — External factors assumed to hold; dependencies on third-party systems.

- **Section 3 — Specific Requirements (the engineering heart)**
  - **3.1 External Interface Requirements** — User, hardware, software, and communication interfaces.
  - **3.2 Functional Requirements** — Detailed, numbered, testable specification of every function.
  - **3.3 Performance Requirements** — Quantitative thresholds for response time, throughput, capacity.
  - **3.4 Design Constraints** — Standards compliance, resource limits, mandated design patterns.
  - **3.5 Software System Attributes (Quality Attributes)** — Reliability, availability, security, maintainability, portability, usability.
  - **3.6 Other Requirements** — Database, legal, installation, training, operations, and maintenance.

A diagrammatic view of this structure is given in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**(b) Characteristics of a Good SRS (7 marks):**

| # | Characteristic | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Correct** | Wrong requirements build the wrong product — no amount of testing can recover. |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | Ambiguity is the single largest source of design errors and contract disputes. |
| 3 | **Complete** | Incomplete SRS causes scope creep and missed stakeholder needs. |
| 4 | **Consistent** | Inconsistencies between requirements force impossible design decisions. |
| 5 | **Ranked / Prioritized** | Enables trade-off decisions and incremental delivery in Agile/large projects. |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | If a requirement cannot be tested, it cannot be guaranteed to be satisfied. |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | Change is inevitable; the SRS structure must allow controlled updates. |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Enables impact analysis, change management, and regulatory compliance. |
| 9 | **Design-Independent** | Premature design in the SRS restricts architectural freedom and inflates cost. |

**Engineering & managerial significance:** Collectively, these characteristics transform the SRS from a *wishlist* into a *contractual, executable, and auditable engineering artifact*. A high-quality SRS reduces rework cost (per Boehm's 1:10:100:1000 ratio), shortens test-design time, simplifies change management, and provides a single source of truth that aligns customers, developers, and testers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, S4 B.Tech]`

> **(a)** Explain the FURPS+ model of classifying non-functional requirements. List each category with one practical example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)? Explain forward traceability and backward traceability. Why is traceability important in software projects? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) FURPS+ Model (7 marks):**

The **FURPS+** model, developed by Robert Grady at Hewlett-Packard, is a widely-adopted taxonomy for classifying *non-functional* (quality) requirements. The acronym stands for:

- **F — Functional requirements**
  - *Definition:* Core capabilities and features of the system.
  - *Example:* The system shall generate a monthly sales report in PDF format.
- **U — Usability**
  - *Definition:* Ease of learning, operation, and accessibility of the user interface.
  - *Example:* A first-time user shall be able to complete a checkout transaction in 5 clicks or fewer.
- **R — Reliability**
  - *Definition:* Ability to perform without failure under stated conditions for a given period.
  - *Example:* The system shall achieve 99.95% availability, equivalent to ≤ 4.38 hours of downtime per year.
- **P — Performance**
  - *Definition:* Quantitative response and throughput behaviour of the system.
  - *Example:* The search feature shall return results within 1.5 seconds for 95% of queries against a 1-million-record index.
- **S — Supportability**
  - *Definition:* Ease of maintenance, localization, configuration, installation, and testing.
  - *Example:* All diagnostic logs shall be exportable in JSON format for ingestion by a SIEM tool.

**The "+" extension** covers categories that the original acronym does not explicitly include:
- **Design constraints** — e.g., *"The system shall be implemented in Java 17."*
- **Implementation requirements** — e.g., coding standards, libraries, frameworks.
- **Interface requirements** — e.g., API contracts, communication protocols.
- **Physical requirements** — e.g., hardware size, weight, power consumption (relevant for embedded systems).

**(b) Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) (7 marks):**

A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is a tabular artifact (typically maintained in a spreadsheet, database, or dedicated ALM tool) that establishes a **direct, two-way mapping** between requirements and the design, code, and test artifacts derived from them.

**Forward Traceability:**

- Direction: *Requirement → Design → Code → Test Case*
- Purpose: Ensures that every requirement is *realized* by at least one design element, implemented in at least one code module, and verified by at least one test case.
- Use case: Detects **missing implementation** — a requirement exists but no test exists for it.

**Backward Traceability:**

- Direction: *Test Case → Code → Design → Requirement*
- Purpose: Ensures that every design element, code module, and test case can be traced back to a *legitimate* originating requirement.
- Use case: Detects **scope creep / gold-plating** — code or test exists for which no requirement is documented.

**Importance of Traceability in Software Projects:**

1. **Impact Analysis:** When a requirement changes, the RTM reveals *exactly* which design elements, code modules, and test cases are affected.
2. **Coverage Verification:** Confirms that *all* requirements have been implemented and tested (a regulatory requirement in safety-critical domains such as avionics, medical devices, and automotive).
3. **Defect Localization:** When a test fails, the RTM helps identify the *set of requirements* potentially impacted.
4. **Audit & Compliance:** Industries like healthcare (FDA), finance (SOX), and aviation (DO-178C) mandate traceable development.
5. **Project Management:** Provides objective progress metrics — the percentage of requirements designed, coded, or tested at any moment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose up to 3 marks):**
> 1. **Conflating traceability with version control.** RTM tracks *requirements-to-artifact* links, not file history.
> 2. **Treating RTM as a one-time document.** It must be **updated continuously** as requirements evolve.
> 3. **Forgetting "+" in FURPS+.** Examiners specifically test whether students know the *extension* categories.
> 4. **Omitting quantitative thresholds** in NFR examples. "Fast", "secure", "reliable" are worth 0; "responds in 1.5 s for 95% of requests" earns full marks.
> 5. **Confusing Verification with Validation.** Verification = "building the product right"; Validation = "building the right product."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this as your **final-night revision sheet** before the KTU ESE.

- **Definition:** SRS = a structured, formal document specifying what the system shall do.
- **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 Structure:** *3 sections* — Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements.
- **Nine Quality Characteristics:** *Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable, Design-Independent* (Mnemonic: **CUC-RVM-T-D** with Consistency folded in).
- **Functional vs Non-Functional:** FR = *what*; NFR = *how well* (with quantitative thresholds).
- **FURPS+:** Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability **+** Design constraints, Implementation, Interface, Physical.
- **EARS Patterns:** Ubiquitous, Event-driven, State-driven, Unwanted behaviour, Optional — the five syntactic templates for well-formed requirements.
- **Boehm's Cost-of-Fix Law:** Cost ratio across phases = **1 : 10 : 100 : 1000** (Requirements → Design → Implementation → Post-deployment).
- **RTM:** Bidirectional — *Forward* (Requirement → Test) and *Backward* (Test → Requirement).
- **Verification vs Validation:** *"Building the product right"* vs *"Building the right product."*
- **Specification Styles:** Structured Natural Language, Graphical (UML/DFD/STD), Formal (Z, VDM, Alloy), Executable (TLA+, Spec++).
- **Golden Rule:** *Specify the WHAT, not the HOW* — the SRS must be **design-independent**.
- **Why It Matters:** A well-engineered SRS cuts defect-fix cost by ~99.4% (per NSR derivation) and is the #1 determinant of project success.
- **Must-Use Sentence in Every KTU Answer:** *"A good SRS is a contract between stakeholders, developers, and testers — verifiable, unambiguous, complete, and traceable."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SRS Template
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SRS Template

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

The **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal, comprehensive technical document that completely describes the **functional**, **non-functional**, and **interface** requirements of a proposed software system. It is the official reference point for validation, verification, and design activities, and acts as a binding contract between the **developer** and the **customer/stakeholder**.

An **SRS Template** is the standardized skeletal structure (typically aligned with the **IEEE 830-1998** or **IEEE 29148-2011** standard) used by software engineers to consistently author these specifications across projects. It ensures that no critical requirement category is accidentally omitted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, the SRS template is positioned as a direct deliverable of the **Software Requirements Analysis** phase, and is explicitly tied to **CO2**: *"Apply the concepts of requirement engineering to develop a well-structured Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document."*

---

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy: "The Architectural Blueprint of Software"

Imagine you are commissioning the construction of a **custom house**. Before the masons lift a single brick, the architect produces a **detailed blueprint**. This blueprint:

| Blueprint Element | SRS Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Layout of rooms, doors, windows | Functional Requirements |
| Structural load limits | Performance Requirements |
| Electrical & plumbing wiring maps | External Interface Requirements |
| Constraints (budget, soil type, climate) | Design and Implementation Constraints |
| User profiles (elderly, children access) | User Classes and Characteristics |
| Assumptions (municipal water supply) | Assumptions and Dependencies |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a missing window on a blueprint can cause a room to be dark, a missing requirement in an SRS can cause the final software to be **incomplete, unsafe, or unusable**. The template acts as a **checklist** to ensure completeness.

### 📐 Standard Metrics in SRS Engineering

- **IEEE 830-1998** → The classical 5-section SRS standard.
- **IEEE 29148-2011** → The modern successor standard aligned with systems engineering.
- **FURPS+ Model** (Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability) by Hewlett-Packard — a popular alternative for requirements categorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧩 The IEEE 830 SRS Template — Section-by-Section Breakdown

#### **Section 1: Introduction**
This section orients the reader to the document and the project.

1. **1.1 Purpose** → Identifies *why* the SRS exists and the audience it serves.
2. **1.2 Document Conventions** → Defines typography, abbreviations, and any special styling.
3. **1.3 Intended Audience and Reading Suggestions** → Maps sections to reader roles (managers, developers, testers).
4. **1.4 Scope of the Project** → States goals, benefits, and a clear definition of the *product* (e.g., *"Library Management System v1.0"*).
5. **1.5 References** → Cites parent documents, standards, and dependencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Without a clear scope, the project suffers from **"scope creep"** — the single largest cause of project failure in industry.

#### **Section 2: Overall Description**
Describes the *context* of the system without enumerating specific functional requirements.

- **2.1 Product Perspective** → Is it a new product, replacement, or member of a product family?
- **2.2 Product Functions** → A high-level summary of major capabilities.
- **2.3 User Classes and Characteristics** → Differentiates users (e.g., *Admin*, *Registered Student*, *Guest*).
- **2.4 Operating Environment** → Hardware, OS, network, and software platform details.
- **2.5 Design and Implementation Constraints** → Language restrictions, regulatory compliance (e.g., **GDPR**, **HIPAA**).
- **2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies** → Things assumed true (e.g., *"The system assumes a constant internet connection"*).

#### **Section 3: External Interface Requirements**
Specifies *how* the software interacts with the outside world.

- **3.1 User Interfaces** → GUI layouts, input formats, validation rules.
- **3.2 Hardware Interfaces** → Sensors, printers, barcode readers.
- **3.3 Software Interfaces** → DBMS versions, third-party APIs (e.g., payment gateways).
- **3.4 Communication Interfaces** → Protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, TCP/IP, MQTT).

#### **Section 4: System Features (Functional Requirements)**
The heart of the SRS. Each feature is detailed with:
- **Stimulus / Response** logic.
- **Pre-conditions** and **Post-conditions**.
- **Use Case** cross-references.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each functional requirement must be **verifiable**, **unambiguous**, **complete**, **consistent**, **traceable**, and **modifiable** — the famous **"Six Quality Goals of a Good Requirement"** by IEEE.

#### **Section 5: Other Non-Functional Requirements**
The categories where the **FURPS+** model becomes essential:

- **5.1 Performance Requirements** → Response time, throughput, capacity.
- **5.2 Safety Requirements** → Failure tolerance, redundancy.
- **5.3 Security Requirements** → Authentication, encryption standards.
- **5.4 Software Quality Attributes** → Maintainability index, reliability (MTBF).
- **5.5 Business Rules** → Organizational policies encoded in software.

---

### 🧪 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **quality metrics** used to evaluate an SRS document in KTU university viva and lab evaluations.

| Metric / Property | Definition | Target Threshold |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Correctness** | Every requirement represents something the system *must* do | 100% coverage of stakeholder needs |
| **Unambiguity** | Only one interpretation possible | Zero subjective adjectives (e.g., "fast") |
| **Completeness** | All responses to stimuli defined | 100% scenario coverage |
| **Consistency** | No conflicting requirements | Zero conflicts (verified by traceability matrix) |
| **Traceability** | Forward & backward links to design/test | Bidirectional matrix maintained |
| **Modifiability** | Easy to update with change requests | Single, numbered requirement IDs |
| **Verifiability** | A test can be written for it | No terms like *"should handle"*, only *"shall handle"* |
| **Effort Estimation** | Approximate review time per page | $\text{Hours} \approx 0.5 \times \text{pages} \times \text{reviewers}$ |

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Safety-Critical industries** (Avionics — DO-178C, Medical Devices — IEC 62304, Automotive — ISO 26262), the SRS is a **legally binding artifact**. A flawed requirement in an Air Traffic Control system or a Pacemaker firmware can cost human lives. Hence, the SRS template's standardization is not academic bureaucracy — it is a **risk mitigation framework**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Building an SRS for a Case Study

### 📋 Case Study: **Online Railway Reservation System (ORRS)**

We will progressively derive each section of the SRS template by applying the *why → how → what* logic.

---

### **Step 1: Deriving the Scope (Section 1.4)**

**Logic:** The scope must be *one paragraph*, answering: *What is the system, who is it for, and what is its boundary?*

$$
\text{Scope} = \{\text{Product Name} \cup \text{Primary Actors} \cup \text{Core Capability} \cup \text{Exclusions}\}
$$

**Derived Scope Statement:**
> *"The **Online Railway Reservation System (ORRS)** is a web-based application designed for **passengers** and **administrators** to search train schedules, reserve/cancel tickets, and generate e-tickets. The system **excludes** catering services and physical payment collection."*

> The exclusion clause is critical — it **prevents scope creep** and protects the development team from assuming unpaid extra work.

---

### **Step 2: Identifying User Classes (Section 2.3)**

**Logic:** Group users by *role*, *frequency of use*, and *skill level*.

| User Class | Role | Skill Level | Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Guest** | Browse schedules, register | Novice | Occasional |
| **Registered Passenger** | Book, cancel, view history | Moderate | Regular |
| **Admin** | Manage trains, schedules, fares | Expert | Daily |
| **Maintenance Staff** | Backup, audit logs | Expert | Weekly |

---

### **Step 3: Deriving Functional Requirements (Section 4)**

For each feature, apply the **EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax)** pattern:

$$
\text{Requirement} = \underbrace{\text{The}}_{\text{Article}} \, \underbrace{\text{System}}_{\text{Subject}} \, \underbrace{\text{shall}}_{\text{Modal Verb}} \, \underbrace{\text{<action>}}_{\text{Behavior}} \, \underbrace{\text{when <trigger>}}_{\text{Stimulus}}
$$

**Example Derivation for "Ticket Booking":**

| Step | Component | Filled Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Subject | "The ORRS" |
| 2 | Modal Verb | "shall" |
| 3 | Action | "deduct the fare, generate a unique PNR, and send a confirmation email" |
| 4 | Stimulus | "when a registered passenger submits valid payment credentials" |
| 5 | Pre-condition | "User is logged in and seat availability is positive" |
| 6 | Post-condition | "PNR is added to the database; seat count decrements by 1" |

**Final EARS-formatted Requirement:**
> *"The ORRS **shall** deduct the fare, generate a unique PNR, and send a confirmation email **when** a registered passenger submits valid payment credentials, **provided** seat availability is positive and the user is authenticated."*

---

### **Step 4: Deriving Non-Functional Requirements (Section 5)**

For performance, we specify **quantifiable metrics** instead of qualitative terms.

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Transactions}}{\text{Second}} \quad ; \quad \text{Response Time} = T_{request \rightarrow response}
$$

| Non-Functional Category | Qualitatively Written (❌ Bad) | Quantitatively Written (✅ Good) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Performance | "The system should be fast." | "Search results shall be returned within **2 seconds** for 95% of queries under 10,000 concurrent users." |
| Reliability | "The system should rarely crash." | "The system shall achieve an **MTBF** of at least **720 hours**." |
| Security | "Login should be secure." | "Passwords shall be hashed using **bcrypt** with a cost factor of 12." |
| Scalability | "Should handle many users." | "Shall support horizontal scaling up to **50 application server nodes**." |

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from qualitative to quantitative is the **single most important quality check** in an SRS. Examiners award full marks only when numbers and units are explicitly stated.

---

### **Step 5: Building the Traceability Matrix**

Each requirement gets a unique ID, and downstream artifacts (design modules, test cases) reference it.

$$
T_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if requirement } R_i \text{ is linked to test case } TC_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

| Req ID | Design Module | Test Case ID | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **FR-001** | BookingEngine.java | TC-001, TC-002 | Verified |
| **FR-002** | CancellationService.java | TC-003 | Verified |
| **NFR-001** | LoadBalancer.config | TC-004 | Pending |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Coverage $= \frac{\sum T_{ij}}{\text{Number of Requirements}}$. An SRS with coverage $< 100\%$ is deemed **incomplete** by KTU valuation standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1: SRS Template Document Hierarchy (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["SRS Document"]
    node2["1. Introduction"]
    node3["2. Overall Description"]
    node4["3. External Interfaces"]
    node5["4. System Features"]
    node6["5. Non-Functional Requirements"]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    node1 --> node6

    node2 --> subA["1.1 Purpose"]
    node2 --> subB["1.2 Scope"]
    node2 --> subC["1.3 References"]

    node3 --> subD["2.1 User Classes"]
    node3 --> subE["2.2 Operating Env"]
    node3 --> subF["2.3 Constraints"]

    node4 --> subG["3.1 UI"]
    node4 --> subH["3.2 Hardware I F"]
    node4 --> subI["3.3 Software I F"]

    node5 --> subJ["4.1 FR 001 Booking"]
    node5 --> subK["4.2 FR 002 Cancellation"]
    node5 --> subL["4.3 FR 003 Refund"]

    node6 --> subM["5.1 Performance"]
    node6 --> subN["5.2 Security"]
    node6 --> subO["5.3 Reliability"]
```

---

### 🔄 Diagram 2: Functional Requirement Lifecycle (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Stakeholder Need"] --> B["Elicitation"]
    B --> C["Analysis and Negotiation"]
    C --> D["SRS Document Section 4"]
    D --> E["Design Specification"]
    E --> F["Implementation"]
    F --> G["Verification and Testing"]
    G --> H["Validation against SRS"]
    H --> I["Change Request"]
    I --> C
```

> The closed loop in the diagram represents the **iterative nature** of requirements engineering. The SRS is never truly "frozen" — it is a **living document**.

---

### 🧭 Diagram 3: SRS Authoring Process (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start1["Identify Stakeholders"]
    start2["Gather Use Cases"]
    start3["Draft Functional Reqs"]
    start4["Draft Non Functional Reqs"]
    start5["Review with QA Team"]
    start6["Baseline and Version Lock"]
    start7["Distribute to Dev Team"]

    start1 --> start2
    start2 --> start3
    start3 --> start4
    start4 --> start5
    start5 --> start6
    start6 --> start7
    start7 --> start1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define the term "Software Requirements Specification (SRS)" and list any four quality attributes that a good SRS document must possess.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> An SRS is a formal document that completely describes the **functional and non-functional requirements** of a software system, serving as a contract between the customer and developer (1 mark).
> Four quality attributes (1/2 mark each):
> 1. **Correctness** — accurately represents stakeholder needs.
> 2. **Unambiguity** — has only one possible interpretation.
> 3. **Completeness** — covers all scenarios.
> 4. **Verifiability** — a test can confirm it.
> 5. *(Also acceptable: Consistency, Modifiability, Traceability)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Functional Requirements (FR):** Describe *what the system does* — specific behaviors under specific inputs. Example: *"The system shall allow a user to reset a password via email."* (1.5 marks)
> - **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** Describe *how well* the system performs — quality attributes and constraints. Example: *"The password reset email shall be delivered within 30 seconds."* (1.5 marks)

---

### 📚 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[Modeled on KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Explain the structure of an SRS document as per the **IEEE 830 standard**. Describe in detail the contents of the **"Introduction"** and **"Overall Description"** sections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **Hospital Management System (HMS)**, draft at least **three Functional Requirements** and **three Non-Functional Requirements** in a properly formatted SRS style. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### **Part (a) — IEEE 830 SRS Structure (7 Marks)**

The IEEE 830 standard prescribes a **five-part structure** for the SRS:

**1. Introduction (2 marks)** — sets the context.
- **Purpose:** Why the document exists and the intended audience.
- **Document Conventions:** Typography, abbreviations, term definitions.
- **Intended Audience & Reading Suggestions:** Maps sections to roles (managers read Section 1, developers read Section 4).
- **Scope of the Project:** Defines product name, goals, and explicit boundaries.
- **References:** Lists standards, parent documents, and dependencies.

**2. Overall Description (2 marks)** — provides the system's "big picture".
- **Product Perspective:** New product / replacement / family member.
- **Product Functions:** High-level capability summary.
- **User Classes & Characteristics:** Differentiated user roles.
- **Operating Environment:** Platform, OS, network, hardware.
- **Design & Implementation Constraints:** Language, regulatory limits.
- **Assumptions & Dependencies:** External factors taken as true.

**3. External Interface Requirements (1 mark)**
- User, Hardware, Software, and Communication interfaces.

**4. System Features (1 mark)**
- Detailed Functional Requirements with stimulus-response logic.

**5. Other Non-Functional Requirements (1 mark)**
- Performance, Safety, Security, Quality Attributes, Business Rules.

---

#### **Part (b) — HMS Requirements Sample (7 Marks)**

**Functional Requirements (3.5 marks — 1 mark each + 0.5 for format):**

- **FR-HMS-001:** *"The HMS shall allow a registered Doctor to view the complete medical history of a patient when the patient ID is provided and the doctor is authenticated."*
- **FR-HMS-002:** *"The HMS shall generate a unique bill ID and deduct the patient's account balance when an Admin confirms a billing transaction."*
- **FR-HMS-003:** *"The HMS shall send an automated SMS alert to the registered mobile number of a patient 24 hours prior to a scheduled appointment."*

**Non-Functional Requirements (3.5 marks):**

- **NFR-HMS-001 (Performance):** *"The system shall display patient records within 1.5 seconds for 95% of queries under 500 concurrent users."*
- **NFR-HMS-002 (Security):** *"All patient health records shall be encrypted using AES-256 both at rest and in transit."*
- **NFR-HMS-003 (Availability):** *"The system shall maintain 99.9% uptime, equivalent to a maximum of 8.77 hours of downtime per year."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** in this question by:
> 1. Writing **qualitative** NFRs like *"The system should be fast"* instead of quantitative ones with **specific numbers and units**.
> 2. Using **"may"** or **"should"** instead of the **mandatory modal verb "shall"** in IEEE 830.
> 3. Forgetting to include **unique IDs** (e.g., FR-HMS-001) — traceability is a key grading criterion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[Modeled on KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **characteristics of a good SRS** as defined by IEEE. Explain any **four** characteristics with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **User Classes** in an SRS? Identify at least **four user classes** for a **University Examination Management System (UEMS)** and describe their characteristics. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### **Part (a) — Characteristics of a Good SRS (7 Marks)**

A high-quality SRS, as defined by IEEE 830 / 29148, exhibits the following properties:

1. **Correct** (1.5 marks) — Every requirement reflects an actual stakeholder need.
   *Example:* If customers want password reset, the SRS must state it, not omit it.
2. **Unambiguous** (1.5 marks) — Only one interpretation is possible.
   *Example:* Instead of *"fast response"*, use *"within 2 seconds"*.
3. **Complete** (1.5 marks) — All responses to all inputs are described, including error cases.
   *Example:* Login failure must specify the message and next allowed action.
4. **Consistent** (1 mark) — No internal contradictions.
   *Example:* FR-001 says "max 8 users", but Section 2.1 says "supports 50 users" → contradiction.
5. **Verifiable** (1 mark) — A test or inspection can confirm it.
6. **Traceable** (0.5 marks) — Each requirement has a unique ID and is linked to design and test artifacts.

#### **Part (b) — User Classes for UEMS (7 Marks)**

A **User Class** in the SRS is a category of users differentiated by *role, skill level, and frequency of use*. It is critical for designing role-based access and UI personalization. (2 marks for definition)

| User Class | Role | Skill Level | Frequency | Characteristics (1.25 marks each) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Student** | View hall tickets, results | Novice | Per semester | Needs intuitive UI, mobile access, multilingual support |
| **Faculty** | Upload marks, attendance | Moderate | Daily | Requires bulk upload, validation, draft-save features |
| **Exam Controller** | Approve results, manage exams | Expert | Daily | Needs audit trails, role delegation, conflict resolution |
| **External Auditor** | Read-only report access | Expert | Occasionally | Needs secure login, printable PDFs, time-bound access |

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> - [ ] **SRS = Contract** between customer and developer. Always define **scope, audience, and purpose** first.
> - [ ] **IEEE 830-1998** → 5 sections (Introduction, Overall Description, Interfaces, System Features, Non-Functional).
> - [ ] **IEEE 29148-2011** → Modern successor; integrates systems & software requirements.
> - [ ] Use the **modal verb "shall"** — never "should", "may", or "would".
> - [ ] Every requirement needs a **unique ID** (e.g., FR-001, NFR-002) for **traceability**.
> - [ ] **Functional** = *what* the system does; **Non-Functional** = *how well* it does it.
> - [ ] **FURPS+** is a high-yield alternative framework to remember.
> - [ ] Quantify everything — *fast* becomes *2 seconds for 95% of queries under 10K users*.
> - [ ] **Traceability Matrix** maps each requirement to design modules and test cases — coverage must be **100%**.
> - [ ] **EARS pattern** (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax) is a KTU-favored way to write clean requirements.
> - [ ] Remember the **Six Quality Goals**: Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Verifiable, Modifiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Requirements Validation** is the process of ensuring that the software requirements specification (SRS) accurately reflects the *real-world needs* of the stakeholders, the system, and the problem domain. It is the **"Are we building the RIGHT product?"** activity in the software engineering lifecycle.

According to the **IEEE Standard 830-1998** and **Sommerville's Software Engineering (10th Edition)** paradigm adopted in the KTU 2024 scheme, Requirements Validation is performed **after** the requirements have been elicited, analyzed, negotiated, and documented in the **SRS (Software Requirements Specification)** document, but **before** the design phase begins. Its objective is to **discover defects, omissions, ambiguities, and inconsistencies** in the documented requirements so that they can be rectified before propagating into the design and implementation phases.

## 1.2 The Validation vs. Verification Distinction

This is one of the **most frequently tested 3-mark questions** in KTU university examinations. Students MUST internalize this difference:

| Dimension | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Asked** | Are we building the product **RIGHT**? | Are we building the **RIGHT** product? |
| **Focus** | Conformance to specifications | Conformance to real-world needs |
| **Phase** | Throughout development (design, code, testing) | Mostly at requirements phase |
| **Artifacts Checked** | Code, design documents, test cases | SRS, user needs, prototypes |
| **Performed By** | Developers, QA testers | Stakeholders, end-users, business analysts |
| **Key Output** | Defect reports (bugs) | Requirement defect logs, change requests |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a Custom House**
>
> Imagine you commission an architect to build your house. **Requirements Elicitation** is when you tell the architect "I want 3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, a modular kitchen, and a garden." The architect drafts the blueprint. **Requirements Validation** is the step where the architect sits down with you, shows the blueprint, and asks: *"Does this match your vision? Is anything missing? Is the kitchen's size correct? Can the plumber actually run pipes through this layout?"* If you approve, only then do construction crews (developers) begin work. If the architect skips validation and starts building, the homeowner might end up with a swimming pool where the garden was supposed to be — a **costly rework** that could have been avoided with a simple review meeting.

## 1.4 Position in the Requirements Engineering Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus places Requirements Validation as the **final stage** of the Requirements Engineering process, after:

1. Requirements **Elicitation** (gathering)
2. Requirements **Analysis** (negotiation & modeling)
3. Requirements **Documentation** (SRS creation)
4. **Requirements Validation** ← *Current Topic*
5. Requirements **Management** (change control)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
