<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Checklist

## 2.1 Properties That a Validated SRS Must Possess

During the validation process, the requirements are checked against the following **eight quality properties**. These are recurring examination questions:

- **Correctness**: Every requirement represents what the customer actually needs. No fabricated or assumed features.
- **Completeness**: All possible scenarios (including error and exception paths) are documented. No `TBD` (To Be Determined) placeholders should remain in a validated SRS.
- **Consistency**: No requirement contradicts another. For example, if R1 says "System shall support 1000 concurrent users" and R2 says "System shall support 500 concurrent users," the SRS is **inconsistent**.
- **Unambiguity**: Each requirement has exactly one interpretation. Use of precise, quantified language (e.g., "within 2 seconds" instead of "fast").
- **Feasibility**: Requirements can realistically be implemented within the known constraints of budget, time, technology, and skill.
- **Verifiability**: Each requirement can be objectively verified. You must be able to write a test case for it.
- **Traceability**: Each requirement has a unique identifier (e.g., `FR-001`, `NFR-PERF-03`) and can be traced forward to design/code and backward to its source.
- **Modifiability**: The SRS is structured so that changes can be made consistently and completely.

## 2.2 Six Canonical Requirements Validation Techniques

The KTU syllabus (Sommerville-aligned) lists six main validation techniques. Examiners **frequently** ask for an "Explain any 4 techniques" for 14 marks.

### 2.2.1 Technique 1: Requirements Reviews
- The SRS is distributed to a panel of reviewers (stakeholders, developers, QA, customers).
- They examine the document offline and submit comments.
- A formal meeting is chaired by a moderator.
- A scribe records the action items.
- **Strength**: Catches defects early at very low cost. **Weakness**: Reviewers may not read the document thoroughly ("review fatigue").

### 2.2.2 Technique 2: Walkthroughs
- The **author** of the SRS walks the audience through the document, explaining each requirement.
- The audience asks clarifying questions and identifies defects.
- **Distinction from Review**: In a walkthrough, the author drives the session. In an inspection (see below), a trained moderator (not the author) drives it.

### 2.2.3 Technique 3: Inspections (Fagan Inspections)
- The most **formal** and **rigorous** of the manual techniques.
- Introduced by **Michael Fagan** at IBM in 1976.
- **Six-stage process**: Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up.
- **Roles**: Moderator, Author, Reader, Scribe, Checker.
- Defects are logged in a standardized classification (severe, major, minor).
- **Strength**: Up to 80% of defects can be removed. **Weakness**: Time-consuming and requires trained personnel.

### 2.2.4 Technique 4: Prototyping
- An **executable model** (throwaway or evolutionary) is built to validate uncertain requirements.
- Users interact with the prototype and provide feedback.
- Particularly useful for validating **UI/UX requirements** and requirements that are hard to express in text (e.g., "the system should feel responsive").
- **Strength**: Highest user engagement. **Weakness**: Users may mistake the prototype for the final product and develop unrealistic expectations.

### 2.2.5 Technique 5: Test-Case Generation (Requirements-Based Testing)
- The requirements are used to **derive test cases**.
- If a test case cannot be written for a requirement, that requirement is likely **non-verifiable** or **ambiguous** — flag it for revision.
- This technique operationalizes the "verifiability" property.

### 2.2.6 Technique 6: Formal Methods (Mathematical Validation)
- Requirements are expressed in a **formal specification language** (e.g., **Z notation**, **VDM**, **B-Method**, **Alloy**).
- Mathematical proofs and model checking are used to verify properties like consistency and completeness.
- Used in **safety-critical systems** (avionics, medical devices, railway signaling).
- **Strength**: Absolute rigor. **Weakness**: Requires specialized mathematicians; not scalable for typical business applications.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it has appeared in KTU ESE papers multiple times.**

| Technique | Formality | Cost | Best Used For | Verifiability Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reviews | Low–Medium | Low | General defects, large SRS | Medium |
| Walkthroughs | Low | Low | Knowledge transfer, training | Low–Medium |
| Inspections (Fagan) | **High** | Medium | Safety-critical, regulated domains | **High** |
| Prototyping | Medium | Medium | UI/UX, ambiguous requirements | High (user-facing) |
| Test-Case Generation | Medium | Low | Ambiguity, verifiability check | **High** |
| Formal Methods | **Very High** | **Very High** | Safety-critical embedded systems | **Absolute** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Requirements validation is the **single highest-ROI activity** in the entire software development lifecycle. Industry studies (Boehm, 1981; NASA SEL studies) consistently show that a defect caught during the **requirements phase** costs approximately **$25–$100**, while the same defect caught during system testing costs **$10,000–$100,000**, and during operational use can cost **millions** (especially in safety-critical systems). This is known as the **1:10:100 Rule** (or the **Rule of Ten**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Rule of Ten** (Cost Amplification Factor): The cost of fixing a defect grows by an order of magnitude at each successive phase. Validating requirements is therefore the most economically rational activity in software engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Validation Checklist

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Validation Workflow

Below is the canonical step-by-step process followed during a requirements validation exercise. Every step must be completed before the SRS is signed off for design.

### Step 1: Pre-Validation Preparation
The requirements document is finalized (no draft watermarks), version-controlled, and distributed to all reviewers at least **48–72 hours** before the formal review meeting. The agenda, defect log template, and a list of attendees are circulated.

### Step 2: Individual Review
Each reviewer reads the SRS independently and notes any defects against the **eight quality properties** (correctness, completeness, consistency, unambiguity, feasibility, verifiability, traceability, modifiability). This is the **most critical** step — groupthink is avoided by enforcing solitude.

### Step 3: Validation Meeting / Inspection
A time-boxed meeting (typically **2 hours**) is held. A moderator enforces discipline:
- Defects are **described**, not solutions.
- Each defect is logged with a unique ID, severity, and the requirement reference.
- The scribe captures all entries.
- No design or implementation discussions are allowed.

### Step 4: Rework and Resolution
The author of the SRS addresses each logged defect by either:
- **Accepting** and modifying the requirement,
- **Rejecting** with justification (e.g., out of scope),
- **Deferring** to a future release.

### Step 5: Follow-Up and Sign-Off
The moderator verifies that all accepted defects have been resolved, and the **Configuration Control Board (CCB)** formally approves the SRS as the **baseline** for design. The SRS is now **frozen** — any subsequent changes must go through formal change management.

## 3.2 The Validation Decision Matrix

A requirements validation session typically applies the following decision logic to each requirement:

$$
\text{Requirement State} = f(\text{Correctness}, \text{Completeness}, \text{Consistency}, \text{Verifiability})
$$

For each requirement, a four-quadrant analysis is performed:

| | **Verifiable** | **Not Verifiable** |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistent** | Approve (Baseline) | Reject — Rewrite with measurable criteria |
| **Inconsistent** | Reject — Reconcile with conflicting requirements | Reject — Fundamental redraft required |

The mathematical expression of the validation cost-benefit is given by:

$$
\text{Validation ROI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{\text{fix-later}}^{(i)} - C_{\text{fix-now}}^{(i)} \right)}{C_{\text{validation-effort}}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{fix-later}}^{(i)}$ = Cost of fixing requirement defect $i$ if discovered in the testing or operations phase.
- $C_{\text{fix-now}}^{(i)}$ = Cost of fixing requirement defect $i$ during validation.
- $C_{\text{validation-effort}}$ = Total effort expended on validation (meetings, reviews, prototypes).
- $n$ = Total number of defects caught during validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry data: A well-run validation program typically yields an ROI between **5× and 30×**. A negative ROI indicates either too-late detection or a poorly scoped validation effort.

## 3.3 The Requirements Validation Checklist (Operational)

For quick reference during KTU practical examinations and viva voce:

1. Have all stakeholders reviewed the SRS?
2. Are all requirements uniquely identified?
3. Are all performance constraints quantified (e.g., latency in **ms**, throughput in **TPS**, capacity in **concurrent users**)?
4. Are external interfaces (APIs, hardware, protocols) fully specified?
5. Are error-handling and exception flows documented?
6. Are security, privacy, and compliance requirements (e.g., **GDPR**, **PCI-DSS**) explicitly listed?
7. Is the glossary free of undefined acronyms?
8. Have all `TBD`/`TBC`/`TBS` markers been resolved or escalated?
9. Can every requirement be traced to a business objective or stakeholder need?
10. Has a prototype been signed off by at least **3 representative end-users**?
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Requirements Validation Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end validation workflow, showing how defects are routed back for rework and how the SRS eventually reaches baseline status.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([SRS Document Drafted]) --> prepNode[Pre-Validation Preparation]
    prepNode --> distNode[Distribute SRS to Reviewers]
    distNode --> indivNode[Individual Review by Stakeholders]
    indivNode --> decisionNode{Defects Found?}
    decisionNode -- Yes --> logNode[Log Defects in Defect Register]
    logNode --> meetingNode[Validation Meeting / Inspection]
    meetingNode --> reworkNode[Author Reworks Defective Requirements]
    reworkNode --> recheckNode{All Defects Resolved?}
    recheckNode -- No --> reworkNode
    recheckNode -- Yes --> signoffNode[Configuration Control Board Review]
    signoffNode --> signDecision{Sign-Off Approved?}
    signDecision -- No --> logNode
    signDecision -- Yes --> baselineNode([SRS Baselined - Frozen for Design])
    decisionNode -- No --> baselineNode
```

## 4.2 Validation Techniques — Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the six canonical validation techniques to their applicability, showing how manual techniques, prototype-based techniques, and formal techniques form three distinct families.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph manualFamily[Manual Validation Techniques]
        rev[Requirements Reviews]
        walk[Walkthroughs]
        insp[Fagan Inspections]
    end
    subgraph execFamily[Executable Validation Techniques]
        proto[Prototyping]
        testgen[Test-Case Generation]
    end
    subgraph formalFamily[Formal Validation Techniques]
        znotation[Z Notation and VDM]
        alloyM[Alloy and B-Method]
    end
    srsInput[Validated SRS Document] --> manualFamily
    srsInput --> execFamily
    srsInput --> formalFamily
    manualFamily --> qualityCheck{Checks Correctness, Completeness, Consistency, Unambiguity}
    execFamily --> qualityCheck
    formalFamily --> qualityCheck
    qualityCheck --> outputNode[Defect Log and Change Requests]
```

## 4.3 Position of Validation in the Software Lifecycle

This diagram shows where Requirements Validation sits relative to the other phases of the requirements engineering process and the broader software development lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elicitNode[Requirements Elicitation] --> analyzeNode[Requirements Analysis and Negotiation]
    analyzeNode --> docNode[SRS Documentation]
    docNode --> validateNode[Requirements Validation]
    validateNode --> manageNode[Requirements Management]
    manageNode --> designNode[Software Design]
    designNode --> codeNode[Coding and Unit Testing]
    codeNode --> testNode[Integration and System Testing]
    testNode --> deployNode[Deployment and Maintenance]
    validateNode -. Feedback Loop .-> elicitNode
    testNode -. Defect Feedback .-> validateNode
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Requirements Validation. How does it differ from Requirements Verification?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Requirements Validation is the process of ensuring that the documented requirements (SRS) actually represent the **real-world needs** of the stakeholders and that the software being specified will meet those needs. It answers the question *"Are we building the **RIGHT** product?"*

It differs from Verification in the following ways:

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question** | Are we building the product **RIGHT**? | Are we building the **RIGHT** product? |
| **Focus** | Conformance to specifications | Conformance to user needs |
| **Timing** | Throughout development (design, code) | Primarily at the requirements stage |
| **Performed by** | Developers and QA team | Stakeholders, end-users, and analysts |
| **Output** | Bug reports, test results | Defect logs, change requests, prototype feedback |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for clear distinction)*

---

### Question 2: List any four properties that a validated SRS must possess.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A validated SRS must possess the following properties:

1. **Correctness** – The requirements must accurately represent stakeholder needs without any fabricated or assumed features.
2. **Completeness** – All possible scenarios, including error and exception paths, must be documented. No `TBD` markers should remain.
3. **Consistency** – No two requirements should contradict each other. (For instance, the system cannot simultaneously support 500 and 1000 concurrent users.)
4. **Verifiability** – Every requirement must be objectively testable; a test case must be derivable for each one.

*(3 marks: ½ mark each for the first four properties with one-line justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Explain the Six Requirements Validation Techniques in Detail

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Explain Requirements Reviews, Walkthroughs, and Inspections. (7 Marks)

**1. Requirements Reviews (2 marks)**
The SRS is distributed to a panel comprising stakeholders, developers, QA engineers, and customer representatives. Reviewers examine the document **offline** and submit written comments. A formal meeting is then chaired by a **moderator**, who enforces discipline and ensures discussions remain focused on defect identification. A **scribe** records all action items. Reviews are cost-effective and catch many defects early, but suffer from *review fatigue* and uneven reviewer engagement.

**2. Walkthroughs (2 marks)**
In a walkthrough, the **author of the SRS drives the session**, presenting each requirement to the audience and explaining the rationale. The audience (developers, testers, users) asks clarifying questions and raises issues. Unlike a review, the walkthrough is **author-led** and is often used for knowledge transfer to new team members. It is informal and best suited for early-stage documents.

**3. Inspections / Fagan Inspections (3 marks)**
The most **formal** of the manual techniques, introduced by **Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976**. The process has **six phases**: Planning, Overview, Individual Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, and Follow-up. Distinct **roles** are defined: **Moderator** (drives the meeting, not the author), **Author** (the writer of the SRS), **Reader** (paraphrases requirements), **Scribe** (logs defects), and **Checker** (verifies completeness of the inspection). Defects are classified by severity (severe, major, minor). Empirical studies show that Fagan inspections can remove up to **80% of defects** before the design phase, making them indispensable in safety-critical domains (avionics, medical devices, nuclear systems).

#### (b) Explain Prototyping, Test-Case Generation, and Formal Methods as Validation Techniques. (7 Marks)

**1. Prototyping (3 marks)**
An **executable model** of the system is built to validate uncertain, ambiguous, or UI-heavy requirements. There are two main types:
- **Throwaway Prototyping**: The prototype is built solely for validation and discarded once requirements are confirmed.
- **Evolutionary Prototyping**: The prototype evolves into the final system.

End-users interact with the prototype and provide concrete feedback that often reveals hidden requirements or misunderstood workflows. Prototyping is particularly powerful for **non-functional** requirements such as usability, responsiveness, and aesthetic feel, which are difficult to express in textual SRS documents. The drawback is that users may mistake the prototype for the final product, leading to **unrealistic expectations** regarding functionality and polish.

**2. Test-Case Generation (2 marks)**
This technique uses the requirements to **derive test cases** *before* any code is written. If a test case cannot be written for a requirement, that requirement is flagged as **non-verifiable** or **ambiguous** and sent back for revision. For example, a requirement stating *"The system should be user-friendly"* cannot be tested objectively and must be rewritten with measurable criteria (e.g., *"A new user shall complete the checkout flow in ≤ 3 attempts"*). This technique operationalizes the *verifiability* property and forces the analyst to think like a tester.

**3. Formal Methods (2 marks)**
Requirements are expressed in a **mathematical specification language** such as **Z notation**, **VDM (Vienna Development Method)**, **B-Method**, or **Alloy**. Mathematical proofs and model checkers are used to verify properties like **consistency, completeness, and refinement correctness**. Formal methods are mandatory in **safety-critical industries** (DO-178C in avionics, IEC 61508 in industrial control, FDA guidance in medical devices). They provide **absolute rigor** but require specialized expertise and are not economically viable for typical business applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Discuss the Various Checks Performed During Requirements Validation. How Do These Checks Ensure a Quality SRS?

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Discuss the Major Validation Checks: Correctness, Completeness, Consistency, and Unambiguity. (7 Marks)

**1. Correctness Check (2 marks)**
Each requirement is verified against its source — be it a stakeholder interview transcript, a business objective, a regulatory mandate, or a use-case diagram. A requirement is "correct" only if it accurately reflects a real, validated need. For instance, if no stakeholder ever asked for biometric login, including it as a requirement would be **incorrect** even if technically well-formed.

**2. Completeness Check (2 marks)**
The SRS must cover **all** functional scenarios, all user roles, all input domains, all error conditions, and all non-functional aspects (performance, security, usability, maintainability). A common incompleteness symptom is the presence of `TBD` (To Be Determined) markers in the document. In a validated SRS, no `TBD` should remain; every marker must either be resolved or formally escalated to a future release.

**3. Consistency Check (2 marks)**
The SRS must be **internally non-contradictory**. Inconsistencies typically arise when multiple stakeholders contribute conflicting requirements (e.g., Marketing says "1000 concurrent users," IT Operations says "500 concurrent users"). Consistency is also checked against external documents such as the system architecture, regulatory standards, and legacy system constraints.

**4. Unambiguity Check (1 mark)**
Each requirement must have **exactly one interpretation**. Ambiguity is the silent killer of software projects. Words like *"fast"*, *"easy"*, *"efficient"*, *"user-friendly"*, and *"intuitive"* are red flags. These must be replaced with quantified, measurable criteria (e.g., *"response time ≤ 200 ms under 500 concurrent users"*).

#### (b) Discuss the Remaining Validation Checks: Feasibility, Verifiability, Traceability, and Modifiability. (7 Marks)

**1. Feasibility Check (2 marks)**
The requirements are evaluated against known constraints: **budget, schedule, technology stack, team skills, regulatory environment, and infrastructure**. A requirement to process 10 million transactions per second on a 2-core VM is **infeasible** and must be renegotiated. Feasibility is often validated by building a **spike** (a time-boxed proof-of-concept) or consulting subject-matter experts.

**2. Verifiability Check (2 marks)**
Every requirement must be objectively testable. This is where the **test-case generation** technique is most powerful — if no test can be written, the requirement is unverifiable. For example, *"The system shall be highly reliable"* fails this check; *"The system shall have a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of ≥ 5,000 hours"* passes.

**3. Traceability Check (2 marks)**
Each requirement receives a **unique identifier** (e.g., `FR-014`, `NFR-SEC-03`). **Forward traceability** links each requirement to its corresponding design elements, code modules, and test cases. **Backward traceability** links each requirement to its origin (stakeholder request, regulatory clause, business goal). Traceability is essential for **impact analysis** during change management.

**4. Modifiability Check (1 mark)**
The SRS must be structured using a consistent template with clear section numbering, an index, a glossary, and a requirement numbering scheme. This allows changes to be made consistently without breaking cross-references. A modifiable SRS drastically reduces the cost of **change management** throughout the project lifecycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Validation with Verification**: Students often write *"validation is testing the code"* — this loses 2 marks immediately. Validation operates on **requirements**, not on code.
> 2. **Listing techniques without explaining them**: A bare list of "Reviews, Walkthroughs, Inspections, Prototyping" scores only 1–2 marks. Examiners want a **one-paragraph description** of each technique, including roles, strengths, and weaknesses.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Why"**: Do not just state that a property is checked. Explain **how** it is checked (e.g., consistency is checked using cross-reference matrices; verifiability is checked by attempting test-case generation).
> 4. **Ignoring Fagan Inspections**: This is a high-frequency KTU question. If your answer covers Reviews and Walkthroughs but skips Fagan, expect to lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Walkthrough vs. Inspection**: Walkthrough is **author-led**; Inspection is **moderator-led**. This is a classic trap.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Requirements Validation**
>
> - **Definition**: Validation = *"Are we building the RIGHT product?"* Verification = *"Are we building the product RIGHT?"*
> - **Position in Lifecycle**: Validation is the **final gate** of the requirements engineering process, occurring **after** SRS documentation and **before** software design.
> - **Eight Properties**: Correctness, Completeness, Consistency, Unambiguity, Feasibility, Verifiability, Traceability, Modifiability.
> - **Six Techniques**: Reviews, Walkthroughs, Fagan Inspections, Prototyping, Test-Case Generation, Formal Methods.
> - **Fagan Inspection**: The most formal manual technique. **Six phases**, **five roles** (Moderator, Author, Reader, Scribe, Checker). Authored by **Michael Fagan (IBM, 1976)**. Removes up to **80% of defects**.
> - **Walkthrough vs. Inspection**: Walkthrough is **author-led** (informal); Inspection is **moderator-led** (formal, structured).
> - **Prototyping**: Best for **UI/UX and ambiguous requirements**. Two types: **Throwaway** vs. **Evolutionary**.
> - **Formal Methods**: Used in **safety-critical systems**. Languages include **Z, VDM, B-Method, Alloy**. Provide mathematical rigor but are expensive.
> - **Rule of Ten**: A requirement defect fixed at validation is **10× cheaper** than fixing it in coding, **100× cheaper** than in system testing, and **1000× cheaper** than in production.
> - **Validation Output**: A **defect log**, a set of **change requests**, and ultimately a **baselined SRS** signed off by the Configuration Control Board.
> - **Examiner's Mantra**: Always state the **"Why"** behind each technique (e.g., *"Prototyping is used because it converts abstract requirements into a tangible artifact that users can critique concretely"*).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Change**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Requirements Change** is the formal process of identifying, evaluating, approving/rejecting, implementing, and tracking modifications to the documented functional, non-functional, or domain requirements of a software system **after** the baseline requirements specification (SRS document) has been formally approved.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Software Engineering syllabus (PCITT503 – Module 2)**, requirements change management is one of the most critical activities of the **Software Configuration Management (SCM)** discipline. It establishes a **controlled, traceable, and auditable** path for any modification request that may originate from stakeholders, developers, regulators, market conditions, or the operational environment.

The three foundational properties of a properly managed requirements change are:

1. **Traceability** — Every change must be linked to its source, author, and affected design/code/test artifacts.
2. **Controlled Evolution** — No change is implemented without formal approval from the **Change Control Board (CCB)**.
3. **Impact Awareness** — The cost, schedule, technical, and risk consequences of every change must be analyzed *before* approval.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a Custom House**
>
> Imagine you hire an architect to design your dream house. The architect draws the **blueprint (SRS)** and the builder starts laying the foundation. Halfway through construction, you say: *"I want the kitchen shifted to the east side, and I now need a swimming pool."*
>
> - The change is **inevitable** (just like in software — requirements never stay frozen).
> - If you simply walk to the site and shout instructions, chaos erupts — workers redo walls, plumbing gets misaligned, costs balloon.
> - Instead, a proper process would be: **submit a written change request → architect reviews impact (cost, time, structural integrity) → you approve/reject → builder updates the blueprint → work proceeds**.
>
> **Requirements Change Management in software engineering is exactly this written, reviewed, impact-analyzed process** — applied to software blueprints (SRS, design, code) instead of physical ones.

## 1.3 Why Requirements Change — The Five Universal Drivers

In KTU board examinations, the "**why**" of change is a frequently asked conceptual question. The five universally accepted drivers are:

| # | Driver | Description |
|---|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **New or Changed Stakeholder Needs** | Customers/users realize new business priorities as the system takes shape. |
| 2 | **Evolving Business Environment** | Market competition, regulations (e.g., GDPR, taxation rules), and technology shifts demand adjustments. |
| 3 | **Misunderstood or Incomplete Initial Requirements** | Requirements that were vague at elicitation time get clarified only during design/implementation. |
| 4 | **Technical Constraints Surfacing Late** | Performance, security, or hardware limitations discovered during coding/testing. |
| 5 | **Organizational/Project Restructuring** | Mergers, budget cuts, change in project sponsors, or re-prioritization. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Industry studies (notably the *Standish Group CHAOS Report* and the *Boehm Curve* on late defect cost) estimate that an **uncorrected requirements defect that escapes to the field can cost 50x to 200x more** to fix than one caught during the requirements phase. This is the very reason **formal change control** is non-negotiable in KTU-evaluated projects.

## 1.4 Categories of Requirements Change

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Primary Categories of Change (KTU Syllabus Mandatory):**

1. **Corrective Change** — Fixes a defect, error, or ambiguity in the original requirement (e.g., a missing exception condition).
2. **Adaptive Change** — Adjusts the requirement to align with the **changing external environment** (new government rule, new OS version, new customer segment).
3. **Enhancement / Perfective Change** — Adds new functionality or improves an existing requirement to increase user satisfaction or performance.
4. **Preventive Change** *(sometimes grouped separately)* — Made to **prevent future defects** (e.g., adding a security requirement to prevent anticipated threats).
5. **Emergency / Urgent Change** — Critical patches (security vulnerability, production outage) that bypass the normal lengthy CCB cycle through an **Emergency Change Advisory Board (ECAB)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Requirements Change Management Process (RCM)

A well-engineered RCM process (as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus and aligned with **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** and **ITIL Change Management** practices) follows a closed-loop, six-stage cycle:

### Stage 1 — Problem Identification & Change Request (CR) Origination

- Any stakeholder (customer, developer, tester, support team, regulator) raises a formal **Change Request (CR)**.
- The CR contains: unique **CR-ID**, requestor details, date, affected requirement(s), business justification, and priority hint.
- Logged into the **Requirements Management Tool (RMT)** — e.g., IBM DOORS, Jama Connect, Azure DevOps.

### Stage 2 — Change Categorization & Initial Triage

- Change is classified as **Minor / Major / Emergency**.
- Minor: typo fix, label change → handled by the Requirements Engineer.
- Major: affects scope, schedule, cost → escalated to **CCB**.
- Emergency: production-breaking → routed to **ECAB** (fast-track approval).

### Stage 3 — Impact Analysis (The Heart of RCM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact Analysis** is the most marks-yielding sub-topic in KTU 2024 exams.

Impact is evaluated across **five dimensions** (memorize the acronym **SCOPE-R**):

| Dimension | What is Analyzed | Typical Question Asked |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **S** — Scope | Which modules, features, requirements are affected? | *How many requirements need to be updated?* |
| **C** — Cost | Additional effort in person-hours or money | *What is the cost to design, code, test, deploy?* |
| **O** — Schedule | Effect on milestones, deadline slippage | *Will the release date move by N days?* |
| **P** — Performance/Quality | Effect on response time, reliability, usability | *Does the new requirement degrade throughput?* |
| **E** — Effort & Risk | Engineer availability, technical risk, training | *Do we have the skills? Is the change risky?* |
| **R** — Regression | Which existing test cases must be re-run? | *What is the regression test coverage?* |

### Stage 4 — Change Control Board (CCB) Decision

- The **CCB** is a cross-functional committee chaired typically by the **Project Manager** or **Configuration Manager**.
- Members: Requirements Engineer, System Architect, Lead Developer, QA Lead, Customer Representative, sometimes a Legal/Compliance officer.
- The CCB evaluates the impact report and chooses one of four outcomes:
  1. **Approve** (with conditions or as-is)
  2. **Defer** (postpone to a later release)
  3. **Reject** (with documented reasoning)
  4. **Request More Information** (sent back to the requestor)

### Stage 5 — Implementation

- Approved changes propagate through the **requirements → design → code → test** chain.
- **Bidirectional traceability links** must be updated to reflect the new state.
- New requirement version is baselined and old version is archived (never deleted).

### Stage 6 — Verification, Closure & Audit

- Implemented change is verified via unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing.
- The CR is formally **closed** in the RMT.
- **Configuration audit** ensures the deployed artifact matches the approved change.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every quantitative formula tested in KTU 2024 Scheme Module-2 questions on Requirements Change.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Change Approval Rate (CAR)** | $\text{CAR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{approved}}}{N_{\text{submitted}}} \times 100$ | All counts are integers; result in % | Measures CCB selectivity |
| 2 | **Change Rejection Rate (CRR)** | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{rejected}}}{N_{\text{submitted}}} \times 100$ | Integers; result in % | Useful for stakeholder negotiation |
| 3 | **Average Change Effort (ACE)** | $\text{ACE} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{n}$ person-hours | $E_i$ = effort of $i$-th change, $n$ = total changes | Indicates engineering load |
| 4 | **Requirements Volatility Index (RVI)** | $\text{RVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | All $R$ are requirement counts; result in % | **Most cited KTU formula**; $R_{\text{baseline}}$ = size of original SRS |
| 5 | **Cost of Change (CoC)** | $\text{CoC}(p) = C_{\text{req}} \cdot k^{p}$ | $C_{\text{req}}$ = cost to fix in requirements phase, $k$ = growth multiplier, $p$ = phase index | **Boehm's exponential cost-of-change model** |
| 6 | **Phase Index $p$** | $p = 0$ (Req) → $4$ (Post-deployment) | Discrete integer | Used in Boehm's formula |
| 7 | **Stability Index (SI)** | $\text{SI} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{changed}}}{R_{\text{baseline}} + R_{\text{added}}}$ | All counts; result is a ratio between **0 and 1** | Closer to 1 = stable; closer to 0 = volatile |
| 8 | **Requirement Density (RD)** | $\text{RD} = \dfrac{N_{\text{requirements}}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | $N_{\text{requirements}}$ count, KLOC = thousand lines of code | Used in productivity estimation |
| 9 | **Regression Test Re-execution Ratio** | $\text{RTR} = \dfrac{T_{\text{rerun}}}{T_{\text{total testcases}}} \times 100$ | All counts; result in % | Gauges impact of a change on test suite |
| 10 | **Defect Leakage to Field** | $\text{DLF} = \dfrac{D_{\text{field}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | All counts; result in % | Indirectly affected by poor change control |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** When writing absolute values or "such that" in tables, never type `|x|`. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — your table will break otherwise.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Medical (FDA / DO-178C):** Every requirements change must be **bi-directionally traced** to a test case; audits can be 100+ pages thick.
- **Banking & FinTech:** A change to a tax-calculation requirement must be propagated across **regulatory reporting, UI, and reconciliation modules** within tight windows.
- **DevOps + Agile:** Modern systems use *lightweight* change boards (e.g., pull-request review boards) instead of heavyweight CCBs — but the **discipline of impact analysis** remains the same.
- **AI/ML Pipelines:** A change in dataset schema triggers cascading retraining, validation, and fairness testing — a perfect software-engineering requirements-change scenario.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Requirements Volatility Index (RVI)

The **RVI** quantifies how "turbulent" the requirements have been compared to the originally baselined specification.

### Step 1 — Define the universe of considered changes

Let the original baseline requirements specification contain $R_{\text{baseline}}$ functional and non-functional requirements.

### Step 2 — Count the changes during a measurement window

During the analysis window (say, from start of project to current date), let:
- $R_{\text{added}}$ = number of brand-new requirements introduced.
- $R_{\text{modified}}$ = number of existing requirements that were edited.
- $R_{\text{deleted}}$ = number of requirements removed from the SRS.

### Step 3 — Formulate the raw change count

$$
N_{\text{change}} = R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}
$$

### Step 4 — Normalize by the baseline

To make the index **size-independent** (so that a 50-requirement project and a 5000-requirement project can be compared), divide by $R_{\text{baseline}}$:

$$
\text{RVI}_{\text{raw}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to percentage (KTU convention)

$$
\boxed{\text{RVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100 \text{ percent}}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Example (KTU-style)

A project started with $R_{\text{baseline}} = 200$ requirements. After 6 months, **30 new** requirements were added, **45 existing** ones were modified, and **10** were deleted. Compute the RVI.

$$
R_{\text{added}} = 30, \quad R_{\text{modified}} = 45, \quad R_{\text{deleted}} = 10
$$

$$
N_{\text{change}} = 30 + 45 + 10 = 85
$$

$$
\text{RVI} = \dfrac{85}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RVI} = 42.5 \text{ percent}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An RVI of 42.5% by month 6 is **moderately high** (healthy projects typically maintain RVI < 20% post-baseline). The KTU examiner will accept an RVI > 30% as a sign of poor elicitation or unstable scope.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boehm's Cost of Change (Exponential Model)

The cost of fixing a defect grows **exponentially** as the project progresses. This is the foundation of the "**fix-it-early**" principle taught in Module 2.

### Step 1 — Identify the base cost

Let $C_{\text{req}}$ be the cost to fix a defect (or implement a change) if caught in the **Requirements Phase** (phase index $p = 0$).

### Step 2 — Define the growth multiplier

Let $k$ be the multiplicative growth factor from one phase to the next. Industry-typical values:

| Phase transition | $k$ value |
|------------------|-----------|
| Requirements → Design | **2** |
| Design → Coding | **2** |
| Coding → Unit Test | **2** |
| Unit Test → Integration / System Test | **2** |
| System Test → Field | **5 to 10** |

For a clean teaching model, KTU typically uses $k = 2$ uniformly.

### Step 3 — Define phase indices

$$
p \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \text{where } \{0=\text{Req}, 1=\text{Design}, 2=\text{Code}, 3=\text{Test}, 4=\text{Field}\}
$$

### Step 4 — Formulate the cost function

At phase $p$, the cost of the same change is:

$$
\boxed{\text{CoC}(p) = C_{\text{req}} \cdot k^{p}}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example (KTU-style)

A defect is introduced during elicitation. If the cost to fix it in the requirements phase is $C_{\text{req}} = \text{INR } 500$, and the company uses $k = 2$, compute the cost of fixing the same defect at each subsequent phase.

$$
\text{Phase 0 (Requirements):} \quad \text{CoC}(0) = 500 \cdot 2^{0} = \text{INR } 500
$$

$$
\text{Phase 1 (Design):} \quad \text{CoC}(1) = 500 \cdot 2^{1} = \text{INR } 1{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Phase 2 (Coding):} \quad \text{CoC}(2) = 500 \cdot 2^{2} = \text{INR } 2{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Phase 3 (Testing):} \quad \text{CoC}(3) = 500 \cdot 2^{3} = \text{INR } 4{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Phase 4 (Field):} \quad \text{CoC}(4) = 500 \cdot 2^{4} = \text{INR } 8{,}000
$$

### Step 6 — Ratio insight (Board-favourite)

$$
\dfrac{\text{CoC}(\text{Field})}{\text{CoC}(\text{Requirements})} = \dfrac{2^{4}}{2^{0}} = 2^{4} = 16
$$

> [!TIP]
> **General rule to memorize:** With $k=2$ over 4 phase transitions, a defect is **16 times more expensive** to fix in the field than in the requirements phase. With $k = 5$ (real-world field), it can be **625 times** more expensive.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stability Index (SI)

The **Stability Index** is the *complement* of RVI, expressed as a **probability-like ratio** (0 to 1).

### Step 1 — Define the total requirements ever present

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{baseline}} + R_{\text{added}}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the change count

Use the same numerator as in RVI: $R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}$. However, only the *changes* to requirements that were *previously baselined* count for SI stability — modifications + deletions of original requirements.

Let us adopt the **KTU standard** form:

$$
R_{\text{changed}} = R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the formula

$$
\boxed{\text{SI} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}} + R_{\text{added}}}}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

Using the same numbers as in 3.1: $R_{\text{baseline}} = 200$, $R_{\text{added}} = 30$, $R_{\text{modified}} = 45$, $R_{\text{deleted}} = 10$.

$$
\text{SI} = 1 - \dfrac{45 + 10}{200 + 30} = 1 - \dfrac{55}{230}
$$

$$
\text{SI} = 1 - 0.2391 = 0.7609
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** SI ≈ 0.76 means the requirements are **76% stable** — the project is in a "maturing" phase, but still has 24% churn that should be tracked carefully.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — RVI vs SI vs RTR

| Metric | Measures | Range | Higher Means | Lower Means |
|--------|----------|-------|--------------|-------------|
| **RVI** | Volatility of SRS | $0$ to $\infty$ % (typically capped at 100) | High turbulence, poor baseline | Stable requirements |
| **SI** | Stability of SRS | $0$ to $1$ | Mature, frozen requirements | Volatile requirements |
| **RTR** | Test suite impact | $0$ to $100$ % | Change touched many tests (high risk) | Change was localized |
| **CAR** | CCB permissiveness | $0$ to $100$ % | Lenient CCB (may signal weak gatekeeping) | Strict CCB |
| **CoC** | Phase-wise fix cost | Increasing exponential | Late-stage fix (expensive) | Early-stage fix (cheap) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Hack:** Always compute and report **RVI and SI together** in long-answer questions — they tell the same story from two directions and fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Requirements Change Management Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Stakeholder Identifies Need for Change]) --> B[Raise Formal Change Request CR]
    B --> C[Log CR in Requirements Management Tool]
    C --> D{Triage by Configuration Manager}
    D -->|Minor| E[Requirements Engineer Implements Directly]
    D -->|Major| F[Conduct Impact Analysis SCOPE-R]
    D -->|Emergency| G[Emergency Change Advisory Board ECAB]
    F --> H[Prepare Impact Report]
    H --> I[Submit to Change Control Board CCB]
    I --> J{CCB Decision}
    J -->|Approve| K[Baseline Updated Requirement]
    J -->|Defer| L[Schedule for Future Release]
    J -->|Reject| M[Document Rejection Reason and Close CR]
    J -->|More Info| B
    G --> K
    E --> N[Propagate Change to Design Code Test]
    K --> N
    L --> O([CR Archived as Deferred])
    M --> P([CR Closed as Rejected])
    N --> Q[Update Traceability Links]
    Q --> R[Execute Regression Test Suite]
    R --> S{All Tests Pass?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Configuration Audit]
    S -->|No| B
    T --> U([CR Closed as Implemented])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note (for KTU):** The loop from `S` (test failure) back to `B` (new CR) represents the iterative refinement that makes requirements change management a **closed-loop quality process**, not a one-time gate.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Roles & Responsibilities in CCB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PM[Project Manager] --> CCB[Change Control Board]
    RE[Requirements Engineer] --> CCB
    SA[System Architect] --> CCB
    LD[Lead Developer] --> CCB
    QA[QA Lead] --> CCB
    CR[Customer Representative] --> CCB
    LG[Legal or Compliance Officer] --> CCB
    CCB --> DEC[Approve / Defer / Reject / Request Info]
    DEC --> RM[Repository of Baselines]
    RM --> AUD[Internal and External Audits]
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Life of a Single Change Request

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SH as Stakeholder
    participant RM as Requirements Mgmt Tool
    participant RE as Requirements Engineer
    participant CCB as Change Control Board
    participant DEV as Development Team
    participant QA as QA Team
    SH->>RM: Submit CR with ID and justification
    RM->>RE: Auto-notify triage
    RE->>RE: Categorize and prioritize
    RE->>CCB: Forward major CR with impact report
    CCB->>CCB: Review SCOPE-R analysis
    CCB-->>RM: Decision Approve/Defer/Reject
    RM->>DEV: Notify approved change
    DEV->>DEV: Update design, code, unit test
    DEV->>QA: Submit for system test
    QA->>QA: Run regression and new test cases
    QA-->>RM: Test result PASS or FAIL
    RM->>RM: Close CR and update audit log
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Five Drivers of Change vs Process Response

```mermaid

  root((Requirements Change))
    Drivers
```
