# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Requirements Change. List any four categories of requirements change.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Requirements Change is the formal, controlled process of modifying an approved baseline of the Software Requirements Specification (SRS) to reflect new, altered, or removed functional/non-functional needs of stakeholders, while preserving traceability, version history, and impact awareness.

> **Four Categories (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
>
> 1. **Corrective Change** — Fixes defects, ambiguities, or omissions in the original requirement.
> 2. **Adaptive Change** — Adapts the system to a changed external environment (regulation, OS, hardware).
> 3. **Enhancement (Perfective) Change** — Adds new features or improves existing ones.
> 4. **Preventive Change** — Made to forestall anticipated future defects or risks.

*(Emergency Change may also be accepted as the 4th.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Change Control Board (CCB)? Mention any two of its primary responsibilities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **Change Control Board (CCB)** is a formally constituted cross-functional committee responsible for **reviewing, evaluating, and deciding** on all major change requests raised against baselined software artifacts (requirements, design, code).

> **Two Primary Responsibilities (1 mark each):**
>
> 1. **Impact Review** — Evaluating the SCOPE-R (Scope, Cost, Schedule, Performance, Effort, Regression) impact of each change request before approval.
> 2. **Decision and Authorization** — Issuing one of four formal decisions: *Approve, Defer, Reject, or Request More Information* for every submitted CR, and recording the decision in the configuration management system.

*(Other acceptable answers: ensuring traceability, enforcing baselining rules, prioritizing changes against project goals.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Requirements Change Management Process** in detail. Describe the six stages with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software project started with a baseline of **400 requirements**. During the first 9 months, the following changes were recorded: **60 new requirements added, 80 existing requirements modified, and 20 requirements deleted**. Compute:
- (i) the **Requirements Volatility Index (RVI)**, and
- (ii) the **Stability Index (SI)** of the project.
Comment on the implications of the computed values on project health. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Requirements Change Management Process (7 marks)**

The RCM process is a **closed-loop, six-stage cycle** that ensures every modification to baselined requirements is justified, evaluated, approved, implemented, and verified. The six stages are:

**Stage 1 — Change Request Origination (1 mark)**
Any stakeholder (customer, developer, tester, support) raises a formal **Change Request (CR)** with a unique ID, justification, priority, and affected requirements. The CR is logged in a Requirements Management Tool (RMT) such as IBM DOORS or JIRA.

**Stage 2 — Triage and Categorization (1 mark)**
The Configuration Manager classifies the CR as **Minor, Major, or Emergency**. Minor CRs are routed to the Requirements Engineer; Major CRs proceed to full impact analysis; Emergency CRs are fast-tracked through an Emergency Change Advisory Board (ECAB).

**Stage 3 — Impact Analysis (1.5 marks)**
The impact is analyzed across the **SCOPE-R dimensions** — Scope, Cost, Schedule, Performance, Effort, Regression. A formal **Impact Report** is produced, quantifying the ripple effect of the proposed change on downstream artifacts (design, code, test cases, documentation).

**Stage 4 — CCB Decision (1 mark)**
The Change Control Board reviews the Impact Report and issues one of four decisions: **Approve, Defer, Reject, or Request More Information**. The decision is documented with reasoning and stored in the configuration baseline.

**Stage 5 — Implementation (1 mark)**
Approved changes are propagated through the **requirements → design → code → test** chain. Bi-directional traceability links are updated. The new requirement version is baselined; the old version is archived (never deleted).

**Stage 6 — Verification, Closure and Audit (1.5 marks)**
Implemented changes are validated through unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing. The CR is formally closed in the RMT. A **configuration audit** confirms the deployed artifact matches the approved change.

**Diagram (included in the answer script):**
*(Draw the flowchart from Section 4.1 of these notes — Stakeholder → CR → Triage → Impact Analysis → CCB → Implement → Test → Audit.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given:
$R_{\text{baseline}} = 400$, $R_{\text{added}} = 60$, $R_{\text{modified}} = 80$, $R_{\text{deleted}} = 20$.

**(i) Requirements Volatility Index (3.5 marks)**

$$
N_{\text{change}} = R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{change}} = 60 + 80 + 20 = 160
$$

$$
\text{RVI} = \dfrac{N_{\text{change}}}{R_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{RVI} = \dfrac{160}{400} \times 100 = 40 \text{ percent}
$$

**(ii) Stability Index (3.5 marks)**

$$
\text{SI} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}} + R_{\text{added}}}
$$

$$
\text{SI} = 1 - \dfrac{80 + 20}{400 + 60} = 1 - \dfrac{100}{460}
$$

$$
\text{SI} = 1 - 0.2174 = 0.7826
$$

**Implications on Project Health (within the 7-mark budget):**

- An **RVI of 40%** by month 9 is **high** and indicates substantial requirements turbulence. Industry-healthy projects aim for **RVI ≤ 20%** post-baseline.
- The **SI of 0.7826** confirms that about **78% of the requirements are stable**, leaving 22% still churning.
- **Conclusion:** The project suffers from **incomplete initial elicitation** or **unstable stakeholder priorities**. The project manager should:
  1. **Freeze scope** for the next iteration and conduct a formal re-baselining.
  2. **Improve elicitation techniques** (e.g., joint application development, prototyping) for the next release.
  3. **Tighten CCB gatekeeping** to prevent further uncontrolled changes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Explain **Boehm's Cost of Change Model** with a suitable diagram. The cost to fix a defect caught in the **Requirements Phase** is **INR 1,000**. If the multiplicative growth factor is **$k = 3$**, compute the cost of fixing the same defect in the **Design, Coding, Testing, and Field/Post-deployment** phases. State two engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Change Request (CR)**? Explain the **SCOPE-R** impact analysis framework with one example for each dimension. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Boehm's Cost of Change Model (7 marks)**

**Theory (3 marks):** Barry Boehm's empirical model states that the **relative cost of fixing a defect grows exponentially** as the software progresses through development phases. The cost at phase $p$ is given by:

$$
\boxed{\text{CoC}(p) = C_{\text{req}} \cdot k^{p}}
$$

where $C_{\text{req}}$ is the cost of fixing the defect if caught in the **Requirements Phase ($p = 0$)**, and $k$ is the multiplicative growth factor from one phase to the next. The standard phase indices are: Requirements (0) → Design (1) → Coding (2) → Unit/Integration Testing (3) → Field (4).

**Diagram (1 mark):** The classic *Boehm Curve* is a steeply rising curve with phase index $p$ on the X-axis and relative cost on the Y-axis. *(Draw a smooth upward-curving line passing through the points computed below.)*

**Computation (2 marks):** Given $C_{\text{req}} = \text{INR } 1{,}000$ and $k = 3$:

$$
\text{CoC}(1) \text{ [Design]} = 1000 \times 3^{1} = \text{INR } 3{,}000
$$

$$
\text{CoC}(2) \text{ [Coding]} = 1000 \times 3^{2} = \text{INR } 9{,}000
$$

$$
\text{CoC}(3) \text{ [Testing]} = 1000 \times 3^{3} = \text{INR } 27{,}000
$$

$$
\text{CoC}(4) \text{ [Field]} = 1000 \times 3^{4} = \text{INR } 81{,}000
$$

**Two Engineering Implications (1 mark each):**

1. **Invest Heavily in Early Phases:** Every rupee spent on requirements review, prototyping, and formal inspection can save up to **81 times** the cost of fixing the same defect in production. Therefore, rigorous requirements engineering and design reviews are highly cost-effective.
2. **Aggressive Regression and Continuous Testing:** Since even a small field defect can cost orders of magnitude more, organizations must invest in **automated regression testing, continuous integration, and canary releases** to catch defects as early as possible.

**(b) Change Request and SCOPE-R Framework (7 marks)**

**Change Request Definition (1 mark):** A **Change Request (CR)** is a formal, documented proposal to modify a baselined software artifact (requirement, design, code, test). It contains: unique CR-ID, requestor identity, date of submission, affected artifact reference, business justification, proposed solution, and priority hint.

**SCOPE-R Framework (6 marks — 1 mark per dimension + example):**

| Dimension | Meaning | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **S — Scope** | Which requirements, modules, or features are affected? | A request to add "biometric login" affects the **Authentication module** and the **User Profile screen**. |
| **C — Cost** | Additional money/person-hours required | Adding biometric login needs **2 developers × 3 weeks = 240 person-hours**, plus hardware (fingerprint sensor drivers) procurement cost of **INR 50,000**. |
| **O — Schedule** | Effect on milestones and release date | Release date slips by **2 weeks** because biometric integration overlaps with the planned security audit. |
| **P — Performance** | Effect on system quality attributes | Biometric matching adds **~150 ms latency** to the login flow, breaching the NFR of *login under 1 second*. |
| **E — Effort & Risk** | Skills needed, technical risks | Risk: third-party SDK has GPL licence — *legal conflict with proprietary code*. Mitigation: switch to MIT-licensed SDK. |
| **R — Regression** | Existing test cases that must be re-run | The **250-test regression suite for the Authentication module** must be re-executed; estimated 18 hours of CI time. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
>
> 1. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables** — the table will break visually. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead.
> 2. **Forgetting the "$+ R_{\text{added}}$" in the SI denominator** — leads to a wrong Stability Index and loses 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Confusing RVI numerator** — students often use only $R_{\text{modified}}$ instead of $R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}$. The full triplet is mandatory.
> 4. **Not showing the units** (percent for RVI; dimensionless ratio for SI) — losing 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark answers** — the KTU board mandates a diagram for the process question; without it, expect a 1–1.5 mark deduction.
> 6. **Mixing up SCOPE-R letters** — memorize the acronym; examiners are strict about correctness in impact-analysis answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month KTU Revision Sheet — Module 2 / Requirements Change**

- **Definition:** Requirements Change is a *controlled, traceable, audited* modification to a baselined SRS.
- **Three Pillars:** Traceability + Controlled Evolution + Impact Awareness.
- **Five Drivers:** Stakeholder needs, business environment, misunderstood requirements, late technical constraints, organizational change.
- **Five Categories:** Corrective, Adaptive, Enhancement, Preventive, Emergency.
- **Six-Stage Process:** Origination → Triage → Impact Analysis → CCB Decision → Implementation → Verification/Closure.
- **CCB:** Cross-functional board; four formal decisions: Approve / Defer / Reject / Request Info.
- **SCOPE-R:** Scope, Cost, Schedule, Performance, Effort, Regression — *the* impact-analysis framework.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\text{RVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{added}} + R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ %
  - $\text{SI} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{modified}} + R_{\text{deleted}}}{R_{\text{baseline}} + R_{\text{added}}}$
  - $\text{CAR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{approved}}}{N_{\text{submitted}}} \times 100$ %
  - $\text{CoC}(p) = C_{\text{req}} \cdot k^{p}$  (Boehm's exponential model)
- **Healthy Project Targets:** RVI < 20%, SI > 0.80, CAR between 40–70% (neither too lenient nor too strict).
- **Tools to name in answers:** IBM DOORS, Jama Connect, Azure DevOps, JIRA, Polarion.
- **Never delete old baselines** — archive them; configuration audit may require them years later.
- **Bidirectional traceability is non-negotiable** in regulated domains (aerospace, medical, banking).
- **Boehm's insight to quote in essays:** "A rupee saved in requirements is a rupee multiplied by 16–625 in the field."
- **Common exam trap:** Stability Index is **not** the inverse of RVI; they use *different denominators* — be careful.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Requirements Analysis** is the software engineering phase that bridges *inception* (the fuzzy understanding of what is needed) and *construction* (the actual building). It is the disciplined, iterative process of studying the **user's problem domain**, discovering **functional and non-functional requirements**, **negotiating priorities**, **modelling the requirements** using precise representations, and producing a validated **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** that becomes the legal and engineering contract for the project.

In IEEE 29148:2018 terminology adopted by KTU, a *requirement* is a:
- **Condition or capability** that must be met or possessed by a system, system element, product, or service to satisfy an agreement, standard, specification, or other formally imposed documents.
- **Documented representation** of such a condition or capability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly groups under Module 2:
> 1. Requirements Determination (Fact-Finding Techniques)
> 2. Requirements Modelling (DFD, ER, UML Use-Case & Class)
> 3. SRS Document Preparation
> 4. Requirements Validation & Negotiation

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Architect" Intuition

Imagine you want to build a house. You don't call the mason first. You sit with an **architect** who:

| Step in House Building | Parallel Step in Requirements Analysis |
|---|---|
| Meet the family, ask how they live, count rooms | **Fact-Finding** (interviews, observation, questionnaires) |
| Sketch floor plans, plumbing layout | **Modelling** (DFD, ER, Use-Case diagrams) |
| Negotiate "should we add a swimming pool or extra bedroom?" | **Requirements Negotiation & Prioritisation** |
| Produce a final blueprint signed by the client | **SRS Document (IEEE 830/29148)** |
| Verify the blueprint satisfies the family | **Requirements Validation** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "F-M-N-S-V":**
> **F**act-Finding → **M**odelling → **N**egotiation → **S**pecification → **V**alidation.

## 1.3 Why Requirements Analysis Matters — The *1-10-100 Rule*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A requirement defect that costs **₹1** to fix during analysis costs **₹10** during coding and **₹100** (or more) after deployment. The Standish CHAOS Report (2023) identifies **incomplete or poorly defined requirements** as the single largest cause of project failure, accounting for **over 42%** of all failed projects.

## 1.4 Core Types of Requirements

```
REQUIREMENTS
├── Functional Requirements (FR)        → WHAT the system does
│   ├── Business rules
│   ├── Data processing
│   ├── Authentication / Authorisation
│   └── External interface behaviour
└── Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)  → HOW WELL it does it
    ├── Performance (response time, throughput)
    ├── Reliability (MTBF, availability %)
    ├── Usability (learnability, satisfaction)
    ├── Maintainability
    ├── Portability
    ├── Security (CIA triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)
    └── Compliance / Standards
```

A third category is **Domain Requirements** (constraints derived from the application domain, e.g., "must comply with RBI banking regulations").

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "fast" or "user-friendly" as non-functional requirements. These are **vague**. NFRs must be **quantifiable**: "The system shall return search results within **2 seconds** for a database of 1 million records on a standard broadband connection."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Requirements Engineering Process

### Stage 1 — Problem Recognition (Domain Understanding)
- The analyst builds a **mental model** of the problem domain by talking to users, reading existing systems, and observing workflows.
- Output: A **problem statement** and glossary of domain terms.

### Stage 2 — Evaluation & Synthesis
- Information is **restructured** to identify the real (latent) needs hidden behind stated (manifest) needs.
- Conflicts between stakeholders are identified and grouped.
- Use the **5W + 1H** heuristic: *Who, What, When, Where, Why, How*.

### Stage 3 — Modelling
- A representation of the requirements is built using graphical and formal languages.
- This is the "thinking tool" — it exposes gaps and inconsistencies early.
- Tools: **DFDs, ER Diagrams, UML Use-Case, Class, Sequence, Activity diagrams**.

### Stage 4 — Review / Negotiation
- Proposed requirements are prioritised, conflicts are negotiated, and risks are noted.
- **MoSCoW** prioritisation: *Must, Should, Could, Won't (this release)*.

### Stage 5 — Specification
- A formal **SRS document** is produced (IEEE 830 / 29148 template).

## 2.2 Fact-Finding Techniques — The Analyst's Toolkit

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Theory:** Five techniques form the syllabus core. Examiners love a tabular comparison.

| # | Technique | Type | When to Use | Key Strength | Key Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Interviews** | Formal / Informal | Deep, sensitive information | Rich qualitative data | Time-consuming, biased |
| 2 | **Questionnaires / Surveys** | Quantitative | Many geographically distributed users | Statistical breadth | Low response rate, no follow-up |
| 3 | **Direct Observation** | Active / Passive | Manual, undocumented processes | Reveals real (vs. stated) workflow | Hawthorne effect |
| 4 | **Document / Artifact Analysis** | Qualitative | Existing manual/paper system | Authoritative data | May be outdated |
| 5 | **Joint Application Development (JAD)** | Group workshop | Complex multi-stakeholder projects | Consensus building | Needs skilled facilitator |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 14-Mark Questions:** Always justify *why* you chose a particular technique. "I used a structured interview with the bank manager because the requirements are sensitive and require deep clarification, but supplemented it with questionnaires to 200 account holders for statistical breadth."

## 2.3 The IEEE 29148 SRS Document Template (Formerly IEEE 830)

| Section | Purpose |
|---|---|
| 1. Introduction | Purpose, scope, definitions, references, overview |
| 2. Overall Description | Product perspective, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions |
| 3. Specific Requirements | Functional, performance, design constraints, software attributes |
| 4. Verification | How each requirement will be verified (test, inspection, demo) |
| Appendices | Use cases, data dictionaries, glossary |

## 2.4 Non-Functional Requirement Categories (FURPS+)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FURPS+** is the canonical KTU-expected NFR framework. Memorise the acronym.

- **F** — Functional (though arguably FR)
- **U** — Usability (human factors, aesthetics)
- **R** — Reliability (frequency of failure, recoverability, predictability)
- **P** — Performance (response time, throughput, efficiency)
- **S** — Supportability (maintainability, configurability, localisability)
- **+** — Design constraints, implementation, interface, physical (hardware)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Requirements Analysis is a **concept-dominant** topic, but a few quantitative formulas appear in KTU ESE papers. Memorise the table below.

| # | Metric / Formula | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Function Point (FP) Count** | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | Size measure of functional requirements |
| 2 | **Unadjusted FP** | $UFP = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \sum_{j} W_{ij} \cdot C_{ij}$ | Sum of weight × count per component |
| 3 | **Value Adjustment Factor** | $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{k=1}^{14} F_k$ | Where $F_k \in [0, 5]$ for 14 GSC's |
| 4 | **Use Case Points (UCP)** | $UCP = (UUCW + UAW) \times TCF \times EF$ | OO analogue of FP |
| 5 | **Unadjusted Use Case Weight** | $UUCW = \sum w_i \cdot n_i$ | 3 weight classes (Simple=5, Average=10, Complex=15) |
| 6 | **Technical Complexity Factor** | $TCF = 0.6 + 0.01 \times \sum T_i$ | 13 technical factors, each $T_i \in [0, 5]$ |
| 7 | **Environmental Factor** | $EF = 1.4 + (-0.03 \times \sum E_i)$ | 8 environmental factors, each $E_i \in [0, 5]$ |
| 8 | **Requirements Volatility** | $RV = \frac{\#\text{Changed requirements}}{\#\text{Total requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Stability indicator |
| 9 | **Requirements Stability Index** | $RSI = \frac{\#\text{Initial requirements}}{\#\text{Initial + Changed requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Better metric than churn |
| 10 | **Risk Priority Number** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA — Severity × Occurrence × Detection |
| 11 | **Requirements Completeness** | $C = \frac{\#\text{Specified requirements}}{\#\text{Identified requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Quality metric |
| 12 | **Effort from FP** | $Effort = FP \times Productivity^{-1}$ (person-months) | Cost estimation baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the pipe character `\|x\|` inside markdown tables.** Use `\vert x \vert` for absolute value. The vertical bar breaks markdown table parsers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** DO-178C compliance demands fully traceable, validated requirements.
- **Healthcare:** IEC 62304 mandates SRS-level documentation for any Class B/C medical software.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 functional safety requires NFRs to be quantified (ASIL levels).
- **Banking:** RBI and SEBI require auditable, version-controlled requirements.
- **IoT/Embedded:** Hardware-software co-design starts with rigorous requirements modelling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Function Point (FP) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A library management system has the following components. Compute the **Unadjusted Function Point (UFP)** and the **Adjusted FP (AFP)**.

| Component | Optimistic | Likely | Pessimistic | Assigned (UFP Weight) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| External Inputs (EI) | 4 | 5 | 6 | **3** (Average) |
| External Outputs (EO) | 2 | 3 | 4 | **4** (Average) |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 1 | 2 | 3 | **3** (Simple) |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 1 | 1 | 2 | **7** (Average) |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 0 | 1 | 1 | **5** (Simple) |

The 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs) are rated (0–5):

$$
F_1 = 3,\; F_2 = 2,\; F_3 = 4,\; F_4 = 2,\; F_5 = 3,\; F_6 = 1,\; F_7 = 2
$$
$$
F_8 = 3,\; F_9 = 2,\; F_{10} = 1,\; F_{11} = 2,\; F_{12} = 0,\; F_{13} = 1,\; F_{14} = 2
$$

### Step 1 — Final Count per Component Type
Use the **likely** value (three-point estimation).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{\text{count}} &= 5 \\
EO_{\text{count}} &= 3 \\
EQ_{\text{count}} &= 2 \\
ILF_{\text{count}} &= 1 \\
EIF_{\text{count}} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — UFP Contribution Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{\text{contrib}} &= 5 \times 3 = 15 \\
EO_{\text{contrib}} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \\
EQ_{\text{contrib}} &= 2 \times 3 = 6 \\
ILF_{\text{contrib}} &= 1 \times 7 = 7 \\
EIF_{\text{contrib}} &= 1 \times 5 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total UFP

$$
\begin{aligned}
UFP &= EI + EO + EQ + ILF + EIF \\
    &= 15 + 12 + 6 + 7 + 5 \\
    &= 45
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum of GSC Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{14} F_k &= 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 0 + 1 + 2 \\
                   &= 28
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Value Adjustment Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAF &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_k \\
    &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times 28 \\
    &= 0.65 + 0.28 \\
    &= 0.93
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Adjusted Function Point (AFP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFP &= UFP \times VAF \\
    &= 45 \times 0.93 \\
    &= 41.85 \approx 42
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Effort Estimation (Productivity = 6.5 FP / person-month)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Effort &= \frac{AFP}{Productivity} = \frac{42}{6.5} \approx 6.46 \text{ person-months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always show the **weight table** with the formula. Showing only the final number earns partial credit. The standard IFPUG 4.x weight table is mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Use Case Points (UCP)

### Problem
A new e-commerce system has:
- **3 Simple** use cases, **5 Average** use cases, **2 Complex** use cases.
- Technical Factors ($T_i$) total = **38**
- Environmental Factors ($E_i$) total = **18**

### Step 1 — UUCW Calculation

Weights: Simple=5, Average=10, Complex=15.

$$
\begin{aligned}
UUCW &= (3 \times 5) + (5 \times 10) + (2 \times 15) \\
     &= 15 + 50 + 30 \\
     &= 95
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — UAW (Assume 8 actors: 2 Simple, 4 Average, 2 Complex; weights 1, 2, 3)

$$
\begin{aligned}
UAW &= (2 \times 1) + (4 \times 2) + (2 \times 3) \\
    &= 2 + 8 + 6 \\
    &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — TCF

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCF &= 0.6 + 0.01 \times \sum T_i \\
    &= 0.6 + 0.01 \times 38 \\
    &= 0.98
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — EF

$$
\begin{aligned}
EF &= 1.4 + (-0.03 \times \sum E_i) \\
   &= 1.4 + (-0.03 \times 18) \\
   &= 1.4 - 0.54 \\
   &= 0.86
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — UCP

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCP &= (UUCW + UAW) \times TCF \times EF \\
    &= (95 + 16) \times 0.98 \times 0.86 \\
    &= 111 \times 0.98 \times 0.86 \\
    &= 93.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Effort (Productivity ≈ 20 person-hours / UCP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Effort &= 93.5 \times 20 = 1870 \text{ person-hours} \approx 9.35 \text{ person-months}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Requirements Quality Attributes

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this matrix for KTU ESE.** Examiners ask 7-mark questions comparing quality criteria.

| Quality Attribute | Definition | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Each requirement represents something the system must do | Inspection, traceability matrix |
| **Unambiguous** | Has exactly one consistent interpretation | Peer review, formal grammar check |
| **Complete** | All user needs are specified, including error cases | Checklist, scenario walkthrough |
| **Consistent** | No conflicts with other requirements | Cross-reference analysis |
| **Ranked (Prioritised)** | Importance and stability stated | MoSCoW / Kano |
| **Verifiable** | A test can confirm satisfaction | Test case design |
| **Modifiable** | Changes can be made consistently and completely | Change impact analysis |
| **Traceable** | Origin and downstream derivation known | Traceability matrix |

## 3.4 Requirements Validation Techniques

| Technique | Purpose | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Reviews / Walkthroughs** | Group reading for defects | Mention author, moderator, scribe roles |
| **Inspections** | Formal Fagan inspection | State the 6 stages: Planning → Overview → Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up |
| **Prototyping** | Build throw-away UI to validate | Mention horizontal vs vertical prototypes |
| **Acceptance Test Cases** | Derive from requirements | Link 1:1 to FRs |
| **Model Checking** | Formal verification of specifications | State tool examples (SPIN, NuSMV) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Requirements Engineering Process Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Problem Recognition\nDomain Familiarization] --> A1[Fact Finding\nInterviews, Observation,\nQuestionnaires, Documents]
    A1 --> A2[Evaluation and Synthesis\nIdentify Conflict, Group,\nPrioritize Latent Needs]
    A2 --> A3[Modelling\nDFD, ER, Use Case,\nClass and Activity Diagrams]
    A3 --> A4[Review and Negotiation\nMoSCoW Priority,\nResolve Conflict]
    A4 --> A5{SRS Document\nIEEE 830 or 29148}
    A5 --> A6[Requirements Validation\nReviews, Inspections,\nPrototyping, Testing]
    A6 -->|Defects Found| A2
    A6 -->|Validated| A7[Approved SRS\nContract Baseline]
    A7 --> A8[Requirements Management\nVersioning, Traceability,\nChange Control]
```

---

## 4.2 UML Use-Case Diagram — Library Management System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Member[Member Actor]
    Librarian[Librarian Actor]
    System[Library System]
    UC1((Issue Book))
    UC2((Return Book))
    UC3((Search Catalog))
    UC4((Add New Book))
    UC5((Generate Overdue Report))
    UC6((Manage Member Account))

    Member --> UC1
    Member --> UC2
    Member --> UC3
    Librarian --> UC3
    Librarian --> UC4
    Librarian --> UC5
    Librarian --> UC6

    UC1 -.includes.-> UC3
    UC2 -.extends.-> UC5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use-Case Diagram Conventions (KTU Examiner checks):**
> - Actors are **stick figures or named rectangles outside the system boundary**
> - Use cases are **ellipses**
> - **Solid line** = association, **dashed arrow** = `\<\<include\>\>` or `\<\<extend\>\>`
> - System boundary is a **rectangle** enclosing all use cases

---

## 4.3 Data Flow Diagram — Context Diagram (Level 0) for ATM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Customer[Customer Actor] -->|PIN, Card, Request| ATMSYS[ATM Software System]
    ATMSYS -->|Cash, Receipt, Message| Customer
    ATMSYS -->|Transaction Request| BankDB[(Central Bank Database)]
    BankDB -->|Account Update| ATMSYS
    ATMSYS -->|Audit Log| AuditStore[(Audit Storage)]
```

---

## 4.4 ER Diagram — Hospital Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PATIENT[Patient Entity] -- admits --> ADMISSION[Admission Entity]
    DOCTOR[Doctor Entity] -- attends --> ADMISSION
    ADMISSION -- assigned to --> WARD[Ward Entity]
    PATIENT -- generates --> BILL[Bill Entity]
    DOCTOR -- writes --> PRESCRIPTION[Prescription Entity]
    PRESCRIPTION -- contains --> MEDICINE[Medicine Entity]
    ADMISSION -- has --> ROOM[Room Entity]
```

---

## 4.5 Requirements Negotiation & Priority Resolution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Stakeholder A Submits Requirement] --> N2{Conflict with B?}
    N2 -- Yes --> N3[Mediation and Trade-off Meeting]
    N3 --> N4[Apply MoSCoW Priority]
    N2 -- No --> N4
    N4 --> N5{Acceptance Test Feasible?}
    N5 -- No --> N6[Refine or Drop]
    N5 -- Yes --> N7[Add to Validated SRS]
    N6 --> N1
```

---

## 4.6 SRS Document Hierarchy (IEEE 29148 View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[SRS Document] --> S2[1 Introduction]
    S1 --> S3[2 Overall Description]
    S1 --> S4[3 Specific Requirements]
    S1 --> S5[4 Verification]
    S1 --> S6[Appendices]

    S2 --> S2A[Purpose]
    S2 --> S2B[Scope]
    S2 --> S2C[Definitions]
    S2 --> S2D[References]

    S3 --> S3A[Functional Requirements]
    S3 --> S3B[Performance Requirements]
    S3 --> S3C[Design Constraints]
    S3 --> S3D[Software System Attributes]

    S3A --> S3A1[Use Case 1]
    S3A --> S3A2[Use Case 2]
    S3A --> S3A3[Business Rules]

    S4 --> S4A[Test Plan Reference]
    S4 --> S4B[Inspection Method]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Differentiate between **Functional** and **Non-Functional** requirements with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specify **what the system does** | Specify **how well** the system performs |
| **Focus** | Behaviour, features, data processing | Quality attributes, constraints |
| **Testability** | Tested by functional test cases | Tested by performance, load, security tests |
| **Example** | "The system shall allow a user to **withdraw cash** from an ATM." | "The ATM shall dispense cash within **5 seconds** of card authentication, with **99.9% availability**." |

> [!NOTE]
> Award **3 marks** if both types are defined, contrasted, and an example is given for each.

---

### Q2. List any **five fact-finding techniques** used during requirements analysis. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer (any 5):**
1. **Interviews** — one-to-one structured/unstructured conversations.
2. **Questionnaires / Surveys** — distributed to a large user base.
3. **Direct Observation** — analyst watches users perform tasks.
4. **Document Analysis** — study of existing manuals, forms, reports.
5. **Joint Application Development (JAD)** — facilitated group workshops.
6. **Brainstorming** — creative idea generation sessions.
7. **Protocol Analysis** — users narrate their thoughts while working.

> [!WARNING]
> Writing "interview" without explaining *type* (structured/unstructured) is a common partial-mark loss.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question Paper Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. KTU gives **internal choice**: students answer **either Question A or Question B** (both covering the same module concepts).

---

### ⭐ CHOICE — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **Q.A (a) [7 marks]** — Explain the **Requirements Engineering Process** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **requirements modelling** in this process. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Requirements Engineering is the systematic process of developing and documenting software requirements. It comprises five well-defined sub-processes as defined in the SWEBOK and adopted by KTU syllabus.

**Five Sub-Processes (3 marks):**
1. **Requirements Elicitation / Determination** — discovery of requirements using interviews, observation, JAD, etc.
2. **Requirements Analysis / Modelling** — representing requirements using DFDs, ER diagrams, UML use-case and class diagrams.
3. **Requirements Negotiation / Prioritisation** — resolving conflicts, applying MoSCoW or Kano.
4. **Requirements Specification** — writing the SRS document (IEEE 830 / 29148).
5. **Requirements Validation** — reviews, inspections, prototyping, acceptance tests.

**Role of Requirements Modelling (2 marks):**
- Acts as a **bridge** between the analyst's mental model and the formal SRS.
- Exposes **inconsistencies, omissions, and ambiguities** early.
- Enables **communication** among developers, users, and stakeholders using a common visual language.
- Supports **traceability** — each model element can be linked to source requirements.
- Tools: Visio, StarUML, Lucidchart, draw.io, Enterprise Architect.

**Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 above.

---

#### **Q.A (b) [7 marks]** — What is an **SRS**? Describe the structure of an SRS document as per **IEEE 830/29148 standard** with a sample functional requirement. *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Model Solution:**

**SRS Definition (1 mark):**
> A **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a comprehensive description of the intended purpose, behaviour, and constraints of a system, written from the user/customer viewpoint. It serves as the **contractual baseline** between the development team and the customer.

**IEEE 29148 SRS Structure (4 marks):**

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **1. Introduction** | Purpose, scope, definitions, acronyms, references, document overview |
| **2. Overall Description** | Product perspective, user characteristics, assumptions, dependencies, constraints |
| **3. Specific Requirements** | Functional, performance, design constraints, software system attributes (FURPS+), external interface requirements |
| **4. Verification** | How each requirement will be verified (inspection, analysis, test, demo) |
| **Appendices** | Use cases, data dictionary, glossary, change history |

**Sample Functional Requirement (2 marks):**

> **FR-07 — Book Issue**
> *Description:* The system shall allow an authenticated member to issue up to **3 books** at a time, provided no overdue items exist on the account.
> *Inputs:* Member ID, Book ID(s)
> *Processing:* Validate member status → check overdue list → check availability → update ILF (Book) and ILF (Loan)
> *Outputs:* Transaction receipt, updated database state
> *Error Conditions:* Member blocked, book already issued, system timeout
> *Verification:* Functional test case FR-07-TC01, FR-07-TC02

---

### ⭐ CHOICE — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### **Q.B (a) [7 marks]** — With neat diagrams, explain **Use-Case** and **Class Diagrams** as UML tools for requirements modelling. *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Model Solution:**

**UML Overview (1 mark):**
The Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5) is the OMG standard visual modelling language for object-oriented systems. It supports 14 diagram types grouped into **structure, behaviour, and interaction** diagrams.

**Use-Case Diagram (3 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Captures the **functional view** of the system — what actors can do.
- **Components:**
  - **Actor** — external entity (human or system) that interacts with the system.
  - **Use Case** — a goal the actor wishes to achieve (shown as an ellipse).
  - **System Boundary** — rectangle delimiting the system.
  - **Relationships:** Association (solid line), `\<\<include\>\>` (mandatory reuse), `\<\<extend\>\>` (optional, conditional).
- **Example:** Refer Section 4.2.

**Class Diagram (3 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Shows the **static structure** — classes, attributes, operations, and relationships.
- **Components:**
  - **Class** — rectangle with three compartments (name, attributes, operations).
  - **Relationships:** Association, Aggregation (hollow diamond), Composition (filled diamond), Generalisation/Inheritance (hollow triangle), Dependency (dashed arrow), Multiplicity.
- **Example:**

```
+----------------------------------+
|         Book                     |
+----------------------------------+
| - isbn: String                   |
| - title: String                  |
| - author: String                 |
| - status: enum {AVAIL, ISSUED}   |
+----------------------------------+
| + issue(member: Member): bool    |
| + returnBook(): void             |
| + searchByIsbn(s: String): Book  |
+----------------------------------+
```

Relationships:
- `Book` "1" ◇── "*" `Loan` (Aggregation)
- `Loan` "*" ──> "1" `Member` (Association)
- `Member` ──▷ `PremiumMember` (Generalisation)

---

#### **Q.B (b) [7 marks]** — Explain the **FURPS+** model for non-functional requirements. State any **four quality attributes** with quantified metrics. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

**FURPS+ Definition (2 marks):**
FURPS+ is a quality-model framework introduced by Robert Grady (Hewlett-Packard, 1992) for categorising software requirements. The acronym stands for:
- **F** — Functional (sometimes included separately)
- **U** — Usability
- **R** — Reliability
- **P** — Performance
- **S** — Supportability
- **+** — Design constraints, implementation, interface, physical constraints

**Four Quality Attributes with Metrics (4 marks):**

| Attribute | Quantified Example |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | "Search results must be returned in **≤ 2 seconds** for a dataset of 1 million records, with 95th-percentile response time **≤ 1.5 s**." |
| **Reliability** | "Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) **≥ 5000 hours**; system availability **99.95%** annually (maximum downtime ≈ 4.38 hours/year)." |
| **Usability** | "A new user must be able to complete the book-issue flow within **3 minutes** after a **15-minute** training session." |
| **Security** | "All data at rest must be encrypted with **AES-256**; passwords stored using **bcrypt with cost factor 12**; session token expiry **15 minutes**." |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
> Vague NFRs (e.g., "the system should be fast") are unverifiable and lead to project failure. Every NFR must be expressed as a **measurable, testable constraint**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common 14-Mark Mark Losers:**
> 1. **No diagram or poor diagram** — Lose up to 2 marks. Always draw **at least one** block/use-case/DFD.
> 2. **Mixing up FURPS+ and FR/NFR definitions** — Be precise; FURPS+ is an NFR categorisation.
> 3. **Skipping the SRS sample requirement** — Always show at least one full **FR with input-process-output-verification**.
> 4. **Mentioning "interview" without distinguishing** structured vs unstructured — partial loss.
> 5. **No justification** when asked "which fact-finding technique?" — always justify.

---

## 🔑 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> Print this section. Tick boxes on the day before the exam.

### ✅ Definitions
- [ ] Requirements, Functional, Non-Functional, Domain Requirements
- [ ] SRS, IEEE 830, IEEE 29148
- [ ] FURPS+ acronym
- [ ] MoSCoW prioritisation
- [ ] Fagan Inspection

### ✅ Process Steps
- [ ] 5-stage RE process: **Determine → Analyse → Document → Verify → Manage**
- [ ] Fact-Finding Techniques: 5 main (Interview, Questionnaire, Observation, Document, JAD)
- [ ] Requirements Negotiation steps

### ✅ Modelling
- [ ] DFD (Context, Level 1, Level 2) — symbols: process, datastore, external entity, data flow
- [ ] ER Diagram — entity, attribute, relationship, cardinality
- [ ] UML Use-Case diagram — actor, use case, boundary, include/extend
- [ ] UML Class diagram — class, association, aggregation, composition, inheritance

### ✅ SRS Document
- [ ] IEEE 29148 5 sections
- [ ] Sample FR template (Description, Input, Output, Error, Verification)

### ✅ Quantitative Formulas
- [ ] $FP = UFP \times VAF$, with $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \cdot \sum F_k$
- [ ] $UCP = (UUCW + UAW) \times TCF \times EF$
- [ ] $RPN = S \times O \times D$
- [ ] $RSI = \dfrac{\#\text{Initial}}{\#\text{Initial + Changed}} \times 100\%$

### ✅ Quality Attributes of a Good Requirement
- [ ] Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable

### ✅ Validation Techniques
- [ ] Reviews, Walkthroughs, Inspections, Prototyping, Acceptance Tests, Model Checking

### ✅ One-Liners for Theory
- [ ] "SRS is the **contractual baseline** between customer and developer."
- [ ] "An NFR is **measurable**, never vague."
- [ ] "A **use case** is a goal-oriented interaction between an actor and the system."
- [ ] "A **class diagram** shows static structure, not behaviour."
- [ ] "**FURPS+** categorises **non-functional** requirements."
- [ ] "**MoSCoW** resolves stakeholder conflict through priority negotiation."

### ✅ Common KTU Vignettes to Recognise Instantly
- [ ] "Speed up the system" → translate to a **quantified performance NFR**
- [ ] "Interview the bank manager" → **structured + unstructured interview**
- [ ] "Draw a diagram" → choose **Use-Case for FR**, **Class for structure**, **DFD for data flow**, **ER for database**
- [ ] "Validate the SRS" → list **Inspection / Prototype / Test / Review**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Software Engineering Case Study?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> A **Software Engineering Case Study** is a comprehensive, end-to-end analysis of a real-world or hypothetical software project that demonstrates the **systematic application** of software engineering principles, methods, tools, and procedures across the entire **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** — from feasibility study and requirement elicitation to design, coding, testing, deployment, and maintenance.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education), the Case Study is *not* merely a "story" of a project. It is an **integrative problem** that requires a student to apply the theoretical tools learned in Modules 1 and 2 (process models, requirement engineering, feasibility analysis, estimation techniques) to a single, cohesive project scenario.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Blueprint" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Think of a Case Study as a **medical case conference** in a hospital. A doctor does not just diagnose; they integrate the patient's history, lab reports, symptoms, and treatment options into one coherent treatment plan. Similarly, a software engineering case study integrates **requirements + estimation + design + testing + risk** into one coherent engineering solution.

Imagine you are asked to build a **house** (the software system). A case study asks you to:

| Real-World House Building | Software Engineering Equivalent |
|--------------------------|---------------------------------|
| 🏠 Client's wishlist (3BHK, garden) | 📋 Software Requirements Specification (SRS) |
| 💰 Budget estimation | 📊 COCOMO / Function Point Estimation |
| 📐 Architectural drawing | 🔷 DFD / ER Diagram / Use Case |
| 🧱 Material & labor schedule | 📅 Gantt Chart / PERT Scheduling |
| 🔨 Construction & inspection | 🧪 Coding & Testing phases |
| 🏗️ Risk (rain, shortage) | ⚠️ Risk Management Matrix |

### 1.3 Standard KTU Case Study Project Domains

KTU examiners typically choose case studies from these **five standard domains**:

1. **Library Management System (LMS)** — *most common*
2. **Hospital Management System (HMS)**
3. **Online Banking / ATM System**
4. **Student Information System (SIS)**
5. **E-Commerce / Online Shopping Portal**

For this module, we will use a **Library Management System (LMS)** as our reference case study because it allows demonstration of **all** SE concepts within a manageable scope.

### 1.4 Core Objectives of a KTU Case Study Answer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the KTU board examiner evaluates a case study answer, they look for these **5 mandatory components**:
> 1. **Problem Statement Definition** with clear scope
> 2. **Feasibility Analysis** (4 types)
> 3. **Requirement Analysis** (Functional + Non-Functional + SRS)
> 4. **Estimation** (FP / COCOMO) with **numerical calculations**
> 5. **Design Artifacts** (at least one DFD + Use Case / ER diagram)
>
> **Missing even ONE component = guaranteed mark deduction.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The KTU Case Study Master Framework — 8-Phase Analysis

Below is the **canonical 8-phase framework** that the KTU board expects for a 14-mark case study answer. Memorize this structure — it guarantees full marks if executed correctly.

### Phase 1: Problem Statement & Scope Definition

The problem statement is a **2-3 sentence crisp description** of *what* the system must do and *who* it serves.

**Reference Case Study — Library Management System (LMS):**

> *"The **Ahalia Central Library** requires a computerized Library Management System to automate book issuance, returns, catalog searching, member registration, fine calculation, and report generation. The system will serve **three user roles**: Librarian, Member (Student/Faculty), and Administrator. The system must support **5,000 books**, **2,000 members**, and handle **200 transactions per day**."*

### Phase 2: Feasibility Analysis (The "4-Feasibility" Study)

| Feasibility Type | Question It Answers | LMS Example | Result |
|------------------|--------------------|-----------  |--------|
| **Technical** | Can we build it with current tech? | Java + MySQL + Tomcat server | ✅ Feasible |
| **Economic** | Is the ROI positive? | Cost ₹4,00,000 vs. Benefit ₹6,00,000/year | ✅ Feasible (NPV > 0) |
| **Operational** | Will users actually use it? | Staff trained; GUI-based interface | ✅ Feasible |
| **Schedule** | Can it be done in time? | 6 months, 3 developers | ✅ Feasible |

### Phase 3: Software Process Model Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a case study with **well-defined, stable requirements** (like LMS), the KTU-expected answer is the **Waterfall Model**. For evolving requirements, suggest the **Incremental** or **Spiral Model**. Always **justify** the choice with 2-3 reasons.

### Phase 4: Requirement Analysis & SRS

Requirements are split into:

- **Functional Requirements (FR)**: What the system *does*.
  - FR1: Issue a book to a registered member
  - FR2: Calculate and collect fine on late returns
  - FR3: Search the catalog by title/author/ISBN
  - FR4: Generate daily/monthly reports
  - FR5: Manage user authentication (Librarian / Member / Admin)

- **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)**: How the system *performs*.
  - NFR1: Response time < **2 seconds**
  - NFR2: Availability **99.5%** uptime
  - NFR3: Support **50 concurrent users**
  - NFR4: Data backup every **24 hours**
  - NFR5: Security via **AES-256 encryption**

### Phase 5: Estimation — Function Point (FP) Analysis

The **Function Point** method measures software size from the **user's perspective**, independent of programming language. This is the **most numerically heavy part** of a KTU case study and the area where students lose marks for arithmetic errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Function Point Formula (Albrecht's Method)** is the most frequently tested equation in KTU Module 2:

### Phase 6: Design — DFDs and Use Cases

The KTU board **always awards marks** for design diagrams. You must provide:
- **Context Diagram (Level 0 DFD)** — single process with external entities
- **Level 1 DFD** — major sub-processes decomposed
- **At least one Use Case Diagram** for an actor-based view

### Phase 7: Testing Strategy

| Test Level | Purpose | LMS Example |
|------------|---------|-------------|
| **Unit Testing** | Test individual functions | Test `calculateFine()` with edge dates |
| **Integration Testing** | Test module interactions | Test book-issue + inventory update flow |
| **System Testing** | Test complete system | End-to-end book issue-return cycle |
| **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | Validate with real users | Librarian signs off after 1 week of trial |

### Phase 8: Risk Management & Project Scheduling

Provide a **Risk Table** and a **Gantt Chart** (described verbally, or as Mermaid gantt).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — these are the ONLY equations you need for a KTU case study on estimation.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|--------------|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Unadjusted FP (UFP)** | $UFP = \sum_{i=1}^{5}(C_i \times W_i)$ | $C_i$ = count, $W_i$ = weight | Base count from 5 FP components |
| 2 | **Complexity Weight (Default Albrecht)** | $W = (3 \text{ Simple}, 4 \text{ Average}, 6 \text{ Complex})$ | Three complexity levels | Per FP component type |
| 3 | **Adjusted FP (AFP)** | $AFP = UFP \times (0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i)$ | $F_i$ = 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs), each rated 0–5 | Final size metric |
| 4 | **Lines of Code (LOC)** | $LOC = AFP \times \text{LOC/FP}$ | Language productivity table | Convert FP to LOC for COCOMO |
| 5 | **COCOMO Basic Effort** | $E = a \times (KLOC)^b$ | $a, b$ = mode constants (organic/semi-detached/embedded) | Person-months effort |
| 6 | **COCOMO Basic Time** | $T_c = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.32}$ | $E$ = effort in person-months | Development time in months |
| 7 | **Average Staff Size** | $SS = E / T_c$ | $E$ = effort, $T_c$ = time | Number of developers required |
| 8 | **Productivity** | $P = KLOC / E$ | $KLOC$ = thousands of LOC | KLOC per person-month |
| 9 | **Cost Estimation** | $Cost = E \times \text{Rate per PM}$ | $E$ = effort, Rate = ₹/person-month | Total project cost |
| 10 | **COQ (Cost of Quality)** | $COQ = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Four quality cost categories | Quality management |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Function Points** are used by **ISBSG (International Software Benchmarking Standards Group)** for industry-wide productivity benchmarking.
- **COCOMO** is used by defense/aerospace organizations (NASA, DRDO) for large project bidding.
- **FP-to-LOC conversion tables** are language-specific (Assembly ≈ 320 LOC/FP, Java ≈ 53 LOC/FP, Python ≈ 16 LOC/FP).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation — LMS Estimation

This section provides a **fully worked-out numerical example** that you can replicate for any KTU case study question. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Function Point Counting (Unadjusted FP)

Consider our Library Management System with the following function point inventory:

| FP Component | Count (Simple) | Count (Average) | Count (Complex) | Weights (S, A, C) |
|--------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| External Inputs (EI) | 3 | 4 | 2 | 3, 4, 6 |
| External Outputs (EO) | 2 | 3 | 1 | 4, 5, 7 |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 4 | 2 | 1 | 3, 4, 6 |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 1 | 2 | 1 | 7, 10, 15 |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 1 | 1 | 0 | 5, 7, 10 |

### 3.1.1 Computing the Unadjusted FP

For **External Inputs (EI)**:

$$
UFP_{EI} = (3 \times 3) + (4 \times 4) + (2 \times 6)
$$

Applying each multiplication step explicitly:

$$
UFP_{EI} = (3 \times 3) + (4 \times 4) + (2 \times 6) = 9 + 16 + 12 = 37
$$

For **External Outputs (EO)**:

$$
UFP_{EO} = (2 \times 4) + (3 \times 5) + (1 \times 7) = 8 + 15 + 7 = 30
$$

For **External Inquiries (EQ)**:

$$
UFP_{EQ} = (4 \times 3) + (2 \times 4) + (1 \times 6) = 12 + 8 + 6 = 26
$$

For **Internal Logical Files (ILF)**:

$$
UFP_{ILF} = (1 \times 7) + (2 \times 10) + (1 \times 15) = 7 + 20 + 15 = 42
$$

For **External Interface Files (EIF)**:

$$
UFP_{EIF} = (1 \times 5) + (1 \times 7) + (0 \times 10) = 5 + 7 + 0 = 12
$$

**Summing all five components to obtain the Total Unadjusted FP:**

$$
UFP = 37 + 30 + 26 + 42 + 12
$$

$$
UFP = 145
$$

## 3.2 Step 2 — Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)

The 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs) are rated 0–5. Suppose after rating the LMS we obtain:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i = 42
$$

Substitute into the Value Adjustment Factor formula:

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 42
$$

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.42 = 1.07
$$

## 3.3 Step 3 — Adjusted Function Point (AFP)

$$
AFP = UFP \times VAF
$$

$$
AFP = 145 \times 1.07
$$

$$
AFP = 155.15 \approx 155
$$

## 3.4 Step 4 — Convert AFP to KLOC

Assume the LMS is built in **Java**. Industry average for Java:

$$
LOC/FP = 53
$$

$$
LOC = AFP \times LOC/FP = 155 \times 53
$$

$$
LOC = 8215
$$

$$
KLOC = 8.215
$$

## 3.5 Step 5 — COCOMO Basic Model Estimation

Assume the project is **semi-detached** mode (medium team, mixed experience):

$$
a = 3.0, \quad b = 1.12
$$

**Effort Calculation:**

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^b
$$

$$
E = 3.0 \times (8.215)^{1.12}
$$

Compute the power term step-by-step using logarithms:

$$
\log_{10}(8.215) \approx 0.9146
$$

$$
1.12 \times 0.9146 \approx 1.0244
$$

$$
10^{1.0244} \approx 10.58
$$

$$
E \approx 3.0 \times 10.58 = 31.74 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

**Development Time Calculation:**

$$
T_c = 2.5 \times (E)^{0.32}
$$

$$
T_c = 2.5 \times (31.74)^{0.32}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(31.74) \approx 1.5017
$$

$$
0.32 \times 1.5017 \approx 0.4805
$$

$$
10^{0.4805} \approx 3.022
$$

$$
T_c \approx 2.5 \times 3.022 = 7.56 \text{ Months}
$$

**Average Staff Size:**

$$
SS = E / T_c = 31.74 / 7.56 \approx 4.2 \approx 4 \text{ Developers}
$$

**Productivity:**

$$
P = KLOC / E = 8.215 / 31.74 \approx 0.259 \text{ KLOC/PM}
$$

**Project Cost (assuming ₹50,000 per person-month):**

$$
Cost = E \times \text{Rate} = 31.74 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹ }15{,}87{,}000
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Full Case Study Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your final-answer-ready summary for the KTU 14-mark question. Reproduce it cleanly in the exam.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Unadjusted Function Point | $UFP$ | **145** | FP |
| Value Adjustment Factor | $VAF$ | **1.07** | Dimensionless |
| Adjusted Function Point | $AFP$ | **155** | FP |
| Lines of Code | $LOC$ | **8,215** | LOC |
| Kilo Lines of Code | $KLOC$ | **8.215** | KLOC |
| Effort (Person-Months) | $E$ | **31.74** | PM |
| Development Time | $T_c$ | **7.56** | Months |
| Average Staff Size | $SS$ | **4** | Persons |
| Productivity | $P$ | **0.259** | KLOC/PM |
| Total Project Cost | $Cost$ | **₹15,87,000** | INR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Every 14-mark case study answer **must include at least two diagrams**: a DFD (Context + Level-1) and a Use Case diagram. Below are the four mandatory architectural diagrams for the LMS Case Study.

## 4.1 Context Diagram (Level 0 DFD) — Library Management System

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is a Software Engineering Case Study? List any four components expected in a KTU case study answer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Software Engineering Case Study is the systematic application of software engineering principles — requirement analysis, estimation, design, testing, and project management — to a single real-world or hypothetical project, demonstrating the entire SDLC integration.

The four mandatory components are:
1. **Problem Statement & Scope Definition**
2. **Feasibility Analysis** (Technical, Economic, Operational, Schedule)
3. **Requirement Specification** (Functional + Non-Functional + SRS outline)
4. **Estimation** using Function Point or COCOMO with numerical calculations

*(Any 4 of the 8 phases described in Section 2.1 are acceptable.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with two examples each from a Library Management System.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Describe *what* the system does | Describe *how* the system performs |
| **Focus** | Features and functions | Quality attributes and constraints |
| **LMS Example 1** | Issue a book to a registered member | System response time < 2 seconds |
| **LMS Example 2** | Calculate fine on late return | 99.5% uptime availability |
| **Verifiable by** | User acceptance / black-box test | Performance / load / security tests |

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> The most common reason students **lose 3-4 marks** on a 14-mark case study:
> - ❌ Writing the problem statement but **forgetting to identify user roles/actors** (loses 1 mark)
> - ❌ Performing FP calculation with **wrong weight values** — KTU expects the canonical Albrecht weights (3, 4, 6) for FP components, **not arbitrary numbers** (loses 2 marks)
> - ❌ Drawing a DFD but **forgetting to show data stores** as double-line rectangles or labeled as "D1, D2" (loses 1 mark)
> - ❌ Providing COCOMO effort but **not calculating development time or staff size** (loses 1-2 marks)
>
> **Golden Rule**: Always finish your estimation with a **summary table** containing all derived parameters.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Case Study on Library Management System

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**
> *"A college library wants to develop a Library Management System (LMS) to automate book issuance, returns, fine calculation, catalog search, and report generation. The system must serve 2000 members, manage 5000 books, and handle 200 transactions per day. Prepare a detailed software engineering case study including:*
> *(a) Feasibility analysis and SRS for the LMS. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Function Point and COCOMO estimation with effort, time, and staff calculation. (7 marks)"*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Problem Statement (1 mark):**
The LMS will automate the day-to-day operations of a college library, replacing the existing manual register-based system. It will serve three actors: **Librarian**, **Member** (Student/Faculty), and **Administrator**.

**2. Feasibility Analysis (2 marks):**

| Feasibility | Result | Justification |
|-------------|--------|---------------|
| Technical | ✅ Feasible | Java + MySQL + Apache Tomcat — proven stack |
| Economic | ✅ Feasible | One-time ₹15L investment saves ₹6L/year in labor |
| Operational | ✅ Feasible | GUI-based; 1-week training sufficient |
| Schedule | ✅ Feasible | 7.5 months duration with 4 developers |

**3. Software Requirements Specification (4 marks):**

**Functional Requirements:**
- FR1: Authenticate users (Librarian, Member, Admin)
- FR2: Search catalog by title / author / ISBN
- FR3: Issue a book to a registered member (max 3 books, 14-day period)
- FR4: Accept returns and compute fine (₹2/day late)
- FR5: Generate daily / monthly / annual reports
- FR6: Manage member registration and profile updates

**Non-Functional Requirements:**
- NFR1: Page response time ≤ **2 seconds**
- NFR2: Support **50 concurrent users**
- NFR3: **99.5%** availability during working hours
- NFR4: Data backup every **24 hours**
- NFR5: Passwords encrypted with **SHA-256**
- NFR6: Browser compatibility: Chrome, Firefox, Edge

**4. Process Model Selection:** Waterfall Model — because requirements are well-defined and stable.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Unadjusted FP Calculation (2 marks):**

Using the FP inventory from Section 3.1:

$$
UFP = 37 + 30 + 26 + 42 + 12 = 145
$$

**2. Value Adjustment Factor (1 mark):**

$$
\sum F_i = 42 \implies VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 42 = 1.07
$$

**3. Adjusted FP and KLOC (1 mark):**

$$
AFP = 145 \times 1.07 = 155.15 \approx 155
$$

$$
KLOC = 155 \times 53 / 1000 = 8.215
$$

**4. COCOMO Estimation (3 marks):**

Using **semi-detached** mode ($a=3.0, b=1.12$):

$$
E = 3.0 \times (8.215)^{1.12} = 31.74 \text{ PM}
$$

$$
T_c = 2.5 \times (31.74)^{0.32} = 7.56 \text{ Months}
$$

$$
SS = E / T_c = 31.74 / 7.56 = 4.2 \approx 4 \text{ Persons}
$$

$$
Cost = 31.74 \times \text{₹50,000} = \text{₹ }15{,}87{,}000
$$

**5. Final Summary Table (reproduce in exam):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| UFP | 145 |
| AFP | 155 |
| KLOC | 8.215 |
| Effort (E) | 31.74 PM |
| Time (T) | 7.56 months |
| Staff | 4 persons |
| Cost | ₹15,87,000 |

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Hospital Management System)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Model Paper]**
> *"A multi-specialty hospital requires a Hospital Management System (HMS) to manage patient registration, doctor appointments, billing, pharmacy, and lab reports. The system serves 4 user roles: Patient, Doctor, Receptionist, and Admin. The hospital handles 500 patients/day. Prepare a software engineering case study covering:*
> *(a) Requirement analysis, use case identification, and DFD for the HMS. (7 marks)*
> *(b) COCOMO estimation assuming organic mode and language productivity of 40 LOC/FP. (7 marks)"*

#### Part (a) — Model Solution Outline (7 marks)

1. **Problem Statement (1 mark):** HMS to automate patient, doctor, billing, and pharmacy workflows for 500 patients/day.

2. **Actors & Use Cases (3 marks):**
   - Patient: Book Appointment, View Lab Report, Pay Bill
   - Doctor: View Patient History, Prescribe Medicine, Update Diagnosis
   - Receptionist: Register Patient, Allocate Bed, Generate Bill
   - Admin: Manage Users, View Reports, Configure System

3. **Context Diagram + Level-1 DFD (3 marks):** Draw using Mermaid format (similar to Section 4.1 & 4.2) with external entities (Patient, Doctor, Receptionist, Admin, Insurance Company) and major processes (Registration, Appointment, Consultation, Billing, Pharmacy, Lab).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** Assume FP inventory yields $UFP = 180$, $\sum F_i = 38$, Organic mode ($a=2.4, b=1.05$), $LOC/FP = 40$.

**Step 1: VAF and AFP (2 marks):**

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 38 = 1.03
$$

$$
AFP = 180 \times 1.03 = 185.4 \approx 185
$$

**Step 2: KLOC (1 mark):**

$$
KLOC = 185 \times 40 / 1000 = 7.4
$$

**Step 3: COCOMO Organic Effort and Time (2 marks):**

$$
E = 2.4 \times (7.4)^{1.05}
$$

Compute power:

$$
\log_{10}(7.4) = 0.8692
$$

$$
1.05 \times 0.8692 = 0.9127
$$

$$
10^{0.9127} = 8.175
$$

$$
E = 2.4 \times 8.175 = 19.62 \text{ PM}
$$

**Step 4: Development Time and Staff (1 mark):**

$$
T_c = 2.5 \times (19.62)^{0.32} \approx 6.85 \text{ months}
$$

$$
SS = 19.62 / 6.85 \approx 3 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 5: Cost (1 mark):**

$$
Cost = 19.62 \times \text{₹60,000} = \text{₹ }11{,}77{,}200
$$

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [x] **Case Study = 8 phases**: Problem → Feasibility → Process Model → Requirements → Estimation → Design → Testing → Risk.
- [x] **Always identify user roles/actors** in the problem statement — KTU deducts 1 mark if omitted.
- [x] **4 Feasibility types** must appear: Technical, Economic, Operational, Schedule.
- [x] **Process Model selection must be justified** — Waterfall for stable, Spiral/Incremental for evolving.
- [x] **Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements** must be listed separately with 2-3 examples each.
- [x] **Function Point weights are FIXED** by Albrecht: $(3, 4, 6)$ for EI/EQ, $(4, 5, 7)$ for EO, $(7, 10, 15)$ for ILF, $(5, 7, 10)$ for EIF. **Memorize them.**
- [x] **The AFP formula** is $AFP = UFP \times (0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i)$ — do not write it as $UFP + VAF$.
- [x] **COCOMO mode constants** must match the chosen project type:
  - Organic: $a=2.4, b=1.05$
  - Semi-detached: $a=3.0, b=1.12$
  - Embedded: $a=3.6, b=1.20$
- [x] **Always compute all 3 COCOMO outputs**: Effort ($E$), Time ($T_c$), Staff Size ($SS$).
- [x] **Draw at least 2 diagrams** — Context Diagram (Level-0 DFD) + Use Case, or Context + Level-1 DFD.
- [x] **DFD symbols to remember**: Rectangle = External Entity, Circle/Rounded = Process, Open Rectangle / Cylinder = Data Store, Arrow = Data Flow.
- [x] **Use Case diagram elements**: Stick figures = Actors, Ovals = Use Cases, Rectangle = System Boundary, Lines = Associations.
- [x] **Risk table** should have at minimum: Risk ID, Description, Probability, Impact, Mitigation Strategy.
- [x] **Final summary table** with all numerical estimates is the examiner's quick-scoring aid — always include it.
- [x] **Unit testing** is on individual functions/methods, **Integration** is on module interaction, **System** is end-to-end, **Acceptance** is by the user.
- [x] **Common KTU case study domains**: LMS, HMS, ATM, SIS, E-Commerce. Be ready to swap any one of them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Object-Oriented Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Object-Oriented Development?

**Object-Oriented Development (OOD)** is a software development methodology that models a system as a collection of interacting **objects**, where each object represents an entity in the problem domain with its own **attributes** (data) and **behaviors** (operations). It encompasses three tightly-coupled phases: **Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA)**, **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)**, and **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> Object-Oriented Development is a software engineering paradigm that uses objects — entities combining data and behavior — as the fundamental building blocks for analysis, design, and implementation of software systems. It is governed by the four (or five) core principles: **Abstraction, Encapsulation, Modularity, Hierarchy (Inheritance), and Polymorphism.**

The unified framework that drives OOD is known as the **OOAD (Object-Oriented Analysis and Design)** process, which produces a set of interrelated **OO models** describing the system from multiple viewpoints.

## 1.2 The OO Trilogy — A Bird's-Eye View

| Phase | Primary Question | Output Artefact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA)** | *What should the system do?* | Object model, Use-case model, Domain classes |
| **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** | *How should the system do it?* | Class diagram, Sequence diagram, System architecture |
| **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** | *Implement the design* | Source code in C++ / Java / Python / C# |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant" Model 🍽️

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a software system as a **busy restaurant**.
> - The **Menu** is the **Class** (the blueprint of what a dish offers).
> - A specific **order of Paneer Butter Masala** is an **Object** (an instance of the "Dish" class).
> - The **kitchen ticket** hides the recipe (the internal data and steps). The waiter doesn't know *how* the dish is cooked — this is **Encapsulation**.
> - The *"Chef-in-Training" inherits skills from the "Head Chef"* — this is **Inheritance**.
> - A single kitchen station can **cook many different dishes** when ordered — this is **Polymorphism**.
> - You order *"something spicy"* without knowing which dish it is — this is **Abstraction**.

## 1.4 The Five (or Six) Core OO Concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Board Coverage:** Every answer on OO Development in KTU exams must explicitly mention these pillars. They are the **highest-weightage** terms in Module 3.

1. **Object** — A real-world entity with identity, state, and behavior. *E.g., A specific student "Rahul" with roll number 47.*
2. **Class** — A template/blueprint defining the common attributes and methods for a group of objects. *E.g., The "Student" class.*
3. **Encapsulation** — Wrapping data and operations into a single unit and hiding internal details from the outside world. Achieved using **access specifiers** (`private`, `public`, `protected`).
4. **Abstraction** — Showing only essential features and suppressing background details. Achieved via **abstract classes** and **interfaces**.
5. **Inheritance** — Mechanism by which a new class (subclass) acquires properties and behaviors of an existing class (superclass). Promotes **reuse**.
6. **Polymorphism** — The ability of a single interface to represent different underlying forms. *E.g., one `draw()` method works for Circle, Square, Triangle.*

## 1.5 Objects vs. Classes — The Foundational Distinction

| Aspect | Class | Object |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Logical blueprint / template | Physical / runtime instance |
| **Memory** | No memory allocated at definition | Memory is allocated upon creation |
| **Keyword analogy** | The *idea* of a car | A *specific* red Maruti Swift with KL-07-AB-1234 |
| **Existence** | Exists in source code | Exists in memory at runtime |
| **Declaration** | `class Student { ... }` | `Student rahul = new Student();` |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Object-Oriented Development (The OOAD Lifecycle)

The OOAD process is iterative and incremental. It is not strictly linear. The following is the **standard KTU-accepted phased breakdown**:

### Phase 1: Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA)
The goal is to understand and model the **problem domain** independently of implementation.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Requirement Gathering** — Elicit functional and non-functional requirements from stakeholders.
2. **Use-Case Modeling** — Identify *actors* and *use cases* to capture external behavior.
3. **Domain Class Identification** — Use **noun-phrase analysis** on the requirements document to extract candidate classes.
4. **Object Modeling** — Build the **Object Model** showing classes, attributes, operations, and relationships.
5. **Dynamic Modeling** — Build **State** and **Sequence** diagrams to capture behavior over time.
6. **Refinement** — Discard redundant classes, merge related ones, identify inheritance hierarchies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tools used in OOA:** Use-Case Diagrams, Class Diagrams (domain-level), Activity Diagrams, CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) Cards.

### Phase 2: Object-Oriented Design (OOD)
The goal is to define **how** the system will be built, refining the OOA model into a technical solution.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **System Decomposition** — Split the system into **subsystems** (each is itself a package of cooperating classes).
2. **Class Design** — Refine domain classes, add **methods**, define **visibility**, and design **interfaces**.
3. **Database Design** — Map persistent objects to relational or NoSQL schemas.
4. **Algorithm & Data Structure Selection** — Decide internal implementation of operations.
5. **Implementation in OO Language** — Code the design in C++, Java, Python, etc.
6. **Pattern Application** — Apply design patterns (Gang-of-Four) like *Factory, Observer, Strategy*.

### Phase 3: Object-Oriented Programming (Implementation)
- Translate the OOD models directly into an OO programming language.
- Each class becomes a file/module; relationships are coded via **inheritance**, **composition**, or **interface implementation**.

## 2.2 OO Models Produced During OOAD

| Model Type | Purpose | KTU Diagram |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Object Model** | Static structure: classes, attributes, operations, relationships | Class Diagram |
| **Dynamic Model** | Time-dependent behavior: states and event-driven transitions | State Diagram |
| **Functional Model** | Data flow and transformations across the system | Data Flow Diagram (within OO context) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three models together form the **Booch triad** (Booch, 1994) and represent the complete specification of an OO system. This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Object-Oriented vs. Structured Development (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Structured Development | Object-Oriented Development |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary unit** | Function / Module | Object (data + behavior) |
| **Approach** | Top-down, function-decomposition | Bottom-up, class-identification |
| **Data handling** | Separate from functions (DFD-centric) | Encapsulated with functions inside objects |
| **Reusability** | Limited (via function libraries) | High (via inheritance and polymorphism) |
| **Modifiability** | Ripple effect — changes affect multiple modules | Localized — changes are confined to classes |
| **Modeling tool** | DFD, ER diagrams | UML (Unified Modeling Language) |
| **Real-world mapping** | Poor — focuses on processes | Excellent — focuses on entities |
| **Maintainability** | Difficult for large systems | Easier — well-suited for large, evolving systems |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Object-Oriented Development

> [!NOTE]
> The "formula" here is conceptual — these are the **standard ratios, notations, and principles** that examiners love to test.

| Concept | Notation / Rule | KTU Exam Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Identifying Classes (Noun-Phrase Approach)** | All *nouns* in problem statement → candidate classes; *verbs* → candidate operations | 7-mark design question |
| **Cardinality Notation** | `1` (exactly one), `*` (many), `0..1` (optional), `1..*` (one or more) | Class diagram questions |
| **Generalization (Inheritance)** | `Superclass --|> Subclass` (UML arrow: hollow triangle) | Inheritance hierarchy |
| **Association** | `ClassA ---- ClassA` (solid line, optional role names) | Relationship modeling |
| **Aggregation (weak "has-a")** | `Class ◇---- Class` (hollow diamond) | Whole-part, parts can exist alone |
| **Composition (strong "has-a")** | `Class ◆---- Class` (filled diamond) | Whole-part, parts die with whole |
| **Encapsulation visibility** | `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected | Class diagram questions |
| **Polymorphism types** | Ad-hoc (overloading), Inclusion (subtyping), Parametric (generics) | Theory 3-mark question |
| **OOAD Process Models** | Booch, OMT (Rumbaugh), OOSE (Jacobson) → Unified into **UML** | 7-mark theory question |
| **GRASP Patterns** | Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller | Class responsibility assignment |
| **CRC Card** | Class — Responsibility — Collaborator (index card sized) | Class identification technique |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of OOD

> [!TIP]
> **Industry relevance:** Object-Oriented Development underpins virtually every modern enterprise system — from **banking software** (where `Account`, `Transaction`, `Customer` are natural classes) to **embedded automotive systems** (where `Sensor`, `Actuator`, `ECU` map directly to objects), to **web frameworks** like Django, Spring, and .NET. The principles learned here are the foundation of **microservices, design patterns, and cloud-native architectures**.

## 2.6 How to Identify Classes — The Three Standard Techniques

1. **Noun-Phrase Analysis (Textual Approach)**
   - Read the requirements document.
   - Highlight every **noun** — each is a candidate class.
   - Highlight every **verb** — each is a candidate operation.

2. **CRC Cards (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator)**
   - Each class is written on a 4×6 index card.
   - **Class** — name of the entity.
   - **Responsibility** — what it knows/does.
   - **Collaborator** — other classes it interacts with.
   - Useful in team brainstorming sessions.

3. **GRASP Methodology**
   - Assign responsibilities to classes using general principles: *Information Expert* (give responsibility to the class with the needed info), *Creator* (who creates whom), *Controller*, *Low Coupling*, *High Cohesion*.
