This is a **3-tuple** constraint, evaluated by holding a *pair* of objects fixed and counting along the third. ### 3.1.3 Generalization to N-ary $$\forall\, (c_1, \dots, c_{i-1}, c_{i+1}, \dots, c_n) \in \prod_{j \neq i} C_j : \quad \text{count}\big(c_i : (c_1, \dots, c_n) \in R\big) \in [a_i,\, b_i]$$ **Interpretation:** at end $i$, hold the other $(n-1)$ objects fixed as a tuple, then count the number of valid $c_i$ values. The count must lie within $[a_i, b_i]$. This is the **canonical definition** expected in KTU 14-mark answers. ## 3.2 Derivation: Converting a Ternary Association into Equivalent Binary Associations ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement Given a ternary association $R \subseteq C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$ with optional attributes $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots\}$ on the link itself, derive an equivalent set of binary associations using an association class $A_R$. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1 — Introduce the Association Class** Create a new class $A_R$ that represents the link as a first-class object. Every instance of $A_R$ corresponds to exactly one tuple in $R$. $$A_R = \{ (c_1, c_2, c_3) \mid (c_1, c_2, c_3) \in R \}$$ **Step 2 — Define Three Binary Associations** Introduce three binary associations $\beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3$: $$ \begin{aligned} \beta_1 &\subseteq A_R \times C_1, & \text{mapped by: } (a, c_1) \mid a.c_1 = c_1 \\ \beta_2 &\subseteq A_R \times C_2, & \text{mapped by: } (a, c_2) \mid a.c_2 = c_2 \\ \beta_3 &\subseteq A_R \times C_3, & \text{mapped by: } (a, c_3) \mid a.c_3 = c_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Migrate the Link Attributes** For every attribute $a_k \in \mathcal{A}$, move it from the diamond (or from an inline attribute list on the link) to the association class $A_R$: $$A_R.\text{attributes} = \mathcal{A} \cup \{ \text{ref}_1, \text{ref}_2, \text{ref}_3 \}$$ where $\text{ref}_i$ is a reference (foreign key in database terms) to the $i$-th class. **Step 4 — Multiplicity Translation** For the original ternary multiplicity $m_i$ at end $i$, the corresponding binary multiplicity on $\beta_i$ becomes: $$\beta_i \text{ multiplicity on } A_R \text{ end} = m_i$$ $$\beta_i \text{ multiplicity on } C_i \text{ end} = 1$$ This is the **standard rule**: when you convert N-ary to binary, the multiplicity that was on the original class end moves to the new association-class end, and the other end of the binary link becomes exactly `1`. **Step 5 — Equivalence Proof (Informal)** The original relation $R$ has cardinality $\vert R \vert$ tuples. The new construction produces $\vert R \vert$ instances of $A_R$, each holding one tuple's worth of information. Reconstruction is: $$(c_1, c_2, c_3) \in R \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \exists\, a \in A_R : a.\text{ref}_1 = c_1 \land a.\text{ref}_2 = c_2 \land a.\text{ref}_3 = c_3$$ Hence the conversion is **semantically lossless**. ### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example Suppose we have a ternary *Faculty–Student–Project* association with multiplicity `1..*`, `1`, `1` and an attribute *role* (Student role: Lead / Member). - Step 1: Create class `ProjectMembership` (the association class). - Step 2: Three binary associations: `Faculty–ProjectMembership`, `Student–ProjectMembership`, `Project–ProjectMembership`. - Step 3: `ProjectMembership` has attributes `{role}` and references `{facultyRef, studentRef, projectRef}`. - Step 4: Multiplicities: - `Faculty` to `ProjectMembership`: `1..*` on the ProjectMembership end, `1` on Faculty end. - `Student` to `ProjectMembership`: `1` on Student end, `1..*` (or `1` per project rule) on ProjectMembership end. - `Project` to `ProjectMembership`: `1` on Project end, `1..*` on ProjectMembership end (a project can have many memberships). This is the kind of explicit, step-by-step conversion that earns full marks in KTU ESE. ## 3.3 Validation: When Conversion is *Not* Lossless In rare cases involving **qualified or constrained N-ary associations** (e.g., XOR constraints across ends), the simple conversion above may lose constraint information. The KTU answer, however, expects only the standard equivalence. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall to Avoid:** Some students mistakenly place the original class's multiplicity on the wrong end of the binary link. **Rule of thumb:** the multiplicity that was at the class end in the N-ary form *migrates* to the *association-class end* of the corresponding binary link; the *class end* of the binary link always becomes `1` (each instance of the original class corresponds to exactly one instance of the association class through that link). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Canonical N-ary (Ternary) Association in UML The diagram below shows a textbook ternary association: a *Doctor* prescribes a *Medication* to a *Patient*, with multiplicity and an association class *Prescription* carrying the *date* and *dosage* attributes. ```mermaid graph LR classD["Doctor"] classP["Patient"] classM["Medication"] diamondA["N"] classRx["Prescription
+ date : Date
+ dosage : String"] classD -- "1..*" diamondA classP -- "1..*" diamondA classM -- "1..*" diamondA diamondA -. "association class" .-> classRx ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The diamond labeled `N` represents the ternary association. - Each class end has a multiplicity of `1..*` (a doctor may prescribe many medications to many patients, etc.). - The dashed arrow to `Prescription` indicates that the link itself is a class with its own attributes. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Conversion of Ternary to Binary with Association Class This block diagram illustrates the equivalent binary decomposition of the same Prescription scenario. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Original["Original N-ary form"] D1["Doctor"] P1["Patient"] M1["Medication"] N1["N"] D1 --- N1 P1 --- N1 M1 --- N1 end subgraph Converted["Equivalent binary form"] D2["Doctor"] P2["Patient"] M2["Medication"] RX["Prescription
refDoctor
refPatient
refMedication
date, dosage"] D2 -- "1" --- RX P2 -- "1" --- RX M2 -- "1" --- RX end Original -. "transform" .-> Converted ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The left subgraph shows the original ternary association (with the central diamond). - The right subgraph shows the equivalent binary decomposition. - The arrows from each class to `Prescription` are binary, each with multiplicity `1` on the class end and `1..*` on the `Prescription` end. - The `Prescription` class absorbs the link attributes and holds the foreign-key references. ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Functional Decision Flow — Should I Model N-ary or Convert to Binary? ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Start: Relationship identified"]) --> q1{"How many classes
participate?"} q1 -- "Exactly 2" --> bin["Model as Binary Association"] q1 -- "3 or more" --> q2{"Does the link carry
attributes?"} q2 -- "No" --> q3{"Is the joint multiplicity
semantically critical?"} q3 -- "Yes" --> nary["Model as N-ary with diamond"] q3 -- "No" --> nary q2 -- "Yes" --> q4{"Is the relationship
central to domain logic?"} q4 -- "Yes" --> assoc["Convert to Association Class
(binary decomposition)"] q4 -- "No" --> nary bin --> stop(["Done"]) nary --> stop assoc --> stop ``` **Reading the diagram:** - This is a decision-support flowchart that KTU students can reproduce in viva or written exams. - The default path for $n \geq 3$ is to use the diamond notation; an association class is added when the link has its own attributes. ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Multiplicity Semantics Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BinarySem["Binary: hold one object, count the other"] Bx["C1 object c1"] By["C2 objects linked to c1"] Bx -- "fixed" --- By end subgraph TernarySem["Ternary: hold a pair, count the third"] T1["C1 object c1"] T2["C2 object c2"] T3["C3 objects linked to pair"] T1 -- "fixed" --- T3 T2 -- "fixed" --- T3 end ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Binary multiplicity is a single-object-fixed scenario. - Ternary (and N-ary) multiplicity is a multi-object-fixed scenario — this is the conceptual distinction that KTU examiners test. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Q: Define an N-ary association in UML. How is it represented in a class diagram?** **Model Answer:** An N-ary association is a UML relationship in which three or more classes participate in a single, simultaneous semantic link. It is the generalization of the binary association for $n \geq 3$ participating classes. In a class diagram, it is represented by a **diamond symbol** (sometimes called a *junction symbol*) placed at the center, with **solid lines** drawn from the diamond to each of the $n$ participating classes. Each line carries its own multiplicity at the class end. If the link itself has attributes, an **association class** is attached to the diamond by a dashed line. The multiplicity on end $i$ means: *for any combination of objects from the other $n-1$ classes, the number of objects of class $i$ participating in the link lies in the multiplicity range.* **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for diamond notation, 1 for multiplicity semantics.)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Q: Differentiate between a binary association and an N-ary association. Give one example of each from a University Information System.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Binary Association | N-ary Association | |---|---|---| | Number of classes | Exactly **2** | **3 or more** | | UML notation | Single line between two classes | Central **diamond** connected to all classes | | Multiplicity semantics | Hold one object, count the other | Hold $(n-1)$-tuple, count the $n$-th | | Example (UIS) | `Student — EnrollsIn — Course` | `Student — Project — FacultyGuide` (ternary, with attribute *role*) | Binary: A *Student* enrolls in a *Course* — two classes suffice. N-ary: A *Student* works on a *Project* under a *FacultyGuide* — the relationship needs all three classes simultaneously; removing any one destroys the meaning. **(3 marks: 1 for conceptual difference, 1 for notation, 1 for example.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, with internal choice between two alternatives. Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Consider the following domain:** *In a hospital, a Doctor prescribes a Medication to a Patient on a particular Date with a specific Dosage. Each prescription is identified uniquely, and a patient may have multiple prescriptions over time.* **(a)** Draw the **ternary association** (Doctor–Patient–Medication) using UML notation, including the association class *Prescription* with attributes *prescriptionId*, *date*, and *dosage*. State reasonable multiplicities. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Show the **conversion** of this ternary association into an equivalent set of binary associations using the association class. Redraw the resulting class diagram. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Identify the participating classes:** - `Doctor` (attributes: `doctorId`, `name`, `specialization`) - `Patient` (attributes: `patientId`, `name`, `dob`) - `Medication` (attributes: `medicationId`, `name`, `dosageForm`) - `Prescription` (association class, attributes: `prescriptionId`, `date`, `dosage`) **Step 2 — Draw the central diamond `<>` connecting the three classes.** **Step 3 — Assign multiplicities (with justification):** - `Doctor` end: `1..*` — a doctor prescribes at least one and possibly many medications. - `Patient` end: `1..*` — a patient receives at least one and possibly many prescriptions. - `Medication` end: `1..*` — a medication can be prescribed to many patients. **Step 4 — Attach the association class via a dashed line from the diamond to `Prescription`.** **Resulting class diagram (textual representation):** ``` Doctor --------<> N <-------- Patient | | (association class) v Prescription - prescriptionId : int - date : Date - dosage : String Medication -----+ ``` **Mark allocation for (a):** - Correct identification of ternary relation: 1 mark - Diamond notation with three classes: 2 marks - Multiplicities: 2 marks - Association class with correct attributes: 2 marks **(Total: 7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 — Create the association class** `Prescription` as a regular first-class entity. **Step 2 — Replace the diamond with three binary associations:** - `Doctor — Prescription` (binary, multiplicity `1` on `Doctor` end, `1..*` on `Prescription` end) - `Patient — Prescription` (binary, multiplicity `1` on `Patient` end, `1..*` on `Prescription` end) - `Medication — Prescription` (binary, multiplicity `1` on `Medication` end, `1..*` on `Prescription` end) **Step 3 — Migrate the link attributes and add foreign keys** into `Prescription`: - `prescriptionId : int` (primary key) - `date : Date` - `dosage : String` - `doctorRef : Doctor` (reference) - `patientRef : Patient` (reference) - `medicationRef : Medication` (reference) **Step 4 — Verify equivalence:** For every original tuple `(d, p, m, date, dosage)` in the ternary relation $R$, there exists exactly one instance of `Prescription` with the corresponding attributes and references. The transformation is semantically lossless. **Mark allocation for (b):** - Identification of three binary links: 2 marks - Correct migration of attributes: 2 marks - Correct multiplicities (multiplicity migrates to the *Prescription* end, class end becomes `1`): 2 marks - Equivalence argument: 1 mark **(Total: 7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — *Internal Alternative* **Consider the academic domain:** *A Student registers for a Course under a Faculty instructor in a particular Semester. Each registration has a grade.* **(a)** Model the above scenario as a **quaternary association** (4 classes) with an association class *Registration* carrying attribute *grade*. State multiplicities and explain the semantic meaning of multiplicity at each end. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss **two practical advantages** of converting this quaternary association into a binary form using the association class approach. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Identify classes:** - `Student` - `Course` - `Faculty` (instructor) - `Semester` - `Registration` (association class with attribute `grade : Char`) **Step 2 — Draw the quaternary diamond with multiplicity at each end:** - `Student` end: `1..*` (a student registers in many semesters) - `Course` end: `1..*` (a course is offered across semesters) - `Faculty` end: `1..*` (a faculty teaches many courses across semesters) - `Semester` end: `1..*` (a semester has many students, courses, and faculty) **Step 3 — Multiplicity semantic explanation (this is the KTU key point):** The multiplicity on `Faculty` end, for instance, means: *for any given triple (Student, Course, Semester), the number of Faculty members who can teach that specific (Student, Course, Semester) combination is between 1 and many.* Equivalently, the multiplicity is evaluated by holding the other $(n-1)$ objects fixed as a tuple. **Mark allocation for (a):** - Correct identification of quaternary relation: 1 mark - Diamond connecting 4 classes: 2 marks - Reasonable multiplicities: 2 marks - Correct semantic explanation of multiplicity: 2 marks **(Total: 7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Two practical advantages of converting to binary with an association class:** 1. **Improved readability and navigability:** A single diamond connecting 4 or 5 classes produces a visually dense and confusing class diagram. Decomposing into multiple binary links yields a cleaner, modular diagram that is easier to maintain and to communicate to stakeholders. 2. **Reusability and extensibility:** Once the association class `Registration` is a first-class entity, additional behavior (methods like `assignGrade()`, `withdraw()`, etc.), additional attributes, and additional binary links (e.g., `Registration — Invoice` for fee tracking) can be added without restructuring the original N-ary construct. In contrast, attributes placed on the original diamond are difficult to extend. **Mark allocation for (b):** - First advantage with explanation: 3.5 marks - Second advantage with explanation: 3.5 marks **(Total: 7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not omit the diamond** when drawing an N-ary association — drawing only lines between three classes (without the central diamond) is treated as a *modeling error* and loses **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Do not confuse N-ary multiplicity with binary multiplicity.** For an N-ary association, the multiplicity at an end refers to the count when the *other* $(n-1)$ objects are jointly fixed. This is the single most common conceptual error in KTU answers. > 3. **Do not place link attributes on the participating classes** (e.g., do not put `grade` on `Student` or `Course`). Link attributes belong on the **association class**. > 4. **During conversion, do not invert multiplicities** — they migrate to the *association-class end* of the new binary link; the original class end becomes `1`. > 5. **Forgetting the dashed line** between the diamond and the association class is a recurring lapse — keep it dashed, not solid. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist for N-ary Association** - **Definition:** An N-ary association is a UML relationship involving $n \geq 3$ classes linked simultaneously. - **Notation:** A single central **diamond** connected by **solid lines** to all participating classes. - **Multiplicity semantics:** At end $i$, the multiplicity counts the number of $C_i$ objects participating in the link for a *fixed $(n-1)$-tuple* of objects from the other classes. - **Arity classification:** Unary (1), Binary (2), Ternary (3), N-ary ($n \geq 3$). - **Association class:** A separate class attached via a **dashed line** to the diamond, carrying the link's own attributes and behaviors. - **Conversion to binary (standard procedure):** 1. Promote the link to an association class. 2. Replace the diamond with $n$ binary associations. 3. Migrate link attributes into the association class. 4. Multiplicities migrate to the *association-class end*; the *class end* becomes `1`. 5. Verify semantic equivalence (lossless transformation). - **When to prefer binary form:** When $n \geq 4$, when the link has its own behavior, or when the diagram is becoming dense. - **When to keep N-ary form:** When the joint constraint is the *primary modeling intent* and the diagram remains readable. - **Common errors to avoid:** omitting the diamond, misplacing multiplicities, putting link attributes on participant classes, drawing a solid line (instead of dashed) to the association class. - **KTU cross-topic linkage:** N-ary associations are frequently combined with **generalization** (specializing one end) and **aggregation/composition** (the whole-part relation is *always* binary, never N-ary — a common KTU trick question). - **Key equation to memorize:** $R \subseteq C_1 \times C_2 \times \dots \times C_n$ with $n \geq 3$ for the N-ary form; $R \subseteq C_1 \times C_2$ for the binary form. - **Practical wisdom:** Most UML tools (StarUML, Visual Paradigm, Eclipse Papyrus) allow direct N-ary drawing, but **reverse-engineering** the binary equivalent is a standard refactoring operation expected in KTU lab/modeling exams.

topics.title: Abstract Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Abstract Classes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) within Software Engineering design, an **Abstract Class** is a class that cannot be instantiated directly and is typically declared using the `abstract` keyword. It serves as a **partial implementation blueprint** that may contain a mix of: - **Abstract methods** — method signatures (declarations) without an implementation body. - **Concrete methods** — fully implemented methods with a body. - **Instance variables, constructors, and static methods**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** > An abstract class is a class that is declared with the `abstract` keyword. It may or may not include abstract methods. Abstract classes **cannot be instantiated**, but they can be **subclassed**. When a concrete subclass inherits an abstract class, it must provide implementations for all inherited abstract methods, otherwise the subclass itself must also be declared `abstract`. The structural contract enforced by an abstract class is governed by three key Java rules: | Rule No. | Compiler-Enforced Rule | |:---:|:---| | 1 | A class containing **one or more abstract methods** must itself be declared `abstract`. | | 2 | Abstract classes **cannot be instantiated** using the `new` keyword. | | 3 | The **first concrete (non-abstract) subclass** must implement *all* inherited abstract methods. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" 🏗️** > > Imagine you are an architect. You draft a **blueprint** for a house that shows: > - The *exact layout* of the kitchen (a **concrete method** — fully specified). > - The *signature* of the bedroom — i.e., "every house must have a bedroom of X square feet with a door and window," but **how** the bedroom is decorated is left to the homeowner (an **abstract method**). > > The blueprint itself is **not a house** — you cannot *live in* a blueprint. But every *actual house* (concrete class) built from it **must** follow the bedroom requirement. > > The blueprint = **Abstract Class** | The house = **Concrete Subclass** ## 1.3 Why Abstract Classes Exist in Software Engineering From a Software Engineering perspective, abstract classes are a foundational tool for: 1. **Enforcing Design Contracts** — ensuring uniform method signatures across related classes. 2. **Promoting Code Reusability** — shared concrete code lives in one place (the abstract class). 3. **Achieving Partial Abstraction** — hiding implementation while exposing structure (a key pillar of OOP). 4. **Supporting the Open/Closed Principle** — open for extension via subclasses, closed for modification. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Abstract classes realize the OOP principle of **Abstraction** — exposing only essential features and hiding background details. They sit between a fully concrete `class` and a fully abstract `interface`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Abstract Class — Structured Logic ### Step 1: Declaration Syntax The class is declared using the `abstract` modifier placed before the `class` keyword. $$\text{access\_modifier}\ \text{abstract}\ \text{class}\ \text{ClassName}\ \{\ \dots\ \}$$ ### Step 2: Method Categorization An abstract class can host **three types** of methods: - **Abstract methods** — declared with `abstract` keyword, ending with a semicolon, **no body**. - **Concrete methods** — fully implemented with `{ }` body. - **Static / Final methods** — class-level methods that cannot be overridden. ### Step 3: Inheritance Enforcement Any non-abstract child class must `override` every abstract method inherited from the parent abstract class. Failure to do so results in a **compile-time error**. ### Step 4: Constructor Presence Abstract classes **can have constructors**, even though they cannot be instantiated. These constructors are invoked when a subclass object is created (via `super()`). ### Step 5: Reference Variable Usage Although you cannot create an object of an abstract class, you **can** create a **reference variable** of its type to achieve runtime polymorphism (upcasting). ## 2.2 Abstract Class vs Interface — The KTU Hot Comparison | Feature | Abstract Class | Interface | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` | | Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract (default), default, static (Java 8+) | | Variables | Instance + Static + Final | `public static final` only (constants) | | Constructor | ✅ Allowed | ❌ Not allowed | | Access Modifiers | Any (public, protected, private) | `public` only (pre-Java 9) | | Multiple Inheritance | ❌ Single inheritance | ✅ Multiple inheritance supported | | `final` keyword | Can be `final` (cannot be inherited) | Cannot be `final` | | Implementation Speed | Fast (concrete methods inherited) | Slower (needs implementation) | | When to Use | Shared code + contract | Pure contract / capability | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024):** Use an **abstract class** when classes share a *common implementation* and a *common type*. Use an **interface** when unrelated classes need to share a *common capability* (e.g., `Serializable`, `Comparable`). ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Syntax Sheet (Markdown Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Rule:** The Java syntax cheat sheet below is to be **memorized verbatim** for the KTU 2024 ESE. | Construct | Exact Syntax (Java) | |:---|:---| | Abstract Class Declaration | `abstract class Shape { }` | | Abstract Method Declaration | `abstract double area();` | | Concrete Method in Abstract Class | `void display() { System.out.println("Shape"); }` | | Abstract Class Inheritance | `class Circle extends Shape { }` | | Overriding Abstract Method | `@Override double area() { return 3.14 * r * r; }` | | Abstract Subclass (chain) | `abstract class Solid extends Shape { }` | | Reference of Abstract Type | `Shape s = new Circle();` (Upcasting) | | Constructor in Abstract Class | `Shape(int dim) { this.dim = dim; }` | ## 2.4 Real-World Software Engineering Utility Abstract classes are heavily used in: - **Framework Design** — Spring Framework, JavaFX use abstract base classes (`Application`, `HttpServlet`). - **Template Method Design Pattern** — defines skeleton of algorithm in abstract class, lets subclasses override specific steps. - **GUI Library Design** — `JFrame`, `Applet`, and `Component` are abstract in AWT/Swing. - **Plugin Architectures** — base abstract plugin class extended by 3rd-party developers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The "Type Completeness" Rule In OOP, a class is considered **"complete"** only when every abstract method it declares (or inherits) has a concrete implementation. We can formalize this as: $$ \text{Completeness}(C) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \forall m \in \text{AbstractMethods}(C),\ \exists\ \text{impl}(m) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ If $\text{Completeness}(C) = 0$, then $C$ itself must be declared `abstract`. > **Logical Reasoning:** > - Let $A$ be the set of abstract methods in parent class $P$. > - Let $I_C$ be the set of concrete implementations in subclass $C$. > - $C$ is a **valid concrete class** only if $A \subseteq I_C$. ### Worked Example — Step-by-Step Trace **Problem:** Consider the abstract class `Vehicle` with abstract method `startEngine()`. The class `Car` extends `Vehicle`. Trace whether `Car` can be instantiated. **Step 1:** Parent class declaration. ```java abstract class Vehicle { abstract void startEngine(); } ``` **Step 2:** Attempt 1 — Incomplete subclass. ```java class Car extends Vehicle { // No implementation of startEngine() } ``` **Step 3:** Compile-time error generated: $$ \text{ERROR: Car is not abstract and does not override abstract method startEngine() in Vehicle} $$ **Step 4:** Corrective Action — Implement the method. ```java class Car extends Vehicle { @Override void startEngine() { System.out.println("Car engine started."); } } ``` **Step 5:** Now the completeness set is satisfied: $$ A = \{\text{startEngine}\},\quad I_{\text{Car}} = \{\text{startEngine}\},\quad A \subseteq I_{\text{Car}} \implies \text{Completeness}(\text{Car}) = 1 $$ **Step 6:** `Car` can now be instantiated. ```java Vehicle v = new Car(); // ✅ Allowed (upcasting) v.startEngine(); // Dynamic dispatch → Car's version ``` ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Method Resolution Table | Method Type in Abstract Class | Allowed in Abstract Class? | Must be Implemented by Concrete Subclass? | |:---|:---:|:---:| | `abstract void methodA();` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | `void methodB() { ... }` (concrete) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Optional | | `static void methodC() { ... }` | ✅ Yes | ❌ Cannot be overridden | | `final void methodD() { ... }` | ✅ Yes | ❌ Cannot be overridden | | `private void methodE() { ... }` | ✅ Yes | ❌ Not inherited | | Constructor | ✅ Yes | ❌ Inherited (used via `super()`) | ## 3.3 Upcasting & Runtime Polymorphism — Analytical Proof When a reference of an abstract type holds a concrete object, Java uses **Dynamic Method Dispatch**. $$ \text{Reference Type} = \text{Shape (abstract)}, \quad \text{Object Type} = \text{Circle (concrete)} $$ At runtime, the JVM resolves: $$ \text{Method Bound} = \text{type}(\text{object on heap}) = \text{Circle} $$ Therefore, calling `s.area()` invokes `Circle.area()`, even though `s` is declared as `Shape`. This is the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** in action. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Class Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid classDiagram class Shape { <> -color : String +Shape(String c) +display() void +area() double +perimeter() double } class Circle { -radius : double +Circle(double r, String c) +area() double +perimeter() double } class Rectangle { -length : double -width : double +Rectangle(double l, double w, String c) +area() double +perimeter() double } class Triangle { -base : double -height : double +area() double } Shape <|-- Circle Shape <|-- Rectangle Shape <|-- Triangle <>Drawable Drawable <|.. Circle ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Object-Creation Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Program Main Method] --> B[Declare Shape ref] B --> C{Is Shape abstract?} C -- Yes --> D[new Circle 5.0 red] C -- No --> E[Direct Instantiation] D --> F[Call super in Circle constructor] F --> G[Set color in Shape field] G --> H[Invoke display] H --> I[Invoke area - runtime dispatch] I --> J[Circle area computed = 78.5] E -.->|Not allowed| K[Compile Error] style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Template Method Pattern Architecture (Using Abstract Class) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AbstractClass: DataMiner] --> B[Template Method: Mine] B --> C[Step 1: OpenFile concrete] B --> D[Step 2: ExtractData abstract] B --> E[Step 3: ParseData concrete] B --> F[Step 4: AnalyzeData abstract] B --> G[Step 5: SendReport concrete] D --> H[ConcreteClass: PDFMiner] D --> I[ConcreteClass: CSVMiner] F --> H F --> I style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Memory Representation Diagram (Heap + Stack) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ST[Stack Memory] S[Shape ref s
Address: 0xABC] end subgraph HEAP[Heap Memory] O[Circle Object
radius = 5.0
color = red
vtable: area= Circle.area] end S -->|points to| O style ST fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#333 style HEAP fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#333 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define an Abstract Class. Can we instantiate it? Justify. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that may contain abstract methods (without body) and concrete methods (with body). It represents a **partial implementation** of a class hierarchy. **(2 Marks)** No, we **cannot instantiate** an abstract class directly using the `new` keyword. The compiler throws an error: *"Shape is abstract; cannot be instantiated."* This is because abstract classes are **incomplete** by design — they may declare methods without implementations. **(1 Mark)** --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Abstract Class and Concrete Class. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Abstract Class | Concrete Class | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Instantiation | Cannot be instantiated | Can be instantiated | | `abstract` keyword | Required | Not used | | Abstract methods | May contain | Cannot contain | | Purpose | Blueprint / Contract | Fully functional implementation | **(3 Marks — award 1 mark per correct row)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### ➤ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`** > **(a)** Explain the concept of abstract classes in Java with suitable syntax. List any **four** rules that must be followed while using abstract classes. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Write a Java program to define an abstract class `BankAccount` with abstract method `calculateInterest()`. Create two subclasses `SavingsAccount` and `FixedDepositAccount` that implement this method differently. Demonstrate polymorphism using an abstract class reference. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Definition (2 Marks):** An abstract class is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword. It may contain both abstract methods (declarations only) and concrete methods (with implementation). It cannot be instantiated and serves as a **base template** for subclasses. **Syntax (1 Mark):** ```java abstract class ClassName { abstract returnType methodName(params); returnType concreteMethod() { /* body */ } } ``` **Four Rules (4 Marks — 1 Mark each):** 1. **Rule 1 — Declaration:** If a class contains even one abstract method, the class itself must be declared `abstract`. 2. **Rule 2 — No Instantiation:** Abstract classes cannot be instantiated using the `new` keyword. 3. **Rule 3 — Implementation Mandate:** The first non-abstract subclass must implement *all* inherited abstract methods. 4. **Rule 4 — Constructor Allowed:** Abstract classes can have constructors, which are invoked by subclass constructors via `super()`. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] ```java // Abstract class definition (1 Mark) abstract class BankAccount { protected double principal; protected double rate; protected int time; BankAccount(double p, double r, int t) { this.principal = p; this.rate = r; this.time = t; } abstract double calculateInterest(); // abstract method (1 Mark) void displayAccount() { // concrete method System.out.println("Principal: " + principal); } } // Subclass 1: SavingsAccount (2 Marks) class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount { SavingsAccount(double p, double r, int t) { super(p, r, t); } @Override double calculateInterest() { return (principal * rate * time) / 100.0; } } // Subclass 2: FixedDepositAccount (2 Marks) class FixedDepositAccount extends BankAccount { FixedDepositAccount(double p, double r, int t) { super(p, r, t); } @Override double calculateInterest() { // FD uses compound interest return principal * Math.pow(1 + rate / 100.0, time) - principal; } } // Main class demonstrating polymorphism (1 Mark) public class BankDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { BankAccount ref; // Abstract reference ref = new SavingsAccount(10000, 5, 3); System.out.println("Savings Interest: " + ref.calculateInterest()); ref = new FixedDepositAccount(10000, 6, 3); System.out.println("FD Interest: " + ref.calculateInterest()); } } ``` **Output Trace:** - Savings: $10000 \times 5 \times 3 / 100 = 1500.0$ - FD: $10000 \times (1.06)^3 - 10000 = 1910.16$ **Polymorphism Proof:** The `BankAccount ref` variable holds different subclass objects, and `calculateInterest()` resolves at runtime to the correct subclass method — a clear demonstration of **dynamic dispatch**. --- ### ➤ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** > **(a)** Discuss the differences between **abstract classes** and **interfaces** in Java. When would you prefer one over the other? Provide examples. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Design an abstract class `Employee` with attributes `name`, `id`, and `basicSalary`, and an abstract method `calculateNetSalary()`. Derive two classes `Manager` and `Developer`. Show how runtime polymorphism is achieved. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Comparison Table (5 Marks):** | Feature | Abstract Class | Interface | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` | | Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + default + static | | Variables | Any access type | `public static final` (constants) | | Inheritance | Single only | Multiple allowed | | Constructor | Allowed | Not allowed | | Access Modifier | Any | `public` only | | `final` allowed | Yes (class can be `final`) | No | | Speed | Fast (concrete code shared) | Slower (requires impl) | | When to Use | "IS-A" with shared code | "CAN-DO" capability | **Preference Rules (2 Marks):** - **Use Abstract Class** when related classes share **both code and contract** (e.g., `HttpServlet`). - **Use Interface** when unrelated classes need to share a **common capability** (e.g., `Comparable`, `Serializable`). **Example:** ```java abstract class Animal { abstract void sound(); } interface Pet { void play(); } class Dog extends Animal implements Pet { void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); } public void play() { System.out.println("Fetch"); } } ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] ```java // Abstract Employee class (2 Marks) abstract class Employee { protected String name; protected int id; protected double basicSalary; Employee(String n, int i, double s) { this.name = n; this.id = i; this.basicSalary = s; } abstract double calculateNetSalary(); void displayDetails() { System.out.println("ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name); } } // Manager subclass (2 Marks) class Manager extends Employee { private double bonus; Manager(String n, int i, double s, double b) { super(n, i, s); this.bonus = b; } @Override double calculateNetSalary() { return basicSalary + bonus - (0.10 * basicSalary); // 10% tax } } // Developer subclass (2 Marks) class Developer extends Employee { private int overtimeHours; Developer(String n, int i, double s, int o) { super(n, i, s); this.overtimeHours = o; } @Override double calculateNetSalary() { return basicSalary + (overtimeHours * 500) - (0.10 * basicSalary); } } // Polymorphism Demo (1 Mark) public class EmpDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Employee e; e = new Manager("Alice", 101, 50000, 10000); System.out.println("Manager Net: " + e.calculateNetSalary()); e = new Developer("Bob", 102, 40000, 20); System.out.println("Developer Net: " + e.calculateNetSalary()); } } ``` **Polymorphism Proof:** Single reference `e` of abstract type invokes two different implementations at runtime — Manager version first, then Developer version. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the `abstract` keyword** on the class when it contains an abstract method → 0 marks for class design. > 2. **Trying to instantiate** the abstract class (`new Shape()`) in code → marks deducted for conceptual misunderstanding. > 3. **Not using `@Override` annotation** in subclasses → 1 mark deducted in code (still works, but shows poor practice). > 4. **Confusing abstract class with interface** in comparison questions → mention BOTH "what" AND "when to use" for full 7 marks. > 5. **Forgetting constructors in abstract class** during code outputs → output traces will be wrong. > 6. **Using `final` and `abstract` together on a method** → compile error; examiners deduct 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 KTU 2024 Quick-Fire Revision — Abstract Classes** - ✅ An **abstract class** is declared with the `abstract` keyword and **cannot be instantiated**. - ✅ It may contain **0 or more abstract methods** (declarations only) and **any number of concrete methods**. - ✅ The first **concrete subclass** MUST implement **all** abstract methods, or it must also be declared abstract. - ✅ Abstract classes **CAN have constructors** — used by subclass via `super()`. - ✅ Abstract classes **CAN have static, final, and private methods**. - ✅ **Reference variables** of abstract type ARE allowed → enables **runtime polymorphism**. - ✅ Abstract classes follow **Single Inheritance** (extend only one class). - ✅ Use **abstract class** for **"IS-A" with shared code**, **interface** for **"CAN-DO" capability**. - ✅ Abstract class = **partial abstraction** | Interface = **full abstraction** (pre-Java 8). - ✅ **Template Method Design Pattern** is the most common software engineering use case. - ✅ Java does NOT support **multiple inheritance with classes** — use interfaces instead. - ✅ `@Override` annotation is **best practice** when implementing abstract methods. - ✅ Abstract methods **cannot be** `private`, `static`, or `final`. - ✅ A class that doesn't override **any** abstract methods from its abstract parent must itself be `abstract`.

topics.title: Aggregation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aggregation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal UML Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Aggregation** is a specialized form of an **Association** relationship in the Unified Modeling Language (UML) that defines a *whole–part* (also called *has-a*) semantic connection between two classes. It is a **weak "has-a" relationship** in which the *part* (child) can exist **independently** of the *whole* (parent). If the whole is destroyed, the part is *not* necessarily destroyed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** "Aggregation is a variant of the association relationship in which there is a whole-part relationship where the part can exist independently of the whole. It is represented by an empty (hollow) diamond at the whole end of the association."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **University** and its **Departments**:

- A Department *belongs to* a University (it is a part of it).
- However, if the University ceases to exist (hypothetically), the Department as a logical academic unit can still survive (perhaps be merged into another University or become autonomous).
- The Department does **not** lose its identity purely because the University is destroyed.

Other classic real-world analogies that KTU examiners frequently use:

| Domain | Whole | Part (Independent) |
|---|---|---|
| Education | Department | Professor |
| Sports | Team | Player |
| Library | Library | Book |
| Corporate | Company | Employee |
| Automobile | Car | Wheel (in aggregation semantics) |
| Organization | Organization | Member |

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard UML multiplicity convention** allows one whole to be associated with **0..\*** parts, and a part can belong to **0..1** or **0..\*** wholes — because sharing is allowed.

### 1.3 Why Is Aggregation Important in KTU Examinations?

Aggregation is one of the highest-yield topics in **Module 3 (UML Structural Modeling)** because it is almost always paired with a contrast question asking: *"Distinguish between Aggregation and Composition."* Examiners test whether the student understands:

1. The **diamond notation** (hollow vs filled).
2. The **lifecycle dependency** (independent vs dependent).
3. The **strength of coupling** (weak vs strong).
4. The **sharing semantics** (shared vs exclusive ownership).
5. The **translation to code** (weak reference vs strong reference / ownership pointer).

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** *Aggregation = Hollow Diamond + Part outlives the Whole + Sharing allowed.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an Aggregation Relationship

An aggregation relationship is documented in a UML class diagram using the following graphical elements:

- **Hollow (empty) diamond** placed at the *whole* (aggregate) end of the association line.
- **Solid line** connecting the whole to its part(s).
- **Role names** (optional) at each end to clarify the meaning of the link.
- **Multiplicity** indicators at each end (e.g., `1`, `0..*`, `1..*`).

The "**whole**" class is called the **Aggregate**, and the "**part**" class is called the **Component**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Aggregation Semantics

1. **Identification of Whole and Part:** A class that *contains* or *logically groups* other classes is the aggregate. The contained classes are the components.
2. **Lifecycle Check:** If the component can exist *without* the aggregate, the relationship is aggregation. If it cannot, the relationship is composition.
3. **Sharing Check:** If the same component instance can be associated with *more than one* aggregate, the relationship is aggregation. Composition forbids sharing.
4. **Multiplicity Assignment:** Apply cardinalities (1, 0..1, 0..\*, 1..\*) at both ends.
5. **Notation Rendering:** Draw a hollow diamond at the whole's end; never use a filled diamond (that would be composition).
6. **Code Translation:** Use a *weak reference* (pointer/handle) in the whole that does **not** own the part.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Property | Aggregation | Composition (for contrast) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | UML Notation | Hollow (empty) diamond | Filled (solid) diamond |
| 2 | Relationship Strength | Weak "has-a" | Strong "has-a" / "part-of" |
| 3 | Lifecycle Dependency | Part is **independent** of whole | Part is **dependent** on whole |
| 4 | Ownership Semantics | Shared ownership (logical) | Exclusive ownership (physical) |
| 5 | Sharing Allowed? | **Yes** (one part → many wholes) | **No** (one part → one whole) |
| 6 | Multiplicity of Part | `0..1` or `0..*` | Exactly `1` (typical) |
| 7 | Garbage Collection | Whole has no delete-rights on part | Whole destroys part |
| 8 | Code Realization | Weak reference (pointer / handle) | Strong reference / by-value member |
| 9 | Example | Team – Player | House – Room |
| 10 | Cardinality Constraint | $\text{whole} \rightarrow \text{part} : 1 \ldots *$ | $\text{whole} \rightarrow \text{part} : 1 \ldots n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Multiplicity Notation (KTU accepted forms):**
> - `1` → exactly one
> - `0..1` → zero or one
> - `*` or `0..*` → zero or many
> - `1..*` → one or many
> - `n..m` → between $n$ and $m$ (inclusive)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Aggregation is extensively used in:

- **Domain Modeling:** Capturing business rules such as "a Project is worked on by many Employees."
- **Database Schema Design:** Translating to *foreign-key* relationships (non-identifying relationships in ER diagrams).
- **Object-Oriented Programming:** Guiding the choice between storing an object by *value* (composition) or by *reference* (aggregation).
- **Microservices / System Architecture:** Mapping loosely coupled services that share common data entities (e.g., a shared `Customer` entity referenced by `Order` and `Invoice` services).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Decision Algorithm — When to Use Aggregation

Below is the formal decision procedure (often asked in KTU as a 7-mark sub-question) to classify a "whole–part" relationship as either **Aggregation**, **Composition**, or **Plain Association**.

$$
\text{Step 1: } \text{Identify two candidate classes } A \text{ (potential whole) and } B \text{ (potential part).}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } \text{Check: Does } B \text{ have a meaningful existence without } A ?
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } \text{If } \textbf{YES} \Rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 4. If } \textbf{NO} \Rightarrow \text{Use Composition (filled diamond).}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: } \text{Check: Can the same instance of } B \text{ belong to multiple } A \text{ simultaneously?}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: } \text{If } \textbf{YES} \Rightarrow \textbf{Aggregation (hollow diamond). If } \textbf{NO} \Rightarrow \text{Use Composition.}
$$

$$
\text{Step 6: } \text{Assign multiplicities: } A \xrightarrow{\;m\;} B \quad \text{and} \quad B \xrightarrow{\;n\;} A
$$

$$
\text{Step 7: } \text{Default: } m = 1 \text{ (one whole has many parts), } n = 0..1 \text{ (part is shared with at most one other whole).}
$$

> **Reasoning (the "Why"):** This algorithm is constructed from the *two distinguishing invariants* of aggregation: (a) **lifetime independence** and (b) **multi-whole sharing**. Both must be true for the relationship to qualify as aggregation. Failing either pushes the relationship to composition.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Contrast Format)

The most common KTU 14-mark question is: *"Differentiate between Aggregation and Composition with suitable examples and UML diagrams."* Below is the model answer matrix used in valuation.

| Comparison Axis | Aggregation | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **UML Symbol** | $\Diamond$ (hollow) | $\blacklozenge$ (filled) |
| **Strength** | Weak association | Strong association |
| **Lifetime Coupling** | Independent | Dependent |
| **Deletion Effect** | Deleting whole does **not** delete part | Deleting whole deletes part |
| **Sharing** | Allowed | Not allowed |
| **Multiplicity (Part end)** | $0..*$ (flexible) | $1$ (mandatory) |
| **Code Translation** | Pointer / reference member | Object-valued member |
| **Example 1** | Team $\rightarrow$ Player | House $\rightarrow$ Room |
| **Example 2** | Library $\rightarrow$ Book | Human $\rightarrow$ Heart |
| **Example 3** | Company $\rightarrow$ Employee | Tree $\rightarrow$ Leaf |
| **Example 4** | Department $\rightarrow$ Professor | Car $\rightarrow$ Engine |
| **Example 5** | Project $\rightarrow$ Task | Document $\rightarrow$ Paragraph |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Library System

Consider a **Library** that holds many **Books**.

- A Book can exist in a catalogue even if the Library is shut down.
- The same Book can be referenced by multiple Library branches (sharing).
- Therefore, the relationship is **Aggregation**.

UML Multiplicity Interpretation:

$$
\text{Library} \xrightarrow{\;\;1\;\;} \text{contains} \xrightarrow{\;\;0..*\;\;} \text{Book}
$$

This equation reads: **One** Library may contain **zero or many** Books, and each Book may belong to **zero or many** Libraries (sharing is permitted).

### 3.4 Anti-Pattern Identification (Common KTU Pitfall)

$$
\text{If } \exists \, b \in B \text{ such that } b \text{ is destroyed when its aggregate } a \in A \text{ is destroyed} \Rightarrow \text{Use Composition, NOT Aggregation.}
$$

$$
\text{If } \exists \, b \in B \text{ such that } b \in A_1 \cap A_2 \text{ simultaneously} \Rightarrow \text{Use Aggregation, NOT Composition.}
$$

> **Reasoning:** These two logical conditions are the *defining boundary conditions* of aggregation. Confusing them is the single biggest reason students lose marks in KTU Module 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Aggregation Example (Library / Book)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Library {
        -libraryID : int
        -name : string
        -location : string
        +addBook(b : Book) void
        +removeBook(b : Book) void
    }
    class Book {
        -ISBN : string
        -title : string
        -author : string
        -price : float
        +displayInfo() void
    }
    Library "1" o-- "0..*" Book : aggregates
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The hollow diamond `o--` indicates **Aggregation**.
- Library is the **Whole (Aggregate)**; Book is the **Part (Component)**.
- `1` → one Library; `0..*` → zero or many Books.

### 4.2 Mermaid Class Diagram — Contrast View (Aggregation vs Composition)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction TB
    class Department {
        -deptID : int
        -deptName : string
    }
    class Professor {
        -profID : int
        -profName : string
        -specialization : string
    }
    class Human {
        -name : string
        -age : int
    }
    class Heart {
        -heartRate : int
    }
    class House {
        -address : string
    }
    class Room {
        -roomNumber : int
        -area : float
    }
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Professor : aggregation
    Human "1" *-- "1" Heart : composition
    House "1" *-- "1..*" Room : composition
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `o--` = hollow diamond → **Aggregation** (Professor can exist without Department).
- `*--` = filled diamond → **Composition** (Heart cannot exist without Human; Room cannot exist without House).

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Whole Class] --> B{Does Part outlive Whole?}
    B -- No --> C[Use COMPOSITION\nFilled Diamond]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is Part shared\nacross many Wholes?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Use AGGREGATION\nHollow Diamond]
    E --> F[Assign Multiplicity 1 to 0..*]
    F --> G[Translate to Weak Reference in Code]
    C --> H[Assign Multiplicity 1 to 1..*]
    H --> I[Translate to Strong Reference or Value Member in Code]
```

This decision flow chart is the **exact valuation pattern** examiners expect when a 7-mark sub-question asks: *"Justify why you chose aggregation over composition for the following scenario."*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Aggregation. How is it represented in a UML class diagram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aggregation is a specialized form of association that represents a **whole–part** relationship in which the part can exist independently of the whole. It is a weak form of the "has-a" relationship.

In a UML class diagram, aggregation is represented by an **empty (hollow) diamond** placed at the *whole* end of the association line connecting the aggregate (whole) to the component (part). For example, a *Department aggregates Professors* — drawn as:

$$
\text{Department} \;\Diamond\!\!-\!\!-\!\!-\!\!-\!\!\!> \;\text{Professor}
$$

The hollow diamond signifies that while a Professor *belongs to* a Department, the Professor retains an independent identity and can be transferred to another Department.

**(1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for diamond notation, **1 mark** for example.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State any two differences between Aggregation and Composition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Aggregation | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Represented by a **hollow diamond** in UML. | Represented by a **filled diamond** in UML. |
| 2 | The part can exist **independently** of the whole (e.g., Team – Player). | The part **cannot exist** without the whole (e.g., Human – Heart). |

**(1.5 marks per valid difference, with correct UML symbol and example.)**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the concept of Aggregation in UML. Discuss its key characteristics and UML notation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Aggregation with Composition using a real-world example for each. State at least four distinguishing points. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a)

**Aggregation** is a whole–part association in which the part has an **independent lifecycle** from the whole. It models a *weak ownership* or *has-a* relationship.

**Key Characteristics:**

1. **Whole–Part Semantic:** The aggregate (whole) logically groups one or more components (parts).
2. **Lifecycle Independence:** The component is *not* destroyed when the aggregate is destroyed.
3. **Sharing Permitted:** The same component may be associated with multiple aggregates.
4. **Weak Ownership:** The aggregate does not have exclusive possession of the component.
5. **Multiplicity:** Typically `1` at the whole end and `0..*` at the part end.

**UML Notation:**

The relationship is drawn using a solid line with a **hollow diamond** at the aggregate (whole) end. Role names and multiplicities are placed at each end of the line.

**Example — Department and Professor:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Department {
        -deptID : int
        -deptName : string
    }
    class Professor {
        -profID : int
        -profName : string
    }
    Department "1" o-- "0..*" Professor : employs
```

**Explanation:** A Department *employs* many Professors, but a Professor continues to exist (and may move to another Department) even if the original Department is dissolved. This independence makes it an aggregation, not a composition.

**(2 marks for definition + characteristics, 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for example explanation.)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b)

| S.No. | Aggregation | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hollow diamond** $\Diamond$ in UML. | **Filled diamond** $\blacklozenge$ in UML. |
| 2 | **Independent lifecycle** — part outlives whole. | **Dependent lifecycle** — part is destroyed with the whole. |
| 3 | **Sharing allowed** — one part can be in many wholes. | **Sharing not allowed** — one part belongs to exactly one whole. |
| 4 | **Weak coupling** — pointer/reference in code. | **Strong coupling** — value member or owning pointer in code. |
| 5 | **Example:** Team – Player (Player may transfer to another team). | **Example:** House – Room (Room cannot exist without House). |

**Aggregation Example — Team and Player:**

A Team *has* Players. If a Team is dissolved, the Players continue to exist and may join other Teams. The same Player can play for multiple Teams across seasons (e.g., international + domestic). Hence, aggregation.

**Composition Example — House and Room:**

A House is composed of Rooms. If the House is demolished, the Rooms are physically destroyed as well. A Room cannot simultaneously belong to two Houses. Hence, composition.

**(1 mark per row of comparison, up to 4 rows for 4 marks; 3 marks for two correct examples with explanations.)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Identify the type of relationship (Aggregation / Composition / Association) for the following scenarios and justify your answer with a UML class diagram for each:

- (i) University — Department
- (ii) Building — Wing
- (iii) Customer — Order **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the choice between Aggregation and Composition impacts the code-level design of an object-oriented system. Give one code-level example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a)

**(i) University — Department → AGGREGATION**

A Department has logical existence even if the University is dissolved (it can be merged into another University or become autonomous). Therefore, the relationship is **Aggregation** (hollow diamond).

**(ii) Building — Wing → COMPOSITION**

A Wing is a physical part of a Building. If the Building is demolished, the Wing is destroyed as well. A Wing cannot belong to two different Buildings. Therefore, the relationship is **Composition** (filled diamond).

**(iii) Customer — Order → AGGREGATION**

A Customer can place many Orders, and an Order belongs to one Customer. However, an Order can exist in a system's records (e.g., a historical invoice) even after the Customer is deleted. Additionally, in some advanced systems, the same Order can be referenced by multiple Customer records (e.g., a corporate customer + a sub-account). Hence, **Aggregation** is preferred.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class University {
        -uniID : int
        -name : string
    }
    class Department {
        -deptID : int
        -deptName : string
    }
    class Building {
        -buildingID : int
        -address : string
    }
    class Wing {
        -wingID : int
        -floorCount : int
    }
    class Customer {
        -custID : int
        -name : string
    }
    class Order {
        -orderID : int
        -date : string
        -amount : float
    }
    University "1" o-- "1..*" Department : aggregation
    Building "1" *-- "1..*" Wing : composition
    Customer "1" o-- "0..*" Order : aggregation
```

**(2 marks per scenario: 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for justification with lifecycle argument.)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b)

The choice between Aggregation and Composition has direct implications for **memory management**, **object lifecycle**, and **coupling** in code.

**Aggregation → Weak Reference (Pointer / Handle):**

The aggregate holds a *pointer* to the part but does not own it. The part's lifetime is managed externally. Deleting the aggregate does **not** delete the part.

> **Illustration (C++ flavor, conceptual only):**
>
> - The `Department` class stores a `std::vector<Professor*>` (raw pointers).
> - The `Professor` objects are created and destroyed by some other component (e.g., the University's HR module).
> - When the Department object is destroyed, only the vector is freed — the Professor objects remain in memory.

**Composition → Strong Reference (Value Member or Owning Pointer):**

The aggregate *owns* the part. The part is created when the aggregate is created and destroyed when the aggregate is destroyed.

> **Illustration (C++ flavor, conceptual only):**
>
> - The `House` class contains `Room` objects as value members: `std::vector<Room> rooms;`.
> - When the `House` object goes out of scope, the `Room` objects are automatically destroyed with it.
> - Alternatively, the `House` may use `std::unique_ptr<Room>` to express exclusive ownership.

**Design Impact Summary:**

- **Memory:** Aggregation is cheaper at destruction time; composition is cheaper at construction (no need to allocate separately).
- **Sharing:** Aggregation allows the same part to be referenced from multiple wholes; composition forbids it.
- **Coupling:** Aggregation reduces coupling (part can be replaced); composition increases coupling (part is integral).

**(3 marks for aggregation code pattern, 3 marks for composition code pattern, 1 mark for design-impact summary.)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Aggregation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Drawing the WRONG diamond:** A filled diamond on a whole–part *with independent lifecycle* is an instant **0/7** on the diagram sub-part. Always verify lifecycle first.
> 2. **Forgetting multiplicities:** KTU valuation explicitly checks for correct multiplicity at both ends. A missing `0..*` or `1` loses 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing "has-a" with "is-a":** Aggregation is *has-a* (whole–part). "is-a" is **Generalization (Inheritance)** — a completely different UML relationship. Do not mix them up.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong example:** Saying "Car has-a Engine" is a *classic* example used by almost every textbook. Examiners prefer fresher, less-rehearsed examples such as *Library–Book*, *Team–Player*, or *Organization–Member*.
> 5. **Omitting role names:** Role names like `employs`, `has`, `contains` are expected in KTU diagrams and earn easy marks.
> 6. **Writing code when not asked:** For a 7-mark "explain with diagram" question, the diagram is the core deliverable. Code is only expected for the specific sub-question that asks for it.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aggregation** = **Hollow diamond** + **Independent lifecycle** + **Sharing allowed**.
- It is a **weak form of association** that models a whole–part relationship without ownership.
- The **part outlives the whole** — this is the *defining* characteristic.
- The **same part may be shared** by multiple wholes.
- **Multiplicity** at the part end is typically `0..*`; at the whole end, it is typically `1`.
- **Code translation:** Aggregation uses a **weak reference** (pointer/handle); Composition uses a **strong reference** (value member or owning pointer).
- **UML symbol cheat sheet for Module 3:**

  $$ \text{Association} : \texttt{------>} \qquad \text{Aggregation} : \texttt{o---->} \qquad \text{Composition} : \texttt{*---->} \qquad \text{Generalization} : \texttt{--}\blacktriangleright \texttt{} $$

- **Always pair aggregation with composition** in answers — KTU examiners reward comparative answers with full marks.
- **Real-world examples to memorize (in priority order):** Team–Player, Library–Book, Department–Professor, Company–Employee.
- **Boundary cases to avoid:** *Car–Wheel* (typically composition), *Car–Engine* (typically composition), *Human–Heart* (definitely composition).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Object-Oriented Software Engineering (OOSE)**, a **Package** (also called a *namespace*, *module*, or *component grouping*) is a logical and physical grouping of related **classes, interfaces, and sub-packages** that share a common purpose, theme, or responsibility. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT503 – Software Engineering, Module 3)**, a package represents the highest granularity of software design — it encapsulates a set of cohesive classes that are designed, built, and released together as a single deployable unit.

Formally, a package is:
> A named collection of logically related classes and sub-packages, characterized by internal **high cohesion** and external **low coupling**, governed by a set of architectural design principles for dependency management, reusability, and stability.

In the context of the **KTU syllabus**, packages are treated as the principal design units above classes, addressing the following architectural concerns:

1. **Grouping** — Logical clustering of related classes.
2. **Encapsulation at scale** — Hiding implementation details of groups of classes.
3. **Reusability** — Designing packages as reusable components.
4. **Independent Deployability** — Making packages releasable in isolation.
5. **Dependency Management** — Controlling inter-package relationships.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> The term "Package" in this module specifically refers to the **physical packaging of OO design elements** (as per the unified process / GRASP / Robert C. Martin's *Clean Architecture* taxonomy). It is **not** the same as Java's `package` keyword alone — it is a broader architectural concept of designing the **package/component diagram** level of software architecture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a **massive library** with millions of books. If you just dump all the books on the floor in random order, finding any single book becomes a nightmare. So, you do the following:

1. You group books by **subject** (Science, Literature, History) → these are your **Packages**.
2. Inside each subject, you group by **sub-topic** (Physics, Chemistry, Biology) → these are **Sub-packages**.
3. You place related books together on a single shelf → this is **High Cohesion**.
4. You keep references between sections minimal (e.g., a History book rarely pulls a Physics book off its shelf) → this is **Low Coupling**.
5. You decide which sections depend on others — for instance, *Applied Physics* depends on *Pure Physics*, but not the other way around → this is **Acyclic, Directed Dependency**.

A software **package works exactly the same way**:

- A **package** is a "section" of the library.
- A **class** is a "book".
- **Dependencies** between packages are like cross-references between sections.
- **Stability** of a package is like the "foundational importance" of a section (e.g., *Mathematics* is referenced by everyone, so it must be stable and rarely changed).

### Plain English Summary

> A **package** is a "drawer" in your software filing cabinet. You put related classes into the same drawer, label the drawer clearly, and minimize the number of times one drawer needs to peek inside another. The rules of how drawers are organized, labelled, and depend on each other are called **Package Design Principles**.

---

## 1.3 Why Packages Matter — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why can't we just keep all classes in one giant file?**
> 1. **Build time explodes** — Changing one class forces recompilation of everything.
> 2. **Reuse is impossible** — You cannot pull out "just the database classes" if they are tangled with UI classes.
> 3. **Parallel development fails** — Multiple teams cannot work independently on overlapping files.
> 4. **Maintenance becomes hazardous** — A single change cascades unpredictably.
> 5. **Architectural decay** — Without package boundaries, the design erodes into a "Big Ball of Mud."

| Term | Standard KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Package** | A logical and physical grouping of related classes and sub-packages. |
| **Cohesion (RFC)** | The degree to which elements inside a package belong together. |
| **Coupling (between packages)** | The measure of inter-dependency between two packages. |
| **Reusability** | The probability that a package can be used in multiple systems. |
| **Stability** | Resistance of a package to change, measured by dependency metrics. |

---

## 1.4 Categories of Package Design

KTU Module 3 groups package design into the following study areas:

1. **Package Design Principles** (Robert C. Martin's principles: REP, CCP, CRP, ADP, SDP, SAP)
2. **Package Cohesion Principles** — what makes a package internally unified.
3. **Package Coupling Principles** — how packages should depend on each other.
4. **Package Metrics** — quantitative evaluation (Instability, Abstractness, Distance).

We will cover all of these exhaustively in the next sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Package Design Principles (Robert C. Martin)

According to the KTU Module 3 syllabus, package design is governed by **six core principles** that fall into two categories: **three cohesion principles** and **three coupling principles**.

### 2.1.1 The Three Package Cohesion Principles

These answer the question: *"Which classes should be grouped together into the same package?"*

#### **Principle 1: REP — Release Reuse Equivalence Principle**

> *"The granule of reuse is the granule of release. Only components that are released together can be reused together."*

- **What it means**: Classes grouped into a package must share a common **version** and **release cycle**. If two classes are reused together, they must be packaged together.
- **Engineering Implication**: A package = a release unit. It must have a version number, a release notes file, and a changelog.
- **Why**: It guarantees that a developer who reuses one class from the package can confidently reuse the others without compatibility issues.

#### **Principle 2: CCP — Common Closure Principle**

> *"Classes that change together, belong together."*

- **What it means**: All classes within a package should be affected by the **same kind of changes** (same axis of change). If a change to one class always forces changes to others, they belong in the same package.
- **Engineering Implication**: This is the **package-level equivalent of the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**. A package should have **one, and only one, reason to change**.
- **Why**: It minimizes the number of packages affected by a single change, reducing build breakage and re-validation effort.

#### **Principle 3: CRP — Common Reuse Principle**

> *"Classes that are used together, are reused together."*

- **What it means**: A package should contain only classes that are **reused together**. If you reuse one class, you typically reuse the others. If a class is rarely reused with the others, it should be split out.
- **Engineering Implication**: Avoid "fat" packages that pull in unwanted dependencies. If a package requires 50 classes when the user needs only one, CRP is violated.
- **Why**: It prevents users of a package from being forced to depend on classes they don't need (which is itself a form of unnecessary coupling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Tension Between CCP and CRP**
> - **CCP** pulls classes *together* (because they change together).
> - **CRP** pulls classes *apart* (because they are not reused together).
> - The art of package design is balancing this tension. The general rule:
>   - **Early in development** → favor **CRP** (decouple aggressively to enable reuse).
>   **Late in development** → favor **CCP** (consolidate to reduce change impact).

---

### 2.1.2 The Three Package Coupling Principles

These answer the question: *"How should packages depend on each other?"*

#### **Principle 4: ADP — Acyclic Dependencies Principle**

> *"The dependency graph of packages must have no cycles."*

- **What it means**: If Package A depends on B, and B depends on C, then A must **not** depend back on C or B in a way that forms a closed loop.
- **Why**: A dependency cycle creates a *release nightmare* — you cannot release A without releasing B, but you cannot release B without releasing A.
- **Solution to cycles**:
  1. Use **DIP (Dependency Inversion Principle)** — invert the dependency using interfaces.
  2. Create a **new package** that both cyclic packages depend on.
  3. Move the offending class to a shared package.

#### **Principle 5: SDP — Stable Dependencies Principle**

> *"Depend in the direction of stability."*

- **What it means**: A package should only depend on packages that are **more stable** than itself. Volatile packages must not be depended upon by stable ones.
- **Why**: If a stable package depends on an unstable one, changes in the unstable package ripple up into the stable one, making it unstable by transitivity.
- **Real-world analogy**: A stone building (stable) should rest on a concrete foundation (stable), not on sand (unstable).

#### **Principle 6: SAP — Stable Abstractions Principle**

> *"A package should be as abstract as it is stable."*

- **What it means**: Stable packages should be **abstract** (contain interfaces, abstract classes) so that they can be extended without modification. Unstable packages should be **concrete** (contain implementations) since they are expected to change.
- **Why**: It satisfies the OCP at the package level — stable packages should be open for extension but closed for modification.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are part of KTU Module 3 and are testable.

| Metric | Formula | Variable Meaning | Range | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Instability (I)** | $I = \dfrac{C_e}{C_a + C_e}$ | $C_e$ = Efferent coupling (dependencies *leaving* the package) | $[0, 1]$ | $I = 1$ for volatile packages |
| | | $C_a$ = Afferent coupling (dependencies *entering* the package) | | $I = 0$ for stable packages |
| **Abstractness (A)** | $A = \dfrac{N_a}{N_c}$ | $N_a$ = Number of abstract classes/interfaces in the package | $[0, 1]$ | $A = 1$ = fully abstract |
| | | $N_c$ = Total number of classes in the package | | $A = 0$ = fully concrete |
| **Distance from Main Sequence (D)** | $D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert$ | Measures how far a package is from the "ideal" balance | $[0, 1]$ | $D = 0$ = ideal |
| | | $D = \sqrt{(A+I-1)^2}$ (Euclidean form) | | $D = 1$ = worst |

### Interpretation of Metrics

| Scenario | I value | A value | D value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stable + Concrete** (Pain Zone) | $0$ | $0$ | $1$ | Bad — rigid, hard to extend |
| **Unstable + Abstract** (Useless Zone) | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | Bad — abstract with no use |
| **Stable + Abstract** (Main Sequence) | $0$ | $1$ | $0$ | **Ideal** — interfaces, foundations |
| **Unstable + Concrete** (Main Sequence) | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | **Ideal** — implementations, leaf packages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility of These Metrics**
> - Used in tools like **JDepend, NDepend, SonarQube** to visualize architectural decay.
> - Helps in **technical debt assessment** for legacy systems.
> - Enables **architectural conformance checking** in CI/CD pipelines.

---

## 2.3 Summary of Principles — Quick Reference

| Principle | Type | One-Line Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **REP** | Cohesion | Reuse granule = Release granule |
| **CCP** | Cohesion | Classes that change together, package together |
| **CRP** | Cohesion | Classes that aren't reused together, don't package together |
| **ADP** | Coupling | No cycles in the package dependency graph |
| **SDP** | Coupling | Depend toward more stable packages |
| **SAP** | Coupling | Stability ↔ Abstractness (they should move together) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since "Packages" is a design-oriented topic, "derivations" here refer to **analytical derivations of package metrics, dependency analysis, and acyclic graph proofs**. Each step is fully shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Instability Index ($I$)

### Given
A package `P` has $C_e$ packages that it depends upon (efferent) and $C_a$ packages that depend on it (afferent).

### Step 1 — Define "volatility"
A package is *volatile* if it changes often. Such a package has many **outgoing** dependencies (efferent) and few **incoming** dependencies (afferent) — because it depends on others but is not relied upon.

$$
\text{Volatility} \propto \dfrac{\text{Outgoing dependencies}}{\text{Total dependencies}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute variables
Outgoing dependencies = $C_e$. Total dependencies = $C_a + C_e$.

$$
I = \dfrac{C_e}{C_a + C_e}
$$

### Step 3 — Boundary analysis
- **Case 1**: No one depends on the package. Then $C_a = 0$.

$$
I = \dfrac{C_e}{0 + C_e} = \dfrac{C_e}{C_e} = 1
$$

This means the package is **maximally unstable** (it can change freely because no one relies on it). ✓

- **Case 2**: The package depends on no one. Then $C_e = 0$.

$$
I = \dfrac{0}{C_a + 0} = 0
$$

This means the package is **maximally stable** (any change would break its dependents). ✓

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The Instability Index $I \in [0, 1]$ linearly maps "no dependencies out" to "maximally stable" and "all dependencies out" to "maximally unstable."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Abstractness Index ($A$)

### Given
A package `P` contains $N_c$ classes total, of which $N_a$ are abstract (interfaces or abstract classes).

### Step 1 — Define "abstractness"
A package is *abstract* if it consists mainly of interfaces and abstract classes — i.e., it cannot be instantiated and is designed for extension.

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Number of abstract types}}{\text{Total number of types}} = \dfrac{N_a}{N_c}
$$

### Step 2 — Boundary analysis
- **Case 1**: All classes are concrete (e.g., utility classes).

$$
A = \dfrac{0}{N_c} = 0 \quad \text{(fully concrete)}
$$

- **Case 2**: All classes are abstract (e.g., a pure interface package).

$$
A = \dfrac{N_c}{N_c} = 1 \quad \text{(fully abstract)}
$$

### Step 3 — Engineering meaning
- $A \approx 0$ → implementation packages (concrete, changeable, leaf-level).
- $A \approx 1$ → abstraction packages (interfaces, foundations, stable contracts).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Distance from the Main Sequence ($D$)

### Given
The "main sequence" is the imaginary line $A + I = 1$ in the $(I, A)$ plane, connecting the two ideal points $(I=0, A=1)$ and $(I=1, A=0)$.
