### Step 1 — Why is $A + I = 1$ ideal?
On this line:
- A package that is **stable** ($I = 0$) is **fully abstract** ($A = 1$) — so it can be extended without modification. ✓
- A package that is **unstable** ($I = 1$) is **fully concrete** ($A = 0$) — so it can change freely. ✓

### Step 2 — The distance formula
The perpendicular distance from a point $(I, A)$ to the line $A + I - 1 = 0$ in 2D is:

$$
D = \dfrac{\vert A + I - 1 \vert}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2}} = \dfrac{\vert A + I - 1 \vert}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

For practical KTU purposes, this is often simplified (and is the version taught in Module 3):

$$
D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert
$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
Consider **Package X** with $C_e = 3$, $C_a = 1$, $N_a = 2$, $N_c = 8$.

**Step 3.1 — Calculate $I$:**

$$
I = \dfrac{C_e}{C_a + C_e} = \dfrac{3}{1 + 3} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3.2 — Calculate $A$:**

$$
A = \dfrac{N_a}{N_c} = \dfrac{2}{8} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3.3 — Calculate $D$:**

$$
D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert = \vert 0.25 + 0.75 - 1 \vert = \vert 0 \vert = 0
$$

**Step 3.4 — Verdict:**
$D = 0$ → Package X is **on the main sequence** → **ideally balanced**. The package is mostly concrete (good for a high-$I$ package) and only slightly abstract — appropriate for its level of instability. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — A Problematic Package

Consider **Package Y** with $C_e = 0$, $C_a = 5$, $N_a = 1$, $N_c = 10$.

**Step 1 — Calculate $I$:**

$$
I = \dfrac{C_e}{C_a + C_e} = \dfrac{0}{5 + 0} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $A$:**

$$
A = \dfrac{N_a}{N_c} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $D$:**

$$
D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert = \vert 0.1 + 0 - 1 \vert = \vert -0.9 \vert = 0.9
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict:**
$D = 0.9$ is **very high** (close to 1, the worst value). Package Y is **stable** ($I = 0$) but **mostly concrete** ($A = 0.1$). This is the **"Pain Zone"** — it is heavily relied upon, yet contains concrete classes that other packages inherit from. Any change ripples catastrophically. This package is in **architectural distress**. 🚨

**Recommended Refactoring**: Extract interfaces for the concrete classes and move them to an abstraction package that Package Y depends on. This increases $A$ and reduces $D$.

---

## 3.5 Detection of Cyclic Dependencies (ADP Enforcement)

### Step 1 — Build the dependency graph
Represent each package as a node, and each dependency as a directed edge: `A → B` means A depends on B.

### Step 2 — Apply DFS with a recursion stack
A cycle exists if, during DFS, we encounter a node that is already in the current recursion stack.

### Step 3 — Resolution
If a cycle `A → B → C → A` is found, apply the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)**:
1. Create an interface `I` in a new package `D`.
2. Make `C` depend on `I` (so the cycle is broken: `A → B → C` and `C → D ← A`).
3. Verify: `A → B → C → D`, no cycle. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Package Dependency Diagram (Acyclic — Ideal Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    UI[UI Layer Package] --> APP[Application Layer Package]
    DOM[Domain Layer Package] --> APP
    APP --> DOM
    APP --> IFACE[Interface Contracts Package]
    IFACE --> DOM
    DB[Database Package] --> IFACE

    classDef stable fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef volatile fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#b35900,color:#000
    classDef abstract fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class IFACE,DOM stable
    class UI,DB volatile
    class APP abstract
```

### Reading the Diagram
- `UI Layer` and `Database` are **volatile** (leaf, can change frequently) — orange.
- `Domain` and `Interface Contracts` are **stable foundations** — blue.
- `Application` mediates between them — green.
- **No back-edges** — the graph is a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**. ✅ ADP is satisfied.

---

## 4.2 Cyclic Dependency Diagram (Violation of ADP)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PkgA[Package A] --> PkgB[Package B]
    PkgB[Package B] --> PkgC[Package C]
    PkgC[Package C] --> PkgA[Package A]

    classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    class PkgA,PkgB,PkgC bad
```

### Reading the Diagram
- This is a **deadly cycle**: A depends on B, B on C, C on A.
- Violates **ADP** — release is impossible without coordinating all three packages simultaneously.
- **Fix**: Apply DIP — create `Package D` with interfaces, then make C depend on D, breaking the cycle.

---

## 4.3 Package Cohesion vs Coupling Principle Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph COHESION[Package Cohesion Principles - WHAT goes together]
        R[REP: Reuse = Release]
        C[CCP: Change Together]
        CR[CRP: Used Together]
    end

    subgraph COUPLING[Package Coupling Principles - HOW they relate]
        A[ADP: No Cycles]
        S[SDP: Depend Stable]
        SA[SAP: Stable = Abstract]
    end

    COHESION --> COUPLING
    R --> A
    C --> S
    CR --> SA

    classDef group fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    class COHESION,COUPLING group
```

---

## 4.4 Main Sequence Diagram (The Ideal Zone for Package Design)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph MAIN[Main Sequence - D = 0 - IDEAL]
        M1[Stable and Abstract - I=0, A=1]
        M2[Unstable and Concrete - I=1, A=0]
    end

    subgraph PAIN[Pain Zone - Stable but Concrete - D close to 1]
        P1[Concrete Foundations - BAD]
    end

    subgraph USELESS[Useless Zone - Unstable but Abstract - D close to 1]
        U1[Abstractions Nobody Uses - BAD]
    end

    M1 --- M2
    P1 -.- M1
    U1 -.- M2

    classDef ideal fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    class M1,M2 ideal
    class P1,U1 bad
```

---

## 4.5 Package Metrics Calculation Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start: Analyze Package P] --> CALC_CE[Count Efferent Couplings Ce]
    START --> CALC_CA[Count Afferent Couplings Ca]
    START --> CALC_NA[Count Abstract Classes Na]
    START --> CALC_NC[Count Total Classes Nc]

    CALC_CE --> CALC_I[Compute I = Ce / Ca + Ce]
    CALC_CA --> CALC_I
    CALC_NA --> CALC_A[Compute A = Na / Nc]
    CALC_NC --> CALC_A

    CALC_I --> CALC_D[Compute D = mod A + I - 1]
    CALC_A --> CALC_D

    CALC_D --> DECIDE{D close to 0?}
    DECIDE -- Yes --> GOOD[Package is Well-Balanced]
    DECIDE -- No --> BAD[Package Needs Refactoring]
    BAD --> REFAC{Which Zone?}
    REFAC -- D high and I low --> FIX1[Extract Interfaces Increase A]
    REFAC -- D high and I high --> FIX2[Reassign Responsibilities Reduce I]
    REFAC -- D high and A low --> FIX3[Move to Higher Level Package]

    classDef good fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    class GOOD good
    class BAD,FIX1,FIX2,FIX3 bad
    class DECIDE,REFAC decision
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Package. List the three package cohesion principles. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **package** is a logical and physical grouping of related classes and sub-packages that share a common purpose, are versioned together, and can be released and reused as a single unit. It represents the highest granularity of software design above the class level.

The three **package cohesion principles** (Robert C. Martin) are:

1. **REP — Release Reuse Equivalence Principle**: The granule of reuse should equal the granule of release. Classes released together should be reused together.
2. **CCP — Common Closure Principle**: Classes that change for the same reasons (same axis of change) should be grouped into the same package. A package should have one reason to change.
3. **CRP — Common Reuse Principle**: Classes that are reused together should be packaged together. Classes that are not commonly reused should be separated.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark listing + 1 mark brief explanation)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Acyclic Dependencies Principle (ADP)? Why is it important? `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Acyclic Dependencies Principle (ADP)** states that the dependency graph of packages in a software system **must not contain any cycles**. That is, if Package A depends on B, and B depends on C, then A and B must not depend on C in a way that forms a closed loop.

**Importance:**

1. **Release Independence**: A cycle forces simultaneous release of all packages in the cycle, breaking the ability to release independently.
2. **Version Management**: Cycles cause "version hell" — version conflicts propagate cyclically.
3. **Build Stability**: A cycle means the build is mathematically undefined in topological sort terms.
4. **Architectural Clarity**: Acyclic graphs form a clear hierarchy of foundations and extensions.

**Resolution of cycles**: Apply the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** by creating an interface package, or extract the conflicting class into a new shared package.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks importance/resolution)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**: Explain the three package **coupling principles** in detail: ADP, SDP, and SAP. How are they related to the Open-Closed Principle?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**: Given a software system with **4 packages**: `UI`, `BusinessLogic`, `Database`, and `Interfaces`. The dependency relationships are:

- `UI` depends on `BusinessLogic`
- `BusinessLogic` depends on `Database` and `Interfaces`
- `Database` depends on `Interfaces`

Compute the **Instability**, **Abstractness**, and **Distance from Main Sequence** for **each package**, and comment on the architectural health of the system.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. ADP — Acyclic Dependencies Principle (2 marks)**

The dependency graph of packages must have **no cycles**. Cycles prevent independent release, create version conflicts, and break the topological build order. For example, if A → B → C → A, you cannot release A without coordinating the release of B and C. The fix is **Dependency Inversion**: introduce an interface in a new package that breaks the cycle.

**2. SDP — Stable Dependencies Principle (2 marks)**

A package should depend only on packages that are **more stable** than itself. The Instability Index $I$ is used to measure stability. If a stable package (low $I$, e.g., a foundation like `java.lang`) depends on a volatile package (high $I$, e.g., a UI helper), any change to the volatile package cascades into the stable one, making the stable package de-facto unstable. Hence, **dependency direction = stability direction**.

**3. SAP — Stable Abstractions Principle (2 marks)**

A package's **abstractness should be proportional to its stability**. Stable packages should consist of abstract classes and interfaces (so they can be extended without modification). Unstable packages should consist of concrete implementations (so they can change freely). This is the package-level expression of the **Open-Closed Principle (OCP)**.

**Relationship to OCP (1 mark)**:
ADP, SDP, and SAP together implement OCP at the package level:
- ADP ensures the architecture is a directed acyclic graph of extensions.
- SDP ensures foundational packages are not destabilized.
- SAP ensures stable packages are open for extension (interfaces) but closed for modification (concrete code is in volatile packages).

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Draw the dependency graph:**

```
UI       → BusinessLogic
BusinessLogic → Database
BusinessLogic → Interfaces
Database    → Interfaces
```

**Step 2 — Build the dependency matrix:**

| Package | Depends On (Ce) | Depended On By (Ca) |
|---|---|---|
| UI | BusinessLogic | (none) |
| BusinessLogic | Database, Interfaces | UI |
| Database | Interfaces | BusinessLogic |
| Interfaces | (none) | UI, BusinessLogic, Database |

**Step 3 — Compute $C_a$ and $C_e$ for each:**

- **UI**: $C_a = 0$, $C_e = 1$
- **BusinessLogic**: $C_a = 1$, $C_e = 2$
- **Database**: $C_a = 1$, $C_e = 1$
- **Interfaces**: $C_a = 3$, $C_e = 0$

**Step 4 — Compute Instability $I = C_e / (C_a + C_e)$:**

- UI: $I = 1 / (0 + 1) = 1.0$
- BusinessLogic: $I = 2 / (1 + 2) = 0.667$
- Database: $I = 1 / (1 + 1) = 0.5$
- Interfaces: $I = 0 / (3 + 0) = 0.0$

**Step 5 — Assume abstractness values** (typical for such an architecture):

- UI: 0 concrete UI classes, $N_c = 10$, $N_a = 1$ (form interface) → $A = 0.1$
- BusinessLogic: 8 concrete, 1 abstract / 10 total → $A = 0.2$
- Database: 5 concrete, 1 interface / 6 total → $A = 0.167$
- Interfaces: 5 abstract / 5 total → $A = 1.0$

**Step 6 — Compute Distance $D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert$:**

- UI: $D = \vert 0.1 + 1.0 - 1 \vert = 0.1$ ✅ (on main sequence)
- BusinessLogic: $D = \vert 0.2 + 0.667 - 1 \vert = 0.133$ ✅ (close to main sequence)
- Database: $D = \vert 0.167 + 0.5 - 1 \vert = 0.333$ ⚠️ (acceptable)
- Interfaces: $D = \vert 1.0 + 0.0 - 1 \vert = 0.0$ ✅ (on main sequence — ideal)

**Step 7 — Architectural comment (1 mark):**

- **Interfaces** is the most stable ($I=0$) and most abstract ($A=1$) → **ideal foundation package**. ✅
- **UI** is most unstable ($I=1$) and concrete ($A=0.1$) → **ideal leaf package**. ✅
- **BusinessLogic** and **Database** are mid-tier. The graph is acyclic and the dependencies flow from volatile to stable — **ADP, SDP, and SAP are all satisfied**. The architecture is **healthy**. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — Common Mark Loss Points:
> 1. **Forgetting to add $C_a$ and $C_e$ for self**: Some students forget that the count must be relative to a single package's direct dependencies, not the whole graph.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_a$ and $C_e$**: Efferent = going **out** (depends on others); Afferent = coming **in** (depended on by others). Reversing these inverts the result.
> 3. **Skipping the architectural comment**: The final 1 mark is reserved for *interpretation*, not just numerical computation.
> 4. **Not verifying ADP**: Always confirm the graph is acyclic before computing metrics. A cyclic graph makes the metrics meaningless.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**: Differentiate between the **Release Reuse Equivalence Principle (REP)** and the **Common Reuse Principle (CRP)**. Illustrate with a real-world example. Which one is more critical during initial design, and why?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**: For a package containing the following:

- 3 abstract classes
- 7 concrete classes
- 4 incoming dependencies (afferent)
- 1 outgoing dependency (efferent)

Calculate the **Instability**, **Abstractness**, and **Distance from the Main Sequence**. Identify which zone the package belongs to (Ideal / Pain / Useless), and suggest one architectural improvement.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Differences between REP and CRP (4 marks):**

| Aspect | REP | CRP |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Release cycle | Reuse coupling |
| **Question answered** | When are classes released together? | Which classes are reused together? |
| **Guideline** | Group classes with the same version and release schedule. | Group classes that are typically used together; separate rarely-co-used classes. |
| **Drives** | Cohesion by release lifecycle | Cohesion by usage patterns |
| **Violation** | Reusing one class forces an incompatible version of another. | A package's user is forced to depend on classes they don't need. |

**Real-world illustration (2 marks):**

Consider a **Java `java.util` package**:
- It contains `ArrayList`, `LinkedList`, `HashMap`, `HashSet`, `Iterator`, etc.
- **REP applies**: All these classes are released together under a single `java.util` JAR with synchronized versions. If you use `ArrayList`, you know you can rely on `Iterator` having the same version.
- **CRP applies**: A developer using `HashMap` is *also* likely to use `Iterator` and `Map.Entry`. These are co-used. So they belong together.
- However, `Timer` and `TimerTask` (also in `java.util` historically) are used in **completely different scenarios** from the Collection classes. Many would argue they violate CRP — they should arguably be in a separate `java.util.concurrent` package (which, in fact, they eventually were moved to in later JDKs).

**Which is more critical during initial design? (1 mark)**

During **initial design**, **CRP is more critical** because:
1. It shapes the *user-facing* boundaries of the package — what the consumer sees and depends on.
2. REP is harder to apply early because release cycles are not yet established; you must design the boundary first.
3. A package with poor CRP creates *unwanted dependencies* that are very hard to break later.
4. REP can be retrofitted (you can bundle classes into a release unit later) more easily than CRP can be retrofitted (breaking a fat package is disruptive).

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N_a = 3$ (abstract classes)
- $N_c = 10$ (total classes: 3 abstract + 7 concrete)
- $C_a = 4$ (afferent)
- $C_e = 1$ (efferent)

**Step 1 — Compute Instability $I$:**

$$
I = \dfrac{C_e}{C_a + C_e} = \dfrac{1}{4 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Abstractness $A$:**

$$
A = \dfrac{N_a}{N_c} = \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Distance $D$:**

$$
D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert = \vert 0.3 + 0.2 - 1 \vert = \vert -0.5 \vert = 0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Zone Identification:**

- $I = 0.2$ → package is **fairly stable** (low instability, mostly depended-upon).
- $A = 0.3$ → package is **mostly concrete** (only 30% abstract).
- $D = 0.5$ → **moderate distance** from main sequence.

This package is in the **"Pain Zone"** (stable but concrete) — though not in extreme pain, it is moving away from the ideal. A stable package should be highly abstract, but this one is mostly concrete, meaning consumers of the package are coupled to concrete implementations. Any change to the concrete classes forces ripple effects.

**Step 5 — Architectural Improvement (1 mark):**

**Refactoring suggestion**: Extract more **abstract classes and interfaces** for the public API of the package. Move the concrete implementations to a sibling "implementation" package. This will:
- Increase $A$ (more abstract classes).
- Reduce $D$ (move toward main sequence).
- Satisfy **SAP** — stable packages should be abstract.

For example, in a `PaymentGateway` package, extract an `IPaymentProcessor` interface into a new `PaymentGateway.Contract` sub-package. The original package then becomes the implementation, and the new one is the stable abstraction. The unstable implementation package will have $I = 1, A = 0, D = 0$ — ideal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Generic Pitfalls Across All Package Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing cohesion and coupling principles**: Cohesion is *internal* to a package; coupling is *between* packages. Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not justifying the "improvement"**: A suggestion without reasoning (e.g., "extract interfaces" without saying *why*) loses the justification mark.
> 3. **Skipping the unit/dimension check**: Always state that $I, A, D \in [0, 1]$. Showing this awareness earns partial credit.
> 4. **Forgetting the OCP connection**: SDP and SAP are essentially package-level OCP. Mentioning this earns a bonus understanding point.
> 5. **Not drawing the dependency graph first**: In metric problems, always draw the graph; it makes $C_a$ and $C_e$ unambiguous.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Packages**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Package**: Logical + physical grouping of related classes and sub-packages, released as a unit.
- **Cohesion** (internal unity) vs **Coupling** (external dependency) at package level.

### 🔑 The Six Package Principles
- **REP**: Reuse granule = Release granule.
- **CCP**: Classes that change together, package together.
- **CRP**: Classes not reused together, don't package together.
- **ADP**: Dependency graph must be acyclic.
- **SDP**: Depend in the direction of stability.
- **SAP**: Stable packages should be abstract; unstable should be concrete.

### 🔑 The Three Metrics
- **Instability**: $I = C_e / (C_a + C_e)$, range $[0, 1]$. Higher = more unstable.
- **Abstractness**: $A = N_a / N_c$, range $[0, 1]$. Higher = more abstract.
- **Distance**: $D = \vert A + I - 1 \vert$, range $[0, 1]$. Lower = better.

### 🔑 The Two Ideal Points on Main Sequence
- $(I=0, A=1)$ → Stable abstractions (foundations, interfaces).
- $(I=1, A=0)$ → Unstable concretes (leaves, implementations).

### 🔑 The Two Bad Zones
- **Pain Zone** ($I \approx 0, A \approx 0$): Stable but concrete — refactor to extract abstractions.
- **Useless Zone** ($I \approx 1, A \approx 1$): Unstable but abstract — refactor to assign usages or merge with consumers.

### 🔑 Conflict to Remember
- **CCP vs CRP tension**: CCP says "merge what changes together"; CRP says "split what isn't reused together." The art is balance — favor CRP early, CCP late.

### 🔑 Resolving ADP Violations
- Apply **DIP** (create interface in new package).
- Or **extract** the cyclic class into a shared package.

### 🔑 Connection to OCP
- ADP + SDP + SAP together implement **OCP at the package level**.

### 🔑 Real-World Tools
- **JDepend** (Java), **NDepend** (.NET), **SonarQube** (multi-language) compute these metrics.
- **Maven / Gradle** enforce physical packaging (REP-style release grouping).

### 🔑 Common Exam Traps
- Confusing $C_a$ (incoming) with $C_e$ (outgoing).
- Forgetting to draw the dependency graph before computing metrics.
- Saying "extract interfaces" without explaining *why* (SAP justification).

### 🔑 One-Line Memory Aid
> "**Group what changes & reuses together, depend only on stable abstractions, and never form a cycle.**"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** State Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 – State Modeling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> **State Modeling** is a dynamic-behavior modeling technique in object-oriented software engineering that captures the **lifecycle behavior** of an object by describing the sequence of **states** it passes through, the **events** that trigger changes between those states, and the **actions** performed during those transitions.

The primary UML artifact produced by state modeling is the **State Machine Diagram** (also called a **Statechart Diagram**), which belongs to the family of UML *behavioral diagrams* (as defined in OMG UML 2.5 specification, which KTU 2024 follows).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** State modeling is the *third* of the four mandatory UML models in Object-Oriented Analysis & Design (OOAD) — the others being **Object Modeling, Dynamic Modeling, and Functional Modeling**. State modeling particularly complements dynamic modeling by focusing on a *single object's internal lifecycle* rather than interactions between multiple objects.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

Imagine a **dominos-style pizza ordering app** on your phone:

- When you first open the app, the app is in the **"Idle"** state.
- The moment you tap the menu, an event **"OpenMenu"** occurs, and the app moves to the **"BrowsingMenu"** state.
- Add an item to your cart, the app moves to **"CartActive"** state.
- Hit "Place Order", the app moves to **"PaymentProcessing"** state.
- If payment succeeds → **"OrderConfirmed"** state. If it fails → back to **"CartActive"** state.

The app is **the same object** the whole time, but it is *behaving* very differently in each state. **State modeling is exactly the act of drawing this "life story" of an object.**

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a state as a **"mood"** of an object. An object cannot be in two moods at once; an event (something that happens) snaps it from one mood to another. Some moods let certain things happen, others don't. That's the entire essence of state modeling.

---

### 1.3 Why is State Modeling Necessary? (The Engineering "Why")

| Engineering Reality | Why State Modeling Solves It |
|---|---|
| Objects behave differently based on context | Captures *context-dependent* behavior in one diagram |
| Real-time / embedded systems (ATMs, pacemakers, elevators) | Models strict state-driven control flow |
| UI workflow design (forms, wizards, multi-step processes) | Defines legal transitions and blocks illegal ones |
| Protocol design (network handshakes, authentication) | Visualizes every legal/illegal sequence |
| Bug localization | Pinpoints which state/event pair caused a crash |

> **Standard Metric / Notation to Remember:**
> - **States** = Rounded rectangles
> - **Initial State** = Filled black circle
> - **Final State** = Bull's-eye (filled circle inside a ring)
> - **Transitions** = Directed arrows
> - **Self-Transitions** = Loop-back arrow

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Building Blocks of a State Model

A state model is composed of **five core building blocks**. KTU examiners love testing each of these.

#### 2.1.1 **State** 🟦
A state represents a condition or situation during the life of an object in which it:
- Satisfies some condition
- Performs some activity
- Waits for some event

**Types of States (KTU High-Yield):**

| State Type | Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple / Plain State** | Rounded rectangle with name | Basic state with no internal structure |
| **Initial State** | Filled black circle | The single starting point of the state machine |
| **Final State** | Bull's-eye (⊙) | The single (or multiple) ending point |
| **Composite State** | Rounded rectangle with internal diagram | A state containing nested sub-states |
| **History State** | Circle with letter `H` inside | Remembers the last active sub-state inside a composite state |

#### 2.1.2 **Event** ⚡
An event is a notable occurrence that can trigger a state transition.

**Four UML Event Types (KTU Mandatory):**

1. **Signal Event** – Asynchronous receipt of a signal/object from outside.
   *Example:* `buttonPressed`, `orderReceived`
2. **Call Event** – Synchronous invocation of an operation.
   *Example:* `deposit(amount)`, `withdraw(amount)`
3. **Time Event** – Occurs after a specified time elapsed.
   *Example:* `after(30 seconds)`, `at(9:00 AM)`
4. **Change Event** – Occurs when a Boolean expression becomes true.
   *Example:* `when(balance > 0)`, `when(temperature >= 100)`

#### 2.1.3 **Transition** ➡️
A transition is a relationship between two states indicating that when an event occurs and guard conditions are satisfied, the object moves from one state to another.

**Transition Syntax (KTU Exam Format):**
```
Event [Guard] / Action
```

**Types of Transitions:**
- **External Transition** – Causes exit from current state, then entry to next
- **Internal Transition** – Does NOT cause exit; processes event silently within the state
- **Self-Transition** – Object exits and re-enters the same state (use case: counters, retries)

#### 2.1.4 **Action** 🎬
An action is an atomic, instantaneous behavior associated with a state or transition.

**Four UML Action Kinds (KTU Mandatory):**

| Action Type | Trigger Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry Action** | On entering the state | Initialization (e.g., `display = ON`) |
| **Exit Action** | On leaving the state | Cleanup (e.g., `display = OFF`) |
| **Do Activity** | While inside the state | Long-running internal work (denoted by `do/` prefix) |
| **Transition Action** | On the transition arrow | Behavior during the move (e.g., `/ debitAccount()`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Entry, Exit, and Do are *always* triggered for the state itself, regardless of which transition was used. This is **run-to-completion semantics**.

#### 2.1.5 **Guard Condition** 🚧
A Boolean expression that must evaluate to `true` for a transition to fire. Written in square brackets.

*Example:* `event = LOGIN_SUCCESS [password == correct]`

> **Standard Notation Rule:** A guard is checked only **once**, at the time the event occurs. If false, the transition does NOT fire.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Element | UML Notation | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial State | `●` (filled circle) | (always at the start) |
| 2 | Final State | `⊙` (bull's-eye) | (always at the end) |
| 3 | Simple State | Rounded rectangle `name` | `Idle`, `Active` |
| 4 | Composite State | Rectangle with internal region | `LoggedIn` containing `Browsing`, `Cart` |
| 5 | History State | `H` inside a circle | `H*` (deep history) |
| 6 | Transition Label | `event [guard] / action` | `buttonPress [valid] / openDoor()` |
| 7 | Entry Action | `entry / action` | `entry / startTimer()` |
| 8 | Exit Action | `exit / action` | `exit / stopMotor()` |
| 9 | Do Activity | `do / activity` | `do / processPayment()` |
| 10 | Internal Transition | Inside state box | `cancel / abortProcess()` |
| 11 | Fork (concurrent) | Thick horizontal/vertical bar | Splits flow into parallel regions |
| 12 | Join (concurrent) | Thick horizontal/vertical bar | Merges parallel regions |
| 13 | Decision | Diamond (informal) | Branching based on guard |

---

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind State Modeling (Engineering Real-World Utility)

> **Real-World Use Cases:**

1. **Embedded Control Systems:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, elevators, traffic lights — every control logic with distinct modes is best modeled as a state machine.
2. **Protocol Verification:** TCP/IP handshakes, SSL/TLS negotiation, ATM transaction flows.
3. **Workflow Engines:** Approval workflows in ERP, claim processing in insurance, KTU exam paper flow.
4. **Game Development:** Character FSMs (Finite State Machines) — Idle / Walking / Attacking / Dead.
5. **Compiler Design:** Lexical analyzers and parsers are built as state machines.
6. **UI/UX Flow Design:** Multi-step form wizards, multi-page checkout.

> [!NOTE]
> The state model is a **formal, executable artifact**. Tools like IBM Rhapsody, MATLAB Stateflow, and Yakindu can generate code directly from statecharts for embedded systems.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Six-Step Process to Build a State Model (KTU High-Yield Procedure)

This is the **standard KTU-recommended procedure** (similar to the one proposed by Bruegge & Dutoit in OOAD methodology, which KTU 2024 follows). Examiners ask 14-mark questions on this procedure applied to a real problem.

---

#### **STEP 1: Identify Candidate Objects**

> **Why?** Not every object needs a state model. Only objects with *notable dynamic behavior* deserve one.

**How to identify:**
- Objects whose behavior depends on history (e.g., `Account` remembers balance changes)
- Objects with distinct modes (e.g., `Order` is "New", "Shipped", "Delivered")
- Reactive objects (e.g., `Sensor`, `Controller`)

**Heuristic:** Ask *"Does this object's response to the same event differ in different situations?"* If yes, it needs a state model.

---

#### **STEP 2: Identify States**

For each candidate object, enumerate all the conditions/situations it can be in.

**Technique:** Brainstorm with stakeholders, examine use cases, check the system requirements.

**Example:** For an `Order` object in an e-commerce app:
- States: `New`, `Paid`, `Shipped`, `Delivered`, `Cancelled`, `Returned`

---

#### **STEP 3: Identify Events**

List all the *notable occurrences* that might affect the object.

**Four event types to consider:**
- **Signal events** — asynchronous notifications from external sources
- **Call events** — invocations of operations
- **Time events** — deadline expirations
- **Change events** — condition becomes true

**Example for `Order`:**
- `paymentReceived` (signal)
- `cancel()` (call)
- `after(7 days)` (time) — auto-cancel
- `when(itemsInStock == false)` (change)

---

#### **STEP 4: Identify Transitions**

For each state-event pair, decide:
- Does the event cause a transition?
- If yes, what is the target state?
- What is the action to be performed?
- Are there guard conditions?

**Example for `Order`:**

| Current State | Event | Guard | Target State | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| New | paymentReceived | — | Paid | sendConfirmationEmail() |
| New | cancel | [stockEmpty] | Cancelled | refundInitiated() |
| Paid | dispatch | — | Shipped | notifyCourier() |
| Shipped | after(5 days) | — | Delivered | closeOrder() |

---

#### **STEP 5: Add Actions, Activities, and Guards**

Refine each transition with:
- Entry actions (initialize resources)
- Exit actions (cleanup)
- Do activities (continuous work)
- Guard conditions (preconditions)

**Example refined state `Paid`:**
```
state Paid {
  entry / generateInvoice()
  exit / logPaymentComplete()
}
```

---

#### **STEP 6: Validate the State Model**

- Are all states reachable from the initial state?
- Is there any unreachable state? (dead state — remove it)
- Are there any "dangling" events (events received but not handled)?
- Does the model satisfy all use cases?
- Apply the **"drink water" test** (Boiler & Jarke method, used in KTU textbooks): simulate every use case step-by-step through the state model.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State-Event-Action Table

This is the **compact tabular form** of a state model — KTU examiners love asking students to convert between the diagram and this table.

**Example: ATM Transaction State Model**

| Current State | Event | Guard Condition | Action Performed | Target State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Idle` | `cardInserted` | `[cardValid]` | `readCard()` | `CardReading` |
| `CardReading` | `pinEntered` | `[pinCorrect]` | `displayMenu()` | `MenuShown` |
| `MenuShown` | `withdrawSelected` | `[balance >= amount]` | `debitAccount()` | `DispensingCash` |
| `MenuShown` | `withdrawSelected` | `[balance < amount]` | `displayInsufficient()` | `MenuShown` |
| `DispensingCash` | `cashTaken` | — | `ejectCard()` | `PrintingReceipt` |
| `PrintingReceipt` | `receiptPrinted` | — | `reset()` | `Idle` |
| `MenuShown` | `cancelPressed` | — | `ejectCard()` | `Idle` |
| `CardReading` | `pinEntered` | `[pinWrong]` | `incrementFailCount()` | `PinRetry` |
| `PinRetry` | `after(3 attempts)` | — | `retainCard()` | `CardRetained` |

**Rows where `Current State == Target State` = Self-Transitions**
**Rows with multiple entries for same `Current State` + `Event` = Conditional branching (guarded transitions)**

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Coffee Vending Machine ☕

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
*"Design a state model for a coffee vending machine. The machine accepts coins, allows selection of beverage only after sufficient balance, dispenses the drink, and returns change."*

**Step 1 — Identify the candidate object:**
The object is the `CoffeeMachine` itself (or equivalently, the `Transaction` object).

**Step 2 — Identify states:**
- `Idle` — Waiting for user interaction
- `CollectingCoins` — Accepting coins
- `DrinkSelected` — A beverage has been chosen
- `Dispensing` — Machine is making the drink
- `OutOfService` — Maintenance mode

**Step 3 — Identify events:**
- `coinInserted(amount)` — call event
- `drinkChosen(drinkType)` — call event
- `cancelPressed` — signal event
- `dispenseComplete` — signal event (from internal sensor)
- `after(30 seconds)` — time event (auto-cancel for timeout)

**Step 4 — Identify transitions, actions, guards:**

$$
\text{Idle} \xrightarrow{\text{coinInserted}(\$10) \ / \ \text{updateBalance}()} \text{CollectingCoins}
$$

$$
\text{CollectingCoins} \xrightarrow{\text{coinInserted}(\$20) \ / \ \text{updateBalance}()} \text{CollectingCoins} \quad (\text{self-transition})
$$

$$
\text{CollectingCoins} \xrightarrow{\text{drinkChosen} \ [\text{balance} \geq \text{price}] \ / \ \text{deductPrice}()} \text{Dispensing}
$$

$$
\text{CollectingCoins} \xrightarrow{\text{drinkChosen} \ [\text{balance} < \text{price}] \ / \ \text{displayError}()} \text{CollectingCoins} \quad (\text{self-transition})
$$

$$
\text{Dispensing} \xrightarrow{\text{dispenseComplete} \ / \ \text{returnChange}()} \text{Idle}
$$

$$
\text{CollectingCoins} \xrightarrow{\text{after(30 seconds)} \ / \ \text{returnAllCoins}()} \text{Idle}
$$

**Step 5 — Add entry/exit actions for `CollectingCoins`:**
- `entry / displayBalance()`
- `exit / resetSelection()`

**Step 6 — Validate:**
- All states reachable ✅
- No dead states ✅
- Every use case simulated successfully ✅

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Machine Diagram — Coffee Vending Machine ☕

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below uses **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **clean uppercase labels** to ensure it compiles correctly without any markdown or HTML interference.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sIdle

    sIdle : IDLE
    sIdle : entry / turnOnDisplay
    sIdle : exit / clearScreen

    sCollecting : COLLECTING COINS
    sCollecting : entry / showBalance
    sCollecting : exit / lockSelection
    sCollecting : do / monitorCoins

    sSelecting : DRINK SELECTED
    sSelecting : entry / showMenu
    sSelecting : exit / resetMenu

    sDispensing : DISPENSING DRINK
    sDispensing : entry / startBrew
    sDispensing : do / brewDrink
    sDispensing : exit / stopBrew

    sService : OUT OF SERVICE
    sService : entry / alertTechnician

    sIdle --> sCollecting : coinInserted / updateBalance
    sCollecting --> sCollecting : coinInserted / updateBalance
    sCollecting --> sCollecting : drinkChosen [balance less than price] / displayError
    sCollecting --> sSelecting : drinkChosen [balance enough price] / lockFunds
    sCollecting --> sIdle : cancelPressed / returnCoins
    sCollecting --> sIdle : after 30 seconds / timeout
    sSelecting --> sDispensing : confirmSelection / startBrew
    sSelecting --> sCollecting : cancelPressed / refund
    sDispensing --> sIdle : dispenseComplete / returnChange
    sService --> sIdle : maintenanceComplete / resetSystem
    sIdle --> sService : errorDetected / logFailure
```

**Diagram Interpretation (For KTU Viva):**

- `[*]` = Initial pseudo-state (UML 2.5 standard)
- `sIdle` = Alphanumeric node ID (PascalCase NOT used because Mermaid prefers lowercase prefixes)
- `sCollecting --> sCollecting` = Self-transition (same source and target)
- `drinkChosen [balance less than price]` = Guard condition in square brackets
- `after 30 seconds` = Time event keyword
- `entry /`, `exit /`, `do /` = Standard UML 2.5 internal action keywords

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

When the system is too complex to draw as a single state diagram, the architecture is decomposed into a **block-level functional architecture flow** that shows how multiple state machines interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External User Inputs] --> B[Event Router and Dispatcher]
    B --> C{Identify Target Object}
    C --> D1[State Machine 1: User Session]
    C --> D2[State Machine 2: Payment Gateway]
    C --> D3[State Machine 3: Inventory Tracker]
    C --> D4[State Machine 4: Notification Service]
    D1 --> E[Shared Context and Database]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Action Executor]
    F --> G[Response and Output]
    G --> A
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Event Router** receives all signals, calls, time, and change events
- **Identify Target Object** classifies events to the correct state machine
- **Four parallel state machines** operate independently but share context
- **Action Executor** runs the actions from each transition
- **Response** feeds back to the user, which may trigger more events (feedback loop)

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> *Define the term **"state"** in the context of state modeling. With an example, explain the difference between an **action** and an **activity** in a state diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**State (1 mark):** A state is a condition or situation in the life of an object during which it satisfies some condition, performs some activity, or waits for some event. Formally, a state represents a period of time during which an object is waiting for an event or performing long-term work.

**Action vs Activity (2 marks):**

| Feature | Action | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | Atomic and instantaneous | Takes time (has duration) |
| Notation | `entry /`, `exit /`, on transition arrow | `do /` inside a state |
| Example | `entry / display = ON` | `do / brewCoffee()` |

*Worked example: When a microwave enters the `Cooking` state, the `entry / startMagnetron()` action fires instantly, while the `do / rotateTurntable()` activity runs continuously for the entire duration of cooking.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

> *Differentiate between **signal events** and **call events** in UML state modeling. Give one example of each from a real-world banking system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Signal Event (1.5 marks):** A signal event is the asynchronous receipt of a signal (a one-way notification) by an object. The sender does not wait for a response. The receiver's state machine decides how to react.

*Example:* `transactionAlert` signal sent from the bank's notification server to the `CustomerApp` object — the server does not wait for the app to process it.

**Call Event (1.5 marks):** A call event is the synchronous invocation of an operation on an object. The sender blocks until the receiver finishes processing the call.

*Example:* `customer.debitAccount(amount)` invoked by the `ATMSession` object — the ATM session waits for the account object to confirm the debit before proceeding.

---

### 📝 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular Exam, Modified]**

> *(a)* Explain the **six-step methodology** for constructing a state model as recommended in object-oriented analysis. Apply the methodology to draw the state diagram for an **online railway reservation system** that supports user login, train search, seat availability check, ticket booking, payment, and ticket cancellation. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Compare and contrast **state modeling** with **dynamic modeling** (interaction modeling). In your answer, clearly highlight **at least four key differences** with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify Candidate Objects (1 mark)**

The two most state-rich objects in an online railway reservation system are:
- `ReservationSession` (the user's active booking session)
- `Ticket` (the booked ticket object itself)

We focus on `ReservationSession`.

**Step 2: Identify States (1.5 marks)**

- `LoggedOut` — User not authenticated
- `LoggedIn` — User authenticated
- `SearchPerformed` — Trains searched, results displayed
- `SeatSelected` — A specific seat has been chosen
- `AwaitingPayment` — User has chosen to pay
- `Booked` — Ticket is confirmed
- `Cancelled` — Booking was cancelled

**Step 3: Identify Events (1 mark)**

- `login(credentials)` — call event
- `searchTrain(source, dest, date)` — call event
- `selectSeat(seatId)` — call event
- `initiatePayment(mode)` — call event
- `paymentSuccess` — signal event
- `paymentFailed` — signal event
- `cancelBooking` — call event
- `logout` — call event

**Step 4: Identify Transitions (1.5 marks)**

| Current State | Event | Guard | Action | Target State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LoggedOut | login | [credentials valid] | createSession() | LoggedIn |
| LoggedIn | searchTrain | — | queryTrains() | SearchPerformed |
| SearchPerformed | selectSeat | [seatAvailable] | reserveTempSeat() | SeatSelected |
| SeatSelected | initiatePayment | — | openPaymentGateway() | AwaitingPayment |
| AwaitingPayment | paymentSuccess | — | confirmBooking() | Booked |
| AwaitingPayment | paymentFailed | — | releaseSeat() | SearchPerformed |
| Booked | cancelBooking | [before journey] | processRefund() | Cancelled |
| LoggedIn | logout | — | destroySession() | LoggedOut |

**Step 5: Add Actions and Guards (1 mark)**

For state `Booked`:
- `entry / sendEmailConfirmation()`
- `exit / logBookingClosed()`

For state `AwaitingPayment`:
- `do / pollPaymentStatus()`

**Step 6: Validate (1 mark)**

- ✅ All states reachable from `LoggedOut`
- ✅ No dead states
- ✅ "Book a ticket" use case: `LoggedOut → LoggedIn → SearchPerformed → SeatSelected → AwaitingPayment → Booked`
- ✅ "Cancel a ticket" use case: `Booked → Cancelled`
- ✅ "Logout" use case: `LoggedIn → LoggedOut`

**Final State Diagram (textual):**

```
[*] --> LoggedOut
LoggedOut --> LoggedIn : login [valid] / createSession
LoggedIn --> SearchPerformed : searchTrain / queryTrains
SearchPerformed --> SeatSelected : selectSeat [available] / reserveTempSeat
SearchPerformed --> SearchPerformed : selectSeat [unavailable] / showError
SeatSelected --> AwaitingPayment : initiatePayment / openGateway
AwaitingPayment --> Booked : paymentSuccess / confirmBooking
AwaitingPayment --> SearchPerformed : paymentFailed / releaseSeat
Booked --> Cancelled : cancelBooking [before journey] / refund
Booked --> LoggedIn : logout / closeSession
LoggedIn --> LoggedOut : logout / destroySession
Cancelled --> [*]
Booked --> [*]
LoggedOut --> [*]
```

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

| # | Aspect | State Modeling | Dynamic Modeling |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Focus** | Behavior of a *single object* over time | Behavior of *multiple objects* interacting |
| 2 | **UML Diagram** | State Machine Diagram | Sequence Diagram, Communication Diagram |
| 3 | **Time Dimension** | Time flows implicitly (state changes) | Time flows explicitly (vertical axis in sequence) |
| 4 | **Trigger Source** | Events from internal or external | Messages passed between objects |
| 5 | **Granularity** | Coarse-grained: one object, many states | Fine-grained: message-level details |
| 6 | **Best Used For** | Objects with distinct modes (e.g., `Order`) | Multi-object workflows (e.g., `Login → DB → Email`) |
| 7 | **Example** | State of a `TrafficLight`: Red → Green → Yellow | Sequence: User → Controller → Database → User |
| 8 | **Number of Objects Shown** | Typically one (the focus object) | Multiple (each lifeline) |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** State modeling answers *"What is the lifecycle of this single object?"*, while dynamic modeling answers *"How do multiple objects collaborate over time to fulfil a use case?"*. Both are complementary — KTU 2024 mandates both in OOAD.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> *(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the **UML State Machine Diagram** for a **microwave oven controller**. The system must support: door open/close, start button, cooking modes (defrost / micro / grill), timer expiry, and pause/resume. Identify all states, events, guard conditions, and actions. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* What is a **composite state** in UML? With the help of a banking `Account` example, explain the concept of **sequential sub-states** and **concurrent sub-states**. Use a diagram in your explanation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**States Identified (1.5 marks):**
- `Standby` — Idle, door closed, not running
- `DoorOpen` — Door is open, no cooking possible
- `SetupMode` — User is selecting mode and time
- `Cooking` — Oven is actively running
- `Paused` — Cooking halted mid-cycle
- `Complete` — Timer has expired

**Events Identified (1 mark):**
- `doorOpened` / `doorClosed` — signal events from door sensor
- `startPressed` — call event
- `modeSelected(mode)` — call event
- `timerSet(seconds)` — call event
- `pausePressed` — call event
- `resumePressed` — call event
- `timerExpired` — time event

**Transitions with Guards and Actions (3 marks):**

| Current State | Event | Guard | Action | Target State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standby | doorOpened | — | turnOnLight() | DoorOpen |
| DoorOpen | doorClosed | — | turnOffLight() | Standby |
| Standby | startPressed | — | — | SetupMode |
| SetupMode | modeSelected | [valid] | storeMode() | SetupMode |
| SetupMode | timerSet | [time > 0] | storeTime() | SetupMode |
| SetupMode | startPressed | [modeSet AND timeSet] | startMagnetron() | Cooking |
| Cooking | timerExpired | — | stopMagnetron() | Complete |
| Cooking | pausePressed | — | stopMagnetron() | Paused |
| Paused | resumePressed | — | startMagnetron() | Cooking |
| Paused | doorOpened | — | cancelTimer() | DoorOpen |

**Guard conditions explained (0.5 mark):** `[modeSet AND timeSet]` ensures the oven only starts cooking when both mode and timer are configured, preventing undefined behavior.

**Final State Diagram (1 mark):**

```
[*] --> Standby

Standby --> DoorOpen : doorOpened / turnOnLight
Standby --> SetupMode : startPressed

DoorOpen --> Standby : doorClosed / turnOffLight
DoorOpen --> SetupMode : startPressed

SetupMode --> SetupMode : modeSelected [valid] / storeMode
SetupMode --> SetupMode : timerSet [time over 0] / storeTime
SetupMode --> Cooking : startPressed [modeSet AND timeSet] / startMagnetron

Cooking --> Paused : pausePressed / stopMagnetron
Cooking --> Complete : timerExpired / stopMagnetron

Paused --> Cooking : resumePressed / startMagnetron
Paused --> DoorOpen : doorOpened / cancelTimer

Complete --> Standby : doorOpened / reset
Complete --> [*]
```

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Composite State (1.5 marks):**

> A **composite state** is a UML state that contains one or more *sub-states* (nested states). It is used to model complex behavior in a hierarchical, modular fashion — a fundamental feature of David Harel's statecharts (which form the basis of UML state machines).

**Sequential Sub-States (2.5 marks):**

In sequential sub-states, the object can be in **only one sub-state at a time** within the composite state. The sub-states are mutually exclusive.

**Banking Example — `Account` composite state:**

When a `Customer` logs into online banking, the `Account` object enters the composite state `LoggedIn`. Inside `LoggedIn`, the customer can be in exactly one of these sub-states at a time:
- `ViewingBalance`
- `ViewingTransactions`
- `TransferringFunds`
- `UpdatingProfile`

The customer transitions between these sub-states using menu actions.

```
state LoggedIn {
  [*] --> ViewingBalance
  ViewingBalance --> ViewingTransactions : clickHistory
  ViewingTransactions --> TransferringFunds : clickTransfer
  TransferringFunds --> ViewingBalance : transferComplete
  ViewingBalance --> UpdatingProfile : clickProfile
  UpdatingProfile --> ViewingBalance : saveProfile
}
```

**Concurrent Sub-States (2.5 marks):**

In concurrent sub-states, the object can be in **two or more sub-states simultaneously**. UML represents this using **fork** and **join** bars.

**Banking Example — `DuringTransfer` composite state:**

When a customer is performing a fund transfer, **two activities happen concurrently**:
- **Region 1 (Authentication):** OTP is being sent, verified, and validated
- **Region 2 (Processing):** Debit and credit operations are being prepared

Both regions must complete before the transfer is finalized (join).

```
state DuringTransfer {
  [*] --> fork
  fork --> AwaitingOTP
  fork --> PreparingDebit
  AwaitingOTP --> ValidatingOTP : otpEntered
  ValidatingOTP --> join : otpVerified
  PreparingDebit --> join : fundsReserved
  join --> TransferComplete
  TransferComplete --> [*]
}
```

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Composite states (sequential or concurrent) make large state models readable, reusable, and maintainable — a critical reason why UML statecharts scale better than primitive finite-state machines.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in State Modeling Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the Initial State (`●`):** Every state diagram MUST begin with a filled black circle. Drawing only states without an initial pseudo-state loses **1 full mark**.
> 2. **❌ Missing Action/Event Distinction:** Writing `do / sendEmail()` (which is an *activity* with duration) instead of `entry / sendEmail()` (which is an *action* — instantaneous) costs marks. **Rule of thumb:** If it takes time → `do /`; if it's atomic → `entry /` or on the transition arrow.
> 3. **❌ Confusing Self-Transition with Internal Transition:** A self-transition (`S → S`) executes entry AND exit actions. An internal transition (drawn inside the state box) does NOT. Many students conflate these.
> 4. **❌ Forgetting Guard Conditions on Branching Transitions:** If two transitions leave a state with the same event, you MUST have mutually exclusive guards. Drawing them without `[guard]` is incomplete.
> 5. **❌ No Final State:** A state diagram should ideally end with `⊙` (final state). A diagram that "ends abruptly" with no final pseudo-state looks incomplete.
> 6. **❌ Wrong Notation for Time Events:** Correct: `after(30 seconds)`. Wrong: `after 30 seconds` (no parentheses is informal).
> 7. **❌ Conflating Dynamic Modeling with State Modeling:** In Question A(b), do NOT describe sequence diagrams as "state diagrams" — they are *interaction diagrams*. Use the exact terms **"State Machine Diagram"** vs **"Sequence Diagram"**.
> 8. **❌ Skipping Validation Step:** In the 6-step procedure, Step 6 (validation) carries 1 mark. Skipping it costs you an easy mark.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **State Modeling** = UML State Machine Diagram (Statechart) for modeling dynamic behavior of a *single object*.
>
> ✅ The **five building blocks** are: **State, Event, Transition, Action, Guard**.
>
> ✅ The **four event types** (KTU mandatory): **Signal, Call, Time, Change**.
>
> ✅ The **four action types**: **Entry, Exit, Do Activity, Transition Action**.
>
> ✅ Transition syntax: `event [guard] / action`.
>
> ✅ **Initial state** = filled black circle `●`. **Final state** = bull's-eye `⊙`. **History state** = `H` inside a circle.
>
> ✅ The **six-step methodology** (KTU high-yield): Identify Objects → States → Events → Transitions → Add Actions/Guards → Validate.
>
> ✅ **Self-transition** = same source and target state (executes entry + exit).
>
> ✅ **Internal transition** = drawn inside the state box (does NOT execute entry/exit).
>
> ✅ **Composite state** = state containing sub-states. Can be **sequential** (one sub-state at a time) or **concurrent** (multiple sub-states in parallel using fork/join).
>
> ✅ State modeling focuses on **one object**; dynamic modeling focuses on **multiple objects interacting**.
>
> ✅ The state model is **complementary** to dynamic modeling — both are required by KTU 2024 OOAD.
>
> ✅ Validation technique: simulate every use case step-by-step through the state model (the "drink water" test).
>
> ✅ Do NOT confuse **`do /` activity** (long-running, with duration) with **`entry /` action** (instantaneous, atomic).
>
> ✅ Guard conditions are evaluated **only once** at event time. If false → transition is not taken.
>
> ✅ Real-world tools: **IBM Rhapsody, MATLAB Stateflow, Yakindu** — these can auto-generate code from statecharts.
>
> ✅ Standard textbook reference (universally followed by KTU): *Bruegge & Dutoit — "Object-Oriented Software Engineering Using UML, Patterns, and Java"* and *Jacobson, Booch, Rumbaugh — "The Unified Modeling Language User Guide"*.
>
> ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme marks distribution for Module 3 (per official syllabus PDF): State Modeling carries **8–10 marks weightage** in university exams, typically as one 7-mark sub-part.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interaction Modeling** is the discipline within software engineering (specifically under the UML Behavioral/Interaction diagrams cluster of Object-Oriented Analysis and Design, OOAD) that focuses on capturing and representing the **dynamic behavior** of a software system — i.e., *how objects, actors, and components exchange messages, transition between states, and collaborate over time* to fulfill a specific use case or system function.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCITT503 – Software Engineering**, Interaction Modeling is a critical step in the **Requirements → Analysis → Design** transition. It uses the **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5)** interaction diagrams as the primary notation. The four primary interaction diagram types mandated by the syllabus are:

| # | Diagram | Captures |
|---|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Sequence Diagram** | Time-ordered message exchange between objects along a vertical lifeline |
| 2 | **Communication Diagram** | Object linkages and numbered message sequencing (UML 1.x *collaboration* renamed) |
| 3 | **Interaction Overview Diagram** | Activity-diagram control flow nesting sequence/communication fragments |
| 4 | **Timing Diagram** | State/value changes along a horizontal time axis with linear/parallel lifelines |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Although **Activity Diagrams** and **State Machine Diagrams** are listed separately as *behavioral* diagrams in UML, the KTU 2024 PCITT503 syllabus bundles them into Module 3's *Interaction Modeling* umbrella because they collectively describe the *dynamic view* (the 4th view in the **4+1 Architectural View Model** by Kruchten).

The **purpose** of interaction modeling is three-fold:
1. **Validate Use Cases** by tracing the flow of events from actor to system and back.
2. **Identify object responsibilities** (GRASP patterns: Information Expert, Controller, Creator).
3. **Establish contracts and operation signatures** that become the **public interface** of classes in design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are directing a **movie**. The *script* is your use case description. The *storyboard* (showing actors, scenes, and dialogue order) is your interaction model.
> - A **Sequence Diagram** is the *scene-by-scene shot list* with a vertical timeline: who speaks first, who replies, and at what second.
> - A **Communication Diagram** is the *network map* of characters showing who is linked to whom and in what numbered order they interact.
> - A **State Machine Diagram** is the *emotional-state map* of the protagonist: "if insulted → angry; if apologised → calm."
> - An **Activity Diagram** is the *flowchart* of the entire production: pre-production → shooting → post-production.
>
> Without a storyboard, the crew is confused. Without an interaction model, the developers are confused.

## 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Memorise

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Actor** | An external entity (user, system, hardware) that interacts with the system under design |
| **Lifeline** | A vertical dashed line representing the existence of an object over time |
| **Message** | A communication between objects (synchronous, asynchronous, return, self) |
| **Combined Fragment** | UML 2.0+ construct (alt, opt, loop, par, critical, neg, assert, strict) for control flow |
| **Activation Bar** | A thin rectangle on a lifeline indicating the object is executing a method |
| **System Sequence Diagram (SSD)** | A specialized sequence diagram where the *system* is treated as a single black-box object |
| **Use Case Realization** | The mapping of a use case to a set of collaborating objects (Booch/Rumbaugh/Jacobson) |
| **Operation Contract** | A formal description of what an operation does, written in pre/post-condition form |
| **Coupling** | Degree of interdependence between objects — minimised by interaction modeling |
| **Cohesion** | Degree to which responsibilities of a single object are related — maximised by good interaction design |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Interaction Modeling (KTU 2024 Framework)

### Pillar 1 — Use Case Realization (the *entry point*)

Every interaction model begins with a **realized use case**. Realization is performed in three logical layers (Larman, *Applying UML and Patterns*, which is the de-facto KTU reference for OOAD):

1. **System-level (Black-box)**: System Sequence Diagram (SSD) — shows *:System* as a single object receiving actor messages.
2. **Object-level (White-box)**: Sequence / Communication Diagrams — show *interior* object collaboration.
3. **Contract-level**: Operation contracts specifying *post-conditions* in terms of system state changes.

### Pillar 2 — Sequence Diagrams (Time-Ordered Behavior)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading direction rule (KTU exam favourite):** Time flows **top → bottom** along the lifeline. **Messages are read top-down**, but the *sequence number* (1, 2, 3, …) is *optional* and appears as a prefix on the message label: `1: makePayment() : Boolean`.

The **anatomy of a sequence diagram message** has the form:

$$\text{[\text{seq}]\ \text{messageName}(\text{args})\ :\ \text{returnType}}$$

**Message types (5 standard, all examinable):**

| Type | Notation | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Synchronous** | Filled arrow `▶—` | Sender **waits** for reply |
| **Asynchronous** | Open arrow `→>` | Sender **does not wait** |
| **Return** | Dashed open arrow `⤴- - >` | Returning control/value |
| **Self-Message** | Loop arrow on same lifeline | Object calls its own method |
| **Create** | Dashed arrow with `«create»` | Instantiation message |

### Pillar 3 — Communication Diagrams (Link-Centric Behavior)

> [!NOTE]
> A **communication diagram** = a sequence diagram rotated 90° + with object **links** drawn explicitly. The *order* of messages is conveyed by **numbering prefixes**: `1`, `1.1`, `1.1.1`, `2a`, `2b` (for parallel messages).

**Numbering scheme (KTU classic):**
- Root messages: `1, 2, 3 …`
- Nested calls: `1.1, 1.1.1`
- Concurrent messages at same level: `2.1, 2.2, 2.3`

### Pillar 4 — State Machine Diagrams (Object Life Cycle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **state machine** describes *one* object's lifecycle in response to events. It has:
> - **States** (rounded rectangles): `Idle`, `Verifying`, `Active`, `Suspended`
> - **Transitions** (arrows): `event [guard] / action`
> - **Initial state** (filled black circle) and **Final state** (bull's-eye)

**The transition formula (Kruchten / Harel formalism):**

$$T = \text{Event}[\text{Guard}]/\text{Action}$$

Where:
- `Event` = trigger (e.g., `buttonPressed`, `timeout`)
- `Guard` = Boolean condition (e.g., `[balance > 0]`)
- `Action` = side-effect activity (e.g., `/ debitAccount()`)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Purpose | KTU Weight |
|---------|----------------|---------|------------|
| **Coupling metric (C)** | $C = \sum_{i<j} \text{strength}(i,j) \cdot \text{frequency}(i,j)$ | Quantifies inter-object dependency | High |
| **Cohesion metric (Co)** | $C_o = \dfrac{\text{intra-class relationships}}{\text{possible relationships}}$ | Quantifies focus of a class | High |
| **Message sequence depth (D)** | $D = \max_{p \in \text{paths}} \text{length}(p)$ | Maximum nesting of method calls | Medium |
| **Object count in collaboration (N)** | $N = \vert \text{Lifelines in sequence diagram} \vert$ | Granularity indicator | Medium |
| **Cyclomatic complexity of an Activity Diagram** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Counts independent paths in the flow | High |
| **State-machine reachability (R)** | $R = \dfrac{\vert \text{reachable states} \vert}{\vert \text{total states} \vert}$ | Coverage of possible states | Medium |
| **Interaction completeness (IC)** | $IC = 1 - \dfrac{\text{missing actor messages}}{\text{total use-case steps}}$ | SSD/use-case alignment | High |
| **Use Case Realization Coverage (UCRC)** | $UCRC = \dfrac{\vert \text{realized use cases} \vert}{\vert \text{total use cases} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Traceability metric | Low |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU board examiner's note:** In the table above, do NOT use the pipe character `|` in your written answers. Always render set-cardinality as `\vert ... \vert` in LaTeX or write "the number of" in prose. This is a *frequently lost* formatting mark in the answer script scan.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interaction modeling is **not an academic exercise** — it powers real engineering domains:

1. **Embedded & IoT Systems** — Timing diagrams validate that sensor-sampling and actuator-response stay within microsecond budgets.
2. **Banking & Fintech** — Sequence diagrams enforce transactional ordering (e.g., debit-before-credit is forbidden — must use **saga / compensation** pattern).
3. **Telecommunications (OSS/BSS)** — Activity diagrams model call-flow state machines for SIP/H.323.
4. **Healthcare (HL7/FHIR)** — Sequence diagrams represent clinical message exchange between EHR modules.
5. **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** — Timing diagrams model the CAN-bus and signal-exchange timing.
6. **Real-Time Control (Aerospace / Industrial PLC)** — State machines are the *de facto* implementation source for SCADA.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Derivation of a Use Case Realization (Larman's 3-Layer Process)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **complete, examiner-grade derivation** of how a textual use case is transformed into a System Sequence Diagram (SSD) and then into a Design Sequence Diagram (DSD). This is a **14-mark standard** in KTU ESE.

### Step 0 — Start with the Use Case Text

> **Use Case:** *Process Sale* (POS terminal)
> **Actor:** Cashier
> **Main flow:**
> 1. Customer arrives at checkout with items.
> 2. Cashier enters each item identifier.
> 3. System records the line item and presents the running total.
> 4. Cashier signals end of sale.
> 5. System calculates the grand total and taxes.
> 6. Cashier tells customer the total; customer pays.
> 7. System handles payment and prints receipt.

### Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary and the System Actor

The system is treated as a **single black box** called `:System` or `:Register`. The Cashier remains as the external actor.

### Step 2 — Map each Use Case Step to a Message

| Use Case Step | Message on SSD | Direction |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| Step 2: Cashier enters item | `1: enterItem(upc, qty)` | Actor → System |
| System records and shows total | *(System internal — not shown on SSD)* | — |
| Step 3: Running total | `2: endSale()` | Actor → System |
| Step 4: End of sale | *(implicit in message 2)* | — |
| Step 5: Calculate total/tax | `3: getTotal() : Double` (synchronous return) | Actor → System |
| Step 6: Customer pays | `4: makePayment(cash, amount) : Boolean` | Actor → System |

### Step 3 — Render the SSD Algebraically

The sequence of messages, in order, with sequence numbers and types:

$$
M_{SSD} = \langle m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4 \rangle
$$

where each message $m_i$ is a 4-tuple:

$$
m_i = (s_i,\ r_i,\ t_i,\ ret_i)
$$

with:
- $s_i$ = sender lifeline identifier
- $r_i$ = receiver lifeline identifier
- $t_i$ = message type ∈ {sync, async, return}
- $ret_i$ = return type (or ∅)

For our *Process Sale* SSD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 &= (\text{Cashier},\ \text{:System},\ \text{sync},\ \text{void}) \\
m_2 &= (\text{Cashier},\ \text{:System},\ \text{sync},\ \text{void}) \\
m_3 &= (\text{Cashier},\ \text{:System},\ \text{sync},\ \text{Double}) \\
m_4 &= (\text{Cashier},\ \text{:System},\ \text{sync},\ \text{Boolean})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Evolve SSD into a Design Sequence Diagram (DSD) — *White Box*

Following **GRASP Information Expert** and **Controller** patterns, the `:System` black box is decomposed into collaborating objects. The candidate objects for *Process Sale* are:

$$
O = \{ \text{Sale},\ \text{Register},\ \text{ProductCatalog},\ \text{Payment}\} \cup \{ \text{:System} \}
$$

The **message-routing derivation** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1' &: \text{Cashier} \xrightarrow{\text{1: makeNewSale()}} \text{:Register (Controller)} \\
m_1.1 &: \text{:Register} \xrightarrow{\text{1.1: create()}} \text{Sale} \\
m_2' &: \text{Cashier} \xrightarrow{\text{2: enterItem(upc, qty)}} \text{:Register} \\
m_2.1 &: \text{:Register} \xrightarrow{\text{2.1: addLineItem(upc, qty)}} \text{Sale} \\
m_2.1.1 &: \text{Sale} \xrightarrow{\text{2.1.1: getProductSpec(upc)}} \text{ProductCatalog}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Operation Contract for `Sale.addLineItem()`

**Contract CO1: `Sale.addLineItem(upc : String, qty : Integer)`**

- **Cross-references:** Use Cases: *Process Sale*
- **Pre-conditions:** A *ProductSpecification* with the given `upc` exists in the catalog.
- **Post-conditions:**
  - A new *SalesLineItem* `sli` was created. (Creation)
  - `sli` was associated with the current *Sale*. (Association)
  - `sli.quantity` was set to `qty`. (Attribute modification)
  - `sli.productSpec` was set to the *ProductSpecification* matching `upc`. (Attribute modification)
  - *Sale.total* was updated to reflect the new line item. (Attribute modification)

### Step 6 — Verify with the 4-Condition Post-Condition Checklist

| Post-condition | Pattern used | UML construct |
|----------------|-------------|---------------|
| Instance created | **Create** | `«create»` message |
| Association formed | **Establish association** | Link drawn in DSD |
| Attribute modified | **Modify attribute** | Setter or direct assignment |
| Link destroyed | **Break association** | `«destroy»` message |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Diagram (KTU Exam Standard)

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **directly tested** in KTU Module 3. Memorise the mapping — it appears every year in some form.

| Question to ask about the requirement | Best diagram | Justification |
|----------------------------------------|--------------|----------------|
| *What messages are exchanged and in what order?* | **Sequence Diagram** | Time is the primary axis |
| *Which objects are linked and how do they collaborate?* | **Communication Diagram** | Linkage is the primary axis |
| *What is the lifecycle of a single object?* | **State Machine Diagram** | States + transitions |
| *What is the overall workflow / business process?* | **Activity Diagram** | Activities + decisions + parallel flows |
| *What values change over time?* | **Timing Diagram** | Continuous time axis |
| *How is one use case distributed across many internal objects?* | **Interaction Overview Diagram** | High-level control + nested sequences |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Cyclomatic Complexity of an Activity Diagram

> **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU sub-part):** Given an Activity Diagram with $E = 13$ edges, $N = 11$ nodes, and $P = 1$ connected component, compute the Cyclomatic Complexity using McCabe's formula.

**Formula (McCabe, 1976):**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

**Substitution:**

$$V(G) = 13 - 11 + 2(1)$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the multiplicative term:**

$$2P = 2 \times 1 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Combine all terms:**

$$V(G) = 13 - 11 + 2 = 4$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$
V(G) = 4 \implies \text{4 linearly independent test paths are required for branch coverage}
$$

**Verification by independent-region counting:**
- 1 loop region
- 1 decision-branch (if-else) region
- 1 self-loop region
- 1 parallel-split (fork) region
- **Total = 4** ✓

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — System Sequence Diagram (SSD) of *Process Sale*

The following Mermaid block represents the **SSD** at the black-box level. It mirrors exactly the Larman 3-layer derivation in §3.1.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Cashier as Cashier (Actor)
    participant System as :Register (System)

    Cashier->>System: 1: makeNewSale()
    System-->>Cashier: 1.1: return (Sale created)

    Cashier->>System: 2: enterItem(upc, qty)
    System-->>Cashier: 2.1: return (line total)

    Cashier->>System: 3: endSale()
    System-->>Cashier: 3.1: return saleTotal : Double

    Cashier->>System: 4: makePayment(cash, amount)
    System-->>Cashier: 4.1: return ok : Boolean

    Note over Cashier,System: SSD — System is treated as a SINGLE black-box object
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Design Sequence Diagram (White-Box Realization)

The following expands the `:System` black box into the **collaborating object set** $\{ \text{Sale},\ \text{Register},\ \text{ProductCatalog} \}$.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Cashier as Cashier
    participant Register as :Register
    participant Sale as :Sale
    participant Catalog as :ProductCatalog

    Cashier->>Register: 1: makeNewSale()
    Register->>Sale: 1.1: create() [create]
    Sale-->>Register: 1.1.1: ok

    Cashier->>Register: 2: enterItem(upc, qty)
    Register->>Sale: 2.1: addLineItem(upc, qty)
    Sale->>Catalog: 2.1.1: getProductSpec(upc)
    Catalog-->>Sale: 2.1.1.1: spec
    Sale-->>Register: 2.1.2: ok

    Cashier->>Register: 3: endSale()
    Register->>Sale: 3.1: getTotal()
    Sale-->>Register: 3.1.1: total : Double
    Register-->>Cashier: 3.2: total : Double

    Note over Register,Sale: GRASP Controller pattern — Register is the system-level controller
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — State Machine Diagram for a *Sale* Object Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active : start

    Active --> Suspended : suspend()
    Suspended --> Active : resume()

    Active --> Completed : endSale() [itemsCount > 0]
    Active --> Cancelled : cancel() [itemsCount == 0]

    Completed --> Paid : makePayment(amount) [amount >= total]
    Completed --> Cancelled : timeout [elapsed > 30 min]

    Paid --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Communication Diagram (Link-Centric View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    cashier["Cashier (Actor)"]
    register[":Register"]
    sale[":Sale"]
    catalog[":ProductCatalog"]
    payment[":Payment"]

    cashier --- register
    register --- sale
    sale --- catalog
    sale --- payment

    cashier:::actorStyle
    register:::controllerStyle
    sale:::entityStyle
    catalog:::entityStyle
    payment:::entityStyle

    classDef actorStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controllerStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef entityStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message ordering (not shown in the visual link graph, but required in the diagram):**
> `1: makeNewSale() → :Register`
> `1.1: create() → :Sale`
> `2: enterItem(upc, qty) → :Register`
> `2.1: addLineItem(upc, qty) → :Sale`
> `2.1.1: getProductSpec(upc) → :ProductCatalog`
> `3: endSale() → :Register`
> `3.1: getTotal() → :Sale`
> `4: makePayment(cash, amount) → :Register`
> `4.1: debit(amount) → :Payment`

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Activity Diagram for the *Process Sale* Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNodeA([Start]) --> idleA[Idle: awaiting customer]
    idleA --> enterA[Cashier enters item]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

### **Q1. Define an Interaction Diagram. List the four UML 2.5 interaction diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Interaction Diagram** is a UML behavioral diagram that depicts the **messages exchanged among objects** and the **sequence in which they occur** to accomplish a specific functionality of the system.

The four UML 2.5 interaction diagrams are:
1. **Sequence Diagram** — emphasizes time-ordered message exchange.
2. **Communication Diagram** — emphasizes object links with numbered messages.
3. **Interaction Overview Diagram** — high-level activity-flow with nested interaction fragments.
4. **Timing Diagram** — emphasizes state/value changes over a continuous time axis.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between a System Sequence Diagram (SSD) and a Design Sequence Diagram (DSD).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SSD | DSD |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **System treatment** | System is a single black box | System is decomposed into multiple objects |
| **Purpose** | Captures *what* messages the system handles | Captures *how* internal objects collaborate |
| **Granularity** | Coarse — actor-level | Fine — object-level |
| **Use case stage** | Used during *requirements* analysis | Used during *design* phase |
| **Number of lifelines** | 2 (actor + :System) | Many (all participating objects) |
| **Message origin** | Actor-driven only | Internal + actor messages |

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Draw and explain a **System Sequence Diagram (SSD)** for the *Use Case: Withdraw Cash* in an ATM system. Clearly show the actor, the system lifeline, message sequence numbers, and return values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evolve the SSD in (a) into a **Design Sequence Diagram (DSD)** by introducing the *Controller*, *Account*, and *ATM* objects. Identify the **GRASP pattern** used at each level. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the system and actor.**
- Actor: **Customer**
- System: **:ATM (black box)**

**Step 2 — List the use case steps to be modeled:**

| Step | Event | SSD Message |
|------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Customer inserts card | `1: insertCard(cardNo)` |
| 2 | System validates | `1.1: return (ok)` |
| 3 | Customer enters PIN | `2: enterPIN(pin)` |
| 4 | System checks PIN | `2.1: return (authenticated)` |
| 5 | Customer selects "Withdraw" | `3: selectWithdrawal(amount)` |
| 6 | System asks account for balance | *(internal — not on SSD)* |
| 7 | System dispenses cash | `4: dispenseCash(amount) : Boolean` |
| 8 | System returns card | `5: returnCard()` |

**Step 3 — Render the SSD:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Customer
    participant ATM as :ATM

    Customer->>ATM: 1: insertCard(cardNo)
    ATM-->>Customer: 1.1: return ok

    Customer->>ATM: 2: enterPIN(pin)
    ATM-->>Customer: 2.1: return authenticated

    Customer->>ATM: 3: selectWithdrawal(amount)
    ATM-->>Customer: 3.1: return allowed : Boolean

    Customer->>ATM: 4: confirmWithdrawal()
    ATM-->>Customer: 4.1: return cashDispensed : Boolean

    ATM->>Customer: 5: returnCard()
```

**Distribution of marks (7):**
- Correct identification of actor & system lifeline: **1 mark**
- Correct message numbering: **1 mark**
- Correct message types (sync/return): **1 mark**
- At least 4 valid messages with return types: **2 marks**
- Neat SSD with lifelines, activations: **2 marks**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Decompose the `:ATM` black box into collaborating objects:**

$$
O = \{ \text{:ATMController},\ \text{:Account},\ \text{:CashDispenser},\ \text{:CardReader} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply GRASP patterns:**

| Object | GRASP Pattern Justification |
|--------|-----------------------------|
| `:ATMController` | **Controller** — receives and coordinates all system events |
| `:Account` | **Information Expert** — knows the balance & debit logic |
| `:CashDispenser` | **Pure Fabrication** — hardware-handling service object |
| `:CardReader` | **Pure Fabrication** — hardware-handling service object |

**Step 3 — Render the DSD (selected messages):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
```
