    actor Customer
    participant ATM as :ATMController
    participant Acc as :Account
    participant Disp as :CashDispenser

    Customer->>ATM: 1: insertCard(cardNo)
    ATM->>ATM: 1.1: readCard()
    Customer->>ATM: 2: enterPIN(pin)
    ATM->>Acc: 2.1: verifyPIN(pin)
    Acc-->>ATM: 2.1.1: ok

    Customer->>ATM: 3: selectWithdrawal(amount)
    ATM->>Acc: 3.1: debit(amount)
    Acc-->>ATM: 3.1.1: ok
    ATM->>Disp: 3.2: dispense(amount)
    Disp-->>ATM: 3.2.1: ok
    ATM-->>Customer: 3.3: return dispensed : Boolean
```

**Distribution of marks (7):**
- Correct decomposition & pattern identification: **2 marks**
- Correct message routing (controller delegates): **2 marks**
- At least 5 nested/sequential messages: **2 marks**
- Neatness and use of activation bars: **1 mark**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Draw a **State Machine Diagram** for an *Order* object in an e-commerce system covering states *New, Confirmed, Packed, Shipped, Delivered, Cancelled*. Write the **transition formula** for any two transitions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An activity diagram of a *Loan Approval Process* has **18 edges, 14 nodes, 2 connected components**. Calculate the **Cyclomatic Complexity** using McCabe's formula and state the minimum number of test cases required. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the state set:**

$$
S = \{ \text{New},\ \text{Confirmed},\ \text{Packed},\ \text{Shipped},\ \text{Delivered},\ \text{Cancelled} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the transition triggers and guards:**

| Transition | Event | Guard | Action |
|------------|-------|-------|--------|
| New → Confirmed | `paymentReceived` | `[amount > 0]` | `/ allocateStock()` |
| Confirmed → Packed | `itemsCollected` | `[allItemsPacked]` | `/ generateShippingLabel()` |
| Packed → Shipped | `dispatched` | `[courier != null]` | `/ notifyCustomer()` |
| Shipped → Delivered | `delivered` | `[signedBy != null]` | `/ closeOrder()` |
| {New, Confirmed, Packed} → Cancelled | `cancel` | `[refundEligible]` | `/ refund()` |

**Step 3 — Transition formula (Kruchten / Harel):**

$$T = \text{Event}[\text{Guard}]/\text{Action}$$

Applied to the first two transitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{paymentReceived}\ [\ \text{amount} > 0\ ]\ /\ \text{allocateStock()} \\
T_2 &: \text{dispatched}\ [\ \text{courier} \neq \text{null}\ ]\ /\ \text{notifyCustomer()}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Render the state diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : orderCreated
    New --> Confirmed : paymentReceived [amount > 0] / allocateStock
    New --> Cancelled : cancel [refundEligible] / refund
    Confirmed --> Packed : itemsCollected [allItemsPacked] / generateLabel
    Confirmed --> Cancelled : cancel [refundEligible] / refund
    Packed --> Shipped : dispatched [courier != null] / notifyCustomer
    Packed --> Cancelled : cancel [refundEligible] / refund
    Shipped --> Delivered : delivered [signedBy != null] / closeOrder
    Delivered --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

**Distribution of marks (7):**
- All 6 states drawn correctly: **2 marks**
- Initial & final states: **1 mark**
- At least 5 transitions with valid events: **2 marks**
- Transition formula written twice: **2 marks**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks**

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$
V(G) = 18 - 14 + 2(2)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the multiplicative term:**

$$2P = 2 \times 2 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Combine all terms:**

$$V(G) = 18 - 14 + 4 = 8$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

- Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G) = 8$
- **Minimum number of test cases** required for full branch coverage = $\max(1, V(G)) = 8$
- **Engineering verdict:** $V(G) \in [1,10]$ is **acceptable and testable**; if $V(G) > 10$, the diagram should be refactored (McCabe's industry rule).

**Distribution of marks (7):**
- Correct formula statement: **2 marks**
- Correct substitution: **2 marks**
- Correct final answer with units / interpretation: **2 marks**
- Test-case conclusion linked to $V(G)$: **1 mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting return messages** on synchronous calls. (–1 mark per omission)
> 2. **Drawing `«create»` messages** without the dashed-line stereotype notation. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing system sequence diagram (SSD) with class diagram** — remember SSD has *lifelines*, class diagram has *class rectangles*. (–3 marks)
> 4. **Writing `|` inside markdown tables** in the answer script — use `\vert` or plain English. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Not labelling the `«»` UML stereotypes** (e.g., `«boundary»`, `«entity»`, `«control»`) — examiners explicitly look for them. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting to show the Initial (filled black circle) and Final (bull's-eye) states** in state machine diagrams. (–1 mark)
> 7. **In McCabe complexity**, students frequently use the wrong formula ($V = E - N + 1$ instead of $V = E - N + 2P$). (–2 marks)
> 8. **Self-messages drawn as a separate lifeline** — must loop back onto the SAME lifeline. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision card for the night before the exam:**

- ☐ **Interaction Modeling** = capturing the *dynamic view* of a system using UML 2.5.
- ☐ Four interaction diagrams: **Sequence, Communication, Interaction Overview, Timing**.
- ☐ Sequence diagrams: **time top → bottom**, message types = sync (▶—), async (→>), return (- - ->).
- ☐ Communication diagrams: **links + numbered messages** (e.g., `1`, `1.1`, `1.1.1`).
- ☐ State machine formula: **Event[Guard]/Action**.
- ☐ Activity diagram complexity: **$V(G) = E - N + 2P$**.
- ☐ Three Larman layers: **Use Case Text → SSD → DSD → Operation Contracts**.
- ☐ GRASP for interaction: **Controller, Information Expert, Pure Fabrication, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion**.
- ☐ SSDs are **black-box** (`:System` as 1 lifeline); DSDs are **white-box** (many objects).
- ☐ UML stereotypes: **`«boundary»`, `«entity»`, `«control»`, `«create»`, `«destroy»`**.
- ☐ Use case realization = **mapping a use case to a set of collaborating objects**.
- ☐ Operation contract has **pre-conditions** and **post-conditions** (4 patterns: Create, Modify Attribute, Establish Association, Break Association).
- ☐ Always label lifelines, activations, return types, and stereotypes — examiners check.
- ☐ Never use raw `|` in tables; use `\vert` or rewrite in words.
- ☐ Activity diagrams use **rounded rectangles** for actions and **diamonds** for decisions.
- ☐ Interaction Overview Diagrams nest **ref** frames referring to other interaction diagrams.
- ☐ Timing diagrams use a **horizontal** time axis; great for embedded / real-time systems.
- ☐ Coupling ↓ and Cohesion ↑ are the two golden metrics interaction modeling tries to optimize.
- ☐ **Traceability matrix**: every SSD message must map to one use-case step; every DSD message must map to one SSD message.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Structural Modeling # MODULE 3 — STRUCTURAL MODELING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Structural Modeling** in the Unified Modeling Language (UML) is the discipline of capturing the *static* architecture of a software-intensive system. It describes the **objects, classes, components, packages, and deployment nodes** that constitute the system, together with the **static relationships** that exist between them at any single moment in time. According to the OMG UML 2.5 specification (which the KTU 2024 syllabus implicitly follows), structural modeling is concerned with the *vocabulary* of the system — *who* the elements are and *how* they are connected — as opposed to behavioral modeling, which addresses *what* they do over time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** > *"Structural modeling represents the static aspects of a system. It shows the classes, objects, interfaces, components, nodes, packages and the relationships that exist between them. The principal structural diagrams are Class, Object, Component, Deployment, Composite Structure, and Package diagrams."* The seven (7) official UML **structural diagrams** classified by the OMG are: 1. **Class Diagram** — Backbone of OO design; classes + relationships. 2. **Object Diagram** — Snapshot of instances at a moment in time. 3. **Component Diagram** — Organization of physical software modules (`.jar`, `.dll`). 4. **Deployment Diagram** — Physical hardware topology and software artifact allocation. 5. **Composite Structure Diagram** — Internal structure of a class (parts, ports, connectors). 6. **Package Diagram** — Logical grouping and dependency between namespaces. 7. **Profile Diagram** *(occasionally taught)* — Domain-specific UML extensions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" View Imagine you are an **architect designing a hospital building**: - The **floor plan** (which rooms exist, how big they are, where the doors and corridors connect them) = **Class Diagram**. It tells you *what* is in the building and *how* things are statically connected. - A **photograph** of the building on a particular day showing *actual* patients in *actual* rooms = **Object Diagram** (an instance snapshot). - A **bill-of-materials / module list** (HVAC module, electrical panel module, plumbing riser) = **Component Diagram**. - A **site map** showing where the building sits, where the transformer is, and which server room is on which floor = **Deployment Diagram**. - A **room-by-room zoomed plan** showing how a specific *Surgical Suite* is built out of sub-parts (scrub area, OR, recovery) = **Composite Structure Diagram**. - A **zoning map** grouping floors into "Public Zones" and "Restricted Zones" with arrows showing dependencies = **Package Diagram**. Just as a civil engineer cannot pour concrete without first inspecting the blueprint, a software engineer cannot proceed to behavioral modeling (Use-Case, Sequence, Activity) without first stabilizing the structural skeleton. **Structure is the noun; behavior is the verb.** > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** > Structural modeling answers **“WHAT is in the system and HOW is it wired together?”** It ignores *time* and *flow*. That time-aware work belongs to behavioral diagrams (covered in Module 4 of PCITT503). ### 1.3 Why Structural Modeling Matters — Engineering Utility | Industry Domain | Real-World Utility of Structural Modeling | |---|---| | **Banking Software** | Defines Account, Customer, Transaction classes and inheritance hierarchies. | | **Embedded/IoT** | Deployment diagrams map firmware to microcontrollers, sensors to buses. | | **Microservices (Cloud)** | Component diagrams model `.jar` / container boundaries and their interfaces. | | **Healthcare HIS** | Class diagrams formalize Patient, Doctor, Prescription, Diagnosis. | | **Compiler Design** | Class structure defines Token, AST-Node, SymbolTable scopes. | | **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Composite structure models SW-C ports wired across ECUs. | ### 1.4 Vocabulary of Structural Modeling — The Core Elements - **Class** — A blueprint of objects having common attributes, operations, and relationships. - **Object (Instance)** — A concrete manifestation of a class at run-time. - **Attribute** — A named property of a class (field). - **Operation / Method** — A function executed by an object. - **Interface** — A contract of operations without implementation. - **Component** — A modular, replaceable software unit. - **Node** — A physical computational resource (server, device, processor). - **Package** — A logical namespace container. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visibility Notation (KTU 2024 standard — must appear in exam answers):** > > - `+` → **Public** (visible to all classes) > - `-` → **Private** (visible only within the class) > - `#` → **Protected** (visible to subclasses) > - `~` → **Package** (visible within the same package) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six (Six!) Core Relationship Types — KTU High-Yield The single most-tested topic under Structural Modeling in the KTU board exam is the **classification of relationships**. You must be able to draw, name, and describe each one with an example. ### 2.1.1 Dependency (`- - - >`) - **Semantic meaning:** "A *uses* B; a change in B may force a change in A." - **Direction:** Source → Target (dashed arrow). - **Strength:** **Weakest** of all UML relationships. - **Example:** A `Controller` class depends on a `Logger` class. ### 2.1.2 Association (`———>`) - **Semantic meaning:** "A *knows-about* B; a structural link between peer classes." - **Direction:** Usually bidirectional, but can be directed. - **Multiplicity:** Annotated at each end (e.g., `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`). - **Example:** `Teacher` teaches `Student`. ### 2.1.3 Aggregation (`———<> `, hollow diamond) - **Semantic meaning:** "A *has-a* B where B can exist independently of A." (weak ownership) - **Example:** A `Department` aggregates `Professor` — professors can exist without a department. - **KTU Keyword:** *"Whole–part relationship where the part can survive without the whole."* ### 2.1.4 Composition (`———<> `, filled diamond) - **Semantic meaning:** "A *owns-a* B; B's lifetime is bound to A." (strong ownership) - **Example:** A `House` is composed of `Room` objects — when the house is destroyed, rooms are destroyed. - **KTU Keyword:** *"Strong form of aggregation with coincident lifetimes."* ### 2.1.5 Generalization / Inheritance (`———▷ `, hollow triangle) - **Semantic meaning:** "A *is-a-kind-of* B; subclass inherits from superclass." - **Example:** `SavingsAccount` is a generalization of `Account`. - **Direction:** Subclass → Superclass. ### 2.1.6 Realization / Implementation (`- - - ▷ `, dashed hollow triangle) - **Semantic meaning:** "A *realizes the contract* of B." Used between a class and an interface. - **Example:** `CreditCardPayment` realizes the `Payment` interface. ## 2.2 Multiplicity Notation — The Formula Sheet | Notation | Meaning | Example Sentence | |---|---|---| | `1` | Exactly one | A `Person` has **exactly 1** `Head`. | | `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) | A `Person` has **0 or 1** `Passport`. | | `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many | A `Library` lends **0 or more** `Books`. | | `1..*` | At least one | A `Team` has **1 or more** `Players`. | | `n..m` | Range between n and m | A `Page` has **2..10** `Paragraphs`. | | `n` | Exactly n | A `Hand` has exactly **5** `Fingers`. | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb for KTU answers:** When you see a diamond on a relationship, **always annotate the multiplicity on the side opposite to the diamond** (i.e., on the *part* side). This is a common marking point. ## 2.3 Class Notation — The Anatomy of a Class Symbol A class in UML is drawn as a **rectangle divided into three compartments**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ClassName} \quad &\leftarrow \text{Compartment 1: Name (centered, bold)} \\ \text{attribute : Type} \quad &\leftarrow \text{Compartment 2: Attributes} \\ \text{operation(arg : Type) : ReturnType} \quad &\leftarrow \text{Compartment 3: Operations} \end{aligned} $$ A worked textbook example for a `BankAccount` class: | Compartment | Content | |---|---| | **Name** | `BankAccount` | | **Attributes** | `- accountNumber : String`
`- balance : Double`
`+ ownerName : String` | | **Operations** | `+ deposit(amount : Double) : void`
`+ withdraw(amount : Double) : Boolean`
`- validate() : Boolean` | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table | # | Concept | Symbol / Notation | Strength Rank (1 = Weakest) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dependency | Dashed arrow (`- - - >`) | **1** (weakest) | | 2 | Association | Solid line (`———`) | 2 | | 3 | Aggregation | Hollow diamond (`◇`) | 3 | | 4 | Composition | Filled diamond (`◆`) | 4 | | 5 | Realization | Dashed hollow triangle (`- - - ▷`) | 5 | | 6 | Generalization | Solid hollow triangle (`———▷`) | **6** (strongest) | ### 2.5 Aggregate vs Composition — A Boundary Case Students Always Confuse | Test Question | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) | |---|---|---| | Can the part exist before the whole? | **Yes** | **No** | | Can the part be shared by 2+ wholes? | **Yes** | **No** | | Lifetime dependency? | Independent | Coincident | | Example | `Department` — `Professor` | `House` — `Room` | | Strong or weak ownership? | Weak | Strong | | Multiplicity on the whole side | `*` or `1..*` | Exactly `1` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of thumb:** If you can replace the relationship in plain English with the phrase **"part of"**, it is **Composition**. If the natural phrase is **"has-a" or "consists of" but items are independent**, it is **Aggregation**. ## 2.6 Abstract Classes & Interfaces — Distinctions KTU Tests - **Abstract Class** — Cannot be instantiated; may have abstract and concrete operations; written in *italics* in UML. - **Concrete Class** — Can be instantiated. - **Interface** — A pure abstract specification; drawn as a *lollipop* (`⊕`—) or as a class stereotype `<>` with the realization arrow. ## 2.7 Components of a Component Diagram - **Component** — A rectangular box with two small rectangles on the left edge (the “latch” symbol). - **Provided Interface** — Lollipop (`⊕`) emerging from the component. - **Required Interface** — Socket (`⊖`) on the component. - **Port** — A small square on the component boundary for typed interaction. - **Artifact** — A physical file (`.exe`, `.jar`, `.war`); drawn with a `document` icon and `<>` stereotype. ## 2.8 Components of a Deployment Diagram - **Node** — A cube (`⬛`) representing a physical device/server. - **Device Node** — A node capable of executing software artifacts. - **Execution Environment Node** — A software container (JVM, web server, OS). - **Artifact** — The software deployed onto a node. - **Communication Path** — A line between two nodes, stereotyped with the protocol (`<>`, `<>`). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Identifying Relationships from a Problem Statement > **Problem (KTU-style):** *"In a University Information System, a Department offers many Courses. A Course is taught by exactly one Professor and may have many Students enrolled. A Student may enroll in many Courses. Each Department is headed by one Professor who is also a member of the Department. A Professor can be the head of only one Department."* ### Step 1 — Identify Nouns (Candidate Classes) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Nouns found} &= \{\text{Department, University, Course, Professor, Student}\} \\ \text{Select classes} &= \{\text{Department, Course, Professor, Student}\} \end{aligned} $$ *(University is a context container, usually modeled as a node or actor.)* ### Step 2 — Identify Verbs (Candidate Relationships) | Verb in Statement | Relationship Type | |---|---| | "Department **offers** many Courses" | **Association** (1 Department to `*` Courses) | | "Course **is taught by** Professor" | **Association** (`*` Courses to 1 Professor) | | "Student **enrolls in** Course" | **Association** (`*` to `*`) | | "Department **is headed by** Professor" | **Aggregation** (whole–part) | | "Professor **is a kind of**..." | Possibly **Generalization** (only if multiple Professor types exist) | ### Step 3 — Determine Multiplicity Numerically $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Department} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Course} \quad &: \quad 1 \;\to\; 1..* \\ \text{Course} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Professor} \quad &: \quad * \;\to\; 1 \\ \text{Student} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Course} \quad &: \quad * \;\to\; * \\ \text{Department} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Professor (head)} \quad &: \quad 1 \;\to\; 1 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of cardinality:** If the statement says "exactly one", multiplicity is `1`. If it says "many" or "a number of", it is `*`. If "at least one", it is `1..*`. If "at most one", it is `0..1`. ### Step 4 — Decide on Whole-Part vs Peer Association - "Department is **headed by** a Professor" → the Professor still has a separate identity (can exist even without heading) ⇒ **Aggregation (hollow diamond)**, multiplicity `1` on the Professor side. - The same Professor also "**is a member of**" a Department → a separate **Association** between Professor and Department with multiplicity `*` (a professor may be in many departments) and `1..*` (each department has at least one member). ### Step 5 — Final Class Diagram Skeleton The class skeleton that emerges is: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Department} \;\; 1 \; \boxed{\diamond} \;\; 1 \;\; \textbf{Professor (head)} \\ &\textbf{Department} \;\; 1 \; \longrightarrow \; 1..* \;\; \textbf{Course} \\ &\textbf{Course} \;\; * \; \longrightarrow \; 1 \;\; \textbf{Professor (teacher)} \\ &\textbf{Student} \;\; * \; \longrightarrow \; * \;\; \textbf{Course} \\ &\textbf{Department} \;\; 1 \; \longrightarrow \; * \;\; \textbf{Professor (member)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Aggregation vs Composition — How to Decide When the KTU paper gives a borderline case (e.g., `Company` and `Employee`), apply this decision algorithm: $$ \text{Decision}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{Composition} & \text{if } \big(\text{lifetime}(B) \subseteq \text{lifetime}(A)\big) \;\land\; \big(\text{sharing}(B) = \text{false}\big) \\ \text{Aggregation} & \text{if } \big(\text{lifetime}(B) \;\text{independent of}\; A\big) \;\land\; \big(\text{sharing}(B) = \text{true or false}\big) \\ \text{Association} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Worked Example — `Company` and `Employee` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Question:} \quad &\text{Can an Employee exist before a Company?} \\ \text{Answer:} \quad &\text{Yes — a person can be a Job-seeker before joining any company.} \\ \text{Conclusion:} \quad &\text{It is } \mathbf{Aggregation} \text{ (hollow diamond).} \end{aligned} $$ ### Worked Example — `Building` and `Room` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Question:} \quad &\text{Can a Room exist before a Building?} \\ \text{Answer:} \quad &\text{No — rooms are constructed as part of a building.} \\ \text{Conclusion:} \quad &\text{It is } \mathbf{Composition} \text{ (filled diamond).} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Component Diagram Derivation — Worked Example > **Problem:** *"Draw a component diagram for a 3-tier web application with a Web Browser, an Apache Web Server running a WAR file, a Java Application Server running an EJB-JAR, and a Database Server. The Web Server invokes a login service exposed by the App Server."* ### Step 1 — Identify Components $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Components} &= \{\texttt{StaticWebApp.war},\; \texttt{LoginService.jar},\; \texttt{OrderService.jar},\; \texttt{DBDriver.dll}\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Identify Provided and Required Interfaces $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{LoginService.jar} &\;\text{provides}\; \texttt{ILogin} \\ \texttt{OrderService.jar} &\;\text{requires}\; \texttt{ILogin},\; \text{provides}\; \texttt{IOrder} \\ \texttt{StaticWebApp.war} &\;\text{requires}\; \texttt{IOrder} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Wire Components The dependency chain becomes: $$ \texttt{StaticWebApp.war} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{IOrder} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{OrderService.jar} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{ILogin} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{LoginService.jar} $$ ## 3.4 Deployment Diagram Derivation — Worked Example For the same 3-tier system: | Node | Type | Artifact Deployed | |---|---|---| | `ClientPC` | Device | `WebBrowser.exe` | | `WebServer` | Device | `Apache.exe` + `StaticWebApp.war` | | `AppServer` | Execution Environment | `LoginService.jar`, `OrderService.jar` | | `DBServer` | Device | `OracleDB.exe` | Communication paths: $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{ClientPC} \;\overset{\langle\langle HTTP \rangle\rangle}{-\!-\!-\!-\!-\!-\!-}\; \texttt{WebServer} \;\overset{\langle\langle RMI \rangle\rangle}{-\!-\!-\!-\!-\!-\!-}\; \texttt{AppServer} \;\overset{\langle\langle JDBC \rangle\rangle}{-\!-\!-\!-\!-\!-\!-}\; \texttt{DBServer} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Generalization vs Realization — Decision Logic $$ \text{Relationship}(X, Y) = \begin{cases} \text{Generalization} & \text{if } (X \; \text{is-a} \; Y) \;\land\; (X \;\text{and}\; Y \;\text{are both classes}) \\ \text{Realization} & \text{if } (X \; \text{implements the contract of} \; Y) \;\land\; (Y \;\text{is an interface or abstract class}) \end{cases} $$ ### Example $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{SavingsAccount} \;\boxed{\triangleright}\; \texttt{Account} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Generalization (both classes)} \\ \texttt{CreditCardPayment} \;\boxed{-\, -\, \triangleright}\; \texttt{IPayment} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Realization (interface target)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.6 Package Diagram Derivation > **Rule:** A package `B` *depends on* package `A` if any class inside `B` uses any class inside `A`. The dependency arrow is drawn from `B` to `A` and is **dashed**. Example: $$ \texttt{<>} \;\overset{--}{-\!-\!\triangleright}\; \texttt{<>} \;\overset{--}{-\!-\!\triangleright}\; \texttt{<>} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU standard package stereotypes to memorize:** > - `<>` (UI layer) > - `<>` (Domain logic) > - `<>` (Database layer) > - `<>` (MVC controller layer) ## 3.7 Object Diagram — Construction from a Class Diagram > **Rule:** An object diagram is an *instance snapshot* of a class diagram. The class name becomes `instanceName : ClassName` and attributes get concrete values, with all underscores indicating underlined object names. ### Example $$ \begin{aligned} &\texttt{rahul : Student} \quad \text{— name = "Rahul", rollNo = 47} \\ &\texttt{amrutha : Student} \quad \text{— name = "Amrutha", rollNo = 12} \\ &\texttt{drK : Professor} \quad \text{— name = "Dr. K", empId = 9001} \end{aligned} $$ A link is drawn from `rahul` to `cs301 : Course` to indicate that Rahul is currently enrolled in CS301. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class Diagram — University Information System (Mermaid) > *This Mermaid diagram is rendered as a Class Diagram and shows the full taxonomy of relationships KTU commonly tests.* ```mermaid classDiagram direction LR class Department { - deptId : String - deptName : String + addCourse(c : Course) : void + listCourses() : List } class Course { - courseId : String - courseName : String - credits : int + assignProfessor(p : Professor) : void + enrollStudent(s : Student) : void } class Professor { - empId : String - name : String - specialization : String + teach(c : Course) : void } class Student { - rollNo : int - name : String - semester : int + register(c : Course) : void } class Person { <> - name : String - dob : Date + getDetails() : String } Department "1" o-- "1" Professor : headedBy Department "1" o-- "1..*" Professor : hasMember Department "1" --> "1..*" Course : offers Course "1..*" --> "1" Professor : taughtBy Student "1..*" --> "1..*" Course : enrollsIn Person <|-- Professor Person <|-- Student ``` > [!NOTE] > In Mermaid, `o--` denotes aggregation (hollow diamond) and `*--` denotes composition (filled diamond). Generalization is `<|--`. ## 4.2 Component Diagram — 3-Tier Web Application (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef component fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#202124 classDef artifact fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#202124 classDef iface fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#202124 browser[Web Browser SWF]:::component war[StaticWebApp WAR]:::artifact login[Login Service EJB JAR]:::artifact order[Order Service EJB JAR]:::artifact dbdrv[DB Driver DLL]:::artifact ilogin((ILogin Interface)):::iface iorder((IOrder Interface)):::iface browser -- HTTP --> war war -- requires --> iorder iorder -- provides --> order order -- requires --> ilogin ilogin -- provides --> login order -- uses --> dbdrv ``` ## 4.3 Deployment Diagram — Cloud-Hosted Application (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef device fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#202124 classDef ee fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#202124 classDef art fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#202124 subgraph clientZone[Client Zone] clientPC[Client Laptop]:::device end subgraph dmz[DMZ Zone] webSrv[Apache Web Server]:::device end subgraph appZone[Application Zone] appEE[Tomcat JVM Container]:::ee war1[Orders WAR]:::art war2[Auth WAR]:::art end subgraph dbZone[Data Zone] dbSrv[PostgreSQL Server]:::device end clientPC -- HTTPS --> webSrv webSrv -- HTTP --> appEE appEE --- war1 appEE --- war2 appEE -- JDBC --> dbSrv ``` ## 4.4 Package Diagram — Layered Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef layer fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#202124 pres[<> UI Layer]:::layer biz[<> Domain Layer]:::layer persist[<> Data Layer]:::layer common[<> Common Layer]:::layer pres -.depends.-> biz biz -.depends.-> persist biz -.depends.-> common persist -.depends.-> common ``` ## 4.5 Object Diagram Snapshot — Library System (Mermaid) ```mermaid classDiagram direction LR class rahul { rollNo = 47 name = "Rahul K" semester = 5 } class amrutha { rollNo = 12 name = "Amrutha S" semester = 3 } class cs301 { courseId = "CS301" courseName = "Software Engg" credits = 4 } class drK { empId = 9001 name = "Dr. K" specialization = "SE" } rahul --> cs301 : enrolled amrutha --> cs301 : enrolled cs301 --> drK : taughtBy ``` ## 4.6 Composite Structure Diagram — Inside a `Car` (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef part fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C,color:#202124 car((Car Assembly)):::part engine[Engine Part]:::part wheel[Wheel Part]:::part batt[Battery Part]:::part car --- engine car --- wheel car --- batt engine -- drives --> wheel engine -- poweredBy --> batt ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1, 3 Marks]** > **Q1.** *Differentiate between aggregation and composition with a suitable example.* **Model Answer:** Aggregation and composition are both forms of *whole–part* relationships in UML structural modeling, but they differ in the strength of ownership and the lifetime dependency between the whole and the part. - **Aggregation** (represented by a *hollow* diamond) is a **weak** whole–part relationship. The part can exist *independently* of the whole. *Example:* A `Department` aggregates `Professor` objects. A professor can exist (as an unemployed researcher, or transfer to another department) without the original department. - **Composition** (represented by a *filled* diamond) is a **strong** whole–part relationship. The part's lifetime is **coincident** with the whole's lifetime; when the whole is destroyed, the parts are destroyed too. *Example:* A `House` is composed of `Room` objects; destroying the house destroys its rooms. > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2, 3 Marks]** > **Q2.** *List the four UML visibility modifiers with their symbols and meaning.* **Model Answer:** | Symbol | Visibility | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `+` | **Public** | Accessible to all classes in any package. | | `-` | **Private** | Accessible only within the defining class. | | `#` | **Protected** | Accessible to subclasses and within the same package. | | `~` | **Package** | Accessible only within the same package (default in Java-like scope). | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > When answering the long questions, students frequently: > 1. Forget the **multiplicity annotations** on at least one end of the relationship (−1 mark). > 2. Mix up **aggregation** (hollow ◇) and **composition** (filled ◆) — examiners look at the diamond's fill, not just the name (−1 mark). > 3. Use a *dashed* arrow for generalization (incorrect — generalization is a *solid* line with hollow triangle) (−1 mark). > 4. Omit the **attributes and operations compartments** in the class symbols (lose 2 marks). > 5. Confuse **node** (deployment diagram cube) with **component** (rectangle with latch). (−1 mark). --- ### Question Choice A — Class Diagram with Relationships (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]** **Q (a).** *For an Online Shopping System, identify the major classes and draw a complete UML class diagram showing the relationships: aggregation, composition, and generalization. Include multiplicities and at least 3 attributes + 2 operations per class.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1 — Identify Classes.** From the noun analysis: `Customer`, `Order`, `Product`, `Cart`, `Payment`, `UserAccount`, `Address`. **Step 2 — Identify Attributes and Operations.** | Class | Attributes | Operations | |---|---|---| | `Customer` | `- custId : int`, `- name : String`, `- email : String` | `+ register() : void`, `+ placeOrder(o : Order) : void` | | `Order` | `- orderId : int`, `- orderDate : Date`, `- total : double` | `+ addItem(p : Product, qty : int) : void`, `+ calcTotal() : double` | | `Product` | `- productId : int`, `- name : String`, `- price : double` | `+ applyDiscount(d : double) : void`, `+ getPrice() : double` | | `Cart` | `- cartId : int`, `- createdOn : Date` | `+ addProduct(p : Product) : void`, `+ checkout() : Order` | | `Payment` | `- paymentId : int`, `- amount : double`, `- status : String` | `+ process() : boolean`, `+ refund() : void` | | `Address` | `- street : String`, `- city : String`, `- pin : String` | `+ validate() : boolean` | **Step 3 — Identify Relationships with Multiplicity.** $$ \begin{aligned} &\texttt{Customer} \;\; 1 \;\; \boxed{\triangleright} \;\; \texttt{UserAccount} && \text{(Generalization — Customer IS-A UserAccount? No, use composition)} \\ &\texttt{Customer} \;\; 1 \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; 1..* \;\; \texttt{Order} && \text{(Association — places orders)} \\ &\texttt{Order} \;\; 1 \;\; \blacklozenge \;\; 1..* \;\; \texttt{Product} && \text{(Composition — order owns line-items)} \\ &\texttt{Customer} \;\; 1 \;\; \lozenge \;\; 1 \;\; \texttt{Cart} && \text{(Aggregation — cart is owned but reusable)} \\ &\texttt{Order} \;\; 1 \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; 1 \;\; \texttt{Payment} && \text{(Association — paid by one payment)} \\ &\texttt{Customer} \;\; 1 \;\; \blacklozenge \;\; 1..* \;\; \texttt{Address} && \text{(Composition — addresses belong to customer)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Render the Class Diagram** (using the Mermaid classDiagram format from Section 4). ```mermaid classDiagram direction LR class Customer { - custId : int - name : String - email : String + register() : void + placeOrder(o : Order) : void } class Order { - orderId : int - orderDate : Date - total : double + addItem(p : Product, qty : int) : void + calcTotal() : double } class Product { - productId : int - name : String - price : double + applyDiscount(d : double) : void + getPrice() : double } class Cart { - cartId : int - createdOn : Date + addProduct(p : Product) : void + checkout() : Order } class Payment { - paymentId : int - amount : double - status : String + process() : boolean + refund() : void } class Address { - street : String - city : String - pin : String + validate() : boolean } Customer "1" --> "1..*" Order : places Order "1" *-- "1..*" Product : contains Customer "1" o-- "1" Cart : has Order "1" --> "1" Payment : paidBy Customer "1" *-- "1..*" Address : owns ``` **Q (b).** *Explain the difference between generalization, realization, and dependency. Give one example of each in the context of the above diagram.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** - **Generalization** is a *taxonomic* "is-a" relationship between a more specific class (subclass) and a more general class (superclass). The subclass **inherits** the public and protected features of the superclass and may override operations. *Example:* In our diagram, if we introduce `PremiumCustomer` and `GuestCustomer` as subclasses of `Customer`, then `PremiumCustomer IS-A Customer` is a generalization. The arrow is a **solid line with a hollow triangle** pointing from subclass to superclass. - **Realization** is the relationship between a *class* and an *interface* (or abstract class) whose contract the class implements. The class promises to provide implementations of every operation declared by the interface. *Example:* If `Order` implements the `Billable` interface that declares a `getInvoice() : Invoice` operation, then `Order — — ▷ Billable` is a realization. The arrow is a **dashed line with a hollow triangle**. - **Dependency** is the **weakest** of all UML relationships. It indicates that a change in the target element may require a change in the source element, but there is no structural link at run time. *Example:* The `Payment` class may depend on a `CurrencyConverter` utility class to convert the order total into the customer's preferred currency before charging. The arrow is a **dashed line with an open (vee) arrowhead**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Strength Ranking:} \quad & \text{Dependency} < \text{Association} < \text{Aggregation} < \text{Composition} < \text{Realization} < \text{Generalization} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Question Choice B — Component & Deployment Diagrams (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]** **Q (a).** *Explain the purpose of a Component Diagram in UML. Draw a component diagram for a Hospital Management System that includes the modules: Patient Registration, Appointment Scheduling, Billing, and a shared Authentication Service. Clearly mark provided and required interfaces.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Purpose of a Component Diagram:** A Component Diagram models the **physical software modules** of a system, the **interfaces** they expose or consume, and the **dependencies** between them. It is a step *closer to implementation* than a class diagram, because each component usually maps to one or more source files, libraries, JARs, DLLs, or executables. Component diagrams help in: - Identifying **reusable** software units. - Specifying **interfaces** for contract-first development. - Managing **build** and **release** artifacts. **Component Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef comp fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#202124 classDef iface fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#202124 reg[Patient Registration Component]:::comp appt[Appointment Scheduling Component]:::comp bill[Billing Component]:::comp auth[Authentication Service Component]:::comp iAuth((IAuthenticate)):::iface iAppt((IAppointment)):::iface iBill((IBilling)):::iface iReg((IRegistration)):::iface auth -- provides --> iAuth reg -- requires --> iAuth appt -- requires --> iAuth bill -- requires --> iAuth reg -- provides --> iReg appt -- requires --> iReg appt -- provides --> iAppt bill -- requires --> iAppt bill -- provides --> iBill ``` **Q (b).** *Draw and explain a Deployment Diagram for a 3-tier online examination system with the following nodes: Student Browser, Web Server (Apache), Application Server (JBoss), and Database Server (MySQL). Use appropriate stereotypes and communication paths.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A deployment diagram shows the **physical architecture** of a system — the hardware devices, the execution environments (containers), and the artifacts installed on them, together with the communication paths between them. **Deployment Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef device fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#202124 classDef ee fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#202124 classDef art fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#202124 subgraph client[Client Tier] br[Student Browser]:::device end subgraph web[Web Tier] ws[Apache HTTP Server]:::device html[Exam Portal WAR]:::art end subgraph app[Application Tier] jvm[<> JBoss JVM]:::ee ejbA[Question Bank EJB]:::art ejbB[Result Calc EJB]:::art end subgraph db[Data Tier] dbs[MySQL DB Server]:::device end br -- HTTPS --> ws ws --> html ws -- HTTP --> jvm jvm --- ejbA jvm --- ejbB ejbB -- JDBC --> dbs ``` **Explanation of Nodes and Paths:** | Node / Path | Stereotype | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Student Browser | `<>` | End-user device running the browser. | | Apache HTTP Server | `<>` | Hardware server hosting Apache. | | JBoss JVM | `<>` | Software container inside the App Server. | | Question Bank EJB | `<>` | Deployed JAR file containing the business logic. | | HTTPS, HTTP, JDBC | Protocol stereotypes | Communication paths with their protocol annotated. | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall in Component / Deployment Diagrams:** > Do **NOT** show *classes* inside a deployment diagram. Deployment diagrams show *artifacts* and *nodes* only. Classes belong in a class or composite structure diagram. Many students lose 2 marks for this confusion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize before the exam:** - **6 structural diagrams:** Class, Object, Component, Deployment, Composite Structure, Package. (Profile diagram is auxiliary.) - **6 relationship types ranked weak → strong:** Dependency → Association → Aggregation → Composition → Realization → Generalization. - **Diamond fill rule:** Hollow ◇ = Aggregation (weak); Filled ◆ = Composition (strong, coincident lifetime). - **Arrowhead rule:** Hollow triangle = Generalization (solid) or Realization (dashed). Vee-arrow = Dependency (dashed). - **Visibility symbols:** `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package. - **Class has 3 compartments:** Name (bold/centered), Attributes, Operations. - **Abstract class** is written in *italics*; **interface** uses `<>` stereotype or lollipop notation. - **Component** = rectangle with two small rectangles on the left edge. **Node** = 3D cube. **Artifact** = document icon. - **Deployment diagram nodes** are typed as `<>` or `<>`. **Communication paths** are typed as `<>` (HTTP, JDBC, RMI, etc.). - **Object diagram** = instance snapshot of class diagram; object names are underlined and prefixed with `instanceName : ClassName`. - **Package diagram** uses dashed dependency arrows; standard stereotypes are `<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>`. - **Multiplicity locations:** Always placed at the *target* end of the arrow. For aggregation/composition, on the *part* side, not the *whole* side. - **Use cases for Class diagrams:** To design domain model, derive database schema, generate skeleton code. - **Use cases for Component diagrams:** To plan build artifacts, identify reuse opportunities, plan release units. - **Use cases for Deployment diagrams:** To plan hardware procurement, network topology, security zones. - **Use cases for Package diagrams:** To plan team ownership, manage import cycles, enforce layered architecture. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Mnemonic:** > *"**D**ependency knows nothing, **A**ssociation knows structure, **A**ggregation **holds loosely**, **C**omposition **binds for life**, **R**ealization **promises a contract**, **G**eneralization **passes on the genes**."* > The bold letters spell **D-A-A-C-R-G** — order from weakest to strongest.

topics.title: Behavioral Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Behavioral Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioral Modeling** is a structured analysis technique in software engineering that depicts the **dynamic behavior** of a system — i.e., *how the system responds to events, how its state evolves over time, and how processes interact with one another* — as opposed to *structural* modeling which captures the static components.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCITT503 – Software Engineering, Module 3), behavioral modeling is treated as a **classical analysis-modeling discipline** that bridges the *requirements phase* and the *design phase* by translating user requirements into **executable visual abstractions**.

The principal behavioral artifacts covered in the syllabus are:

| # | Behavioral Artifact | Primary Purpose |
|---|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** | Models the *flow of information* between processes, external entities, and data stores. |
| 2 | **State Transition Diagram (STD)** | Captures the *state-dependent behavior* of a system/object in response to events. |
| 3 | **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** | Represents *programmatic control flow* (sequence of statements, branches, loops) for testing. |
| 4 | **Process Specification (PSPEC)** | Describes *what a low-level process in a DFD does* in narrative/mini-spec form. |
| 5 | **Control Specification (CSPEC)** | Specifies *when processes are activated* (event-driven control). |
| 6 | **Data Dictionary** | Central repository defining *every data element, file, and data flow* used in the model. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Route Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a railway network.
> - **Stations** = *Processes* (activities the system performs).
> - **Trains moving between stations** = *Data flows* (information in transit).
> - **Tracks connecting stations** = *External interfaces*.
> - **A train waiting at a signal** = *A system in a particular state*.
> - **The timetable** = *Behavioral model* — it tells you **when** each train moves, **what** it carries, and **how** the whole network behaves dynamically.

Just as a railway timetable captures *movement* (behavior) and not just the geography (structure), behavioral modeling captures the *dynamic, temporal, event-driven behavior* of a software system — something structural diagrams (like ER diagrams or class diagrams) cannot do alone.

---

## 1.3 Why Behavioral Modeling is Essential

> [!TIP]
> - **Requirements clarity:** Stakeholders understand *what happens* under different conditions.
> - **Test case design:** CFGs and STDs directly drive *white-box* and *state-based* test cases.
> - **Reduced ambiguity:** Eliminates *“what does the system do when…?”* confusion.
> - **Foundation for design:** Acts as the *bridge between analysis and design*.

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Mapping with Behavioral Modeling |
|-------|---------------|----------------------------------|
| **CO3** | *Apply software engineering modeling techniques to represent functional and behavioral aspects of a system.* | Directly aligned — behavioral modeling *is* this CO. |
| **CO4** | *Analyze software requirements using structured analysis tools.* | DFDs, STDs, PSPECs. |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) — The Core Behavioral Artifact

### 2.1.1 What is a DFD?

A **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** is a graphical representation that depicts the *flow of data* through an information system. It models the *processes, external entities, data stores, and data flows* — collectively called the **PEDS model**.

### 2.1.2 The Four DFD Symbols (Yourdon / Gane-Sarson Notation)

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Example |
|--------|------|---------|---------|
| 🔵 **Circle (or rounded rectangle)** | **Process** | Transforms inputs into outputs. | `Calculate Salary` |
| ▭ **Rectangle** | **External Entity** | Source or sink of data (outside the system boundary). | `Customer`, `Bank` |
| ▭ **Open rectangle (or two parallel lines)** | **Data Store** | Repository of data (file, table, DB). | `D1 – Employee Records` |
| ➡️ **Arrow** | **Data Flow** | Movement of data between elements. | `Order Details` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Rule:** DFDs follow a strict **hierarchy** (Context → Level 0 → Level 1 → ...). Each subprocess must balance with its parent (the **Levelling/Partitioning** rule).

### 2.1.3 Levels of DFD

| Level | Name | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **Level 0** | **Context Diagram** | Entire system as a single bubble; shows external entities & I/O. |
| **Level 1** | **System Overview** | Breaks the context diagram into 3–7 major processes. |
| **Level 2+** | **Detailed Subprocesses** | Each Level-1 process is further refined. |
| | **Primitive DFD** | Lowest level — no further decomposition needed. |

### 2.1.4 Rules for Constructing a DFD (KTU Board Examiner's Hot List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **No two processes can be connected without an intermediate data flow.**
> 2. **Every process must have at least one input and one output.**
> 3. **Data stores cannot connect directly to external entities** (must pass through a process).
> 4. **Maintain consistency of data flow labels across all levels** (Balancing).
> 5. **Number processes hierarchically**: `1.0`, `1.1`, `1.1.1`, etc.
> 6. **Data flows are labelled with noun-phrases** (data names), *not verbs*.

---

## 2.2 State Transition Diagram (STD)

### 2.2.1 Definition

A **State Transition Diagram (STD)** is a directed graph in which:
- **Nodes** = system *states*.
- **Edges** = *transitions* triggered by *events*, possibly guarded by *conditions*, and resulting in *actions*.

$$STD = \{S, E, T, A\}$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of states
- $E$ = set of events
- $T$ = transition function $T: S \times E \rightarrow S$
- $A$ = actions (optional, associated with transitions)

### 2.2.2 Components

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| **State** | Rounded rectangle | A condition or situation during the system's life. |
| **Initial State** | Filled black circle | Where the system starts. |
| **Final State** | Bullseye (concentric circles) | Termination point. |
| **Transition** | Labeled arrow | `Event [Guard] / Action` |

### 2.2.3 Event-Action-guard Format

$$State_{current} \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{Event [Guard] / Action}\;\;} State_{next}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - **Event:** The trigger (e.g., "button pressed").
> - **Guard (Condition):** A Boolean expression that must be true for the transition to fire.
> - **Action:** The activity performed when transition occurs (e.g., "open valve").

---

## 2.3 Control Flow Graph (CFG)

### 2.3.1 Definition

A **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is a directed graph $G = (N, E_{cfg})$ where:
- $N$ = nodes representing *statements or basic blocks*.
- $E_{cfg}$ = edges representing *control flow* between statements.

**Basic Block:** A maximal sequence of statements with no internal branches.

### 2.3.2 Cyclomatic Complexity — The Key Formula (Exam Favorite!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity (CC)** is a software metric that quantifies the *number of linearly independent paths* through a program's source code. Proposed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)**.

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $V(G)$ = Cyclomatic complexity
- $E$ = Number of edges in the CFG
- $N$ = Number of nodes in the CFG
- $P$ = Number of connected components (typically $P = 1$)

For a **single connected component** ($P = 1$):

$$V(G) = E - N + 2$$

Alternative formula (using **predicate nodes** $P_n$ — nodes with out-degree $\geq 2$):

$$V(G) = P_n + 1$$

Yet another form (using **regions** $R$ of the planar graph):

$$V(G) = R$$

### 2.3.3 Interpretation Table (High-Yield)

| Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$ | Risk Evaluation | Test Effort |
|-----------------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **1 – 10** | Simple, low risk | Minimal |
| **11 – 20** | Moderate, testable | Medium |
| **21 – 50** | Complex, high risk | Extensive |
| **> 50** | Untestable, refactor required | Re-architect |

---

## 2.4 Process Specification (PSPEC) & Control Specification (CSPEC)

| Artifact | Purpose | Notation |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **PSPEC** | Describes the *what* of a low-level process in a DFD. | Narrative, pre/post-conditions, decision tables, mathematical formulas. |
| **CSPEC** | Describes the *when* — when processes activate. | State transition diagram + activation table. |

### 2.4.1 PSPEC Components

> [!NOTE]
> A good PSPEC contains:
> 1. **Process name & ID**
> 2. **Input data flows**
> 3. **Output data flows**
> 4. **Pre-conditions** (what must be true *before*)
> 5. **Post-conditions** (what will be true *after*)
> 6. **Logic** (narrative / decision table / equation)

---

## 2.5 Data Dictionary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Data Dictionary** is the *central repository* of all data definitions in the system. It is often called the *"metadata of metadata"*.

### 2.5.1 Notation Conventions

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `=` | is composed of | `Customer = Name + Address + Phone` |
| `+` | and (concatenation) | `Name = FirstName + LastName` |
| `[]` | optional | `MiddleName [optional]` |
| `{}` | iteration (0 or more) | `{Order Item}` |
| `()` | alternative (OR) | `Status = (Active \| Inactive)` |
| `**` | comment | `** SSN format: 9 digits **` |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table — these are the most-tested formulas/definitions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Exam Frequency |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Cyclomatic Complexity (edges-nodes) | $V(G) = E - N + 2$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Cyclomatic Complexity (predicate) | $V(G) = P_n + 1$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Cyclomatic Complexity (regions) | $V(G) = R$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Transition Function (STD) | $T: S \times E \rightarrow S$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | DFD balancing | Inflows at parent = Inflows at child | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | PSPEC pre-condition | Boolean assertion before execution | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Data Dictionary `{}` | Iteration (zero or more) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | CSPEC activation | Event-based process trigger | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | DFD level numbering | 1.0, 1.1, 1.1.1, ... | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Control Flow Graph edge | Directed flow between blocks | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Behavioral modeling is *not* academic. It is used in:
> - **Embedded systems** (aircraft, automotive) — state diagrams model mode transitions.
> - **Banking software** — DFDs trace transaction flows for audit.
> - **Telecommunications** — STD models call states (idle, dialing, ringing, connected).
> - **Compiler design** — CFGs drive code optimization & static analysis.
> - **Safety-critical systems** (medical, nuclear) — STDs are *mandatory* for certification (e.g., DO-178C).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Cyclomatic Complexity Derivation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** Given the following pseudocode, draw the CFG and compute the cyclomatic complexity using all three methods.

```text
1: START
2: int a, b, sum
3: READ a, b
4: IF (a > 0) THEN
5:     sum = a + b
6: ELSE
7:     sum = a - b
8: END IF
9: IF (sum > 10) THEN
10:    PRINT "Large"
11: ELSE
12:    PRINT "Small"
13: END IF
14: END
```

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify Basic Blocks

| Block | Statements | Reason |
|-------|------------|--------|
| $B_1$ | 1, 2, 3 | Sequential entry block |
| $B_2$ | 5 | True branch of first IF |
| $B_3$ | 7 | False branch of first IF |
| $B_4$ | 10 | True branch of second IF |
| $B_5$ | 12 | False branch of second IF |
| $B_6$ | 14 | Exit block |

Total nodes: $N = 6$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Identify Edges (Control Flow)

| # | From | To | Reason |
|---|------|-----|--------|
| 1 | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | First IF condition is TRUE |
| 2 | $B_1$ | $B_3$ | First IF condition is FALSE |
| 3 | $B_2$ | $B_4$ | Convergence after first IF |
| 4 | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | Convergence after first IF |
| 5 | $B_4$ | $B_5$ | Second IF condition is FALSE |
| 6 | $B_4$ | $B_6$ | Second IF condition is TRUE (if PRINT is the last step) |
| 7 | $B_5$ | $B_6$ | Convergence after second IF |

Total edges: $E = 7$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Apply Formula $V(G) = E - N + 2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2 \\
&= 7 - 6 + 2 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** There are **3 linearly independent paths** through the program.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Verify Using $V(G) = P_n + 1$

Predicate nodes (nodes with out-degree $\geq 2$): $B_1$ and $B_4$.

$$
P_n = 2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P_n + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= 3 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Verify Using $V(G) = R$ (Regions)

Regions in the CFG (including the *outer* infinite region): **3**.

$$
V(G) = 3 \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** All three methods **must** yield the same value. If they don't, the CFG drawing is wrong.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Behavioral Model

| Scenario / Question | Best Behavioral Model | Justification |
|---------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| *"Trace how customer order data moves through the billing system."* | **DFD** | Tracks data flow, not state. |
| *"Model how a microwave transitions from Idle → Cooking → Done."* | **STD** | Pure state-dependent behavior. |
| *"How many test paths are needed to cover all branches?"* | **CFG + Cyclomatic Complexity** | Quantitative test metric. |
| *"Describe what the 'Validate Order' process actually does."* | **PSPEC** | Low-level process logic. |
| *"When should the 'Send SMS Alert' process be triggered?"* | **CSPEC** | Control/event-based activation. |
| *"Define all data elements used in the model."* | **Data Dictionary** | Central metadata repo. |
| *"Sequence of method calls between objects."* | **Sequence Diagram (UML)** | Object interaction over time. |
| *"Parallel/branching workflows with synchronization."* | **Activity Diagram (UML)** | Workflow modeling. |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of a DFD — Worked Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Scenario:** A library's book-issuing process. Members request books, the librarian checks availability, the book is issued, and a record is updated.

### Step 1 — Identify External Entities

- `Member`
- `Library Staff`

### Step 2 — Identify Processes (Context → Level 1)

| Level 0 Process | Description |
|-----------------|-------------|
| `0 — Library Book Issue System` | Whole system |

| Level 1 Process | Description |
|-----------------|-------------|
| `1.0 — Receive Request` | Capture member's book request |
| `2.0 — Check Availability` | Verify book exists & is not already issued |
| `3.0 — Issue Book` | Update database & generate receipt |

### Step 3 — Identify Data Stores

- `D1 — Book Catalog`
- `D2 — Issue Records`

### Step 4 — Identify Data Flows

- `Book Request` (Member → 1.0)
- `Request Details` (1.0 → 2.0)
- `Availability Status` (2.0 → 1.0)
- `Book Issue Command` (1.0 → 3.0)
- `Issue Confirmation` (3.0 → Member)
- `Book Record` (2.0 → D1)
- `Issue Entry` (3.0 → D2)

### Step 5 — Verify DFD Balancing

> [!NOTE]
> The inflows/outflows at Level 0 **must match** the aggregated inflows/outflows at Level 1. This is the **Levelling Consistency** rule.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Behavioral Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Requirements Phase] --> A2[Functional Modeling]
    A1 --> A3[Behavioral Modeling]
    A1 --> A4[Data Modeling]

    A3 --> B1[DFD]
    A3 --> B2[State Transition Diagram]
    A3 --> B3[Control Flow Graph]
    A3 --> B4[PSPEC and CSPEC]
    A3 --> B5[Data Dictionary]

    B1 --> C1[Context Level]
    B1 --> C2[Level 1]
    B1 --> C3[Level 2 and beyond]
    C1 --> D1[External Entities and I O]
    C2 --> D2[Subprocesses]
    C3 --> D3[Primitive DFDs]

    B2 --> E1[States]
    B2 --> E2[Events]
    B2 --> E3[Transitions]
    B2 --> E4[Guards and Actions]

    B3 --> F1[Basic Blocks]
    B3 --> F2[Edges]
    B3 --> F3[Cyclomatic Complexity V G]

    B4 --> G1[Process Logic]
    B4 --> G2[Control Activation Table]

    B5 --> H1[Data Element Definitions]
    B5 --> H2[File Definitions]
    B5 --> H3[Data Flow Definitions]

    F3 --> I1[Test Case Design]
    F3 --> I2[Path Coverage]
    F3 --> I3[Risk Assessment]
```

---

## 4.2 DFD Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Identify External Entities] --> stepB[Step 2 Identify Top Level Process]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Identify Data Stores]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Identify Data Flows]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Draw Context Diagram Level 0]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Decompose to Level 1]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Verify Balancing]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Create Data Dictionary]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9 Write PSPEC for Primitives]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10 Define CSPEC for Control]
```

---

## 4.3 STD Example — ATM Transaction

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> idle
    idle --> cardInserted: card inserted
    cardInserted --> pinEntry: prompt for pin
    pinEntry --> authenticated: pin correct
    pinEntry --> blocked: three wrong attempts
    pinEntry --> cardInserted: try again
    authenticated --> menu: show options
    menu --> withdraw: choose withdraw
    menu --> deposit: choose deposit
    menu --> balance: choose balance
    menu --> idle: eject card
    withdraw --> dispensing: dispense cash
    dispensing --> receipt: print receipt
    receipt --> idle: return card
    deposit --> idle: return card
    balance --> idle: return card
    blocked --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Mermaid labels:** All node labels above are deliberately raw, unformatted text (no `**bold**` or `*italics*`) so the diagram renders without parse errors.

---

## 4.4 CFG Example — IF–ELSE Construct

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start Read a b] --> node2{a greater than 0}
    node2 -- True --> node3[sum equals a plus b]
    node2 -- False --> node4[sum equals a minus b]
    node3 --> node5{sum greater than 10}
    node4 --> node5
    node5 -- True --> node6[Print Large]
    node5 -- False --> node7[Print Small]
    node6 --> node8[End]
    node7 --> node8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node count $N = 6$**, **Edge count $E = 7$**, therefore:
> $V(G) = 7 - 6 + 2 = 3$ — three independent paths.

---

## 4.5 PSPEC Template Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Process ID and Name] --> p2[Input Data Flows]
    p2 --> p3[Output Data Flows]
    p3 --> p4[Pre Conditions]
    p4 --> p5[Logic Description]
    p5 --> p6[Post Conditions]
    p6 --> p7[Reference to Data Dictionary]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) and a State Transition Diagram (STD).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DFD | STD |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Purpose** | Models the *flow of data* between processes. | Models the *state changes* of a system in response to events. |
| **Symbols** | Process, Entity, Data Store, Data Flow. | States, Transitions, Events, Guards, Actions. |
| **Time dimension** | Implicit; shows *what flows where*. | Explicit; shows *when* transitions occur. |
| **Application** | Suitable for *transaction processing* systems. | Suitable for *real-time, control* systems. |
| **Notation** | Yourdon / Gane-Sarson. | Harel / UML State Machines. |

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark answers** should be **comparison tables or 3–4 crisp bullet pairs** — not paragraphs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Data Dictionary? Mention any four notations used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Dictionary** is a centralized repository that stores definitions of all data elements, files, and data flows used in a DFD.

**Four notations:**

1. `=` → *is composed of* (e.g., `Customer = Name + Address + Phone`)
2. `+` → *and / concatenation* (e.g., `Name = FirstName + LastName`)
3. `[]` → *optional* (e.g., `MiddleName [optional]`)
4. `{}` → *iteration / repetition* (e.g., `{Order Item}`)
5. `()` → *alternative / OR* (e.g., `Type = (Savings \mid Current)`)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing `[]` for *iteration* and `{}` for *optional* — it is the **opposite**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** with neat diagrams. Illustrate the construction of a **Level-0 (Context Diagram)** and a **Level-1 DFD** for a *“College Admission System”*. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **Process Specification (PSPEC)** and **Control Specification (CSPEC)** with examples. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Definition:** A Data Flow Diagram (DFD) is a graphical tool that represents the flow of data through a system, depicting processes, external entities, data stores, and data flows.

**Context Diagram (Level 0) for College Admission System:**

- The entire system is represented as a **single process bubble** labelled `0 — College Admission System`.
- **External Entities:** `Applicant`, `Admission Office`, `University Database`.
- **Data Flows:** `Application Form`, `Admission Status`, `Merit List`.

**Level 1 DFD** decomposes the system into:

| Process | Name | Function |
|---------|------|----------|
| `1.0` | Receive Application | Collect & validate application form |
| `2.0` | Process Merit List | Rank applicants by score |
| `3.0` | Allocate Seat | Match rank with seat availability |
| `4.0` | Generate Admission Letter | Issue letter to selected candidates |

**Data Stores:** `D1 — Application Database`, `D2 — Student Records`.

**Data Flows:** `Validated Application`, `Rank List`, `Allocation Result`, `Admission Letter`.

**Rules followed:**

- Every process has at least one input and one output.
- Data stores do not directly connect to external entities.
- All flows are labeled with data names (nouns).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **7-mark answers** must include **both a Context and a Level-1 DFD diagram** + **explanation of every element** + **rules of DFD construction**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Process Specification (PSPEC):**

PSPEC is a *narrative* description of what a **low-level (primitive) DFD process** does. It answers the question *"How is input transformed into output?"*

**Components of a PSPEC:**

1. Process ID and name
2. Input data flows
3. Output data flows
4. **Pre-conditions** (must be true before execution)
5. **Post-conditions** (will be true after execution)
6. **Logic** — narrative, decision table, or mathematical formula

**Example (Process 2.0 — Compute Merit Score):**

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Process ID | 2.0 |
| Inputs | `Application Details` |
| Outputs | `Merit Score` |
| Pre-condition | Application must be validated. |
| Logic | `MeritScore = 0.6 * EntranceScore + 0.4 * BoardMarks` |
| Post-condition | A non-negative numeric score exists. |

**Control Specification (CSPEC):**

CSPEC specifies **when** processes are activated — i.e., the *control flow* governing process execution.

**Two parts of CSPEC:**

1. **State Transition Diagram** — shows system states (e.g., `Open`, `Closed`, `Processing`).
2. **Activation Table** — maps `(State, Event) → Activated Process`.

**Example Activation Table:**

| State | Event | Activated Process |
|-------|-------|-------------------|
| Open | Application Received | 1.0 (Receive Application) |
| Processing | Validation Done | 2.0 (Compute Merit) |
| Processing | Merit Computed | 3.0 (Allocate Seat) |

> [!TIP]
> **CSPEC vs PSPEC:** *PSPEC = What happens?* **|** *CSPEC = When does it happen?*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of a **State Transition Diagram (STD)**. Discuss the role of **events, guards, and actions** in an STD. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Construct a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** for the given code snippet and compute its **Cyclomatic Complexity** using all three methods: $E - N + 2$, $P_n + 1$, and regions. **[7 Marks]**
>
> ```text
> 1: int n, fact = 1, i = 1
> 2: READ n
> 3: WHILE (i <= n) DO
> 4:     fact = fact * i
> 5:     i = i + 1
> 6: END WHILE
> 7: PRINT fact
> 8: END
> ```

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Definition:** A State Transition Diagram (STD) is a behavioral model that represents the various *states* a system can occupy and the *events* that cause transitions between those states.

**Components of an STD:**

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **State** | A condition of the system at a point in time. | `Logged Out`, `Logged In` |
| **Initial State** | The starting state. | Filled black circle |
| **Final State** | Termination of the system's lifecycle. | Bullseye (concentric circles) |
| **Transition** | Movement between states. | Arrow with label |
| **Event** | The trigger causing transition. | `User clicks login` |
| **Guard** | Boolean condition that must be true. | `[password is correct]` |
| **Action** | Side-effect of the transition. | `/ open dashboard` |

**Example — Login State Diagram:**

States: `LoggedOut`, `AwaitingPassword`, `LoggedIn`, `Locked`

Transitions:

$$
LoggedOut \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{enterCredentials}\;\;} AwaitingPassword
$$

$$
AwaitingPassword \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{submit [valid] / authenticate}\;\;} LoggedIn
$$

$$
AwaitingPassword \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{submit [invalid] / reject}\;\;} LoggedOut
$$

$$
LoggedIn \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{logout}\;\;} LoggedOut
$$

$$
LoggedIn \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{3 fails}\;\;} Locked
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 7 marks:** Provide the diagram + definitions of all 4 STD elements (event, guard, action, transition) + one fully labeled example.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Identify Basic Blocks:**

| Block | Statements | Reason |
|-------|------------|--------|
| $B_1$ | 1, 2 | Initialization & input |
| $B_2$ | 4, 5 | Loop body (predicate at entry) |
| $B_3$ | 7 | Output |
| $B_4$ | 8 | Termination |

Total nodes: $N = 4$

**Step 2 — Identify Edges:**

| # | From | To |
|---|------|-----|
| 1 | $B_1$ | $B_2$ |
| 2 | $B_2$ | $B_2$ | (Loop back) |
| 3 | $B_2$ | $B_3$ | (Loop exit) |
| 4 | $B_3$ | $B_4$ |

Total edges: $E = 4$

**Step 3 — Cyclomatic Complexity (Method 1: $V(G) = E - N + 2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2 \\
&= 4 - 4 + 2 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Method 2: $V(G) = P_n + 1$**

Predicate nodes (out-degree $\geq 2$): only $B_2$ (the WHILE predicate).

$$
P_n = 1
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P_n + 1 \\
&= 1 + 1 \\
&= 2 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Method 3: $V(G) = R$ (Regions)**

Regions in the CFG: 1 inside the loop + 1 outside = **2**.

$$
V(G) = 2 \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> All three methods give $V(G) = 2$, meaning there are **2 independent paths**:
> 1. The loop is never entered ($n \leq 0$).
> 2. The loop is entered and executed at least once ($n \geq 1$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Behavioral Modeling Questions:**
>
> 1. **DFD:** Drawing **flowcharts instead of DFDs** — DFDs do **not** show control flow or loops. A loop in a DFD is a *data store*.
> 2. **STD:** Confusing *events* with *actions* — events **trigger** transitions; actions are **effects** of transitions.
> 3. **CFG / Cyclomatic Complexity:** Forgetting the **+2** in the $E - N + 2$ formula (most common mistake). Forgetting to count the **loop-back edge** separately.
> 4. **Data Dictionary:** Mixing up `{}` and `[]` notations.
> 5. **P-spec / C-spec:** Writing **CSPEC logic** inside a PSPEC (or vice versa).
> 6. **Balancing:** Not verifying that **inflows/outflows balance** between DFD levels.
> 7. **Numbering:** Using non-hierarchical process IDs (e.g., jumping from `1.0` to `3.0` without `2.0`).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Behavioral Modeling**

- **Behavioral modeling** = *dynamic*, *event-driven*, *time-dependent* view of the system (as opposed to *structural* view).
- **DFD Symbols:** Process (circle/rounded), External Entity (rectangle), Data Store (open rectangle / two parallel lines), Data Flow (named arrow).
- **DFD Levels:** Context (Level 0) → Level 1 → Level 2 → ... → Primitive. **Levelling must be balanced.**
- **DFD Rules:** No process with only inputs/only outputs; data stores ≠ external entities; data flows labelled with *nouns*.
- **STD Elements:** State, Initial state, Final state, Transition, Event, Guard `[ ]`, Action `/`.
- **STD Notation:** $State_{current} \xrightarrow{\;\;\text{Event [Guard] / Action}\;\;} State_{next}$
- **CFG:** Nodes = basic blocks; Edges = control flow.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity:**
  - $V(G) = E - N + 2$
  - $V(G) = P_n + 1$
  - $V(G) = R$
  - Risk thresholds: 1–10 simple, 11–20 moderate, 21–50 complex, > 50 untestable.
- **PSPEC** = *What* a process does (logic, pre/post-conditions).
- **CSPEC** = *When* a process is activated (event + state).
- **Data Dictionary** = central metadata repository — uses `=`, `+`, `[ ]`, `{ }`, `( )`, `**` symbols.
- **PEDS** = **P**rocess + **E**ntity + **D**ata Store + **D**ata Flow (the 4 DFD elements).
- **McCabe (1976)** proposed Cyclomatic Complexity.
- **DFD ≠ Flowchart:** DFDs are *data-centric*; Flowcharts are *control-centric*.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Design Process** in Software Engineering is a systematic, iterative, and structured sequence of activities that transforms the *requirements specification* (what the system must do) into a *detailed implementation blueprint* (how the system will be built). As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the design process is a multi-stage engineering discipline that bridges the gap between software analysis and construction, ensuring that the resulting architecture is **modular**, **maintainable**, **scalable**, and **verifiable** against the original requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Design is the technical kernel of software engineering — it is the activity that establishes the system architecture, the data structure abstractions, the component interfaces, and the algorithmic decomposition that collectively form a complete engineering model satisfying the defined requirements.*

The design process is **not** coding. It is a *thinking* and *modeling* activity. It produces a hierarchy of representations — moving from *high-level architectural models* down to *component-level blueprints* — each adding increasing detail (a concept called **stepwise refinement**).

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU syllabus, "Design Process" is mapped to **CO3 (Course Outcome 3)**: *Apply software engineering principles and design patterns to create software architecture and detailed design for moderately complex systems.* Expect direct 14-mark questions on design concepts, architectural styles, and component design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

### The "House Blueprint" Analogy

Imagine you want to build your dream house. You wouldn't hand a carpenter a one-line description like *"Make me a house with 3 rooms"* and expect him to start hammering wood. Instead, you would:

1. **Sketch the overall layout** (where the kitchen is, where bedrooms sit) — this is **Architectural Design**.
2. **Detail the wiring and plumbing** — this is **Interface Design**.
3. **Specify the exact door, window, beam dimensions** — this is **Component-Level Design**.
4. **Decide on a particular style** (colonial, modern, Kerala-Nalukettu) — this is **Design Pattern / Architectural Style Selection**.

The *Design Process* in software engineering is **exactly this workflow**, but applied to a software system. The *requirements document* plays the role of the customer's wishes, and the *design model* plays the role of the architect's blueprint.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** Think of the design process as the **"A-I-C-D"** ladder:
> - **A**rchitectural Design (big picture / modules)
> - **I**nterface Design (how modules talk to each other)
> - **C**omponent-Level Design (internals of each module)
> - **D**ata Structure & Algorithm Design (the data + logic of each module)

### The "Pressure Cooker" View

Another useful way to internalize design: think of requirements as the **ingredients** (rice, water, salt) and design as the **recipe + cooker design** (proportions, heat settings, pressure limits). Implementation is the actual cooking. You cannot cook well without the recipe, no matter how good your ingredients are.

---

## 1.3 Why Design is the Heart of Software Engineering

- **Cost of fixing a defect multiplies ~10x at each later stage.** Fixing a design error during testing is **10x** costlier than fixing it during design itself.
- **Design is the only artifact that lets multiple developers work in parallel** without stepping on each other.
- It enables **reuse** — well-designed components can be harvested for future systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metric (Remember for KTU viva):** A typical industry study shows that **design phase consumes ~15% of total project effort** but **prevents ~70% of downstream defects**. This is a high-yield KTU viva question.

---

## 1.4 Position of Design in the Software Life Cycle

In the **Waterfall model**, design sits between *Software Requirements Analysis* and *Coding*. In **Agile / Iterative models**, design is a *continuous activity* refined at the end of every iteration (Sprint). KTU expects you to mention both, with an emphasis on the **Waterfall** representation for board exams.

The two classical design layers recognized by KTU are:
1. **Architectural (Preliminary) Design** — high-level structure.
2. **Detailed Design** — internals of every component.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phases of the Design Process — A Stepwise Logical Breakdown

The KTU 2024 scheme structures the design process into **four primary phases**, executed (in practice) with significant feedback loops.

### Phase 1: Establishing the Architectural Framework

The very first design activity is to **establish the overall system architecture**. This answers the question: *"What are the major structural units of the system, and how do they interact?"*

Key decisions made at this phase:
- Choice of **architectural style** (e.g., layered, client-server, microservices, pipe-and-filter).
- Identification of **subsystems** and their **responsibilities**.
- Definition of **top-level data flow** between subsystems.
- Allocation of **functional requirements** to subsystems (a process called *architectural mapping*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Term — "Subsystem" vs "Module":** A *subsystem* is a self-contained system-within-a-system exposing a well-defined interface. A *module* is the implementation unit (often a file or a class) within a subsystem. Many students lose marks in board exams by using these interchangeably.

---

### Phase 2: Identifying and Describing Software Components

Once architecture is set, the next logical step is to **decompose each subsystem into software components**. A *component* is a modular, deployable, replaceable unit that encapsulates a coherent set of functions.

Criteria used to recognize a component:
- **Single Responsibility** — does one thing well.
- **Well-defined interface** — exposes a contract.
- **Loose coupling** — minimal dependencies on other components.
- **High cohesion** — internal parts belong together.
- **Reusable** across the system (and ideally across projects).

> [!TIP]
