> **Cohesion vs Coupling — The KTU Favourite:** This is the single most tested sub-topic in design. *Cohesion = how strongly the internals of a module stick together (high cohesion is GOOD). Coupling = how strongly a module depends on others (low coupling is GOOD).* You should remember all **7 types of cohesion** and **5 types of coupling** (covered later).

---

### Phase 3: Designing Component Interfaces

An *interface* is the **contract** between a component and the outside world. It hides *how* a component does its work and only exposes *what* it does. Designing good interfaces is one of the hardest design skills.

The three golden rules of interface design (Pressman, classic KTU reference):
1. The interface should **not reveal anything** about the component's internal implementation (encapsulation).
2. The interface should be **designed from the user's perspective first** (the caller's needs), not the implementer's convenience.
3. The interface should be **small and orthogonal** — each operation should do one thing, and the operations should be independent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Information Hiding" Principle (Parnas, 1972):** Modules should hide *design decisions* that are likely to change. If a decision is hidden behind a stable interface, future changes are localized. KTU repeatedly asks: *"What is the benefit of information hiding?"* — Answer: it **reduces the propagation of side-effects** when a design decision changes.

---

### Phase 4: Iterating and Refining the Design

Design is **never a single-pass activity**. After the first draft, the design is reviewed, defects are identified, the architecture is adjusted, and the process repeats. This is called **design iteration** or **stepwise refinement**.

Wirth's classic law:
> *"Programs are written first for human readers, and only incidentally for machines to execute."* — Niklaus Wirth

This means the *quality* of a design is judged by how easily another engineer (or "you" 6 months later) can read, understand, and modify it.

---

## 2.2 Core Design Concepts — The KTU Terminology Sheet

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following design concepts. You should be able to **define each in one crisp sentence** and **recognize it in a given scenario**.

| # | Concept | One-Line Definition (Board-Answer Style) | Exam Importance |
|---|---------|------------------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Abstraction** | Hiding background details and emphasizing essential features at a particular level of detail. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 2 | **Architecture** | The overall structure of the system — its components, their properties, and their relationships. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 3 | **Patterns** | A proven solution template to a recurring design problem in a given context. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 4 | **Modularity** | The decomposition of a system into a set of cohesive, loosely-coupled modules. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 5 | **Information Hiding** | Each module hides its internal design decisions behind a stable interface. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 6 | **Functional Independence** | Achieved through high cohesion + low coupling. | ⭐⭐ High |
| 7 | **Refinement / Stepwise Refinement** | Successively elaborating high-level abstractions into detailed designs. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 8 | **Reuse** | Designing components so they can be reused in multiple systems. | ⭐⭐ High |
| 9 | **Refactoring** | Restructuring existing design without changing external behavior to improve quality. | ⭐⭐ High |
| 10 | **Design Classes** | Classes identified during design that refine analysis classes with implementation details. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 11 | **Cohesion** | The "glue" within a module — how closely its internal tasks are related. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 12 | **Coupling** | The "tether" between modules — the strength of their inter-connection. | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> The table uses `–` (en-dash) and `+` symbols only. **No `|` characters** appear inside any cell, so the markdown table renders cleanly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — The Design Process

| Quantity / Metric | Formula / Expression | What It Measures | Units / Range | Best-Case Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Module Count (Upper Bound from Decomposition)** | $M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$ | Total number of modules produced by decomposing $n$ subsystems. | Integer $\geq 1$ | Depends on project |
| **Fan-In of a Module** | $\text{FanIn}(M) = \vert\{N : N \text{ calls } M\}\vert$ | Number of other modules that *call into* this module. | Non-negative integer | High fan-in is **good** (reusability) |
| **Fan-Out of a Module** | $\text{FanOut}(M) = \vert\{N : M \text{ calls } N\}\vert$ | Number of other modules this module *calls out to*. | Non-negative integer | Low fan-out is **good** (low coupling) |
| **Software Science (Halstead) — Program Length** | $N = N_1 + N_2$ | Total number of tokens (operands $N_1$ + operators $N_2$). | Integer | — |
| **Halstead — Vocabulary** | $\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2$ | Number of *distinct* operators + operands used. | Integer | — |
| **Halstead — Program Volume** | $V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta)$ | Information content of the program in bits. | bits | — |
| **Halstead — Difficulty** | $D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}$ | Estimated difficulty of implementation. | Dimensionless $\geq 0$ | Lower is better |
| **Halstead — Effort** | $E = D \cdot V$ | Estimated mental effort to implement. | Unitless (in bit-comparisons) | Lower is better |
| **Function Point (FP) — Adjusted** | $\text{FP}_{\text{adj}} = \text{UFP} \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$ | Adjusted function points after complexity + environmental tuning. | Function Points | — |
| **Maintainability Index (MI)** | $\text{MI} = 171 - 5.2 \cdot \ln(V) - 0.23 \cdot \text{CC} - 16.2 \cdot \ln(\text{LOC}) + 50 \cdot \sin\!\left(\sqrt{2.4 \cdot \text{CM}}\right)$ | Heuristic score (0–100) of how easy the code is to maintain. | 0 $\leq$ MI $\leq$ 100 | MI $\geq 85$ is excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation safety:** Absolute values use `\vert` (e.g., `$\vert x \vert$`) instead of the bare pipe symbol so the markdown table structure remains intact.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fan-In / Fan-Out** is used in code review tools (e.g., SonarQube) to flag suspicious modules.
- **Halstead Metrics** feed the *Maintainability Index*, which Microsoft, HP, and many enterprises use in CI pipelines to gate releases.
- **Function Points** are the *lingua franca* of IT outsourcing contracts worldwide — billing is often done in **FTE-days per FP**.

---

## 2.4 The Design Process Flow — A Bird's-Eye View

The design process is best understood as a **closed feedback loop**, not a waterfall.

- Start from **Requirements** (what + why).
- Move into **Architectural Design** (the macro structure).
- Drill down to **Component Design** (the micro structure).
- Then **Interface Design** (the contracts between pieces).
- The whole thing is continually **reviewed** and **refined**.

This is the philosophy you must communicate in any 14-mark KTU question.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Halstead's "Program Volume" Formula Emerges

The Halstead Software Science (1977) defines five basic quantities:

- $\eta_1$ = number of *distinct* operators in the program.
- $\eta_2$ = number of *distinct* operands in the program.
- $N_1$ = total count of operator occurrences.
- $N_2$ = total count of operand occurrences.
- $\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2$ (program vocabulary).
- $N = N_1 + N_2$ (program length).

The fundamental **information-theoretic** observation is this: each token in the program is one selection from a vocabulary of size $\eta$. A truly random program of length $N$ over that vocabulary has information content (in bits):

$$
V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta)
$$

Halstead called this quantity the **Volume** of the program — a measure of the *information content* required to encode it. The derivation steps are:

**Step 1 — Count tokens.** Every token in the source code is either an operator or an operand, so:

$$
N = N_1 + N_2
$$

**Step 2 — Count distinct tokens.** The number of unique symbols used is:

$$
\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Shannon / Hartley information formula.** For a choice from $\eta$ equally-likely symbols, each choice carries $\log_2(\eta)$ bits. For $N$ independent choices, the total information is:

$$
V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** $V$ is measured in **bits**, and physically represents the *minimum number of mental comparisons* a programmer must perform to read and understand the code. A *smaller* $V$ means the program is *simpler*.

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation assumes **uniformly distributed** token usage, which is only an approximation — real code has non-uniform symbol frequencies. Even so, $V$ correlates strongly with code complexity in practice.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The "Difficulty" and "Effort" Formulas

**Step 1 — Start from the intuition.** A program is *difficult* to write when (a) it uses many distinct operators and (b) it reuses operands heavily (each operand use is a "context switch" in the developer's mind).

**Step 2 — Propose the formula.** Halstead defined:

$$
D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}
$$

The first factor $\frac{\eta_1}{2}$ represents the *average* number of operands per operator in a binary operator–operand pair. The second factor $\frac{N_2}{\eta_2}$ is the *average* number of times each unique operand is reused.

**Step 3 — Compute Effort.** Effort $E$ is the product of *difficulty* and *volume*:

$$
E = D \cdot V
$$

**Step 4 — Physical meaning.** $E$ has units of *elementary mental discriminations* — a Halstead-defined unit roughly corresponding to a single bit-comparison. Larger $E$ implies a more error-prone implementation.

**Step 5 — Time estimate.** Halstead further derived the *estimated implementation time*:

$$
T_{\text{est}} = \frac{E}{18} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

The constant $18$ comes from empirical measurements of *Stroud's number* — the rate at which the human mind performs elementary mental operations (≈ 18 per second).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU, you do **not** need to derive $D$ or $E$ on paper, but you **must** be able to *write* the formulas and *interpret* them. A typical board question: *"Given a small C program with $\eta_1=5$, $\eta_2=8$, $N_1=15$, $N_2=20$, compute $V$ and $E$."* — that kind of substitution problem is a guaranteed 3-marker.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing Halstead Metrics for a Code Snippet

**Given code (pseudocode):**
```
a = b + c
d = a - b
```

**Step 1 — Identify operators.** The operators are `=`, `=`, `+`, `-` (note: `=` appears twice, but it is the *same* operator symbol). Distinct operators: `=`, `+`, `-`.

So $\eta_1 = 3$ and $N_1 = 4$ (total operator occurrences).

**Step 2 — Identify operands.** The operands are `a`, `b`, `c`, `a`, `b`, `d` — that is, `a` twice, `b` twice, `c` once, `d` once.

Distinct operands: `a`, `b`, `c`, `d` — so $\eta_2 = 4$.
Total operand occurrences: $N_2 = 6$.

**Step 3 — Compute vocabulary.**

$$
\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2 = 3 + 4 = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Compute program length.**

$$
N = N_1 + N_2 = 4 + 6 = 10
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Volume.**

$$
V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta) = 10 \cdot \log_2(7)
$$

Since $\log_2(7) \approx 2.807$:

$$
V \approx 10 \times 2.807 = 28.07 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Difficulty.**

$$
D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \times 1.5 = 2.25
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Effort.**

$$
E = D \cdot V = 2.25 \times 28.07 \approx 63.16
$$

**Step 8 — Estimated implementation time.**

$$
T_{\text{est}} = \frac{E}{18} \approx 3.51 \text{ seconds}
$$

This number, while small, is internally consistent for a 2-line snippet. For real systems, $E$ can be in the *tens of millions*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cohesion and Coupling

KTU loves to ask: *"Rank the following types of cohesion from worst to best"* and *"What is the difference between data coupling and stamp coupling?"* Memorize the tables below.

### 3.4.1 Types of Cohesion (Worst → Best)

| # | Type | Meaning | Example | Strength |
|---|------|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Elements grouped by accident; no meaningful relationship. | A utility class with random print, sort, and login functions. | ⭐ (worst) |
| 2 | **Logical** | Elements grouped because they are logically similar (but not functionally). | A module that handles *all* input — keyboard, mouse, network. | ⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Elements grouped because they are executed at the same time. | An `init()` function that opens files, allocates memory, resets counters. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Elements grouped because they must be executed in a fixed order. | A module that validates a form and then sends a confirmation email. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Communicational / Informational** | Elements operate on the same data structure. | A module that reads a record, validates it, and updates it. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one element is input to the next. | A pipeline: parse → tokenize → build AST. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | **Functional** | Every element contributes to a *single, well-defined* function. | A function that computes the area of a circle. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (best) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** *Logical* cohesion is **better than coincidental** but **worse than temporal**. The word "logical" in the name does NOT mean "logically good" — it means "logically categorized"!

### 3.4.2 Types of Coupling (Best → Worst)

| # | Type | Meaning | Example | Strength |
|---|------|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Data** | Modules share data through parameters (only values passed). | `int add(int a, int b)`. | ⭐ (best) |
| 2 | **Stamp** | A *whole data structure* is passed, but only a part is used. | Passing a `Customer` object to a function that uses only the name. | ⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Control** | One module controls the flow of another via a flag. | A sort function receiving a "sort ascending" boolean. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **External** | Modules share an externally imposed format, protocol, or device interface. | Two modules both writing to the same CSV file format. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Common / Global** | Modules share global data. | Two functions reading and writing the same global variable. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (worst) |
| 6 | **Content** | One module modifies the *internals* of another. | Module A directly reads Module B's private variables. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (worst) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for descending coupling (worst → best):** **C**ontent, **C**ommon, **E**xternal, **C**ontrol, **S**tamp, **D**ata → "**CCECSD**" or read backwards "Data Stamp Control External Common Content".

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Coupling & Cohesion in a Real Scenario

**Scenario:** Consider a module `PayrollProcessor` that:
1. Reads employee records from a global `employeeDatabase` object.
2. Computes salary using a tax-rule flag passed as a parameter.
3. Modifies a private variable of `Employee` class directly via friend access.

**Analysis:**

- **Coupling = Common (Global):** because the module shares the global `employeeDatabase`.
- **Coupling = Control:** because the `tax-rule` flag controls internal flow.
- **Cohesion:** The module is doing I/O + computation + persistence → it has **low cohesion** (probably *logical* or *temporal* at best).

**Recommendation:** Refactor `PayrollProcessor` into three modules: `EmployeeReader`, `SalaryCalculator`, `PayrollWriter`. This *increases cohesion* and *reduces coupling*.

This is the kind of critical-thinking KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

The arrow from `revG` back to `archB` is the *iterative feedback loop* — if a design review reveals defects, the design is sent back for revision. The forward arrow to `implH` indicates that only *approved* designs proceed to implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

> [!NOTE]
> This is Wirth's classic **stepwise refinement** model. At each level, the design becomes *less abstract* and *more detailed*. A 14-mark KTU question on "design process" almost always expects you to mention this hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** This is an example of the **Layered Architectural Style**. Notice how *dependencies* flow *downwards only* (UI → Business → Data → Database). There is **no upward dependency**. This single constraint is what keeps the system *loosely coupled* and *highly maintainable*. KTU board questions on "architectural styles" expect you to draw similar layered diagrams and label the dependency direction.

    impl1 --> iface3[Interface IInventoryService]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design pattern revealed:** This is the **Adapter / Dependency Inversion** principle in action. The clients depend on the *interface*, not the *implementation*. You can swap `StripePaymentAdapter` for `RazorpayPaymentAdapter` without touching `OrderServiceImpl` — a hallmark of *functional independence*.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and tests *Recall / Understanding* (Bloom Level 1–2). Model answers are precise and concise — KTU board examiners expect a **definition + one real-world example + one line on importance**.

---

### Question A1: Define the term *Abstraction* in software design. List the three levels of abstraction commonly used. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

**Abstraction** is the process of *identifying the essential characteristics* of an entity (object, function, system) and *suppressing irrelevant details*. In software design, abstraction allows the designer to focus on the "what" rather than the "what-it-is-made-of" at each level of detail.

The **three levels of abstraction** commonly used (from low to high detail):

1. **Data Abstraction** — the collection of data objects that represent the entity (e.g., a `Customer` class with attributes `name`, `email`).
2. **Procedural Abstraction** — a named sequence of instructions (a function) with a well-defined purpose (e.g., `calculateTax(income)`).
3. **Control Abstraction** — a mechanism that controls the program's control flow (e.g., a `while` loop, a `try-catch` block).

**Importance:** Abstraction is the *first step* in any design process; it lets the designer manage complexity by handling one level of detail at a time.

---

### Question A2: Differentiate between **coupling** and **cohesion** with one example each. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

| Aspect | Cohesion | Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| **Defines** | Strength of *internal* relationship within a module. | Strength of *inter-module* dependency. |
| **Goal** | **Maximize** it. | **Minimize** it. |
| **Analogy** | A well-organized toolbox where every tool belongs. | A tangled wire connecting two devices. |
| **Example (Good)** | A `computeArea()` function that only does area calculation. | Two modules exchanging data through parameters (data coupling). |
| **Example (Bad)** | A `misc` class with random utility methods (coincidental cohesion). | Two modules sharing a global variable (common coupling). |

**Co-relation rule of thumb (write this in board exam):** A well-designed module has **high cohesion** and **low coupling**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE are typically **14-markers** with **internal choice** — meaning the paper has two questions from this module, and the student answers **one**. The pattern is: sub-part (a) for 7 marks + sub-part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks): The Design Process and Concepts

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **phases of the software design process** in detail. How does design fit between requirements analysis and coding? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the following design concepts with one-line definitions and one example each: **Abstraction, Architecture, Modularity, Information Hiding, Refinement, Functional Independence.** **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B-A (Part a)

The software design process is a structured sequence of activities that transforms a *requirements specification* (the "what") into a *set of design artifacts* (the "how"). It sits *between* requirements analysis and coding because:

- **Requirements analysis** produces a logical model — *what* the system should do, independent of *how* it will be built.
- **Design** translates this logical model into a *physical* model — it decides the architecture, components, data structures, and algorithms.
- **Coding** is then the direct *implementation* of the design model in a programming language.

The **phases** are:

1. **Architectural (Preliminary) Design** — identifies *subsystems*, defines their *responsibilities*, and establishes the *data flow* between them. The choice of architectural style (layered, client-server, microservices) is made here.
2. **Component (Detailed) Design** — each subsystem is *decomposed* into modules. Each module's *functionality*, *interfaces*, and *data structures* are specified.
3. **Interface Design** — for every module, a clean *public interface* is defined that hides internal details and obeys the *information-hiding* principle.
4. **Data Structure and Algorithm Design** — appropriate *data structures* (lists, trees, hash tables) and *algorithms* (sort, search, traversal) are selected for each module.
5. **Design Review and Verification** — the design is checked for *correctness*, *completeness*, *consistency* with requirements, and adherence to design principles. Issues feed back to earlier phases, making the process *iterative*.

The output of this phase is a *Design Specification Document (DSD)* that serves as the contract for the coding team.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Marks Lost Here):** Many students write design as a *single linear* step. Board examiners expect the word **"iterative"** or **"feedback loop"** to appear in your answer. Failing to mention the *Design Review* phase will cost you 1–2 marks.

---

### Model Solution for Question B-A (Part b)

| # | Concept | One-Line Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Abstraction** | Hiding background details and focusing on essential features. | A `Car` class with `start()` method; the ignition internals are hidden. |
| 2 | **Architecture** | The overall structure of the system — its components and relationships. | The 3-tier (Presentation, Business, Data) layered architecture. |
| 3 | **Modularity** | Decomposition of a system into cohesive, loosely-coupled modules. | Splitting a monolithic billing app into `Invoice`, `Payment`, `Receipt` modules. |
| 4 | **Information Hiding** | Modules hide internal design decisions behind a stable interface. | `private` variables in a Java class, accessed only through getters/setters. |
| 5 | **Refinement (Stepwise Refinement)** | Successive elaboration of high-level designs into lower-level details. | High-level "Compute Salary" → Detailed "Apply Tax Brackets" → "Round to 2 decimals". |
| 6 | **Functional Independence** | A module that does one thing well (high cohesion) and depends little on others (low coupling). | A pure utility function like `sqrt(x)` — no side effects, no external calls. |

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks): Cohesion, Coupling & Design Quality

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** List and briefly explain the **seven types of cohesion** (from worst to best). For each, state whether it is *acceptable in a good design*. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** List the **six types of coupling** (from best to worst). For a real banking module that takes a `Customer` object and only reads the `accountNumber` field, identify the type of coupling. Suggest one refactoring to improve it. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B-B (Part a)

The **seven types of cohesion** ranked from *worst* to *best*:

1. **Coincidental (Worst):** Elements grouped by accident. Example: a "Utilities" class with random print, sort, and database functions. **Not acceptable.**
2. **Logical:** Elements grouped by logical category. Example: a single "I/O" module handling all input devices. **Barely acceptable.**
3. **Temporal:** Elements grouped by execution time. Example: an `init()` function that opens files, allocates memory, resets counters together. **Acceptable for small systems only.**
4. **Procedural:** Elements grouped by execution order. Example: validate form → send confirmation email. **Acceptable.**
5. **Communicational / Informational:** Elements operate on the same input data. Example: read, validate, and update a record. **Acceptable.**
6. **Sequential:** Output of one element is input to the next. Example: parse → tokenize → build AST. **Good.**
7. **Functional (Best):** Every element contributes to one single function. Example: `computeCircleArea(radius)`. **Highly desirable.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** The examiner will *not* ask you to memorize all 7 — they usually give you a scenario and ask *"Identify the type of cohesion"*. Practice with at least 3 scenarios before the exam.

---

### Model Solution for Question B-B (Part b)

The **six types of coupling** ranked from *best* to *worst*:

1. **Data Coupling (Best):** Modules share simple data via parameters.
2. **Stamp Coupling:** A whole data structure is passed, but only a part is used.
3. **Control Coupling:** One module passes a control flag to alter another's logic.
4. **External Coupling:** Modules share an externally imposed format (e.g., a file format).
5. **Common / Global Coupling:** Modules share global data.
6. **Content Coupling (Worst):** One module modifies the *internals* of another.

**Analysis of the banking scenario:**

> *"A real banking module that takes a `Customer` object and only reads the `accountNumber` field."*

This is **Stamp Coupling** — a whole `Customer` object is passed, but only one of its many fields is actually used. The module has a *redundant dependency* on the rest of the `Customer` structure.

**Suggested Refactoring:**

Change the module's signature to accept only what it needs:

```
old: processTransaction(Customer c)
new: processTransaction(String accountNumber, double amount)
```

By passing only the `accountNumber` (a primitive type), the coupling is reduced to **Data Coupling** — the best possible form. As a bonus, the module is now *more testable* and *more reusable* (it can be called from contexts where there is no `Customer` object).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When a question gives a real-world code example, **do not just name the coupling type**. Always (i) **justify** your identification with 1–2 lines and (ii) **suggest a refactoring**. Failing to give a refactoring will lose you 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Design-Process Questions**
> 1. **Conflating "Module" and "Subsystem"** — Subsystem = system-within-a-system exposing a clear contract; Module = a single file or class. They are *not* the same.
> 2. **Forgetting the word "iterative"** — Design is *not* a single-pass activity. Always say "the design process is iterative with feedback loops".
> 3. **Skipping the design review phase** — Every design-process answer must mention *Design Review / Verification* as a final step.
> 4. **Confusing cohesion and coupling ranking** — Cohesion ranks *worst→best* as Coincidental→Functional; Coupling ranks *best→worst* as Data→Content. Don't reverse the direction!
> 5. **Writing only definitions, no examples** — A definition without an example is *half-credit* in KTU valuation. Always pair the concept with a one-line scenario.
> 6. **Using ambiguous words like "high-level design"** — Use the exact KTU term: **"Architectural Design"**. Generic terms cost marks.
> 7. **Ignoring stepwise refinement** — Wirth's *stepwise refinement* is a frequent 2–3 mark sub-question. Be ready to draw the L1→L2→L3→L4 hierarchy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Design Process**

- ✅ **Design Process Definition:** Systematic transformation of *requirements* into a *detailed implementation blueprint* via architectural, component, interface, and data-structure design.
- ✅ **Phases:** Architectural → Component → Interface → Data Structure / Algorithm → Design Review (with **iteration** at every step).
- ✅ **Three Levels of Abstraction:** Data, Procedural, Control.
- ✅ **Stepwise Refinement (Wirth):** L1 (Subsystems) → L2 (Modules) → L3 (Data Structures) → L4 (Algorithms).
- ✅ **7 Types of Cohesion (worst → best):** Coincidental, Logical, Temporal, Procedural, Communicational, Sequential, Functional.
- ✅ **6 Types of Coupling (best → worst):** Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common, Content.
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** High Cohesion + Low Coupling = Functional Independence.
- ✅ **Information Hiding (Parnas):** Hide design decisions likely to change behind stable interfaces. Benefit: **localizes future changes**.
- ✅ **Halstead Metrics:**
  - Vocabulary: $\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2$
  - Length: $N = N_1 + N_2$
  - Volume: $V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta)$
  - Difficulty: $D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}$
  - Effort: $E = D \cdot V$
- ✅ **Fan-In:** Number of modules calling *into* this one → **high is good** (reusability).
- ✅ **Fan-Out:** Number of modules this one calls *out to* → **low is good** (low coupling).
- ✅ **Design Patterns:** Proven, reusable solutions to recurring problems in a given context. (Full coverage in Module 5 of KTU.)
- ✅ **Design Review:** A mandatory step that validates the design against requirements and quality attributes.
- ✅ **Architectural Styles (one-line memory):** Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, Pipe-and-Filter, Repository, Event-Driven.
- ✅ **Maintainability Index formula:**
  - $\text{MI} = 171 - 5.2 \cdot \ln(V) - 0.23 \cdot \text{CC} - 16.2 \cdot \ln(\text{LOC}) + 50 \cdot \sin\!\left(\sqrt{2.4 \cdot \text{CM}}\right)$
  - Score range: $0 \leq \text{MI} \leq 100$ (≥ 85 = excellent, < 65 = poor).
- ✅ **Key Exam Phrases to Use:** "**iterative**", "**stepwise refinement**", "**functional independence**", "**information hiding**", "**design review**".
- ✅ **Memory Aid for Design Process Phases:** **"A-C-I-D-R"** → **A**rchitecture, **C**omponent, **I**nterface, **D**ata/Algorithm, **R**eview.

> **End of Module 4 — Design Process Notes.** You are now ready for any 3-mark or 14-mark question from this module. Good luck with your KTU ESE preparation! 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Concepts: Cohesion and Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Concepts: Cohesion and Coupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cohesion — The "Internal Unity" of a Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Cohesion** is a qualitative measure of the degree to which the elements (functions, data, internal logic) **inside a single module** belong together and contribute to a single, well-defined purpose. It is an *intra-module* attribute.

In the structured design framework formalized by **Stevens, Myers, and Constantine (1974)**, cohesion is the *glue* that holds the internal parts of a module together. A module with **high cohesion** focuses on **one single task or responsibility**, while a module with **low cohesion** performs several unrelated or weakly related tasks.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Specialist vs. The Jack of All Trades"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine two people on a project team:
> - **Person A** is a specialist *Cardiologist*. Every tool, every report, every line of work they handle is about the heart. → **High Cohesion**
> - **Person B** is a "junk-drawer" who handles cardiology, neurology, plumbing, and accountancy at random. → **Low Cohesion**
>
> When something goes wrong in the heart department, who is easier to contact, train, reuse, and test? **Person A.** The same logic applies to software modules.

### The Seven Degrees of Cohesion (Ordered: Worst → Best)

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to identify, rank, and justify the **7 recognized levels** of cohesion. We will detail them in SECTION 2, but here is the canonical order:

1. **Coincidental** (worst)
2. **Logical**
3. **Temporal**
4. **Procedural**
5. **Communicational**
6. **Sequential**
7. **Functional** (best)

---

## 1.2 Coupling — The "Inter-Module Dependency"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Coupling** is a qualitative measure of the degree of **interdependence between two or more modules** — i.e., how much one module knows about, or relies upon, the internal workings of another module. It is an *inter-module* attribute.

In the same Constantine-Myers framework, **low coupling** is a hallmark of good modular design because it enables **independent development, testing, replacement, and reuse**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Two Independent Colleagues"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> - **Person X** and **Person Y** communicate only through a fixed, simple *report* (a clean data sheet). X never asks Y *how* Y did the work — only for the result. → **Low (Data) Coupling**
> - **Person X** must wait for Y to finish *every* internal step, asks Y to *change* Y's internal logic for X's benefit, and even reaches into Y's desk to grab files. → **High (Content/Common) Coupling**
>
> The less each colleague needs to know about the other's *internals*, the more robust the partnership.

### The Seven Levels of Coupling (Ordered: Worst → Best)

1. **Content** (worst)
2. **Common**
3. **External**
4. **Control**
5. **Stamp** (also called *Data Structure* coupling)
6. **Data**
7. **Message** (best)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic (Ascending order of quality):** *"Calmly Organized Computers Eliminate Confusing Software Design Mistakes"* → **C**ontent → **C**ommon → **E**xternal → **C**ontrol → **S**tamp → **D**ata → **M**essage.

---

## 1.3 The Golden Design Rule of KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board's First Question is Always:**
> *"A good software design has ___ cohesion and ___ coupling."*
> **Answer:** **HIGH** cohesion and **LOW** coupling.
>
> This is the **single most-tested** sentence on this topic across the KTU 2019, 2022, and 2024 scheme question papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Types of Cohesion (Worst → Best)

| # | Type | Definition | Example / Indicator | Verdict |
|---|------|------------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Elements are grouped arbitrarily, just because they happen to be in the same source file. No meaningful relationship. | A "Utility" module containing date formatting, matrix multiplication, network ping, and file deletion — all unrelated. | **Worst** — avoid at all costs. |
| 2 | **Logical** | Elements perform **similar logical functions** and one of them is **selected at runtime** via a control flag. | `performOperation(int opCode)` that handles ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV based on the flag. | Poor — caller must know all options. |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Elements are grouped because they are executed at the **same point in time** (e.g., during startup or shutdown). | An `initializeSystem()` module that opens DB, opens log file, allocates memory, and prints banner — all at startup. | Weak — unrelated tasks bound by a clock. |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Elements must be executed in a **specific order**, but the order is set by the *caller* and the data is not shared. | `editRecord()` → calls `validate()`, `unlockRow()`, `displayForm()`, `commit()`. They share a procedure, not a purpose. | Moderate. |
| 5 | **Communicational** | Elements operate on the **same input data** or produce the **same output data**. | A module `processCustomer(c)` that both validates `c` and computes discount for `c`. | Good. |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one element **feeds as input** to the next, forming a data pipeline. | `readRawSensor() → applyFilter() → calibrate() → store()`. | Very good. |
| 7 | **Functional** | All elements contribute to **exactly one well-defined function or goal**. | `calculateIncomeTax(salary, deductions)`. The module has *one* reason to exist. | **Best** — always aim here. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Functional is the gold standard:** A functionally cohesive module has *one reason to change* (Robert C. Martin's **SRP — Single Responsibility Principle**, which is a direct legacy of the Constantine-Myers cohesion scale).

---

## 2.2 The Seven Types of Coupling (Worst → Best)

| # | Type | Definition | What Gets Leaked | Verdict |
|---|------|------------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Content (Pathological)** | One module **directly modifies or branches into** the internal code of another (e.g., `goto` into the middle, direct memory access). | Internal control flow. | **Worst** — breaks encapsulation. |
| 2 | **Common (Global)** | Two or more modules **share global data**. | Global variables. | Very bad — hidden side effects. |
| 3 | **External** | Modules share an **externally imposed data format, protocol, or device interface** (e.g., I/O device, file format spec). | External format. | Bad — locks design to environment. |
| 4 | **Control** | One module passes a **flag/control parameter** that *alters the control flow* of the other. | Control logic. | Bad — caller dictates internal behavior. |
| 5 | **Stamp (Data-Structure)** | A module passes a **whole data structure** when the receiver only needs a few fields. | Unused parts of the data structure. | Mediocre — encourages unnecessary dependence. |
| 6 | **Data** | Modules share data only through **parameters and return values**, with all data being *elementary* (single values). | Only the value. | **Good** — minimum necessary information. |
| 7 | **Message (No Direct Coupling)** | Modules communicate **only by passing messages** (in OO, this is the foundation of true message-passing; in SOA/microservices, this is API calls). | Nothing more than the message. | **Best** — true independence. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparative Cheat Sheet

| Design Attribute | Cohesion | Coupling |
|------------------|----------|----------|
| Scope | **Intra-module** (inside one module) | **Inter-module** (between two modules) |
| Goal | Elements should *belong together* | Modules should *know as little as possible* about each other |
| Ideal Value | **High** (ideally Functional) | **Low** (ideally Data or Message) |
| Measured by | Degree of relatedness of internal tasks | Degree of information/control leaked across boundaries |
| OOP Mapping | Class responsibility (SRP) | Class dependencies (DIP) |
| Indicator of Bad Design | Module does many unrelated things | A change in one forces changes in many |
| Introduced by | **Stevens, Myers & Constantine (1974)** | **Stevens, Myers & Constantine (1974)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Independence Theorem" of KTU Module 4:**
> High cohesion *within* a module and low coupling *between* modules together produce software that is **modular, testable, maintainable, and reusable**. The two metrics are *complementary*, not redundant — you cannot achieve one while ignoring the other.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern engineering practice, the cohesion-coupling framework is the **theoretical bedrock** of:

1. **Microservices Architecture** — each microservice must be *functionally cohesive* and communicate via *message coupling* (REST/gRPC).
2. **SOLID Principles** — SRP (cohesion) and DIP (coupling).
3. **API Design** — data coupling is enforced by sending only required fields (avoiding stamp coupling).
4. **Embedded Systems** — minimizing content coupling is critical for safety-critical firmware.
5. **Compiler Design** — phases (lexical, syntax, semantic) are *temporally/sequentially cohesive* and communicate via well-defined IR — *data coupling*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Evaluations

This section provides the **analytical procedure** the KTU examiner expects when a student is asked to **"Identify and justify the type of cohesion/coupling"** in a given module.

---

## 3.1 The Six-Step Decision Procedure for Identifying Cohesion

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this exact decision tree in your exam answer to score full marks:**

**Step 1 →** Ask: *"Do the elements share any meaningful relationship at all?"*
- If **NO** → **Coincidental Cohesion**.

**Step 2 →** Ask: *"Are the elements different functions selected by a control flag at runtime?"*
- If **YES** → **Logical Cohesion**.

**Step 3 →** Ask: *"Are the elements bound only by the time they execute (e.g., init, shutdown)?"*
- If **YES** → **Temporal Cohesion**.

**Step 4 →** Ask: *"Must the elements execute in a specific caller-imposed order, but on different data?"*
- If **YES** → **Procedural Cohesion**.

**Step 5 →** Ask: *"Do the elements operate on the same input data OR produce the same output data?"*
- If **YES** → **Communicational Cohesion**.

**Step 6 →** Ask: *"Does the output of one element become the input of the next?"*
- If **YES** → **Sequential Cohesion**.

**Step 7 (Default) →** *"Do all elements collaborate to perform exactly one single, well-defined function?"*
- If **YES** → **Functional Cohesion** ✅.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Evaluating Cohesion (14-Mark Style)

> **Question (KTU-style):** *Consider the following module written in pseudo-code. Identify and justify the type of cohesion.*

```text
MODULE  startSystem()
    openDatabaseConnection()
    openLogFile()
    allocateMemoryPool()
    printWelcomeBanner()
    startNetworkListener()
END MODULE
```

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Relationship?** All five operations are unrelated in function (DB, file, memory, print, network).
→ Not coincidental, because they *are* related — by **time**.

**Step 2 — Control flag?** No flag is passed.
→ Not logical.

**Step 3 — Time-bound?** Yes — all five are typically executed at **system startup**.
→ **Temporal Cohesion** is the strongest justified answer.

**Step 4 (Examiner's tip):** Note that some authors argue that this is *procedural* (order matters), but KTU's standard textbook answer for this *exact* pattern is **Temporal Cohesion**, because the unifying factor is *when* they run, not *what* they produce.

**Final Answer:** **Temporal Cohesion — level 3.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Evaluating Coupling (14-Mark Style)

> **Question:** *Module A passes a flag `isAdmin = TRUE` to Module B. Based on the flag, Module B branches and sends the user either to the admin dashboard or the user dashboard. Identify and justify the type of coupling.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Does A modify B's internal code?** No.
→ Not **Content**.

**Step 2 — Do they share global data?** No.
→ Not **Common**.

**Step 3 — Do they share an external format/protocol?** No.
→ Not **External**.

**Step 4 — Does A pass a control parameter that alters B's control flow?** **YES** — the `isAdmin` flag dictates which branch B takes.
→ **Control Coupling** ✅.

**Step 5 — Improvement for full marks:** A better design would split Module B into two separate modules, `adminDashboard()` and `userDashboard()`, and let Module A *call the right one* — this reduces coupling from **Control → Data/Message**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Quick Reference

| Symptom in Code | Diagnosed Issue | Recommended Type |
|----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| `goto` into another module's code | Content coupling | Use function calls / interfaces |
| `extern int globalX;` shared across files | Common coupling | Encapsulate in a module with getters |
| A flag `mode` passed that changes control flow | Control coupling | Split into two distinct modules |
| Passing a full `struct Employee` when only `empID` is needed | Stamp coupling | Pass only the primitive `empID` |
| Passing single primitive `radius` to `area(radius)` | Data coupling | ✅ Ideal |
| Two services exchanging JSON over HTTP | Message coupling | ✅ Ideal |

---

## 3.5 Quantitative Illustration — The Cost of Bad Design

Although cohesion and coupling are *qualitative*, we can express their impact on maintenance cost using a simple KTU-acceptable approximation:

$$C_{maintenance} \;\propto\; \frac{C_{coupling} \times N_{modules}}{C_{cohesion}}$$

where:
- $C_{coupling}$ = average coupling strength per module pair,
- $N_{modules}$ = total number of modules in the system,
- $C_{cohesion}$ = average cohesion score of a module (higher = better).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Maintenance cost rises *linearly* with coupling and module count, and falls *as cohesion rises*. This single line is the **mathematical justification** your examiner will accept for why we pursue *high cohesion + low coupling*.

**Worked numerical sanity-check:**

$$C_{coupling} = 4, \quad N_{modules} = 10, \quad C_{cohesion} = 8$$

$$C_{maintenance} \;\propto\; \frac{4 \times 10}{8} \;=\; \frac{40}{8} \;=\; 5.0 \;\text{(relative units)}$$

If we improve coupling from $4$ to $2$ and cohesion from $8$ to $9$:

$$C'_{maintenance} \;\propto\; \frac{2 \times 10}{9} \;=\; \frac{20}{9} \;\approx\; 2.22 \;\text{(relative units)}$$

> **Result:** A ~**55.6 % drop** in relative maintenance cost — a number examiners love seeing in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate (a) a **high-cohesion / low-coupling** design (the KTU ideal) versus (b) a **low-cohesion / high-coupling** design (the KTU anti-pattern). These diagrams are examinable as drawing-based 7-mark sub-questions.

## 4.1 Ideal Design — High Cohesion, Low (Data) Coupling

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef module fill:#dff5e1,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iface fill:#fff7c2,stroke:#a07a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[Module: UserInterface]:::module
    B[Module: OrderProcessing]:::module
    C[Module: InventoryUpdate]:::module
    D[Module: PaymentGateway]:::module

    IF1[Interface: IOrder]:::iface
    IF2[Interface: IPayment]:::iface

    A -- customerID, cartItems --> IF1
    IF1 -- orderTotal --> B
    B -- stockDelta --> C
    B -- amount, currency --> IF2
    IF2 -- txnResult --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each module has **one responsibility** (high cohesion).
- Communication is through **interfaces** and **primitive data only** (data coupling).
- No module reaches into another module's internals.

---

## 4.2 Anti-Pattern — Low Cohesion, High (Common/Content) Coupling

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef bad fill:#fbd3d3,stroke:#a02020,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef glob fill:#ffe7b3,stroke:#a06000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[Module: PrintReport]:::bad
    B[Module: ValidateUser]:::bad
    C[Module: ComputeTax]:::bad
    D[Module: SendEmail]:::bad
    G[Global Variable: stateFlag]:::glob

    A -- read/write --> G
    B -- read/write --> G
    C -- read/write --> G
    D -- read/write --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All four modules touch the **same global variable** → **Common Coupling** (worst kind).
- The `PrintReport` module does printing, validation, tax math, and emailing → **Coincidental Cohesion** (worst kind).
- One change to `stateFlag`'s format breaks all four modules simultaneously.

---

## 4.3 Control Coupling — Diagram of the Anti-Pattern and Its Fix

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef bad fill:#fbd3d3,stroke:#a02020,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#dff5e1,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A1[Caller Module]:::bad
    B1[Receiver Module]:::bad
    A1 -- flag = TRUE / FALSE --> B1
    B1 -- if flag then AdminPath else UserPath --> B1

    A2[Caller Module]:::good
    B2[AdminHandler Module]:::good
    C2[UserHandler Module]:::good
    A2 -- direct call --> B2
    A2 -- direct call --> C2
```

**Reading the diagram (top = bad, bottom = good):**
- **Top:** Caller passes a flag that controls the callee's branching → **Control Coupling**.
- **Bottom:** Caller directly invokes the right specialized module → **Data/Message Coupling** ✅.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define **coupling**. List the **seven types of coupling** in the correct order (worst → best).

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Coupling (1 mark):** Coupling is a measure of the degree of **interdependence between modules**. A good software design aims for **low coupling**.

**Seven types in order (2 marks):**

1. Content coupling
2. Common coupling
3. External coupling
4. Control coupling
5. Stamp coupling
6. Data coupling
7. Message coupling (best)

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> What is **functional cohesion**? Why is it considered the **best** form of cohesion?

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A module exhibits *functional cohesion* when **all of its elements contribute to the performance of a single, well-defined function or task**.

**Why it is best (1.5 marks):**
- The module has **one single responsibility** (aligns with SRP).
- It is **easy to understand, test, maintain, and reuse**.
- Any change request maps to a clear, single purpose, reducing the ripple effect.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **seven types of cohesion** with a **suitable example** for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **seven types of coupling** with a **suitable example** for each. *(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Seven Types of Cohesion (7 Marks)

> Use the canonical table you have already studied in SECTION 2.1. For full marks, write **1 mark per type**: 0.5 for definition + 0.5 for example.

| # | Type | One-Line Definition | One-Line Example |
|---|------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Arbitrary grouping of unrelated elements. | A "Misc" module containing `sortArray()`, `sendEmail()`, `drawChart()`. |
| 2 | **Logical** | Similar functions selected by a runtime flag. | `printReport(type)` selecting Sales/Inventory/HR report via a flag. |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Grouped by when they execute. | `systemStartup()` opening DB, log, memory at the same moment. |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Must execute in a specific order. | `processOrder()` calling `validate → lock → update → log`. |
| 5 | **Communicational** | Operate on the same input/output data. | `updateCustomer(c)` validating and computing discount for the same record. |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one is input of the next. | `read → filter → transform → store` pipeline. |
| 7 | **Functional** | All elements serve **one** function. | `calculateSimpleInterest(principal, rate, time)`. |

**Closing line for full marks (0.5 bonus):** *The order from worst to best is Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional, and a good design always targets Functional.*

#### Part (b) — Seven Types of Coupling (7 Marks)

| # | Type | One-Line Definition | One-Line Example |
|---|------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Content** | One module branches into another's code. | `goto` into the middle of another function. |
| 2 | **Common** | Modules share global data. | Two modules read/write `extern int counter`. |
| 3 | **External** | Modules share an external data format/protocol. | A module that reads/writes a specific `.csv` schema. |
| 4 | **Control** | A flag alters the callee's control flow. | `processOrder(urgentFlag)` where `urgentFlag` switches the path. |
| 5 | **Stamp** | A whole data structure is passed but only some fields are used. | Passing full `Employee` struct when only `empID` is needed. |
| 6 | **Data** | Only elementary parameters are passed. | `area = computeArea(radius)` — only `radius` is passed. |
| 7 | **Message** | Communication is purely through messages (no shared state). | Two microservices exchanging JSON over HTTP. |

**Closing line for full marks (0.5 bonus):** *The order from worst to best is Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data → Message, and a good design always targets Data or Message coupling.*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**(a)** Differentiate between **cohesion and coupling** with a **neat diagram** showing the relationship between them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a module `libraryMgmt()` that performs the following tasks: *(i)* adds a new book, *(ii)* calculates the fine for a late return, *(iii)* prints the bill, *(iv)* sends an email reminder, and *(v)* updates the central server log. **Identify and justify** the type of cohesion and suggest **two refactoring steps** to improve it. *(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Cohesion vs Coupling (7 Marks)

**Tabular comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Cohesion | Coupling |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Scope | Within a single module | Between two or more modules |
| Goal | Internal elements should be strongly related | Modules should be as independent as possible |
| Ideal | **High** (Functional) | **Low** (Data / Message) |
| Effect of Increase | Improves maintainability | Worsens maintainability |

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef good fill:#dff5e1,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    M1[Module A<br/>Functional Cohesion]:::good
    M2[Module B<br/>Functional Cohesion]:::good
    M3[Module C<br/>Functional Cohesion]:::good
    M1 -- Data Coupling --> M2
    M2 -- Data Coupling --> M3
    M1 -.-> M3
```

**Conclusion line (1 mark):** *"High cohesion within a module and low coupling between modules together produce a robust, modular, and maintainable software design."*

#### Part (b) — Evaluating `libraryMgmt()` (7 Marks)

**Identification (1.5 marks):** This module performs **five logically unrelated tasks** (book addition, fine calculation, bill printing, email, server log). Since the only thing binding them is that they all relate to the library system (and not even at the same time, for the same data, or in a strict order), this is best classified as **Coincidental Cohesion** — the *worst* level.

**Justification (2.5 marks):**
- No common input/output data flows through all five operations → not communicational or sequential.
- No single unifying function → not functional.
- They are arbitrarily grouped into one module for convenience → **coincidental**.

**Two refactoring steps (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Split by responsibility:** Create separate modules — `addBook()`, `calculateFine()`, `printBill()`, `sendEmailReminder()`, `updateServerLog()`. Each will be **functionally cohesive**.

2. **Introduce a coordinator:** Create a `LibraryController` module that **calls** the above five modules in the correct order. This gives the system a clean **orchestrator**, with each worker being a *functionally cohesive, data-coupled* unit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Listing types in the wrong order** is a **2-mark penalty** even if all seven are named. Always present them in the canonical order.
> 2. **Confusing Logical and Control coupling:** *Logical* is a *cohesion* level (grouped by logic + flag), while *Control* is a *coupling* level (one module's flag controls another). Examiners **dedicate 2 marks** to spotting this confusion.
> 3. **Saying "coupling = cohesion"** in part-(a) contrast questions → **0 marks** for the comparison. Use the exact phrases: *intra-module* (cohesion) vs. *inter-module* (coupling).
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in part-(a) of Question B = lose **2 of 7 marks**. Always draw a labelled block diagram.
> 5. **Writing "high coupling" as a goal:** this is a **common 1-mark deduction** — the goal is *low* coupling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-this / Memorize-this list before every KTU exam:**

- ✅ Cohesion = **intra-module** measure; Coupling = **inter-module** measure.
- ✅ Good design = **HIGH** cohesion + **LOW** coupling.
- ✅ Cohesion order (worst → best): **Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional**.
- ✅ Coupling order (worst → best): **Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data → Message**.
- ✅ **Functional cohesion** is the gold standard (one module, one purpose).
- ✅ **Data coupling** and **Message coupling** are the gold standards.
- ✅ **Content coupling** includes `goto`-into-another-function and direct memory poking.
- ✅ **Common coupling** is caused by **global variables** shared across modules.
- ✅ **Control coupling** is when a flag parameter changes the callee's control flow.
- ✅ **Stamp coupling** is when a full data structure is passed but only a few fields are used.
- ✅ **Temporal cohesion** = "things done at the same time" (e.g., startup, shutdown).
- ✅ **Logical cohesion** = "things grouped by logic + selected by flag" — a cohesion concept, not coupling.
- ✅ **Communicational** cohesion = "operate on the same input/output data".
- ✅ **Sequential** cohesion = "output of one feeds the input of the next".
- ✅ **Coincidental** cohesion = "junk-drawer module" — the worst.
- ✅ The framework was introduced by **Stevens, Myers & Constantine (1974)**.
- ✅ Mnemonic for coupling: *"Calmly Organized Computers Eliminate Confusing Software Design Mistakes"* → Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data → Message.
- ✅ Always end cohesion/coupling answers with: *"This produces modular, testable, maintainable, reusable software."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — THE DESIGN MODEL

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Design Model** is a complete, representation-oriented artifact that describes the **architecture**, **data structures**, **interfaces**, **components**, and **deployment** characteristics required to build a software system. It is the technical blueprint that bridges the *Analysis Model* ("what the system must do") with the *Implementation* ("how the system will be built and deployed").

In the KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020-aligned framework, the Design Model is mapped against:
- **CO4 (Course Outcome 4):** *Apply systematic knowledge to develop and validate software design using architectural styles, patterns, and component-based principles.*

### 🌉 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint of a Building"

Imagine you are constructing a **multi-storey hospital**. You do not hand the builder a narrative like *"we need a place to treat patients."* Instead, you provide:
1. A **floor plan** (Architecture) — how rooms are laid out.
2. A **plumbing/electrical map** (Data Design) — how resources flow.
3. A **door/window schedule** (Interface Design) — how people interact with each floor.
4. A **materials list** (Component Design) — exact bricks, lifts, panels.
5. A **site logistics plan** (Deployment Design) — where the generator, parking, and oxygen tanks go.

The **Design Model is exactly this — but for software.** Each view becomes a *lens* through which different stakeholders (architect, DBA, UI designer, DevOps, tester) examine the same system without conflicting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Design Model is *not a single diagram*. It is a **multi-view artifact set** comprising Data Design, Architectural Design, Component-Level Design, Interface Design, and Deployment Design — each at increasing levels of abstraction.

---

## 1.2 The Five Elements of the Design Model

| # | Design Element | Focus | KTU Map |
|---|----------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Data Design** | Persistent data structures, classes, schemas | CO4 / K3 |
| 2 | **Architectural Design** | Layering, topology, system structure | CO4 / K3 |
| 3 | **Component-Level Design** | Methods, procedures, internal logic | CO4 / K3 |
| 4 | **Interface Design** | External and internal contracts (APIs, UIs) | CO4 / K3 |
| 5 | **Deployment Design** | Server topology, container placement, hosting | CO4 / K3 |

These five elements align directly with the KTU prescribed text of **Roger S. Pressman, "Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach"**, McGraw-Hill.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is to confuse the *Analysis Model* with the *Design Model*. If the answer contains "user requirements" or "actors and use-cases", it is **Analysis**, not Design. Design answers must talk about **classes, architecture, modules, and interfaces**.
> 
> **Analysis Model → "WHAT" the system does.**
> **Design Model → "HOW" the system will be built.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Data Design

Data Design transforms the analysis-class model into design-level data structures. It serves **three audiences**:

1. **System Architects** — define storage and access.
2. **Component Designers** — define internal data exchange.
3. **DBAs** — implement persistent schema.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- **Step 1:** Review the Analysis Class Model.
- **Step 2:** Identify persistent classes vs transient classes.
- **Step 3:** Refine attributes → data types, default values, constraints.
- **Step 4:** Identify relationships → cardinality, foreign keys, association classes.
- **Step 5:** Apply **Data Design Patterns** (e.g., *Repository*, *Active Record*, *Data Mapper*) from Martin Fowler.
- **Step 6:** Normalize relational data (typically up to **3NF** for OLTP systems).
- **Step 7:** Define file/table structures for non-DB stores.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Data Design important in KTU?**
> Because KTU repeatedly tests the *difference between design classes and persistent entities*. Marks are awarded for showing how classes map to **tables**, **documents**, or **files** with proper keys and constraints.

## 2.2 Architectural Design

The **Software Architecture** represents the **overall structure** and **control hierarchy** of the system. It identifies:
- Major **components** (subsystems, packages, services).
- **Communication** patterns (synchronous / asynchronous).
- **Coordination** mechanisms (message bus, event loop, RPC).
- **Constraints** (performance, scalability, security).

### Architectural Styles — The 5 KTU Categories

| Style | Topology | When to Use | KTU Weight |
|-------|----------|-------------|------------|
| **Data-Centered** | Central repository (DB / blackboard) | Shared data → multiple modules | High |
| **Data-Flow** | Pipeline / batch / streaming | ETL, compilers, audio pipelines | Medium |
| **Call-and-Return** | Main-sub, O-O, Layered | Mainstream business apps | High |
| **Object-Oriented** | Objects + message passing | Reusable component systems | High |
| **Layered** | n-tier (Presentation / Business / Data) | Web & enterprise apps | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Style:** *Layered Architecture* — always remember the dependency rule: **higher layers depend on lower layers, NEVER the reverse.**

## 2.3 Component-Level Design

A **Component** is a *modular, deployable, replaceable unit* with well-defined interfaces. Component design focuses on:

- **Algorithmic structure** of each module.
- **Data structures** local to each module.
- **Interface contracts** (pre-conditions, post-conditions, invariants).
- **Cohesion** (intra-module bond) and **Coupling** (inter-module bond).

### The 7 Types of Cohesion (High → Low)
1. **Functional** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
2. **Layer**
3. **Communicational**
4. **Sequential**
5. **Procedural**
6. **Temporal**
7. **Coincidental**

### The 6 Types of Coupling (Low → High)
1. **Data** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
2. **Stamp**
3. **Control**
4. **External**
5. **Common**
6. **Content**

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:**
> *Aim for High Cohesion + Low Coupling.*
> If asked to "improve the design", always justify using **cohesion-coupling** metrics.

## 2.4 Interface Design

The interface represents the **boundary** between system and external actor. Three interface classes:

| Interface Type | Description | Example |
|----------------|-------------|---------|
| **User Interface (UI)** | Human ↔ System | Login screen |
| **External Interface** | System ↔ Other System | REST API to payment gateway |
| **Internal Interface** | Module ↔ Module | DAO calling repository |

### Interface Design Principles (KTU Pattern)
- **SRP** — Single Responsibility Principle.
- **OCP** — Open/Closed Principle.
- **LSP** — Liskov Substitution Principle.
- **ISP** — Interface Segregation Principle.
- **DIP** — Dependency Inversion Principle.

These are the **SOLID Principles** of object-oriented design and are an absolute **high-yield** topic for KTU 2024.

## 2.5 Deployment Design

Deployment Design models:
- **Hardware nodes** (servers, VMs, containers).
- **Network topology**.
- **Middleware** (app servers, message brokers).
- **Container placement**.

> [!NOTE]
> Deployment Design in KTU is often tested via **UML Deployment Diagrams** showing `<<artifact>>` nodes connected by `<<communicationPath>>` associations.

## 2.6 Design Patterns — The KTU Reference Set

The **Gang of Four (GoF)** patterns are categorized into 3 families:

| Family | Purpose | Common Patterns |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| **Creational** | Object creation mechanisms | Singleton, Factory, Builder, Prototype, Abstract Factory |
| **Structural** | Class/object composition | Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Proxy |
| **Behavioral** | Communication & responsibility | Observer, Strategy, State, Command, Mediator, Iterator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Trick:** Students are often asked "Which pattern would you use to ensure only ONE instance of a class exists?" → **Singleton (Creational).**
> Or "Decouple abstraction from implementation" → **Bridge (Structural).**
> Or "Notify multiple objects on state change" → **Observer (Behavioral).**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Design Model from the Analysis Model

The transformation from Analysis to Design is **systematic**. We will derive each step explicitly so you can reproduce it in the exam hall.

### 3.1.1 Class Refinement Equation

Let $\mathcal{C}_A$ be the set of analysis classes and $\mathcal{C}_D$ be the set of design classes.

$$
\mathcal{C}_D \;=\; f(\mathcal{C}_A) \;=\; \text{Refactor}(\mathcal{C}_A) \cup \text{NewDesign}(\mathcal{C}_A)
$$

where the `Refactor` operator applies:
1. Add **navigation visibility** (+, -, #).
2. Add **method signatures**.
3. Add **type definitions** and default values.
4. Add **cardinality** to associations.

> Each **analysis class** becomes **one or more** design classes, because boundary, control, and entity classes may split into MVC layers in the design.

### 3.1.2 Cohesion Score Derivation

For a module $M$ containing $n$ tasks $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$, **Cohesion $C(M)$** is computed as:

$$
C(M) \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \sigma(T_i, T_j)}{\binom{n}{2}}
$$

where $\sigma(T_i, T_j) = 1$ if $T_i$ and $T_j$ share local data, else $0$.

The **Target Cohesion** for a well-designed component is:

$$
C(M) \;\geq\; 0.85
$$

### 3.1.3 Coupling Score Derivation

Coupling between two modules $M_a$ and $M_b$:

$$
K(M_a, M_b) \;=\; w_1 \cdot D \;+\; w_2 \cdot S \;+\; w_3 \cdot C \;+\; w_4 \cdot E \;+\; w_5 \cdot Cm
$$

where the weights correspond to the five coupling types (Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common), and $w_i$ are calibrated with $w_1 < w_2 < w_3 < w_4 < w_5$.

**Target Coupling:**

$$
K(M_a, M_b) \;\leq\; 0.30
$$

### 3.1.4 Pattern Selection Matrix

When asked "which pattern?", use the **decision function**:

$$
P^* \;=\; \arg\max_{P \in \mathcal{P}} \; \big[\, \alpha \cdot \text{Reusability}(P) \;+\; \beta \cdot \text{Decoupling}(P) \;-\; \gamma \cdot \text{Complexity}(P) \,\big]
$$

subject to constraints:
- $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$
- $\text{Complexity}(P) \le \tau$ where $\tau$ is a problem-specific threshold.

> In KTU exam language, this translates to: *"Choose the pattern with high reusability, high decoupling, and acceptable complexity for the given scenario."*

### 3.1.5 Layered Architecture Dependency Rule

For an $n$-layered architecture with layers $L_1$ (top) through $L_n$ (bottom):

$$
\forall\, i,j \;:\; \text{Dep}(L_i, L_j) \;\Rightarrow\; i < j
$$

Meaning a layer can **only** depend on layers below it. This is formalized as:

$$
\nexists\; (L_i, L_j) \;:\; \text{Dep}(L_i, L_j) \;\wedge\; \text{Dep}(L_j, L_i)
$$

> This **acyclic dependency principle (ADP)** is the heart of every layered architecture question in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Model — Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DESIGN MODEL] --> B[DATA DESIGN]
    A --> C[ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN]
    A --> D[COMPONENT LEVEL DESIGN]
    A --> E[INTERFACE DESIGN]
    A --> F[DEPLOYMENT DESIGN]

    B --> B1[Persistent Classes]
    B --> B2[Data Structures]
    B --> B3[Schema Definitions]

    C --> C1[Architectural Styles]
    C --> C2[Design Patterns]
    C --> C3[Subsystems]

    D --> D1[Procedures]
    D --> D2[Algorithms]
    D --> D3[Data Flow]

    E --> E1[User Interface]
    E --> E2[External Interfaces]
    E --> E3[Internal Interfaces]

    F --> F1[Network Topology]
    F --> F2[Container Layout]
    F --> F3[Middleware Placement]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff

```

```

## 4.3 Gang of Four Design Pattern Family Tree

```mermaid

    P[GOFF DESIGN PATTERNS] --> P1[CREATIONAL]

    style P fill:#6a040f,color:#fff
