
## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Cohesion & Coupling Evaluation

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[DESIGN PROPOSAL] --> ANALYZE[ANALYZE MODULES]
    ANALYZE --> CHK_COH[CHECK COHESION]
    ANALYZE --> CHK_COU[CHECK COUPLING]
    CHK_COH --> COH_SCORE[Cohesion Score >= 0.85]
    CHK_COU --> COU_SCORE[Coupling Score <= 0.30]
    COH_SCORE --> DECIDE{Acceptable}
    COU_SCORE --> DECIDE
    DECIDE --> YES[ACCEPT DESIGN]
    DECIDE --> NO[REFACTOR DESIGN]
    NO --> INPUT

    style INPUT fill:#3a86ff,color:#fff
    style YES fill:#06d6a0,color:#000
    style NO fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    style DECIDE fill:#ffd166,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Analysis Model and Design Model.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Analysis Model:** Represents the *WHAT* of the system — captures user requirements, use-cases, actors, and analysis classes. It is technology-independent and focuses on problem domain. *(1 Mark)*
- **Design Model:** Represents the *HOW* of the system — defines architecture, classes with attributes/methods, interfaces, components, and deployment. It is technology-aware and provides implementation roadmap. *(1 Mark)*
- **Bridge:** The Design Model is derived *from* the Analysis Model by adding architectural styles, design patterns, persistent data structures, and component-level logic. *(1 Mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the five elements of the Design Model. What is the role of Interface Design?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):
The five elements are:
1. Data Design *(½ M)*
2. Architectural Design *(½ M)*
3. Component-Level Design *(½ M)*
4. Interface Design *(½ M)*
5. Deployment Design *(½ M)*

**Role of Interface Design:** It defines the **contract** between internal modules and the external world (users, other systems, hardware). It applies the **SOLID principles** to ensure loose coupling, and provides specifications for APIs, UI screens, and inter-module communication. *(1 Mark)*

---

## 🅱️ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **five elements of the Design Model** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **architectural styles** in software design. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What are **Design Patterns**? Classify the **GoF patterns** with **two examples each** from creational, structural, and behavioral categories. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**(a)** *7 Marks*

**Definition (1 M):** The Design Model is a complete, multi-view technical representation that bridges the Analysis Model and implementation.

**Five Elements (4 × ½ = 2 M):**
1. **Data Design** — defines persistent data structures, schemas, and file/table layouts.
2. **Architectural Design** — describes overall structure, subsystems, and their interaction.
3. **Component-Level Design** — refines modules with algorithms, data flow, and procedures.
4. **Interface Design** — specifies external and internal contracts (UIs, APIs, protocols).
5. **Deployment Design** — models hardware, network, container, and middleware topology.

**Block Diagram (1 M):** Refer Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — five branches emanating from Design Model.

**Architectural Styles Discussion (3 M):**
- **Data-Centered Style** — central repository (e.g., DBMS-driven apps).
- **Data-Flow Style** — pipe-and-filter, batch sequential (e.g., compiler, ETL).
- **Call-and-Return Style** — main-sub and RPC (e.g., distributed microservices).
- **Object-Oriented Style** — components communicate via messages (e.g., Java Swing).
- **Layered Style** — n-tier (Presentation → Business → Data). Dependency rule: $i < j$ for layer $L_i$ depending on $L_j$.

Architectural styles help achieve **scalability**, **maintainability**, and **performance** by enforcing proven structural templates. They reduce design risk and aid stakeholder communication.

**(b)** *7 Marks*

**Definition (1 M):** A **Design Pattern** is a *reusable, time-tested solution* to a recurring design problem in object-oriented software development. It is *not* code — it is a template that can be applied in varying contexts.

**Why Patterns? (1 M):** They encapsulate expert knowledge, promote reusability, improve communication among developers, and avoid reinvention.

**Classification with Examples (3 × 1.5 = 4.5 M):**

| Category | Purpose | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|----------|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Creational** | Object creation | **Singleton** (only one instance) | **Factory Method** (delegate instantiation) |
| **Structural** | Class/object composition | **Adapter** (incompatible interface conversion) | **Decorator** (adds behavior dynamically) |
| **Behavioral** | Object interaction & responsibility | **Observer** (notify dependents on change) | **Strategy** (family of interchangeable algorithms) |

**Conclusion (½ M):** Patterns must be chosen carefully — over-application leads to **over-engineering** and code bloat.

---

### **📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** What is **Architectural Design**? Explain any **four architectural styles** with neat block diagrams. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **Cohesion and Coupling**. Differentiate between **functional cohesion** and **sequential cohesion** with examples. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**(a)** *7 Marks*

**Definition (1 M):** Architectural Design establishes the **overall structure** of the software — its components, their relationships, and the principles guiding their evolution.

**Need (1 M):** It manages complexity, supports reasoning about non-functional qualities (security, scalability, performance), and provides a foundation for parallel development.

**Four Styles (4 × 1 = 4 M):**

1. **Layered Architecture (n-tier):**
   - Topology: Stack of layers (Presentation → Business → Persistence → Database).
   - Example: Any enterprise web app (Spring Boot + React frontend + MySQL).
   - Block Diagram: Rectangle subdivided into horizontal slices.

2. **Repository Architecture (Data-Centered):**
   - Topology: Central repository with multiple client subsystems reading/writing.
   - Example: IDE (compilers, debuggers, editors all share AST).
   - Diagram: Central circle with radiating spokes.

3. **Pipe-and-Filter (Data-Flow):**
   - Topology: Data streams through a chain of filters; each performs one transformation.
   - Example: Unix shell pipeline `cat → grep → sort → uniq`.

4. **Client-Server (Call-and-Return):**
   - Topology: Clients invoke services on server(s); server manages shared resources.
   - Example: Web browser ↔ Web server.

**Selection Criteria (1 M):** The architectural style should match **non-functional requirements** like latency, throughput, modifiability, and security.

**(b)** *7 Marks*

**Cohesion (2 M):** Cohesion is the **intra-module** measure of how strongly the elements of a single module belong together. *Goal: HIGH cohesion.*

**Coupling (2 M):** Coupling is the **inter-module** measure of dependency between modules. *Goal: LOW coupling.*

**Types of Cohesion (in order High → Low):**
1. Functional, 2. Layer, 3. Communicational, 4. Sequential, 5. Procedural, 6. Temporal, 7. Coincidental.

**Functional Cohesion (1.5 M):** Every element of the module contributes to **exactly one well-defined task**. All elements are essential to that single purpose.
**Example:** A function `calculateInvoiceTotal(cart)` performs only the task of computing the total.

**Sequential Cohesion (1.5 M):** Output of one part of the module serves as **input to the next part** within the same module. The activities are ordered, but each may perform a different sub-task.
**Example:** A function `readFile → parseContent → generateReport` — the output of `readFile` flows into `parseContent`, then into `generateReport`.

**Key Difference:**

| Aspect | Functional | Sequential |
|--------|------------|------------|
| Task scope | Single, well-defined | Multiple chained tasks |
| Reusability | High | Low |
| Cohesion level | Highest | 4th best |
| Example | `validateEmail()` | `read-process-write()` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students write "functional and sequential cohesion are the same" — **they are not**. Sequential still performs multiple sub-tasks; functional performs one.
> - A common 2-mark loss occurs when students list coupling types in the wrong order. Memorize **Data → Stamp → Control → External → Common → Content** as the GOOD → BAD sequence.
> - For architectural styles, students draw **flowcharts instead of block diagrams** — that fetches **zero marks**. Always draw topology (boxes + connecting arrows).
> - When asked about patterns, writing the **definition only** without an example = **partial marks only**. Always include at least one real-world example.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4 (Design Model)**

- ✅ The **Design Model** is the *technical blueprint* derived from the Analysis Model.
- ✅ It has **5 elements**: Data, Architectural, Component, Interface, Deployment.
- ✅ Analysis = **WHAT**, Design = **HOW**. Never confuse in the exam.
- ✅ **Architectural styles**: Data-Centered, Data-Flow, Call-and-Return, Object-Oriented, Layered.
- ✅ **Layered architecture dependency rule**: $\text{Dep}(L_i, L_j) \Rightarrow i < j$. Acyclic.
- ✅ **Cohesion target**: $\ge 0.85$. **Coupling target**: $\le 0.30$.
- ✅ **Cohesion order (High → Low)**: Functional → Layer → Communicational → Sequential → Procedural → Temporal → Coincidental.
- ✅ **Coupling order (Low → High)**: Data → Stamp → Control → External → Common → Content.
- ✅ **GoF Patterns** split into **3 families** — Creational, Structural, Behavioral — with 5 + 7 + 11 = 23 patterns.
- ✅ **Singleton** = one instance; **Observer** = notify dependents; **Strategy** = interchangeable algorithms; **Adapter** = bridge incompatible interfaces; **Decorator** = dynamic behavior addition.
- ✅ **SOLID principles** (SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP) govern Interface Design.
- ✅ Always cite **non-functional requirements** (scalability, maintainability, performance) when justifying an architectural choice.
- ✅ **UML Deployment Diagrams** use `<<artifact>>`, `<<node>>`, and `<<communicationPath>>` stereotypes.
- ✅ When answering "which pattern?", follow the **decision function** $P^* = \arg\max_{P \in \mathcal{P}} [\alpha \cdot \text{Reusability} + \beta \cdot \text{Decoupling} - \gamma \cdot \text{Complexity}]$.
- ✅ The **Design Model is iterative** — it evolves with each refinement of requirements; it is *never* a one-shot artifact.
- ✅ **Avoid over-engineering** — applying every pattern for the sake of it is a mark-loser in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Design** is the phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) that translates the analyzed requirements (from the Software Requirement Specification — SRS) into a **blueprint** for constructing the software system. It establishes the **architecture**, **modules**, **interfaces**, **data structures**, and **algorithms** necessary to satisfy the specified functional and non-functional requirements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, System Design is the **bridge between problem analysis and implementation**. It answers the question:

> *"How will the system be built to satisfy what the user wants?"*

System Design is classified into two principal levels by the KTU syllabus:

| Design Level | Purpose | Output Artefact |
|---|---|---|
| **Architectural / High-Level Design** | Defines the overall system structure, major components, and their relationships | System Architecture Diagram, Data Flow Diagram (Level-wise) |
| **Detailed / Component-Level Design** | Specifies the internal logic of each module, algorithms, and data structures | Module Description, Procedural Design (PDL/Pseudocode) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Architect–Builder Analogy**
> Think of system design as the work of an **architect** designing a building:
> - The **SRS** is the client's brief ("I want a 3-bedroom house with a garden, solar panels, and earthquake resistance").
> - The **System Design** is the **architect's blueprint** — it specifies how many floors, the placement of rooms, the load-bearing walls, electrical routing, and plumbing.
> - The **coding/implementation** is the **builder** physically constructing the house according to that blueprint.

Without a blueprint, the builder is lost. Similarly, without proper system design, developers produce inconsistent, unmaintainable, and unscalable software.

## 1.3 Significance in the KTU 2024 Scheme

- It is the **first engineering activity** where cost-quality-time trade-offs become explicit.
- The **IEEE Std 830-1998 (SRS)** feeds directly into design; the **IEEE Std 1016-2009 (Software Design Description)** defines design outputs.
- It directly maps to the KTU Course Outcome **CO4**: *Apply software engineering principles to design software systems*.
- It carries **high weightage** in KTU university exams (typically Part B questions of 14 marks from this module).

## 1.4 Goals of Good System Design

A well-engineered system design, as per KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) principles, must achieve:

1. **Correctness** — satisfies the SRS.
2. **Efficiency** — optimal use of resources (time, memory).
3. **Maintainability** — easy to modify, fix, and extend.
4. **Reliability** — performs consistently under stated conditions.
5. **Understandability** — readable and traceable by other engineers.
6. **Reusability** — components usable across other systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Design Concepts (KTU-Mandated)

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4), the following design principles are **explicitly required** to be understood by the student. These are the *axioms* of software design:

### 2.1.1 Abstraction
- Hiding background details and emphasizing only the essential features.
- **Two levels** taught in KTU:
  - **Data Abstraction** — a named collection of data that describes a data object (e.g., a `Student` object with `name`, `rollNo`).
  - **Procedural Abstraction** — a named sequence of instructions (e.g., `sortArray()`).
  - **Control Abstraction** — using control statements without specifying internal details.

### 2.1.2 Architecture
- The **overall structure** of the software — the organization of components, their externally visible properties, and the relationships among them.
- KTU expects students to know architectural styles: **Layered, Client-Server, Pipe-Filter, MVC, Repository, Event-Driven**.

### 2.1.3 Patterns
- A **Design Pattern** is a reusable, named solution to a recurring design problem in a particular context (Gang of Four — GoF).
- Examples: **Observer, Strategy, Singleton, Factory, MVC**.

### 2.1.4 Modularity
- The property of a system being decomposed into a set of cohesive and loosely coupled modules.
- **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** — *one module, one reason to change*.

### 2.1.5 Information Hiding (Encapsulation)
- Each module hides its internal design decisions (data structures, algorithms) from other modules, exposing only a **public interface**.

### 2.1.6 Functional Independence
- Achieved through **high cohesion** + **low coupling** (explained in Section 2.3 below).

### 2.1.7 Refinement
- A top-down design strategy where complex problems are decomposed into sub-problems iteratively until each sub-problem is solvable.

### 2.1.8 Reuse
- Designing new systems using **existing components** (commercial off-the-shelf COTS, open-source libraries, or legacy in-house modules).

### 2.1.9 Design for Change
- Anticipating future changes (Anticipatory Design) vs. accommodating changes on demand (Adaptive Design).

## 2.2 Design Strategies / Methodologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme requires the following two design strategies to be distinguishable by the student:

| Strategy | Direction | Approach | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Function-Oriented (Top-Down)** | Starts from functions/tasks and decomposes them | Decomposition | Suitable for systems with clear procedural flow (e.g., payroll systems) |
| **Object-Oriented (Bottom-Up)** | Starts from identified objects/data and composes them | Composition | Suitable for systems with rich domain entities (e.g., banking systems) |

## 2.3 Coupling and Cohesion — The Heart of KTU Design Questions

### 2.3.1 Cohesion (Strength within a module)

Cohesion measures how strongly the elements inside a single module are related. The KTU syllabus lists **7 levels** (from worst to best):

| # | Level | Description (KTU-Standard) | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Elements grouped arbitrarily; no meaningful relationship | Worst |
| 2 | **Logical** | Elements that perform similar functions are grouped (e.g., all I/O routines) | Poor |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Elements activated at the same time (e.g., initialization routines) | Poor |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Elements grouped by the order in which they are executed | Medium |
| 5 | **Communicational / Informational** | Elements operate on the same data / share same data structure | Good |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one element is the input of the next | High |
| 7 | **Functional** | All elements contribute to a single, well-defined function | Best |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Rule:** "Functional cohesion is the **best** and coincidental is the **worst**." This statement frequently appears in Part A questions.

### 2.3.2 Coupling (Interconnection between modules)

Coupling measures the degree of interdependence between modules. KTU lists **6 levels** (from best to worst):

| # | Level | Description | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data** | Modules share only data via parameters | Best (Lowest) |
| 2 | **Stamp** | Modules share a composite data structure but use only part of it | Low |
| 3 | **Control** | One module passes a control flag that dictates the other's logic | Medium |
| 4 | **External** | Modules share an externally imposed data format (e.g., communication protocol) | Medium |
| 5 | **Common / Global** | Modules share global data | High |
| 6 | **Content** | One module directly modifies the internals of another | Worst (Highest) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** Aim for **Functional Cohesion** + **Data Coupling** for maximum software quality.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although software design is qualitative, the KTU syllabus formalizes several **quantitative metrics** for design quality and estimation.

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)** | $V(G) = e - n + 2p$ | Measures control-flow complexity of a module |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (Alternate)** | $V(G) = \text{Number of decision points} + 1$ | Quick estimation for KTU problems |
| **Function Points (FP)** | $\text{FP} = UFP \times VAF$ | Measures software size from user's view |
| **VAF (Value Adjustment Factor)** | $\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i$ | Adjusts for 14 general system characteristics |
| **LOC Estimation (Basic)** | $\text{Estimated LOC} = \text{FP} \times \text{LOC per FP}$ | Converts FP to expected lines of code |
| **Effort (COCOMO Basic)** | $\text{Effort} = a \times (\text{KLOC})^b$ person-months | Estimates development effort |
| **Cohesion (Qualitative)** | Rank 1 (Coincidental) → Rank 7 (Functional) | Measures intra-module strength |
| **Coupling (Qualitative)** | Rank 1 (Data) → Rank 6 (Content) | Measures inter-module dependency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Where** in these formulas:
> - $e$ = number of edges in the control flow graph
> - $n$ = number of nodes
> - $p$ = number of connected components (usually 1)
> - $UFP$ = Unadjusted Function Points
> - $F_i$ = rating of the $i$-th complexity factor (0–5)
> - $a, b$ = COCOMO constants (e.g., $a=2.4, b=1.05$ for organic mode)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of System Design

| Industry Sector | Application of System Design |
|---|---|
| **Banking (Kerala Federal Bank Case Study)** | Layered architecture for core banking systems |
| **Healthcare (e-Health Kerala)** | MVC + Microservices for patient data management |
| **Automotive (Tesla OTA Updates)** | Event-driven architecture for real-time firmware upgrades |
| **E-Commerce (Flipkart/Amazon)** | Microservices + Load-balanced client-server for scaling |
| **Embedded / IoT (Kerala Start-ups)** | RTOS-based design with publish-subscribe patterns |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

The Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$ is a quantitative measure of the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It is derived from graph theory applied to a Control Flow Graph (CFG).

### 3.1.1 Definition of the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

A CFG $G = (N, E)$ is constructed from a program's source code where:
- Each **node** $n_i \in N$ represents one or more sequential statements.
- Each **edge** $e_j \in E$ represents a possible flow of control.
- A **predicate node** (decision point such as `if`, `for`, `while`, `case`) has two outgoing edges (True / False).

### 3.1.2 Derivation from Graph Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a connected, directed graph, the number of independent cycles is given by the **cyclomatic number** (first derived by Gustave-Adolphe Dugué and popularized by McCabe for software).

$$
V(G) = e - n + 2p
$$

where:
- $e$ = total number of edges in the CFG
- $n$ = total number of nodes in the CFG
- $p$ = number of connected components (for a single program, $p = 1$)

When $p = 1$ (typical for a single module):

$$
V(G) = e - n + 2
$$

### 3.1.3 Alternate Derivation (Decision-Point Method)

For any structured program, the cyclomatic complexity can also be expressed as:

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1
$$

where $\pi$ is the number of **predicate (decision) nodes** in the program.

**Proof Sketch (Step-by-Step):**

Given a structured program with $\pi$ decision points, the CFG can be transformed as follows:

- Start with a single node (representing the entry): contributes 1 to $n$.
- Each sequential statement adds a node.
- Each `if-then-else` adds 1 decision node and 2 outgoing edges.
- Each `while/for` loop adds 1 decision node and 2 outgoing edges.
- Each `case` with $k$ branches adds 1 decision node and $k$ edges (or sometimes converted to multiple `if`s, contributing $k-1$ decision nodes).

After constructing the CFG, every decision point contributes exactly 1 extra edge beyond what a purely linear program would have. Therefore:

$$
e_{\text{linear}} = n_{\text{linear}} - 1 \quad \text{(path from entry to exit)}
$$

Adding $\pi$ decision points adds $\pi$ extra nodes and $2\pi$ extra edges (one TRUE edge, one FALSE edge, but joining the FALSE back into a single merge point — net $+1$ edge per decision in the cycle count).

Thus:

$$
V(G) = e - n + 2 = (n - 1 + \pi) - n + 2 = \pi + 1
$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A module of a payroll system contains the following pseudocode. Calculate its Cyclomatic Complexity using both the graph-theoretic and decision-point methods.

```text
START
  READ empId
  IF (empId > 0) THEN
      IF (basicPay > 50000) THEN
          hra = 0.20 * basicPay
      ELSE
          hra = 0.10 * basicPay
      END IF
  END IF
  FOR i = 1 TO 12
      grossPay = basicPay + hra
  END FOR
STOP
```

**Solution using Decision-Point Method:**

Count the decision points:
- 1 `IF (empId > 0)` → 1
- 1 `IF (basicPay > 50000)` → 1
- 1 `FOR i = 1 TO 12` → 1 (loop is treated as a decision)

Total decision points: $\pi = 3$

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Verification using Graph-Theoretic Method:**

Construct the CFG with $n$ nodes and $e$ edges:

- Node 1: START
- Node 2: READ empId
- Node 3: decision (empId > 0)
- Node 4: decision (basicPay > 50000)
- Node 5: hra assignment
- Node 6: hra assignment (else)
- Node 7: FOR loop decision
- Node 8: grossPay assignment
- Node 9: STOP

Count: $n = 9$

Count the edges:
- 1→2, 2→3 (sequential)
- 3→4 (TRUE), 3→9 (FALSE, bypass)
- 4→5 (TRUE), 4→6 (FALSE)
- 5→7, 6→7 (merge)
- 7→8 (TRUE), 7→9 (FALSE, loop exit)
- 8→7 (loop back)

Count: $e = 11$, $p = 1$

$$
V(G) = e - n + 2p = 11 - 9 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Both methods yield $V(G) = 4$.**

### 3.1.5 Interpretation (KTU Board Perspective)

| $V(G)$ Value | Risk Assessment | Testing Effort |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 10 | Simple, low risk | Test linearly |
| 11 – 20 | Moderate risk | More thorough testing needed |
| 21 – 50 | High risk, complex | Refactoring recommended |
| $> 50$ | Untestable | Mandatory redesign |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Function Point Estimation

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A point-of-sale (POS) system is being designed for a retail store. The following information is given:
> - External Inputs (EI) = 4 (simple) + 2 (average)
> - External Outputs (EO) = 3 (average) + 1 (complex)
> - External Inquiries (EQ) = 5 (simple)
> - Internal Logical Files (ILF) = 2 (average)
> - External Interface Files (EIF) = 1 (simple)
>
> The complexity weights are:
>
> | Function Type | Simple | Average | Complex |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | EI | 3 | 4 | 6 |
> | EO | 4 | 5 | 7 |
> | EQ | 3 | 4 | 6 |
> | ILF | 7 | 10 | 15 |
> | EIF | 5 | 7 | 10 |
>
> Given the sum of 14 complexity factors $\sum F_i = 42$, calculate the **Function Points**.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Unadjusted Function Points (UFP)**.

$$
\text{UFP} = \sum (\text{Function Type Count} \times \text{Weight})
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{\text{EI}} = (4 \times 3) + (2 \times 4) = 12 + 8 = 20
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{\text{EO}} = (3 \times 5) + (1 \times 7) = 15 + 7 = 22
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{\text{EQ}} = 5 \times 3 = 15
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{\text{ILF}} = 2 \times 10 = 20
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{\text{EIF}} = 1 \times 5 = 5
$$

$$
\text{Total UFP} = 20 + 22 + 15 + 20 + 5 = 82
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)**.

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i
$$

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 42 = 0.65 + 0.42 = 1.07
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **Function Points (FP)**.

$$
\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{VAF}
$$

$$
\text{FP} = 82 \times 1.07 = 87.74
$$

**Result:** The system's size is approximately **88 Function Points**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Design Strategy

| Problem Domain Characteristic | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Clear, well-defined sequential workflows | Function-Oriented (Top-Down) | Decomposition matches natural flow |
| Rich, identifiable domain objects (Bank Account, Customer) | Object-Oriented (Bottom-Up) | Objects are first-class citizens |
| Real-time embedded system with strict timing | Function-Oriented with State Machines | Predictable, minimal overhead |
| Evolving requirements / frequent changes | Object-Oriented with Design Patterns | Encapsulation + polymorphism support change |
| Data-intensive analytics | Data-Centric / Repository Architecture | Centralized data access |
| Distributed microservices | Object-Oriented + Service-Oriented | Each service is an autonomous object |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The System Design Process (KTU-Standard Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SRS Document] --> B[Architectural Design]
    B --> C[Identify Modules / Subsystems]
    C --> D[Component-Level Design]
    D --> E[Define Interfaces]
    E --> F[Design Data Structures]
    F --> G[Design Algorithms]
    G --> H[Software Design Description]
    H --> I[Design Review and Validation]
    I --> J{Design Approved?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Proceed to Coding]
    J -->|No| B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cohesion Hierarchy (7 Levels)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Coincidental] --> L2[Logical]
    L2 --> L3[Temporal]
    L3 --> L4[Procedural]
    L4 --> L5[Communicational]
    L5 --> L6[Sequential]
    L6 --> L7[Functional]
    style L1 fill:#ffcccc
    style L7 fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Coupling Hierarchy (6 Levels)

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Data] --> C2[Stamp]
    C2 --> C3[Control]
    C3 --> C4[External]
    C4 --> C5[Common Global]
    C5 --> C6[Content]
    style C1 fill:#ccffcc
    style C6 fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Architectural Style Classification (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ARCH[Software Architecture]
    ARCH --> A1[Layered Architecture]
    ARCH --> A2[Client-Server]
    ARCH --> A3[MVC Model View Controller]
    ARCH --> A4[Pipe and Filter]
    ARCH --> A5[Event Driven]
    ARCH --> A6[Microservices]
    ARCH --> A7[Repository Architecture]

    A1 --> A1A[Presentation Layer]
    A1 --> A1B[Business Logic Layer]
    A1 --> A1C[Data Access Layer]

    A3 --> A3A[Model - Data and Business Rules]
    A3 --> A3B[View - User Interface]
    A3 --> A3C[Controller - Input Handler]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Module Decomposition (Top-Down Design Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS[POS Point of Sale System]
    SYS --> M1[User Authentication Module]
    SYS --> M2[Inventory Module]
    SYS --> M3[Billing Module]
    SYS --> M4[Reporting Module]
    SYS --> M5[Database Module]

    M2 --> M2A[Add Item]
    M2 --> M2B[Update Stock]
    M2 --> M2C[Remove Item]

    M3 --> M3A[Generate Bill]
    M3 --> M3B[Apply Discount]
    M3 --> M3C[Tax Calculation]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: MVC Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant View
    participant Controller
    participant Model
    User->>View: 1 Clicks Submit Order
    View->>Controller: 2 Forwards request
    Controller->>Model: 3 Update order data
    Model->>Model: 4 Validates and persists
    Model-->>Controller: 5 Returns status
    Controller-->>View: 6 Renders response
    View-->>User: 7 Displays order confirmation
```

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram: Coupling Visualization (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MODA[Module A Employee]
    MODB[Module B Payroll]
    MODC[Module C Tax]

    MODA -->|Data: empId, basicPay| MODB
    MODB -->|Data: grossPay| MODC
    MODC -->|Data: taxAmount| MODB

    style MODA fill:#e0f7fa
    style MODB fill:#fff9c4
    style MODC fill:#f8bbd0
```

## 4.8 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Independence (Cohesion + Coupling)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Design Quality Quadrant
    x-axis Low Coupling --> High Coupling
    y-axis Low Cohesion --> High Cohesion
    quadrant-1 Ideal Design
    quadrant-2 Good Coupling Poor Cohesion
    quadrant-3 Worst Design
    quadrant-4 Good Cohesion Poor Coupling
    Functional Module: [0.15, 0.95]
    Data Coupled System: [0.20, 0.85]
    Procedural Module: [0.50, 0.55]
    Coincidental Module: [0.85, 0.10]
```
