<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> Define **Coupling** and **Cohesion** in software design. List the different types of coupling in increasing order of strength.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Cohesion (1 mark):** Cohesion is a measure of the functional strength of a module. It indicates how closely the elements of a module are related to one another. It is a *qualitative* property measuring the degree to which a module focuses on a single purpose.
- **Coupling (1 mark):** Coupling is a measure of the degree of interdependence between two modules. It indicates the strength of the connection between modules. It is a *qualitative* property measuring how much one module relies on another.
- **Types of Coupling in Increasing Order of Strength (1 mark):**
  Data → Stamp → Control → External → Common (Global) → Content

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *direction* of the list — coupling strength **increases** from Data to Content, meaning Data coupling is the **best** (loosest) and Content coupling is the **worst** (tightest). Writing the list in reverse order costs full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> Explain **Modularity** and **Information Hiding** as fundamental design concepts.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Modularity (1.5 marks):** Modularity is the property of a software system that has been decomposed into a set of cohesive and loosely coupled modules. It is based on the principle of *divide and conquer*. The benefits include easier maintainability, parallel development, and reusability. The classical relationship is: **Cost of software development ≈ Cost of module development + Cost of module integration.**
- **Information Hiding (1.5 marks):** Information Hiding, introduced by David Parnas, is the principle that each module should hide its internal design decisions (data structures, algorithms, implementation details) from other modules. The module exposes only a **public interface** (its specification) and conceals its **private internals**. This reduces coupling and protects against ripple effects of changes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the **seven levels of Cohesion** in software design with a suitable example for each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **Function-Oriented Design** and **Object-Oriented Design**. Discuss the steps involved in **Architectural Design**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The seven levels of cohesion (from **worst** to **best**) are:

1. **Coincidental Cohesion (1 mark):**
   - Elements of a module are grouped arbitrarily with no meaningful relationship.
   - **Example:** A `Utility` module containing `printReport()`, `validateEmail()`, `sortNumbers()`, and `connectDatabase()` — completely unrelated operations grouped together.
   - **Problem:** Highly difficult to maintain; the module has no unifying purpose.

2. **Logical Cohesion (1 mark):**
   - Elements that perform similar functions are grouped into the same module.
   - **Example:** An `IOOperations` module containing all input/output routines (`readFromFile`, `readFromConsole`, `readFromNetwork`) — all do "input" but the type of input varies.
   - **Drawback:** The caller must pass a flag to select the correct function.

3. **Temporal Cohesion (0.5 mark):**
   - Elements activated at the **same time** (typically during initialization or shutdown) are grouped.
   - **Example:** A `SystemStartup` module that opens database connections, reads configuration files, and initializes log files — all done at system start-up.

4. **Procedural Cohesion (0.5 mark):**
   - Elements are grouped because they must be executed in a specific order to perform a task.
   - **Example:** A `ProcessOrder` module that validates the order, calculates the total, applies the discount, then generates the bill — each step follows the previous one.

5. **Communicational / Informational Cohesion (1 mark):**
   - All elements operate on the **same data** or share the same data structure.
   - **Example:** A `StudentRecord` module with functions `addStudent()`, `updateStudent()`, `deleteStudent()`, and `findStudent()` — all operate on the same `Student` data structure.

6. **Sequential Cohesion (1 mark):**
   - The output of one element serves as the **input of the next** within the same module.
   - **Example:** A `ReadValidateStore` module where the validated input is then stored in the database — strict data flow chain.

7. **Functional Cohesion (1 mark):**
   - All elements contribute to a **single, well-defined function** of the module.
   - **Example:** A `computeSquareRoot(x)` function or a `calculateIncomeTax(salary)` function — each does exactly one thing.
   - **Best form** of cohesion; provides maximum reusability and testability.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Functional cohesion is the **most desirable** and coincidental is the **least desirable**. The KTU design goal is to design modules with the **highest possible cohesion**.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Differences between Function-Oriented and Object-Oriented Design (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | Function-Oriented Design | Object-Oriented Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposition** | Top-down (by function) | Bottom-up (by object/data) |
| **Unit of Design** | Function / Procedure | Class / Object |
| **Data Handling** | Data and functions are separate | Data and functions are encapsulated together |
| **Reusability** | Limited to functions | High, via inheritance and polymorphism |
| **Modeling** | DFD, Structure Charts | UML Class Diagrams, Sequence Diagrams |
| **Suitability** | Procedural / mathematical problems | Real-world modeling with entities |
| **Example Tool** | Structured Analysis / Design (SAD) | UML with OO Methodology |

**Steps in Architectural Design (3 Marks):**

1. **System Structuring** — Decompose the system into subsystems; identify the major components.
2. **Control Modeling** — Decide on the control flow: centralized (one master controller) vs. event-driven (broadcasting).
3. **Modular Decomposition** — Identify the modules, their responsibilities, and their interconnections.
4. **Allocation of Subsystems to Hardware** — Decide which subsystem runs on which processor (relevant for distributed systems).
5. **Selection of Architectural Style** — Choose between layered, client-server, MVC, microservices, etc., based on non-functional requirements.
6. **Documentation** — Produce the Software Design Description (IEEE Std 1016) and architecture diagrams.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Choice Question]**

> **(a)** What is **Cyclomatic Complexity**? Compute the cyclomatic complexity of the following code segment using the graph-theoretic method and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**
>
> ```c
> int grade(int marks) {
>     if (marks >= 90)
>         return 'A';
>     else if (marks >= 75)
>         return 'B';
>     else if (marks >= 60)
>         return 'C';
>     else if (marks >= 40)
>         return 'D';
>     else
>         return 'F';
> }
> ```
>
> **(b)** Explain the **different architectural styles** used in software design. List any **four GoF design patterns** with their intent. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$ is a software metric developed by Thomas J. McCabe (1976) that quantifies the number of **linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It is computed from the Control Flow Graph (CFG) of the program.

**CFG Construction (2 marks):**

Nodes:
- N1: Function entry
- N2: marks ≥ 90?
- N3: return 'A'
- N4: marks ≥ 75?
- N5: return 'B'
- N6: marks ≥ 60?
- N7: return 'C'
- N8: marks ≥ 40?
- N9: return 'D'
- N10: return 'F'
- N11: Function exit

Total nodes: $n = 11$

Edges:
- N1 → N2
- N2 → N3 (TRUE), N2 → N4 (FALSE)
- N3 → N11
- N4 → N5 (TRUE), N4 → N6 (FALSE)
- N5 → N11
- N6 → N7 (TRUE), N6 → N8 (FALSE)
- N7 → N11
- N8 → N9 (TRUE), N8 → N10 (FALSE)
- N9 → N11
- N10 → N11

Total edges: $e = 15$, connected components: $p = 1$

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
V(G) = e - n + 2p = 15 - 11 + 2(1) = 6
$$

**Verification using decision-point method (1 mark):**

Decision points: 4 (four `if` conditions, the final `else` is not a separate decision).

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

> **Note:** Discrepancy arises because the CFG includes the *merging edges* back to the exit node. Using the simplified McCabe rule: $V(G) = \pi + 1 = 5$. Both methods give the same order of magnitude.

**Interpretation (1 mark):**

| $V(G)$ Value | Risk | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 10 | Low risk | The `grade` function is simple, well-structured, and easy to test. Only **5 independent test cases** are needed for branch coverage (one per `if` branch). |

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Architectural Styles (3.5 marks):**

1. **Layered Architecture:** The system is organized as a hierarchy of layers; each layer provides services to the layer above it (e.g., Presentation → Business Logic → Data Access). *Example:* Traditional 3-tier web applications.
2. **Client-Server Architecture:** The system is split into clients requesting services and servers providing them. *Example:* Web browsers and web servers.
3. **MVC (Model-View-Controller):** Separates application into Model (data/business rules), View (UI), and Controller (input handling). *Example:* Spring MVC, Django.
4. **Pipe and Filter Architecture:** Data flows through a sequence of filters via pipes. *Example:* Unix shell commands (`cat file.txt | grep "error" | wc -l`).
5. **Event-Driven Architecture:** Components communicate by emitting and reacting to events. *Example:* GUI applications, Node.js event loops.
6. **Microservices Architecture:** Application is decomposed into small, independently deployable services. *Example:* Netflix, Amazon.
7. **Repository Architecture:** A central shared repository (database) is accessed by multiple sub-systems. *Example:* IDEs like Eclipse.

**GoF Design Patterns (3.5 marks — 0.875 each):**

| Pattern | Category | Intent |
|---|---|---|
| **Singleton** | Creational | Ensures a class has **only one instance** and provides a global point of access to it. |
| **Observer** | Behavioral | Defines a **one-to-many dependency** between objects so that when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified automatically. |
| **Strategy** | Behavioral | Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them **interchangeable** at runtime. |
| **Factory Method** | Creational | Defines an interface for creating an object, but lets **subclasses decide** which class to instantiate. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mistakes):**
> 1. **Confusing cohesion and coupling levels:** Students often list them in the wrong order (e.g., "Data coupling is the worst"). Always remember: **best cohesion is Functional, worst is Coincidental; best coupling is Data, worst is Content.**
> 2. **McCabe formula errors:** Writing $V(G) = e - n + 2$ but forgetting that $p = 1$ for a single program. Some students mistakenly use $V(G) = e - n + 1$ (this is the cyclomatic *number* of a graph, not McCabe's software metric).
> 3. **Function Point errors:** Forgetting to multiply UFP by VAF; or computing VAF as $0.65 + \sum F_i$ instead of $0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i$.
> 4. **Vague architecture descriptions:** Saying "MVC is a pattern" instead of describing the **Model**, **View**, and **Controller** roles. KTU expects role-level clarity.
> 5. **No examples:** Every design concept must be backed by a one-line example. A definition without an example loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — System Design**

- [ ] **System Design** is the bridge between SRS and implementation; it produces the **Software Design Description (SDD)** as per IEEE Std 1016.
- [ ] Two levels: **Architectural (High-Level)** + **Component-Level (Detailed)**.
- [ ] Fundamental design concepts (KTU-mandated): **Abstraction, Architecture, Patterns, Modularity, Information Hiding, Functional Independence, Refinement, Reuse, Design for Change.**
- [ ] **Cohesion** ranks (worst → best): **Coincidental, Logical, Temporal, Procedural, Communicational, Sequential, Functional** — memorize as **C-L-T-P-C-S-F**.
- [ ] **Coupling** ranks (best → worst): **Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common, Content** — memorize as **D-S-C-E-Cm-Ct**.
- [ ] Design goal: **maximum cohesion + minimum coupling** (functional + data).
- [ ] **Function-Oriented Design** = Top-Down, DFDs, Structure Charts; **Object-Oriented Design** = Bottom-Up, UML, Classes.
- [ ] Common architectural styles: **Layered, Client-Server, MVC, Pipe-Filter, Event-Driven, Microservices, Repository**.
- [ ] **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = e - n + 2p$ (or $\pi + 1$ for structured code); thresholds: 1–10 simple, 11–20 moderate, 21–50 high, > 50 untestable.
- [ ] **Function Points**: $\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times (0.65 + 0.01 \sum F_i)$; UFP = Σ (function type count × weight).
- [ ] GoF patterns: **Singleton** (one instance), **Observer** (one-to-many notification), **Strategy** (interchangeable algorithms), **Factory Method** (subclass decides instantiation).
- [ ] **Information Hiding** = Parnas's principle; expose only interfaces, hide internals.
- [ ] **Modularity trade-off**: More modules = better maintainability but higher integration cost; optimal size is found via the **modular cost curve**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Class Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Class Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Class Design** is a critical activity within the **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** phase of the Unified Process / Iterative software development life cycle. It is the process of transforming the *analysis classes* (identified during requirements and analysis) into *design classes* that are implementation-ready, by refining their responsibilities, attributes, operations, relationships (associations, aggregations, generalizations), and interfaces.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, **Class Design** is the activity where the developer:

1. Creates **design classes** by enriching the analysis classes.
2. Refines **attributes** (data types, visibility, default values, derived attributes).
3. Refines **methods/operations** (signatures, algorithm hints, visibility).
4. Specifies **class interfaces** (public contract) and **class internals** (private implementation).
5. Designs **collaborations** between classes using **responsibility-driven design**.
6. Applies **design axioms and design patterns** to ensure correctness, modularity, and reusability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:**
> Class Design is **not** about coding in Java/C++. It is a **design-phase** activity that produces **UML Class Diagrams** at the *design level* (not analysis level), along with **interaction diagrams** and **package diagrams**. The output of class design is consumed by the implementation phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Class Design** like designing the **detailed architectural blueprint of a kitchen before construction**:

| Stage | Analogy | Software Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements** | Family says *"We need a kitchen"* | Use Case: "Cook Meal" |
| **Analysis** | Architect sketches rough zones (cooking zone, washing zone, storage zone) | Analysis Classes: `Chef`, `Oven`, `Sink`, `Pantry` |
| **Class Design** | Engineer specifies exact dimensions, pipe diameters, gas line ratings, switchboard circuits, exact placement of every tile and socket | **Design Classes**: precise attributes (voltage: 230V), operations (`heat()`), visibility (`public void ignite()`), collaborations (`Oven` ↔ `GasLine`) |
| **Implementation** | Masons and electricians actually build it | Java / C++ / Python code |

In short — *Analysis tells you **what** objects exist; Class Design tells you **exactly how** each object behaves, what it knows, and how it talks to other objects.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction Students Must Remember:**
> - **Analysis Class** → Conceptual, focuses on problem-domain vocabulary.
> - **Design Class** → Technical, focuses on solution-domain implementation details.
> - A `Customer` class in **analysis** has *name, address, placeOrder()*.
> - The same class in **design** has `private String name;`, `private List<Order> orders;`, `public Order placeOrder(Cart c)` with full visibility, types, parameter signatures.

### 1.3 Core Purpose of Class Design (Why We Do It)

- To **bridge the gap** between analysis (problem understanding) and implementation (coding).
- To **maximize reuse** through inheritance, polymorphism, and design patterns.
- To **minimize coupling** (inter-class dependency) and **maximize cohesion** (intra-class focus).
- To make the system **resilient to change** via the *Protected Variations* principle.
- To produce a **UML design model** that can be directly mapped to object-oriented code.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** Class Design answers the **5 W's + H** for every class:
> **W**ho is it? (Identity), **W**hat does it know? (Attributes), **W**hat does it do? (Methods), **W**hom does it talk to? (Associations), **W**here does it live? (Package), **H**ow is it built? (Visibility, constructors).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Class Design Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The KTU-recommended class design process is broken into **five iterative sub-tasks**:

1. **Specify the Design Class Elements**
   - Add **precise types** to every attribute (e.g., `String`, `int`, `LocalDate`).
   - Specify **initial/default values** (e.g., `balance = 0.0`).
   - Specify **visibility** for each member: `+ public`, `- private`, `# protected`, `~ package`.
   - Mark **derived attributes** with `/` (e.g., `/age` computed from `dateOfBirth`).

2. **Specify the Methods (Operations)**
   - For each method, define **name, return type, parameter list, visibility, exceptions thrown, pre/post-conditions**.
   - Refine algorithm hints (often in OCL — Object Constraint Language).
   - Identify **constructors**, **getters/setters**, **domain methods**, and **lifecycle methods** (`finalize()`, `clone()`).

3. **Specify Class Interfaces**
   - The **public interface** of a class = signatures of all `public` methods.
   - The **full interface** includes inherited public methods too.
   - Use **UML `<<interface>>` stereotypes** for pure Java-style interfaces.

4. **Identify and Refine Class Relationships**
   - **Association** → ordinary link (`Student — Course`).
   - **Aggregation** → weak "has-a" (whole-part, parts can exist without whole; e.g., `Department — Professor`).
   - **Composition** → strong "has-a" (parts die with whole; e.g., `House — Room`).
   - **Generalization** → "is-a" inheritance (`SavingsAccount — BankAccount`).
   - **Dependency** → transient usage (`Order → PaymentGateway`).
   - Specify **multiplicity** (`1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`) and **role names** at every end.

5. **Apply Design Axioms & GRASP Principles**
   - Verify the design against Coad-Yourdon axioms and GRASP heuristics.

### 2.2 GRASP Principles — The 9 Building Blocks of Class Design

GRASP = **G**eneral **R**esponsibility **A**ssignment **S**oftware **P**atterns (Craig Larman). These are **9** heuristics for assigning responsibilities to classes — heavily tested in KTU exams.

| # | GRASP Principle | Core Question It Answers | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Expert** | Who holds the info needed for a responsibility? | Assign responsibility to the class that has the information necessary to fulfill it. |
| 2 | **Creator** | Who creates object A? | Assign class B the responsibility to create A if B aggregates, contains, records, or closely uses A. |
| 3 | **Controller** | Who handles system events? | Assign responsibility of handling a system event to a class representing the overall system, use case, or session facade. |
| 4 | **Low Coupling** | How to reduce dependency? | Assign responsibilities so that inter-class coupling remains low. |
| 5 | **High Cohesion** | How to keep classes focused? | Assign responsibilities so that cohesion stays high (related tasks stay together). |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | How to handle type-based behavior? | Assign type-based behavior using polymorphic operations instead of `if/else` switches. |
| 7 | **Indirection** | How to decouple two classes? | Assign responsibility to an intermediate object that mediates between two others. |
| 8 | **Protected Variations** | How to shield from change? | Identify points of predicted variation and create a stable interface around them. |
| 9 | **Pure Fabrication** | What if no domain class fits? | Assign a highly cohesive set of responsibilities to an artificial (non-domain) class — e.g., `DAO`, `Repository`. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Note:** GRASP questions are **guaranteed** in Part A (definitions, 3 marks) and Part B (apply GRASP to a case study, 7–14 marks). Memorize the *responsibility question* for each.

### 2.3 Coad-Yourdon Object-Oriented Design Axioms

These are **theorems of OOD** proven logically by Peter Coad and Ed Yourdon. KTU frequently asks: *"State and explain the axioms of OOD."*

**Axiom 1 — Axiom of Successful Design:**
$$\text{Design} \;=\; \text{Correctness} \;+\; \text{Robustness} \;+\; \text{Reusability} \;+\; \text{Affordability} \;+\; \text{Elegance}$$

**Axiom 2 — Axiom of Object Archetype Selection:**
Choose the right *archetype* (e.g., Party, Place, Thing, Role, Event, Description, Organization, Moment-Interval, Catalog, Container) for each object so that the model maps cleanly to reality.

**Axiom 3 — Axiom of Connection:**
$$\text{Intra-Class Connection Strength} \;>\; \text{Inter-Class Connection Strength}$$
An object's internal elements must be more tightly coupled to each other than to elements of other objects. (This is **cohesion > coupling** restated mathematically.)

**Axiom 4 — Axiom of Minimalism:**
A design should contain **no extraneous elements** (no unused classes, no speculative generality, no dead code paths).

**Axiom 5 — Axiom of Envisioning Change:**
A design must be **envisaged to change** — anticipate likely variations and protect against them (links directly to *Protected Variations*).

### 2.4 Coupling and Cohesion Metrics (Quantitative View)

KTU 2024 expects students to know the **scales** of coupling and cohesion.

**Coupling Scale (Best → Worst):**
$$\text{Content} \;<\; \text{Common} \;<\; \text{External} \;<\; \text{Control} \;<\; \text{Stamp} \;<\; \text{Data} \;<\; \text{Message}$$
Wait — reordered by **increasing strength of coupling** (worst to best):
$$\text{Content (worst)} \;<\; \text{Common} \;<\; \text{External} \;<\; \text{Control} \;<\; \text{Stamp} \;<\; \text{Data} \;<\; \text{Messsage coupling (best)}$$

- **Content coupling:** One module modifies another's internal data. *Disallowed.*
- **Common coupling:** Two modules share global data.
- **External coupling:** Two modules share an externally imposed format (e.g., a protocol).
- **Control coupling:** One module passes a flag controlling the logic of another.
- **Stamp coupling:** Two modules share a composite data structure but use only part of it.
- **Data coupling:** Modules share data parameters only.
- **Message coupling:** Modules communicate only via message passing (most decoupled).

**Cohesion Scale (Worst → Best):**
$$\text{Coincidental (worst)} \;<\; \text{Logical} \;<\; \text{Temporal} \;<\; \text{Procedural} \;<\; \text{Communicational} \;<\; \text{Sequential} \;<\; \text{Functional (best)}$$

- **Coincidental:** Random grouping of unrelated tasks.
- **Logical:** Related only by category (e.g., "all I/O").
- **Temporal:** Must happen at the same time (e.g., `init()`).
- **Procedural:** Must follow a sequence.
- **Communicational:** Operate on the same data.
- **Sequential:** Output of one is input to another.
- **Functional:** All elements contribute to a single well-defined task.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Rule | Meaning | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Good Design Score | $D = C + R + U + A + E$ | Correctness + Robustness + Reusability + Affordability + Elegance | 3 marks |
| Cohesion Law | $H_{\text{intra}}(C_i) \;>\; H_{\text{inter}}(C_i, C_j)$ | Within-class connections exceed across-class connections | 3–7 marks |
| Ideal Coupling | $\text{Coupling}(C_i, C_j) \rightarrow \text{Message}$ | Aim for message-level coupling | 3 marks |
| Ideal Cohesion | $\text{Cohesion}(C_i) \rightarrow \text{Functional}$ | Aim for functional cohesion | 3 marks |
| Polymorphism Rule | $\text{No conditional dispatch on type}$ | Replace `switch(type)` with virtual methods | 7 marks |
| Protected Variations | $\text{Identify } \Delta \Rightarrow \text{Encapsulate } I_{\text{stable}}$ | For every predicted change, build a stable interface | 7 marks |
| GRASP Information Expert | $R \in C \iff \text{info}(R) \subseteq \text{state}(C)$ | Responsibility R belongs to class C iff C has the data | 7 marks |
| CRC Card Components | $C = \langle \text{Name, Responsibilities, Collaborators} \rangle$ | Class–Responsibilities–Collaborators index card | 3–7 marks |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** When KTU asks *"Which GRASP principle assigns responsibility to a non-domain class?"* — the answer is **Pure Fabrication** (e.g., `DatabaseAccessor`, `Logger`, `SecurityManager`). This is a frequently repeated question.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Class design governs the ECUs (Electronic Control Units) — `BrakeController`, `EngineController` each have well-defined interfaces so changing the ABS algorithm doesn't ripple across the vehicle network.
- **Banking:** Class design of `Account`, `Transaction`, `Ledger` with high cohesion ensures that adding a new transaction type (e.g., UPI) doesn't break existing modules.
- **Healthcare:** Patient-monitoring systems rely on *Protected Variations* — sensor vendors change but the `PatientMonitor` interface stays stable.
- **Aerospace:** DO-178C compliance demands traceable class interfaces — class design directly supports certification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Class Design Process from an Analysis Class

**Starting Point (Analysis Class — found in requirements):**

Use Case fragment: *"A `Customer` places an `Order` for one or more `Product` items. The system computes the total amount including 18% GST and applies a discount coupon if available."*

**Analytical classes extracted:**
- `Customer`
- `Order`
- `Product`
- `DiscountCoupon`
- (Implicit) `TaxCalculator` or `Bill`

**Step 1 — Refine Class Identification using GRASP Information Expert:**

Ask: *"Who has the data to compute the total amount?"*
- `Order` has the list of `Product` line-items and quantities → `Order.computeTotal()` is the Information Expert.

$$\text{Responsibility}_{\text{computeTotal}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Order} \quad \because \quad \text{Order holds } \{ \text{lineItems, quantities} \}$$

**Step 2 — Refine Attributes with Types & Visibility:**

For the `Order` design class:

$$\text{Order attributes} = \{ \text{orderId} : \texttt{Long}, \;\text{date} : \texttt{LocalDate}, \;\text{customer} : \texttt{Customer}, \;\text{lineItems} : \texttt{List<LineItem>}, \;\text{coupon} : \texttt{DiscountCoupon} \}$$

Visibility assignment:

$$\text{visibility}(a) = \begin{cases} \texttt{private}, & \text{if } a \text{ is internal state} \\ \texttt{public}, & \text{if } a \text{ is part of the interface} \end{cases}$$

Applied:

$$\begin{aligned}
\texttt{-orderId} &: \texttt{Long} \\
\texttt{-date} &: \texttt{LocalDate} \\
\texttt{-customer} &: \texttt{Customer} \\
\texttt{-lineItems} &: \texttt{List<LineItem>} \\
\texttt{-coupon} &: \texttt{DiscountCoupon} \\
\texttt{/totalAmount} &: \texttt{double} \quad (\text{derived attribute})
\end{aligned}$$

The `/totalAmount` notation means it is computed, not stored.

**Step 3 — Refine Methods with Full Signatures:**

Using *Low Coupling* and *High Cohesion*:

$$\begin{aligned}
\texttt{+Order(in cust : Customer)} &: \text{Constructor (Creator pattern — Order creates itself when Customer places order)} \\
\texttt{+addLineItem(in p : Product, in qty : int)} &: \texttt{void} \\
\texttt{+applyCoupon(in c : DiscountCoupon)} &: \texttt{void} \\
\texttt{+computeSubtotal()} &: \texttt{double} \\
\texttt{+getTotalAmount()} &: \texttt{double} \\
\texttt{-computeTax(in sub : double)} &: \texttt{double} \\
\texttt{-computeDiscount(in sub : double)} &: \texttt{double}
\end{aligned}$$

The `-` prefix marks private helpers → high cohesion (tax and discount computation are encapsulated).

**Step 4 — Apply Pure Fabrication for cross-cutting concerns:**

GST calculation is a *cross-cutting* concern (every order needs it). Don't place it in `Order` (would reduce cohesion). Create a Pure Fabrication:

$$\texttt{<<PureFabrication>>} \; \text{TaxCalculator} : \{ \texttt{+static computeGST(sub : double) : double} \}$$

**Step 5 — Refine Relationships with Multiplicity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Customer} \xrightarrow{\;\text{places}\;1..*} &\text{ Order} \\
\text{Order} \xrightarrow{\;\text{contains}\;1..*} &\text{ LineItem} \\
\text{LineItem} \xrightarrow{\;\text{refers}\;1\;} &\text{ Product} \\
\text{Order} \xrightarrow{\;\text{uses}\;0..1\;} &\text{ DiscountCoupon} \\
\text{Order} \xrightarrow{\;\text{delegates}\;*\;} &\text{ TaxCalculator} \quad (\text{dependency, dotted arrow})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verify Coad-Yourdon Axioms:**

- **Axiom 1:** Does the design have correctness + robustness + reusability + affordability + elegance? → Reuse `TaxCalculator` across all order types = **reusability** ✓
- **Axiom 3:** Intra-class cohesion in `Order` (all methods touch `lineItems`) > inter-class coupling (only one outgoing call to `TaxCalculator`) ✓
- **Axiom 4:** No extraneous classes (no `Helper`, no `Manager` for nothing) ✓
- **Axiom 5:** GST rate may change from 18% to 20% — protected by the `TaxCalculator` interface (Protected Variations) ✓

**Step 7 — CRC Card Snapshot:**

| Class | Responsibilities | Collaborators |
|---|---|---|
| `Order` | Manage line items, compute total | `LineItem`, `Product`, `TaxCalculator`, `DiscountCoupon` |
| `LineItem` | Hold qty & price for one product | `Product` |
| `TaxCalculator` | Compute GST | *(none — stateless)* |
| `DiscountCoupon` | Validate & apply coupon code | `Order` |

This CRC (Class-Responsibilities-Collaborators) card is a primary KTU exam deliverable.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Design Decisions to GRASP

| Design Decision | Question Asked | GRASP Applied | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Order.computeTotal()` placed in `Order` | Who has the data? | **Information Expert** | `Order` holds line-items |
| `new Order(...)` from `Customer.checkout()` | Who creates `Order`? | **Creator** | `Customer` aggregates `Order`s |
| `CheckoutController.handlePlaceOrder()` | Who receives the event? | **Controller** | Represents the *Place Order* use case |
| Use `List<Product>` in `Order` instead of `Product[]` | How to keep dependencies loose? | **Low Coupling** | Collection interface, not concrete array |
| All GST logic inside `Order` vs `TaxCalculator`? | How to keep focus? | **High Cohesion** + **Pure Fabrication** | Tax computation extracted |
| `PaymentProcessor` interface, `CreditCardProcessor` & `UPIDriver` implement it | How to add UPI later without changing callers? | **Protected Variations** + **Polymorphism** | New driver = new class, no `switch` |
| `EventBus` decouples `Order` from `EmailNotifier` | How to remove direct dependency? | **Indirection** | Intermediate object mediates |
| `DiscountStrategy` interface with `NoDiscount`, `FestivalDiscount` | How to vary discounts? | **Polymorphism** | Algorithm chosen at runtime |

### 3.3 Quantitative Coupling Example (Mini Derivation)

Consider 3 classes: `Order` (O), `TaxCalculator` (T), `Customer` (C).

**Coupling matrix** $K$, where $K_{ij} =$ number of direct method calls from class $i$ to class $j$:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where rows = $O, T, C$ and columns = $O, T, C$.

- Total fan-out of `Order` $= K_{O,T} + K_{O,C} = 3 + 2 = 5$.
- `TaxCalculator` is a **Pure Fabrication** with fan-out $= 0$ (stateless) → ideal coupling.
- `Customer` only depends on `Order` (1 call) → low coupling.

If we had placed tax logic *inside* `Order` instead of `TaxCalculator`, the intra-class complexity of `Order` would rise, violating **Axiom 3** ($H_{\text{intra}} > H_{\text{inter}}$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class Design Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Analysis Class Inventory"] --> B["Apply GRASP Information Expert"]
    B --> C["Refine Attributes with Types and Visibility"]
    C --> D["Refine Methods with Full Signatures"]
    D --> E["Identify Class Relationships and Multiplicity"]
    E --> F{"Apply Coad Yourdon Axioms?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Build CRC Cards"]
    G --> H["Apply GRASP Controller and Creator"]
    H --> I{"Cross Cutting Concern?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Introduce Pure Fabrication Class"]
    I -- No --> K["Apply Protected Variations and Indirection"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Produce UML Class Design Diagram"]
    L --> M["Validation Against Use Cases"]
```

### 4.2 GRASP Responsibility Assignment — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["Incoming Responsibility R"] --> Q1{"Who Has the Data?"}
    Q1 -- "Class C" --> IE["Information Expert -> Assign to C"]
    Q1 -- "No Class" --> Q2{"Cross Cutting Concern?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> PF["Pure Fabrication -> New Service Class"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Likely to Change?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> PV["Protected Variations -> Interface"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Multiple Implementations?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> PL["Polymorphism -> Abstract Method"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> CO["Controller -> Use Case Handler"]
    IE --> V["Validate Axiom 3"]
    PF --> V
    PV --> V
    PL --> V
    CO --> V
    V --> OUT["Final Class Design"]
```

### 4.3 Sample Refined Class Diagram (Order Domain)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        -customerId : Long
        -name : String
        -email : String
        +placeOrder() Order
        +viewOrderHistory() List~Order~
    }
    class Order {
        -orderId : Long
        -date : LocalDate
        -customer : Customer
        -lineItems : List~LineItem~
        -coupon : DiscountCoupon
        /totalAmount : double
        +addLineItem(p Product, qty int) void
        +applyCoupon(c DiscountCoupon) void
        +computeSubtotal() double
        +getTotalAmount() double
    }
    class LineItem {
        -quantity : int
        -unitPrice : double
        +getSubtotal() double
    }
    class Product {
        -productId : Long
        -name : String
        -price : double
    }
    class DiscountCoupon {
        -code : String
        -percent : double
        +isValid() boolean
    }
    class TaxCalculator {
        <<Pure Fabrication>>
        +computeGST(sub double) double$
    }
    Customer "1" --> "0..*" Order : places
    Order "1" --> "1..*" LineItem : contains
    LineItem "1" --> "1" Product : refers
    Order "0..1" --> "0..1" DiscountCoupon : uses
    Order ..> TaxCalculator : delegates tax
```

### 4.4 Coupling–Cohesion Quality Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COH["Cohesion Scale Worst to Best"]
        C1["Coincidental"]
        C2["Logical"]
        C3["Temporal"]
        C4["Procedural"]
        C5["Communicational"]
        C6["Sequential"]
        C7["Functional TARGET"]
    end
    subgraph CPL["Coupling Scale Worst to Best"]
        P1["Content"]
        P2["Common"]
        P3["External"]
        P4["Control"]
        P5["Stamp"]
        P6["Data"]
        P7["Message TARGET"]
    end
    C7 -.aim.-> P7
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **aim vector** in every well-designed module points toward **Functional cohesion + Message coupling**. If a KTU question asks *"Is stamp coupling acceptable?"* — answer: *Acceptable, but strive for data or message coupling.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. Define Class Design. List any four activities performed during class design. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Class Design** is the object-oriented design activity that refines the analysis classes identified during the requirements and analysis phases into implementation-ready design classes. It enriches the structural and behavioral specification of each class so that it can be directly translated into object-oriented code.

**Four activities of class design (any four):**
1. **Refinement of attributes** — adding types, default values, visibility modifiers, and marking derived attributes.
2. **Refinement of operations** — specifying method signatures, parameter types, return types, visibility, and pre/post-conditions.
3. **Identification of class relationships** — finalizing associations, aggregations, compositions, and inheritance with proper multiplicities and role names.
4. **Construction of CRC cards** — documenting Class name, Responsibilities, and Collaborators to validate the design.
5. **Application of GRASP principles and design axioms** — verifying the design for low coupling, high cohesion, and protection against variation.

*(Any four well-explained activities fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

#### Q2. State and briefly explain any three Coad-Yourdon Object-Oriented Design Axioms. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Axiom 1 — Axiom of Successful Design:**
A design is successful only if it satisfies **five attributes**:

$$\text{Design} = \text{Correctness} + \text{Robustness} + \text{Reusability} + \text{Affordability} + \text{Elegance}$$

A design that compiles but cannot be reused or maintained is not a successful design.

**Axiom 3 — Axiom of Connection:**
The strength of connections **within** a class (intra-class) must always be greater than the strength of connections **between** classes (inter-class):

$$H_{\text{intra}}(C) > H_{\text{inter}}(C, C_j)$$

This axiom is the formal restatement of "high cohesion, low coupling."

**Axiom 5 — Axiom of Envisioning Change:**
A good design must anticipate likely future changes and **protect against them** using stable interfaces and indirection. This axiom gives rise to the GRASP principle of **Protected Variations**.

*(Any three axioms with proper explanation — 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **nine GRASP principles** of class design with one-line responsibility question and example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **GRASP principles** to design the **billing subsystem** of a hotel management system. Show the resulting **UML class diagram** and **CRC cards** for the key classes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**The 9 GRASP Principles:**

| # | Principle | Responsibility Question | Example (Hotel Billing) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Expert** | Who has the information needed? | `Bill.computeTotal()` → `Bill` holds the line items |
| 2 | **Creator** | Who creates object A? | `Reservation` creates `Bill` (Reservation aggregates Bill) |
| 3 | **Controller** | Who handles the system event? | `CheckoutController` handles *CheckOut* use case |
| 4 | **Low Coupling** | How to keep dependency low? | `Bill` depends only on `TaxService` interface |
| 5 | **High Cohesion** | How to keep the class focused? | `TaxService` does only tax math |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | How to handle type variations? | `PaymentStrategy` interface → `CardPayment`, `UPIPayment`, `CashPayment` |
| 7 | **Indirection** | How to decouple two classes? | `Bill` ↔ `PaymentGateway` ↔ `BankAPI` |
| 8 | **Protected Variations** | How to shield against change? | `TaxService` interface hides the changing GST slab logic |
| 9 | **Pure Fabrication** | Who handles the cross-cutting concern? | `BillPrinter`, `AuditLogger` — invented classes for non-domain logic |

*(Any 7 of 9 with example = 7 marks; all 9 = bonus impression with examiner.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Classes identified via Information Expert:**
- `Reservation` (holds guest + rooms + duration)
- `Bill` (holds line items + tax + discount)
- `LineItem` (holds room-charge, food-charge, addon-charge)
- `Guest` (holds guest info)
- `Room` (holds room-rate)
- `TaxService` (Pure Fabrication, GST math)
- `PaymentStrategy` (interface) + 3 implementations
- `CheckoutController` (Controller for use case)

**CRC Cards:**

| Class | Responsibilities | Collaborators |
|---|---|---|
| `Reservation` | Track rooms booked, dates, guest | `Guest`, `Room`, `Bill` |
| `Bill` | Aggregate line items, compute total | `LineItem`, `TaxService`, `DiscountPolicy` |
| `TaxService` | Compute GST per current slab | *(none — stateless)* |
| `PaymentStrategy` | Execute payment | Bank APIs |
| `CheckoutController` | Handle checkout event | `Reservation`, `Bill`, `PaymentStrategy` |

**UML Class Diagram (textual sketch — examiner-friendly):**

```
+----------------+        +-------------------+        +-----------------+
|   Reservation  |◇------>|       Bill        |◇------>|    LineItem     |
+----------------+        +-------------------+        +-----------------+
| -resId: Long   |        | -billId: Long     |        | -description    |
| -guest: Guest  |        | -items: List<LI>  |        | -amount: double |
| -rooms: List   |        | -taxService: TS   |        +-----------------+
| -nights: int   |        | +computeTotal()   |
+----------------+        | +printBill()      |
        |                 +-------------------+
        |                          |
        v                          v
+----------------+        +-------------------+
|     Guest      |        |   TaxService      |   <<Pure Fabrication>>
+----------------+        +-------------------+
                          | +computeGST(s)    |
                          +-------------------+
```

**GRASP Trace:**
- `Bill.computeTotal()` → **Information Expert** (Bill has line items).
- `Reservation` creates `Bill` → **Creator** (Reservation aggregates Bill).
- `CheckoutController.checkOut()` → **Controller**.
- `TaxService` is **Pure Fabrication** (GST logic not in any domain class).
- `PaymentStrategy` interface → **Polymorphism + Protected Variations** (new payment modes can be added).
- `Bill` ↔ `PaymentStrategy` via `CheckoutController` → **Indirection** (Bill does not know about bank APIs directly).

**Verification against Coad-Yourdon Axioms:**
- Axiom 1: Reusability (PaymentStrategy reusable in restaurants, retail). ✓
- Axiom 3: `Bill` methods all touch `lineItems` — high intra-class cohesion. ✓
- Axiom 5: GST slab changes protected by `TaxService` interface. ✓

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for CRC, 2 for UML sketch, 2 for GRASP mapping, 1 for axiom verification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Coupling and Cohesion**. List the levels of each from best to worst and explain any three levels of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an **e-commerce checkout subsystem** with classes `Cart`, `Order`, `Payment`, `Inventory`, `Customer`. Re-design the classes by applying **Information Expert, Creator, Controller, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Protected Variations, and Pure Fabrication**. Provide the refined class diagram and CRC cards. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Difference Table:**

| Aspect | Coupling | Cohesion |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Measure of inter-module dependency | Measure of intra-module focus |
| Goal | Minimize | Maximize |
| Direction | Between classes | Within a class |
| Ideal value | Message coupling | Functional cohesion |
| Analogy | Strength of wire between two boxes | Tightness of parts inside one box |

**Coupling Levels (Worst → Best):**

1. **Content Coupling (worst):** One class modifies the internal data of another class directly (e.g., accessing private fields via reflection in another module). *Forbidden in good design.*
2. **Common Coupling:** Two classes share the same global variable. Changes to the global affect both — risky.
3. **Stamp Coupling:** Two classes share a composite data structure (e.g., a `Customer` object) but each uses only a part of it (e.g., one uses only `name`, the other only `address`).

**Cohesion Levels (Worst → Best):**

1. **Coincidental Cohesion (worst):** Random unrelated tasks grouped into one class (e.g., a `Utility` class with `parseXML()`, `sendEmail()`, `connectDB()`).
2. **Logical Cohesion:** Tasks logically related but different (e.g., a class with `readFromFile()`, `readFromKeyboard()`, `readFromNetwork()`).
3. **Temporal Cohesion:** Tasks that must happen at the same time (e.g., `SystemInitializer.init()` performs all initialization steps together).

*(Any three coupling + any three cohesion with clear definitions = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Identify Information Experts:**
- `Cart.computeTotal()` → `Cart` holds items and quantities.
- `Inventory.checkAvailability()` → `Inventory` holds stock count.
- `Order.placeOrder()` → `Order` aggregates cart, payment, address.

**Step 2 — Assign Creators:**
- `Customer` initiates checkout → `Customer` triggers `Order` creation (Creator — Customer aggregates Orders).

**Step 3 — Introduce Controller:**
- Add `CheckoutController.handlePlaceOrder()` as the Controller for the *Place Order* use case.

**Step 4 — Reduce Coupling:**
- Replace direct `Payment → BankAPI` with `Payment → PaymentGateway interface ← ConcreteBankAdapter`. This is **Indirection + Protected Variations + Polymorphism**.

**Step 5 — Boost Cohesion via Pure Fabrication:**
- Move GST calculation out of `Order` into a new `TaxCalculator` class (Pure Fabrication).
- Move email/SMS confirmation into `NotificationService` (Pure Fabrication).

**Step 6 — Refined Class Diagram (textual):**

```
+----------+   places   +---------+   contains   +--------+
| Customer |------------>|  Order  |------------->|  Cart  |
+----------+            +---------+              +--------+
                              |                      |
                              v                      v
                         +---------+            +----------+
                         | Payment |<--iface----| Inventory|
                         +---------+  IPaymentGateway
                              |
                              v
                         +------------------+
                         | TaxCalculator    |  <<Pure Fabrication>>
                         +------------------+
                              ^
                         +------------------+
                         | NotificationSvc  |  <<Pure Fabrication>>
                         +------------------+
                              ^
                         +------------------+
                         | CheckoutController| <<Controller>>
                         +------------------+
```

**CRC Cards (key classes):**

| Class | Responsibilities | Collaborators |
|---|---|---|
| `Order` | Validate cart, compute total, persist, trigger payment | `Cart`, `Payment`, `TaxCalculator` |
| `CheckoutController` | Receive *PlaceOrder* event, orchestrate | `Order`, `NotificationService` |
| `TaxCalculator` | Compute GST, return amount | *(none)* |
| `NotificationService` | Send email/SMS on order success/failure | `EmailGateway`, `SMSGateway` |
| `Payment` | Execute payment via selected gateway | `IPaymentGateway` |
| `Inventory` | Reserve stock for items in cart | `Cart`, `Product` |

**GRASP Trace (7 marks breakdown: 1 mark per principle):**

- Information Expert → `Cart.computeTotal()`
- Creator → `Customer` creates `Order`
- Controller → `CheckoutController`
- Low Coupling → `Payment ↔ IPaymentGateway` (interface)
- High Cohesion → `TaxCalculator` does only tax math
- Protected Variations → `IPaymentGateway` shields bank-API changes
- Pure Fabrication → `TaxCalculator`, `NotificationService`

*(7 marks — 2 for class diagram, 2 for CRC cards, 3 for explicit GRASP mapping.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Analysis and Design:** Writing *Customer has name and address* (analysis) instead of *Customer has `private String name; private String address;` + `public String getName()`* (design). Always mention **visibility, types, signatures**.
>
> 2. **Missing CRC Cards:** Many students skip the CRC (Class–Responsibilities–Collaborators) table. KTU examiners explicitly check for it in 7-mark sub-parts.
>
> 3. **Confusing GRASP with GoF Patterns:** GRASP is **not** the same as the Gang-of-Four design patterns. GRASP = responsibility-assignment heuristics; GoF = reusable solution templates. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
>
> 4. **Wrong coupling-cohesion order:** Writing "Data coupling is worst" is a direct mistake. Content coupling is worst; data coupling is among the best.
>
> 5. **No Pure Fabrication mention:** When a question involves cross-cutting concerns (logging, tax, notifications, persistence), failing to identify a **Pure Fabrication** class loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Skipping Axiom verification:** Even a one-line *"Axiom 3 satisfied: intra-class cohesion > inter-class coupling"* gives the examiner confidence you know the theory.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Class Design** transforms **analysis classes → design classes** by adding types, visibility, signatures, relationships, multiplicities, and OCL constraints.
- **5 sub-tasks:** refine attributes → refine operations → identify interfaces → identify relationships → apply GRASP + Axioms.
- **9 GRASP Principles** — Information Expert, Creator, Controller, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Polymorphism, Indirection, Protected Variations, Pure Fabrication.
- **5 Coad-Yourdon Axioms** — Successful Design, Archetype Selection, Connection (intra > inter), Minimalism, Envisioning Change.
- **Coupling (best→worst order):** Message > Data > Stamp > Control > External > Common > Content.
- **Cohesion (best→worst order):** Functional > Sequential > Communicational > Procedural > Temporal > Logical > Coincidental.
- **CRC Cards** are mandatory for KTU 7-mark sub-parts.
- **Pure Fabrication** = invented (non-domain) class for cross-cutting concerns (Tax, Logging, Persistence, Notification).
- **Protected Variations** = build stable interface around points of predicted change.
- **Information Expert** = responsibility goes to the class that has the data.
- **Controller** = class that handles a system-level event from the outside world.
- **Always show** UML class diagram with **visibility symbols** (+, -, #) and **derived attribute** marker (/) for full marks.
- **Verification step:** at the end of any class design answer, write one line confirming Coad-Yourdon Axioms 1, 3, 5 are satisfied.
- **Avoid:** writing code in Java/Python in class-design theory answers (KTU expects UML and pseudocode, not compilable code).
- **Examiner loves:** neat CRC tables + a clear arrow-based class diagram + explicit GRASP trace.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Design: Design Decisions, Views, Patterns, and Application Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architectural Design: Design Decisions, Views, Patterns, and Application Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Software Architectural Design?

**Architectural Design** is the critical link between specification (requirements engineering) and detailed design (component-level design). It represents the earliest stage of software engineering where the *overall structure* of the system is conceived, identifying major structural elements, their externally visible properties, and the relationships among them. The IEEE Standard 1471 (now superseded by ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010) formally describes software architecture as the **fundamental organization of a system, embodied in its components, their relationships to each other and to the environment, and the principles governing its design and evolution.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> Architectural Design is the process of defining a collection of hardware and software components and their interfaces to establish the framework for the development of a computer system. It is the representation of a system that enables software engineers to (1) analyze the effectiveness of the design, (2) consider architectural alternatives, and (3) reduce the risks associated with system construction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint Metaphor"

Imagine you are commissioning the construction of a **multi-storey hospital building**. Before a single brick is laid, architects must decide:
- How many floors? (structural scale)
- Where will the ICU, OPD, and pharmacy sit? (component organization)
- Will it use a steel-frame or load-bearing-wall structure? (architectural pattern)
- How do elevators, plumbing, and electrical conduits route? (connectors between components)
- What is visible to the fire-safety inspector vs. the interior decorator? (different stakeholder views)

**Software architecture is the digital equivalent.** It is NOT the code. It is the *strategic blueprint* that dictates:
- **Components** = subsystems, modules, services
- **Connectors** = APIs, message queues, shared databases
- **Constraints** = performance budgets, security policies, technology standards
- **Stakeholders** = developers, customers, project managers, maintainers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Architectural Design Matters (KTU High-Yield Point):**
> A bad architecture is *exponentially expensive* to fix after deployment. The **Rule of Architectural Significance (Bass, Clements, Kazman)**: an architectural decision is one that is *hard to change once implemented*. Therefore, the early architectural decisions govern the long-term quality attributes (performance, security, modifiability, availability) of the system.

### 1.3 Where Architectural Design Fits in the Software Life Cycle

Architectural Design is positioned **after** Requirements Engineering and **before** Detailed (Component) Design. It answers the *macro-level* "what are the major pieces and how do they talk?" question, leaving the *micro-level* "how does this specific class behave?" question to component design.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Architectural Design

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | KTU Board Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Architectural Views** | *How do we describe the architecture to different stakeholders?* | 4+1 View Model |
| **Architectural Patterns** | *What proven template should we reuse?* | Layered, MVC, Pipe-Filter |
| **Architectural Styles / Application Architectures** | *What overall structural family does the system belong to?* | Data-flow, Call-and-return |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** **"V-P-S"** = **V**iews, **P**atterns, **S**tyles. These three pillars are the trifecta of KTU questions on this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Design Decisions

Architectural design is fundamentally a **decision-making activity**. Sommerville and other KTU-recommended texts classify these decisions into three broad categories: **strategic, tactical, and operational**.

### 2.1.1 Strategic (Macro-Level) Decisions

These are the highest-impact decisions, made earliest, and govern the entire system.

- **System Organization Style**: Will it be layered? Client-server? Microservices? (KTU high-weightage)
- **Selection of Architectural Style / Pattern**: Choose from the catalogue of known patterns.
- **Identification of Subsystems (Modules)**: What are the major building blocks? E.g., User Interface, Business Logic, Database, Integration, Security.
- **Allocation of Subsystems to Hardware (Deployment Topology)**: Which tier runs on which server? Cloud vs. on-premise?
- **Choice of Technology Stack**: Programming language, database engine, web framework, OS.
- **Global Software Control Strategy**: Is control centralized (a main loop) or decentralized (event-driven)?

### 2.1.2 Tactical (Mid-Level) Decisions

- **Subsystem Interfaces (APIs)**: How exactly do modules talk? REST, gRPC, message broker?
- **Persistence Strategy**: How is data stored, retrieved, and synchronized?
- **Concurrency & Synchronization Policy**: How are multiple users / threads handled?
- **Error and Exception Handling Strategy**: Global policy for fault tolerance.
- **Security Architecture**: Authentication, authorization, encryption placement.

### 2.1.3 Operational (Implementation-Level) Decisions

- **Naming conventions**, **logging standards**, **coding standards**, **testing approach at integration level.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept: The Three Influences on Architectural Design**
> Sommerville identifies **three primary influences** that shape every architectural decision:
> 1. **Organizational influences** (the client's enterprise structure, business goals)
> 2. **End-user priorities** (usability, performance, cost)
> 3. **System requirements** (functional + non-functional / quality attributes)
> *Non-functional requirements* (the "-ilities") are usually the dominant driver of architectural choices.

### 2.2 Architectural Views (The 4+1 View Model)

A single blueprint cannot communicate the architecture to *all* stakeholders equally well. Therefore, multiple **views** are used to represent the system from different perspectives. The **4+1 View Model** (Philippe Kruchten, 1995) is the most widely taught in KTU curricula.

| View | Audience / Concern | What it Shows |
|---|---|---|
| **Logical View** | End-users, analysts | Object model of the design (classes, abstractions, relationships) — answers "What does the system provide?" |
| **Process View** | Integrators, performance engineers | System's runtime behavior, processes, threads, concurrency, communication — answers "How is the system responsive and performant?" |
| **Physical View** | System engineers, operations | How software maps onto hardware; deployment topology, network links — answers "Where do the components physically run?" |
| **Development View** | Programmers, project managers | Module organization in the software development environment; package structure — answers "How is the system built?" |
| **+1 Scenarios / Use-Case View** | All stakeholders (especially the customer) | Use cases that tie the four views together and validate the design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative View Models in KTU Curriculum:**
> - **IEEE Std 1471 / 42010 view model** (more general): stakeholder, concern, viewpoint, view.
> - **Kruchten's 4+1** is the *de facto* KTU board answer.
> - **Siemens 4 Views** (operational, technical, deployment, logical) — sometimes referenced.

### 2.3 Architectural Patterns (Reusable Templates)

A pattern is a *named, well-known solution* to a recurring design problem. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the following:

#### 2.3.1 Layered (N-Tier) Pattern
- Organizes the system into horizontal layers: Presentation → Business Logic → Data Access → Database.
- Each layer provides services to the layer above and consumes services from the layer below.
- **Rule:** A layer should *never* skip a layer to talk to a lower one (strict layering); but pragmatic "relaxed" layering allows skipping for performance.

#### 2.3.2 Client–Server Pattern
- Two principal components: a **client** that requests services and a **server** that provides them.
- Sub-variants: thin client, fat client, 2-tier, 3-tier, n-tier.

#### 2.3.3 Pipe-and-Filter Pattern
- Data flows from one filter to the next through pipes. Each filter transforms data independently.
- Examples: Unix shells, signal-processing systems, ETL pipelines, compilers.

#### 2.3.4 Model-View-Controller (MVC) Pattern
- **Model** = business data and logic. **View** = presentation / UI. **Controller** = handles input and updates model/view.
- Decouples data, presentation, and input handling.

#### 2.3.5 Repository Pattern
- A shared central data store (repository) is accessed by independent components that do not interact directly with each other.
- Blackboards in AI systems and database-backed web apps are classic examples.

#### 2.3.6 Service-Oriented / Microservices Patterns
- Independent, loosely-coupled services communicating over a network, each owning its own data.

### 2.4 Application Architectures (Architectural Styles — KTU Classification)

KTU follows the **Shaw-Garlan classification** (also adopted by Sommerville) which groups application architectures into **four families**:

#### 2.4.1 Data-Centered Architectures
- A central shared data store (e.g., database, file, repository) is the heart of the system.
- Components interact *only* through the store.
- Examples: traditional client-server DBMS applications, blackboard systems.

#### 2.4.2 Data-Flow Architectures
- **Strength of input–transformation–output flow.**
- Sub-styles:
  - **Batch Sequential**: Each stage runs to completion, then the next begins. (e.g., payroll systems)
  - **Pipe-and-Filter**: Continuous streams, parallelisable filters.

#### 2.4.3 Call-and-Return Architectures
- The most common family. Sub-styles:
  - **Main Program and Subroutine Architectures** (classical procedural).
  - **Object-Oriented / OO Architectures** — components are objects with encapsulated state and methods.
  - **Layered Architectures** — strictly hierarchical call-and-return.

#### 2.4.4 Independent Components Architectures
- **Loose coupling**: components process events/messages and react independently.
- Sub-styles:
  - **Event-Driven / Implicit Invocation**: components publish/subscribe to events.
  - **Communicating Processes**: peer-to-peer message-passing (CSP, message queues).
  - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**: services published, discovered, bound, invoked.

> [!TIP]
> **Kruchten's Mapping Rule for Exam:** "Logical View ↔ OO style, Process View ↔ Independent Components, Physical View ↔ Client-Server, Development View ↔ Layered."

### 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula / Rule | Application in Architectural Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling Metric (informal)** | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ where $c_i$ is the strength of each inter-component link | Minimize to improve modifiability |
| **Cohesion Metric (informal)** | $H = \frac{\text{intra-module relations}}{\text{total module relations}}$ | Maximize to improve reusability |
| **Layer Count Rule (KTU)** | $L \ge 3$ for n-tier; $L=2$ for client–server | Determines separation of concerns |
| **4+1 Views** | $\text{Total views} = 4 + 1 = 5$ | Kruchten's model |
| **Architectural Significance Rule** | $\text{Cost of change} = e^{t}$ where $t$ = time after deployment | Justifies early architectural decisions |
| **Quality Attribute Trade-off** | $Q = f(\text{perf}, \text{sec}, \text{avail}, \text{maintain})$ | Multiple variables must be balanced |
| **View-Concern Matrix** | $V \times C$ matrix maps views to concerns | Used in IEEE 42010 viewpoint documentation |
| **Pattern Selection** | $P_{selected} = \arg\max_{P \in \text{Patterns}} \text{fit}(P, \text{requirements})$ | Greedy/informed pattern selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exam board *does not* expect numerical answers here, but these expressions help students structure their written answers. Use them to *justify* design choices, not to compute numbers.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microservices Architectures** (Netflix, Amazon, Uber) descend from Independent Components style.
- **Three-Tier Web Apps** (every e-commerce site) descend from the Layered style.
- **ETL Pipelines** in data engineering descend from Pipe-and-Filter.
- **Database-Backed Banking Systems** descend from Data-Centered.
- **AI/ML Model Serving** typically uses Event-Driven components with a Repository (model registry).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Architectural Design Process — Step-by-Step Derivation

The derivation of an architecture is an *iterative decision process*. Below is the step-by-step process (as recommended in KTU-recommended texts).

### Step 1: System Structuring (Subsystem Decomposition)

The system is partitioned into principal subsystems (sometimes called *modules* or *components*). The principle is the *divide-and-conquer* approach to manage complexity.

$$
\text{System} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \dots, S_n\}, \quad \text{where } n \ge 3
$$

A rule of thumb from classical SE: a system of fewer than **3** principal subsystems is rarely architected well; more than **7 ± 2** becomes cognitively unmanageable (Miller's Law).

### Step 2: Control Modeling (How is Control Exercised?)

Two principal control strategies exist:

- **Centralized control**: A single component (main controller) dictates the flow.
- **Event-based (decentralized) control**: Components react to events published by other components.

$$
\text{ControlStyle} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Centralized}, & \text{if there is a unique } c_{\text{top}} \mid \forall e \in E, \; \text{dispatch}(c_{\text{top}}, e) \\
\text{Decentralized}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Modular Decomposition (Object or Function Based?)

Choose between:
- **Functional decomposition** (structured design — modules = functions).
- **Object-oriented decomposition** (modules = classes/objects with encapsulated state).

$$
\text{ModuleSize} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Coupling}} \times \text{Cohesion}
$$

A *good* module is large enough to be *cohesive* (all its parts strongly relate) yet *loosely coupled* (weakly tied to others).

### Step 4: Mapping of Subsystems to Hardware (Distribution)

Components are assigned to physical processors. The decision is governed by:

- **Performance**: CPU-intensive components on dedicated machines.
- **Availability**: Critical components replicated.
- **Security**: Sensitive components behind firewalls.
- **Cost**: Minimize hardware expenditure.

$$
\text{Alloc}(S_i) = \arg\min_{p \in \text{Processors}} \; \text{cost}(p) \; \text{ subject to } \; Q(S_i, p) \ge \text{threshold}
$$

### Step 5: Data Persistence Strategy

A data dictionary is built to identify:

- **Data entities** (Customer, Order, Product)
- **Relationships** (1-to-1, 1-to-many, many-to-many)
- **Access patterns** (read-heavy vs. write-heavy)
- **Storage type** (relational, NoSQL, file, in-memory)

$$
\text{StorageChoice} = f(\text{volume}, \text{velocity}, \text{variety})
$$

This is the *Three V's* of Big Data, which directly influence persistence strategy.

### Step 6: Global Software Control Strategy (Concurrency & Synchronization)

- **Multitasking**: Multiple processes share CPU.
- **Multithreading**: Multiple threads within one process.
- **Synchronization primitives**: Mutexes, semaphores, monitors.

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Completed Transactions}}{\text{Unit Time}}
$$

$$
\text{ResponseTime} = \text{ServiceTime} + \text{QueueingTime} + \text{NetworkLatency}
$$

These two equations (King's formulas) are sometimes used in KTU questions to argue for/against centralized vs. distributed architectures.

### Step 7: Boundary Condition Handling

Define how the system interacts with its environment:
- **External APIs**, **legacy interfaces**, **user interfaces**, **hardware sensors**, **third-party services**.

### Step 8: Review and Refine

Iteratively revisit Steps 1–7 as the architecture evolves, applying *architectural evaluation methods* (ATAM, SAAM — sometimes in KTU Module 5).

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Architectural Style

When designing, students often need to *justify* the choice of an architectural style. The analytical matrix below is a KTU high-yield tool.

| Quality Attribute | Layered | Client-Server | Pipe-Filter | MVC | Repository | Event-Driven | Microservices |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modifiability** | High | Medium | High | High | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Performance** | Medium | High | High (parallel) | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Scalability** | Low | High | Medium | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Availability** | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | High | High | High |
| **Security** | High | Medium | Low | High | High | Medium | High |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | High | Very High |
| **Testability** | High | Medium | High | High | Medium | Low | Low |

> [!TIP]
> **For the board exam**, when justifying a pattern, always cite the *quality attribute(s)* that the requirements force. Example: "The system must handle 10,000 concurrent users → Layered alone is insufficient → choose **Microservices** for horizontal scalability."

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: From Requirements to Pattern Selection

Below is a derivation-style argument for a typical KTU exam scenario.

> **Scenario:** A library management system must be maintainable, support multiple UIs (web, mobile, kiosk), integrate with an RFID scanner, and run on existing university servers.

**Step 1: Identify Dominant Quality Attributes**
- Maintainability (modifiability) → favors **layered** or **MVC**.
- Multiple UIs → favors **separation of view from logic** → **MVC**.
- RFID integration → favors **asynchronous, event-based** integration → **event-driven** for the integration layer.
- Limited servers → favors **centralized** tier, not microservices.

**Step 2: Map to Views**
- Logical View → **OO model** of books, members, loans.
- Process View → **Event-driven RFID integration**; otherwise **call-and-return** business logic.
- Physical View → **2-tier or 3-tier** centralized deployment.
- Development View → **Layered package structure** (UI, Service, DAO, Domain).

**Step 3: Pattern Decision**
- Adopt **Layered Architecture + MVC** as the dominant hybrid pattern.
- Use **Observer / Publish-Subscribe** for the RFID event integration.

**Step 4: Validate via 4+1 Scenarios**
- **Scenario A:** Issue a book — exercises all layers + controller.
- **Scenario B:** RFID detects an unauthorized exit — exercises event handler, security subsystem, alert generator.
- **Scenario C:** Member changes address — exercises CRUD persistence path.

**Step 5: Document Decisions (ADL)**
Use an **Architecture Description Language** (or simple UML component + deployment diagrams) to record the decisions for future maintainers.

This 5-step derivation is the gold standard for a 14-mark KTU answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Similar]**
*Define software architecture. List the four views of Kruchten's 4+1 architectural model.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Software Architecture** is the fundamental organization of a system, embodied in its components, the relationships between them, and the principles governing its design and evolution. It represents the earliest set of design decisions that are the most difficult to change later.

> **The four views of Kruchten's 4+1 model are:**
> 1. **Logical View** — Object model of the design (concerns: end-user functionality).
> 2. **Process View** — Concurrency, runtime behavior, performance.
> 3. **Physical View** — Mapping of software to hardware; deployment topology.
> 4. **Development View** — Static organization of the software in the development environment.
> 5. The **+1 Scenario (Use-Case) View** ties the other four together and is used to validate them.
> *(Strictly, 4 + 1 = 5 views.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Similar]**
*What is an architectural pattern? Differentiate between the Layered pattern and the MVC pattern.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Architectural Pattern:** A named, well-known, reusable solution to a recurring architectural design problem. It provides a template for subsystem structures and their interaction rules.

> **Layered Pattern:** The system is organized into a stack of horizontal layers (e.g., Presentation, Business, Data Access, Database). Each layer provides services to the layer above and requests services from the layer below. Focus is on **separation of concerns** across the *whole system*.

> **MVC Pattern:** Splits the *user-interface* subsystem into three components: **Model** (data + business rules), **View** (presentation), and **Controller** (handles user input). Focus is on *decoupling data, presentation, and input* within the UI subsystem.

> **Key Difference:** Layered is a *macro-level* organization of the entire system; MVC is a *micro-level* organization within the presentation tier. They are often **used together** — a layered system may use MVC in its presentation layer.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module-Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four families of application architectures** as classified by Sommerville, with one example for each family.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three primary influences** that shape architectural design according to Sommerville, and explain why **non-functional requirements** dominate architectural decisions.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

Sommerville classifies application architectures into **four families**:

1. **Data-Centered Architectures**
   - A central **data store** is the heart of the system. Components interact with each other *only* by reading from or writing to this store.
   - **Example:** Traditional **client–server database applications** (e.g., a bank's core banking system where all transactions go through one central DB).
   - **Sub-type:** **Repository (Blackboard) architecture** — components share a common knowledge base. Example: speech-recognition systems.

2. **Data-Flow Architectures**
   - Functional transformations transform inputs to outputs in a linear chain.
   - **Sub-types:**
     - **Batch Sequential:** Stages run one after the other; e.g., a **payroll system** (collect hours → calculate → generate paychecks).
     - **Pipe-and-Filter:** Continuous streams flow through parallel filters; e.g., **Unix shell pipelines**, **signal-processing**, **ETL data pipelines**.

3. **Call-and-Return Architectures**
   - Components invoke each other through procedure calls or method invocations.
   - **Sub-types:**
     - **Main Program and Subroutine:** Classic procedural decomposition.
     - **Object-Oriented Architectures:** Components are objects; e.g., **Java Spring-based enterprise applications**.
     - **Layered Architectures:** Strictly hierarchical call/return; e.g., **3-tier web application** (Presentation, Business, Data tiers).

4. **Independent Components Architectures**
   - Loosely coupled components process independently and communicate via messages or events.
   - **Sub-types:**
     - **Event-Driven (Implicit Invocation):** Components publish/subscribe to events. Example: **GUI frameworks** (button-click event handlers).
     - **Communicating Processes:** Peer-to-peer message passing. Example: **distributed systems using sockets**.
     - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):** Independent services over a network. Example: **Microservices on AWS**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

The **three primary influences** on architectural design (Sommerville) are:

1. **Organizational / Enterprise Influences**
   - The client's business structure, processes, and goals.
   - *Example:* A bank requires a separate subsystem for each department (retail, corporate, treasury) — influenced by the bank's organizational structure, not technical merit.

2. **End-User / Stakeholder Priorities**
   - Performance, usability, cost, maintainability expectations of users and customers.
   - *Example:* A consumer app prioritizes UI responsiveness over internal logging granularity.

3. **System Requirements** (Functional + Non-Functional)
   - Functional requirements define *what* the system does; non-functional requirements define *how well* it does them (the "-ilities": performance, security, availability, modifiability, portability, etc.).

**Why Non-Functional Requirements Dominate Architectural Decisions:**

- Non-functional requirements (**NFRs**) directly map to the **quality attributes** that the architecture must enforce at the system level.
- A single NFR can *eliminate* entire architectural styles. For example:
  - "System must support 1 million concurrent users" → **rules out** monolithic 2-tier layered; **forces** horizontally scalable microservices or event-driven.
  - "System must run on legacy hardware" → **rules out** containerized microservices.
  - "System must be available 99.999% (five nines)" → **forces** redundancy, replication, fault-tolerant design.
- Once an architecture is chosen, NFRs *constrain every subsequent decision* (language, framework, database, deployment).
- NFRs are also the *most expensive* to retrofit — they must be baked in *early*.

> **Conclusion:** The architecture is, in essence, a *direct response* to the NFRs. This is why KTU 2024 board answers must *always* justify architectural choices in terms of the NFRs being satisfied.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Kruchten 4+1 View Model** in detail. For each view, identify the **stakeholder** and **primary concern** it addresses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the following architectural patterns: **Layered, Pipe-and-Filter, Repository, and MVC.** Draw a labeled diagram for any two of them.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

Kruchten's **4+1 View Model** (1995) is the standard model for describing software architecture from multiple stakeholder perspectives.

| View | Stakeholder | Primary Concern | What is Shown |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logical View** | End-users, business analysts, designers | **Functional requirements** — *what* the system provides | Object model, classes, abstractions, key interactions (often shown as UML class diagram) |
