| **Process View** | System integrators, performance engineers | **Non-functional — performance, availability, concurrency** | Runtime processes, threads, inter-process communication, synchronization |
| **Physical View** | System engineers, network engineers, operators | **System topology, deployment, communication paths** | Mapping of software to hardware; servers, network links, protocols |
| **Development View** | Programmers, project managers, build engineers | **Software module organization, code management, build dependencies** | Package/module structure, library dependencies, source-code organization |
| **+1 Scenario / Use-Case View** | All stakeholders, especially the customer | **Validation, end-to-end coherence** | Concrete use cases (sequences) that exercise the four views and prove the architecture is consistent |

> **Key Insight:** The "+1" scenarios are not a separate view to be designed — they are the *glue* used to verify that the other four views are *internally consistent and externally valid*. Each scenario typically corresponds to a critical or architecturally significant use case.

**Why Multiple Views Are Needed:**
A single diagram cannot express the architecture to all stakeholders. A programmer needs the *development view*; a deployer needs the *physical view*; a customer needs the *scenarios*. Multiple views also decouple concerns — you can change the physical topology without affecting the logical structure.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Layered | Pipe-and-Filter | Repository | MVC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Family (Shaw-Garlan)** | Call-and-Return | Data-Flow | Data-Centered | Call-and-Return (sub-pattern) |
| **Structure** | Hierarchical stack | Linear chain of filters | Central store with multiple clients | Triad: Model, View, Controller |
| **Coupling** | Low (vertical) | Low (one-way) | Medium (via store) | Low (UI concerns separated) |
| **Reusability** | High | Very High | High | High |
| **Concurrency** | Low (synchronous) | High (parallel filters) | High (independent clients) | Low to medium |
| **Best For** | Enterprise business apps | Data pipelines, signal processing | Knowledge-based systems | Interactive UIs |
| **Example** | 3-tier web app | Unix `cat | sort | uniq` | Speech recognition blackboard | Django, Spring MVC |

**Two Required Diagrams (Mermaid):**

**Diagram 1: Layered Pattern**
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Presentation Layer\nUI and Controllers"]
    L2["Business Logic Layer\nDomain Services"]
    L3["Data Access Layer\nRepositories and DAOs"]
    L4["Database Layer\nPersistent Storage"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

**Diagram 2: Pipe-and-Filter Pattern**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Source"] --> F1["Filter 1\nParse"]
    F1 --> F2["Filter 2\nValidate"]
    F2 --> F3["Filter 3\nTransform"]
    F3 --> F4["Filter 4\nFormat"]
    F4 --> P1["Sink"]
```

**Justification Snippet (for board answer):**
- **Layered** is chosen when *separation of concerns* and *modifiability* are priorities.
- **Pipe-and-Filter** is chosen when *throughput* and *parallelism* are priorities and the data is naturally a stream.
- **Repository** is chosen when *shared central data* is the dominant asset and components must reason about it collectively (e.g., AI blackboard).
- **MVC** is chosen when *interactive UIs with rich data models* must decouple presentation from business logic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "Architectural Style" (family) with "Architectural Pattern" (specific template). Example: MVC is a *pattern*, not a *style*; the *style* is Call-and-Return. Boards deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Do NOT** list all 4+1 views without mentioning the *stakeholders* for each — a view without its stakeholder loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Do NOT** draw a Layered diagram that violates the *layer rule* (e.g., Presentation layer directly calling Database layer).
> 4. **Do NOT** call MVC a "style" — it is a *pattern within* the Call-and-Return style. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. Always tie architectural choice to **non-functional requirements** — a pattern chosen "because it's popular" is worth 0 marks; chosen "to satisfy scalability" is worth full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Architectural Design =** the *earliest, hardest-to-change* set of design decisions governing components, connectors, and constraints.
- **Sommerville's 3 influences** on architecture: organizational, end-user, system requirements — with NFRs dominating.
- **Kruchten 4+1 Views:** Logical, Process, Physical, Development, +1 Scenarios. *Memorize the stakeholder for each view.*
- **Four Families of Application Architectures (Shaw-Garlan):**
  - Data-Centered (Repository / Blackboard)
  - Data-Flow (Batch Sequential / Pipe-and-Filter)
  - Call-and-Return (Main+Subroutine / OO / Layered)
  - Independent Components (Event-Driven / Communicating Processes / SOA / Microservices)
- **Layered Rule:** A layer should request services from *only the layer immediately below* (strict layering). Skipping requires justification.
- **MVC Triad:** Model (data + logic) | View (UI) | Controller (input handler). MVC is a *pattern* within the Call-and-Return *style*.
- **Client-Server** has 2-tier (client + DB server) and 3-tier (client + app server + DB server) variants.
- **Pipe-and-Filter** shines for *batch and stream processing*; filters are independent and stateless.
- **Repository / Blackboard** is best for *knowledge-based or AI* systems where data is the central asset.
- **Microservices** = modern descendant of SOA; each service owns its data and exposes an API.
- **Architectural Significance:** an architectural decision is one whose change after deployment is *extremely costly*.
- **Architectural Views = "Different windows into the same building."** No single view is sufficient.
- **Mermaid mnemonics used in this module:**
  - 4+1 model = one big flowchart with 5 view boxes plus stakeholder branches.
  - Layered = 4 stacked subgraphs (Presentation → Logic → Data → DB).
  - MVC = triangular cycle User → View → Controller → Model → DB.
  - Microservices = API Gateway → fan-out to services → fan-in to message bus.
- **Exam focus (KTU 2024):** questions stress (1) views and their stakeholders, (2) classification of application architectures, (3) pattern selection *justified by NFRs*, (4) layered vs. MVC distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component-Level Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Component-Level Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component-Level Design (CLD)** is the third and most granular stage of the software design process, positioned *after* **Data Design** and **Architectural Design**, but *before* **User Interface Design**. It defines the **internal data structures, algorithmic logic, interface characteristics, and communication mechanisms** of every individual software component identified during architectural design.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCITT503 - Module 4), Component-Level Design is the process of:

- **Transforming** the **architectural model** (developed in Module 3) into **operational software components**.
- **Defining** the *processing logic*, *data structures*, *interfaces*, and *inter-component collaboration protocols* for each unit.
- Establishing the foundation for **construction (coding) and unit testing**.

In essence, **CLD is the "blueprint of a single brick" in the software building**, where the architect has finalized the layout of the building (architectural design), and now each brick is engineered with precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A software component is defined as a *modular, deployable, replaceable, and well-defined unit of software functionality* that encapsulates its contents and has a well-defined set of **public interfaces**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a large restaurant:

- **Architectural Design** = the floor plan showing the kitchen layout, the dining hall, and the storage rooms.
- **Component-Level Design** = the engineering of **one specific workstation** inside the kitchen (e.g., the pastry station): what ingredients it holds, what tools it uses, what its input/output trays look like, and what recipes it executes.

Each workstation (component):

1. Has a **clear purpose** (e.g., make desserts).
2. **Knows nothing** about other workstations (low coupling).
3. Has all related tools and ingredients **bundled together** (high cohesion).
4. Exposes a **service window** (interface) for the head chef to request items.
5. Can be **replaced or upgraded** independently (e.g., swap a manual mixer with a planetary mixer).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Recap:** Component-Level Design asks the question: *"How does each individual software module work, store data, and talk to its neighbours?"* — and provides the algorithmic, structural, and interface-level answer.

## 1.3 Why Component-Level Design Matters in KTU 2024 NEP-Aligned Education

The KTU 2024 Scheme, aligned with **NEP 2020**, emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Component-Level Design maps directly to:

- **CO3 (Module 4 typical):** *Design the internal structure of software components using cohesion, coupling, and design guidelines.*
- **Employability Skill:** Building reusable, testable, and maintainable software units.
- **Engineering Skill:** Bridging the gap between high-level architecture and low-level code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Predecessor Concepts (covered earlier in KTU syllabus):**
> - *Module 2:* Software Process Models.
> - *Module 3:* Architectural Design (Data Flow, Patterns, Styles).
> **Successor Concepts (Module 5 in KTU 2024):**
> - *User Interface Design* and *Pattern-Based Design* integration.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Component-Level Design Process (Structured)

The CLD process is executed in a **3-tier structured flow**:

### Tier-1: Foundation Decisions
1. **Identify** all design components (from architectural model).
2. **Determine** the component type:
   - **Class-based components** (OO paradigm)
   - **Traditional components** (function modules, procedures)
   - **WebApp / MobileApp / Cloud components**
3. **Select** an appropriate design strategy.

### Tier-2: Component Engineering
4. **Refine** the interface descriptions.
5. **Define** the **algorithmic logic** for each operation.
6. **Design** the **internal data structures**.
7. **Establish** the **collaboration protocol** between components.

### Tier-3: Validation & Quality
8. Apply **cohesion-coupling** heuristics.
9. Perform **Walkthroughs / FTRs** (formal technical reviews).
10. Generate the **Component Specification** (deliverable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The deliverable of CLD is the **Component Specification Document**, which includes:
> - Functional description
> - Interface signatures (pre/post conditions)
> - Data structure definitions
> - Algorithmic pseudocode
> - Dependency declarations

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why (Rationale) | How (Mechanism) |
|------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Identify components | Reduce system complexity | Decomposition from architectural model |
| Choose component type | Match paradigm to problem | OO → class; Procedural → function module |
| Refine interfaces | Enable component reuse & substitution | Contracts, API specs |
| Design data structures | Optimize memory & access time | Arrays, lists, trees, hash tables |
| Establish collaboration | Enable inter-component communication | Message passing, function calls, events |
| Validate quality | Ensure maintainability | Cohesion/Coupling analysis |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for CLD. Memorize these for the KTU University Exam.

| Concept | Definition / Formula | KTU Standard Range / Threshold | Unit / Measure |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| **Cohesion (C_h)** | Functional relatedness within a component | **Acceptable: Functional (best) → Coincidental (worst)** | Categorical (7 levels) |
| **Coupling (C_p)** | Interdependence between components | **Acceptable: Data (best) → Content (worst)** | Categorical (6 levels) |
| **Component Reusability (R)** | $R = \dfrac{N_{\text{reused}}}{N_{\text{total components}}} \times 100\%$ | Higher is better | Percentage (%) |
| **Interface Complexity (I_c)** | $I_c = \vert M_{\text{public}} \vert + \vert P_{\text{public}} \vert$ | Lower is better | Count of public members |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | **Acceptable: $\leq 10$ per component** | Numerical |
| **Fan-In (F_i)** | Number of components that call/use this component | High Fan-In $\Rightarrow$ reusable | Integer count |
| **Fan-Out (F_o)** | Number of components called/used by this component | Low Fan-Out $\Rightarrow$ independent | Integer count |
| **Information Hiding Index (I_h)** | Ratio of hidden data to total data | Higher is better | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Module Strength Score (M_s)** | Sum of cohesion weight × complexity | Minimize for coincidental | Weighted score |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing Cyclomatic Complexity, do **not** confuse the **independent paths (P)** with the **predicate nodes (N)**. The formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ requires:
> - $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph
> - $N$ = number of nodes
> - $P$ = number of connected components (for a single function, $P = 1$)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of CLD

| Industry | CLD Application |
|----------|-----------------|
| **Automotive (Kerala based: Tata Elxci, Visteon)** | ECU component design (highly cohesive, low coupling) |
| **FinTech (Kerala startups: Razorpay, etc.)** | Microservice component design for payment gateways |
| **Healthcare IT (Trivandrum Technopark)** | Patient-record module (high cohesion for legal compliance) |
| **Cloud (Kerala Digital University projects)** | Serverless function component design |
| **E-Commerce** | Cart, Payment, Catalog — independently deployable components |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric)

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**. Let us derive it step by step.

**Given:** A control flow graph (CFG) of a component with:
- $E = 12$ edges
- $N = 10$ nodes
- $P = 1$ connected component

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (KTU 2024 standard):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2(1)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the arithmetic:**

$$
V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2
$$

$$
V(G) = 2 + 2 = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

$$
V(G) = 4
$$

This means the component has **4 independent execution paths**, which is **well within** the KTU-recommended threshold of $V(G) \leq 10$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative formula (used as a cross-check in KTU valuation):**
> $$V(G) = \text{Number of predicate nodes} + 1$$
> If the CFG has 3 decision (predicate) nodes, then $V(G) = 3 + 1 = 4$. ✅ Matches.

**Step 5 — Engineering decision:**

Since $V(G) = 4$, the component can be safely implemented, tested with **4 independent test cases**, and is **maintainable** without refactoring.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The 7 Levels of Cohesion (KTU 2024 Standard Order)

| # | Level (Best → Worst) | Definition | Example | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|----------------------|------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Functional** | All elements contribute to a single, well-defined task | `calculateTax(income)` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Best) |
| 2 | **Layer** | Similar components grouped at same abstraction level | `InputLayer` class | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Communicational** | All elements operate on same data | `updateAndLog(record)` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Elements must be executed in order | `init() → load() → run()` | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Temporal** | Elements executed at the same time (e.g., init phase) | `startup()` — logs, loads, connects | ⭐ |
| 6 | **Logical** | Elements of similar nature, chosen by a flag | `process(type)` — file/DB/network | ⚠️ Avoid |
| 7 | **Coincidental** | Random grouping (worst, "bag of stuff") | `utility()` doing 10 unrelated things | ❌ Refactor immediately |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The 6 Levels of Coupling

| # | Level (Best → Worst) | Definition | KTU Verdict |
|---|----------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Data** | Components share only data parameters | ✅ Acceptable |
| 2 | **Stamp** | Share a data structure (partial) | ✅ Acceptable |
| 3 | **Control** | One passes control flags to the other | ⚠️ Use cautiously |
| 4 | **External** | Share an external format (e.g., protocol, I/O) | ⚠️ Use cautiously |
| 5 | **Common** | Share global data | ❌ Refactor |
| 6 | **Content** | One component modifies the internals of another | ❌ Refactor immediately |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Component Specification for a "Payment" Component

> [!NOTE]
> This is a KTU 2024 typical 14-mark question scenario.

**Scenario:** Design the *PaymentProcessor* component for an e-commerce system.

**Step 1 — Component Identification:**

Component Name: `PaymentProcessor` (from architectural model)

**Step 2 — Interface Specification:**

$$
\text{Interface}_{\text{PaymentProcessor}} = \{ \text{processPayment}(), \text{refundPayment}(), \text{validateCard}() \}
$$

**Step 3 — Pre/Post Conditions (using Design-by-Contract):**

| Method | Pre-condition | Post-condition |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| `processPayment(amount, card)` | $\text{amount} > 0$ AND $\text{card}$ is valid | Returns transaction $\text{ID}$ and $\text{status} = \text{"SUCCESS"}$ |
| `refundPayment(txID)` | $\text{txID}$ exists in DB | Refund processed, status = "REFUNDED" |

**Step 4 — Algorithmic Logic (pseudocode):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{FUNCTION processPayment(amount, card)} \\
&\quad \text{IF} \text{ amount} \leq 0 \text{ THEN return ERROR} \\
&\quad \text{IF validateCard(card) = FALSE THEN return ERROR} \\
&\quad \text{txID} \leftarrow \text{generateTransactionID()} \\
&\quad \text{DB.send(transaction record)} \\
&\quad \text{RETURN txID, "SUCCESS"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Quality Check (using cohesion-coupling heuristics):**

- **Cohesion:** Functional (all operations relate to payment) ✅
- **Coupling:** Data (only data is passed via parameters) ✅
- **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = 3$ (two IFs + implicit branch) → acceptable ✅

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Valuation Insight:** The examiners award full marks only if all five steps above are present. Missing the **Pre/Post Conditions** typically costs **2 marks**.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Reusability Calculation

**Given:** A software project has **40 components** in total. **12 components** are reused in **3 different projects**.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
R = \frac{N_{\text{reused}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
R = \frac{12}{40} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
R = 0.3 \times 100\% = 30\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

A reusability of **30%** is considered **moderately good** in KTU industry standards (target: $\geq 40\%$).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Component-Level Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Architectural Model] --> B[Identify All Components]
    B --> C{Choose Component Type}
    C -->|OO Paradigm| D1[Class-Based Components]
    C -->|Procedural| D2[Traditional Function Modules]
    C -->|WebApp| D3[WebApp Components]
    C -->|MobileApp| D4[MobileApp Components]
    D1 --> E[Refine Interfaces]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Define Algorithmic Logic]
    F --> G[Design Internal Data Structures]
    G --> H[Establish Collaboration Protocol]
    H --> I[Apply Cohesion-Coupling Heuristics]
    I --> J{Quality Acceptable?}
    J -->|No| K[Refactor Component]
    K --> E
    J -->|Yes| L[Perform FTR Walkthrough]
    L --> M[Generate Component Specification]
    M --> N[End: Ready for Construction]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Cohesion-Coupling Quality Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Good["DESIRABLE COMPONENT"]
        H1[High Functional Cohesion]
        H2[Low Data Coupling]
    end
    subgraph Bad["UNDESIRABLE COMPONENT"]
        L1[Coincidental Cohesion]
        L2[Content Coupling]
    end
    H1 -->|Improves| Q1[Reusability]
    H2 -->|Improves| Q1
    Q1 --> Q2[Maintainability]
    Q2 --> Q3[Testability]
    L1 -->|Degrades| Q1
    L2 -->|Degrades| Q1
    Q1 --> Q4[High Defect Density]
    Q4 --> Q5[High Maintenance Cost]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Component Collaboration Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UI["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        U1[LoginUI Component]
        U2[DashboardUI Component]
    end
    subgraph BL["BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER"]
        B1[AuthController Component]
        B2[PaymentProcessor Component]
        B3[OrderManager Component]
    end
    subgraph DL["DATA LAYER"]
        D1[UserRepository Component]
        D2[PaymentRepository Component]
        D3[OrderRepository Component]
    end
    U1 -->|Interface Call| B1
    U2 -->|Interface Call| B3
    B1 -->|Data Coupling| D1
    B2 -->|Data Coupling| D2
    B3 -->|Data Coupling| D3
    B3 -->|Function Call| B2
    B2 -->|Notification| U2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: The WebApp Component Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WebApp Root Component] --> B[Client-Side Components]
    A --> C[Server-Side Components]
    A --> D[Database Components]
    B --> B1[Presentation Components]
    B --> B2[Client-Side Validation]
    C --> C1[Business Logic Components]
    C --> C2[REST API Endpoints]
    C --> C3[Authentication Service]
    D --> D1[Persistent Storage]
    D --> D2[Connection Pool]
    C1 -->|Interacts With| D1
    C2 -->|Routes Request To| C1
    B1 -->|HTTP Request| C2
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Define the term "Component" in the context of software engineering. List any FOUR characteristics of a well-designed software component.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** A **software component** is a modular, deployable, and replaceable unit of software functionality that encapsulates its internal implementation behind a well-defined set of public interfaces.

**Four Characteristics (0.375 each = 1.5 Marks):**

1. **Modular** — has an independent, well-defined identity.
2. **Encapsulated** — internal details are hidden from external components.
3. **Independently deployable** — can be installed/updated without affecting the rest of the system.
4. **Well-defined interfaces** — exposes a contract (public methods, properties) for other components.
5. **Reusable** — can be used in multiple contexts.
6. **Replaceable** — can be swapped with an equivalent component.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often **miss the "encapsulation"** characteristic. Always include it — it earns the *highest weightage* in valuation.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

> **Differentiate between "Coupling" and "Cohesion" in component-level design. Which is preferable and why?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Coupling | Cohesion |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Definition | Inter-component dependency | Intra-component relatedness |
| Goal | **Minimize** | **Maximize** |
| Best Level | Data coupling | Functional cohesion |
| Worst Level | Content coupling | Coincidental cohesion |
| Scope | Between two components | Within a single component |

**Preferable Combination (1 Mark):** We aim for **High Cohesion + Low Coupling**, which produces a system that is *modular, testable, and maintainable*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write only "low coupling and high cohesion" without stating that **this combination is the design goal**. The full statement is required for the third mark.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B question has **internal choice**. The student attempts **one** of the two alternatives. Both alternatives are presented here with full model solutions.

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **(a) Explain in detail the concept of "Component-Level Design" (CLD). What are its key activities and deliverables? (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) Discuss the various levels of COHESION with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Component-Level Design is the third step of the software design process that defines the **internal processing logic, data structures, interfaces, and communication protocols** of each individual component derived from the architectural design.

**Key Activities (3 Marks):**

1. **Component Identification** — selecting components from the architectural model.
2. **Interface Refinement** — specifying pre-conditions, post-conditions, and exception behavior.
3. **Algorithm Design** — developing pseudocode and logic for each operation.
4. **Data Structure Design** — selecting optimal storage for the component's data.
5. **Collaboration Design** — establishing how components interact (e.g., call-based, event-based).
6. **Quality Validation** — applying cohesion-coupling analysis and review.

**Deliverables (2 Marks):**

- **Component Specification Document** (includes purpose, interface, pre/post conditions, data structures, algorithm, dependencies).
- **Updated architectural model** with component-level details.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

The **7 levels of cohesion** (arranged from best to worst), each illustrated with an example:

1. **Functional Cohesion (1 Mark):** All elements contribute to a single, well-defined function. *Example:* `calculateIncomeTax(salary)` — every statement computes the tax.

2. **Layer Cohesion (1 Mark):** Similar components grouped at the same abstraction layer. *Example:* A `NetworkLayer` class containing all network-related methods.

3. **Communicational Cohesion (1 Mark):** All elements operate on the same data. *Example:* A `printAndUpdateEmployeeRecord()` function uses the same record.

4. **Procedural Cohesion (1 Mark):** Elements are ordered to perform a sequence of related tasks. *Example:* `init() → load() → connect()` (all startup-related).

5. **Temporal Cohesion (1 Mark):** Elements executed at the same time (e.g., during startup). *Example:* A `startup()` function that logs, loads config, and opens DB.

6. **Logical Cohesion (1 Mark):** Elements of similar nature, selected by a flag. *Example:* `processInput(source)` — processes file, DB, or network input based on flag.

7. **Coincidental Cohesion (1 Mark):** Random grouping of unrelated elements. *Example:* A `doEverything()` function that handles login, payment, and printing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** When asked to **arrange** the levels (best to worst or worst to best), students frequently **swap the order of "Layer" and "Functional"** — losing 1 mark. Memorize the order: **Functional → Layer → Communicational → Procedural → Temporal → Logical → Coincidental**.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **(a) Define "Coupling". Explain its different types with examples. (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) What is Cyclomatic Complexity? For a given control flow graph with E=15, N=13, P=1, calculate the cyclomatic complexity and interpret the result. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 Mark):** **Coupling** is the measure of *interdependence* between two software components. The lower the coupling, the more modular, independent, and maintainable the system.

**Six Types (1 Mark each, totalling 6 Marks):**

1. **Data Coupling (Best):** Components share only data parameters.
   *Example:* `computeArea(radius)` — only `radius` is passed.

2. **Stamp Coupling:** Components share a data structure but use only a part of it.
   *Example:* Passing an `Employee` object, but only `Employee.name` is used.

3. **Control Coupling:** One component passes a control flag to influence the other's logic.
   *Example:* `processOrder(order, isPriority)` — the flag changes the order's processing path.

4. **External Coupling:** Components share an external data format or protocol.
   *Example:* Two components reading the same XML file format.

5. **Common Coupling:** Components share global data.
   *Example:* Multiple components accessing a global `CONFIG` variable.

6. **Content Coupling (Worst):** One component directly accesses/modifies the internals of another.
   *Example:* Component B uses a pointer to Component A's private variable.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):** **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** is a software metric developed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)** that quantifies the *number of linearly independent paths* through a program's source code. It is used to estimate the testing effort and the risk associated with a component.

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

**Substitution (1 Mark):**

Given $E = 15$, $N = 13$, $P = 1$:

$$
V(G) = 15 - 13 + 2(1)
$$

**Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$
V(G) = 15 - 13 + 2 = 4
$$

**Interpretation (2 Marks):**

- The component has **4 independent execution paths**.
- The KTU-recommended threshold is $V(G) \leq 10$; hence, the component is **of acceptable complexity**.
- The component requires **at least 4 independent test cases** to achieve branch coverage.
- It is **not prone to high defect density** and is **maintainable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to multiply $P$ by **2** in the formula. Many students write $V(G) = E - N + P$, which is **incorrect** for McCabe's original formula.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **interpretation** — KTU 2024 valuation specifically awards 2 marks for the interpretation.
> - If the problem provides only the number of **predicate nodes**, use the **alternative formula** $V(G) = \pi + 1$, where $\pi$ is the number of predicate (decision) nodes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4 — Component-Level Design):**

- **CLD Position** in the design process: *After* data/architectural design, *before* UI design.
- A **component** is **modular, encapsulated, deployable, replaceable, reusable**, with **well-defined interfaces**.
- The **deliverable** of CLD is the **Component Specification Document**.
- **Cohesion (intra-component)** has **7 levels**: Functional → Layer → Communicational → Procedural → Temporal → Logical → Coincidental.
- **Coupling (inter-component)** has **6 levels**: Data → Stamp → Control → External → Common → Content.
- **Design Goal:** **High Cohesion + Low Coupling**.
- **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity** formula: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$.
- **Acceptable** cyclomatic complexity: $V(G) \leq 10$.
- **Alternative** cyclomatic complexity formula: $V(G) = \pi + 1$ (where $\pi$ = predicate nodes).
- **Reusability Formula:** $R = (N_{\text{reused}} / N_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Fan-In** = number of components calling this one (high = reusable).
- **Fan-Out** = number of components called by this one (low = independent).
- **Design by Contract** uses **pre-conditions, post-conditions, and invariants** to specify interfaces.
- **Class-based components** are used in **OO** paradigm; **traditional components** are used in **procedural** paradigm.
- **WebApp components** include **client-side, server-side, and database** layers.
- **MobileApp components** include **UI, business logic, data, and platform-specific** modules.
- **Coincidental cohesion** and **content coupling** must be **refactored immediately**.
- **Information hiding** (Parnas, 1972) is a cornerstone principle of CLD.
- **KTU 2024 favourite exam questions:** Levels of cohesion, levels of coupling, McCabe's complexity, and component specification with pre/post conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation: Object-Oriented Design Using UML
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation: Object-Oriented Design Using UML

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Object-Oriented Design (OOD)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** is a software design methodology that models a system as a collection of cooperating *objects*, where each object represents an instance of a class, and classes are organized into hierarchies using inheritance. OOD focuses on designing the **static structure** (class/object relationships), the **dynamic behavior** (interactions and state changes), and the **functional semantics** (operations and responsibilities) of a system before implementation begins.

OOD is the **bridge** between the *what* (captured in analysis/requirements) and the *how* (captured in code/implementation). It transforms the *analysis model* into a *design model* that programmers can directly implement.

### 1.2 Unified Modeling Language (UML)

**Definition:**
> **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** is a standardized, general-purpose, visual modeling language managed by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It provides a set of graphical notations (diagrams) to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artifacts of a software-intensive system. UML is **not a programming language**; it is a *modeling* language.

The current standard is **UML 2.5** (released 2015, current OMG specification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** OOD is the **methodology** (what to think about), whereas UML is the **notation** (how to draw what you think about). They are not synonyms. OOD without UML is possible; UML without OOD is just pretty pictures.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "House Blueprint" View

Imagine you have decided to build a house. You know the requirements: *3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, a kitchen, a garden, must face south.* That's the **analysis stage**.

Now, before the masons arrive, an architect creates:

| Real-World Element | Software Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The architect's mental model of rooms & flow | **Object-Oriented Design** |
| The drawn blueprint (plan, elevation, electrical, plumbing) | **UML Diagrams** (class, sequence, activity, etc.) |
| The builder reading the blueprint to lay bricks | **Implementation (coding)** |
| The completed house | **Final Software System** |

**OOD** is the *thinking* — deciding that "Bedroom" and "Bathroom" are distinct *classes* with *attributes* (size, color) and *methods* (open door, switch on light). **UML** is the *drawing* — the class diagram on paper, the sequence diagram showing how a Person enters a Door to reach a Room. You cannot have a sound building without both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCITT503 Module 4):** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically expects students to (1) explain the OOD process, (2) draw all relevant UML 2.0 diagrams, (3) apply design principles (SOLID/GRASP), and (4) recognize common design patterns.

### 1.4 Why OOD + UML Together?

- **Encapsulation** → Data and behavior bundled together (like a capsule pill).
- **Reuse** → Inheritance lets new classes reuse existing code.
- **Maintainability** → Well-modeled classes localize changes.
- **Communication** → UML provides a *lingua franca* between developers, analysts, and clients across the globe.
- **Tooling** → UML integrates with CASE tools (Rational Rose, StarUML, Visual Paradigm, Lucidchart, PlantUML).

---

### 1.5 The Three Models of OOD (Rumbaugh's Model)

According to **Rumbaugh et al.**, the OOD process produces three intertwined models:

1. **Object Model** → *Static structure*: classes, objects, attributes, operations, relationships. (Best captured by **Class Diagram**.)
2. **Dynamic Model** → *Time-dependent behavior*: states of objects, state changes, event-response sequences. (Best captured by **State Machine Diagram** and **Sequence Diagram**.)
3. **Functional Model** → *Data transformation*: inputs, outputs, processes, computations. (Best captured by **Use Case Diagram** and **Activity Diagram**.)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** **O-D-F = Object, Dynamic, Functional.** Examiner's favorite three-letter question: *"Explain the three models of OOD."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Object-Oriented Design Process (Step-by-Step)

The KTU syllabus and standard OOD methodology prescribe the following sequence:

| Step | Activity | Primary UML Output |
|:----:|:---------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Identify classes from the problem statement (noun analysis) | Candidate class list |
| 2 | Identify attributes (data members) and operations (methods) of each class | Class responsibilities |
| 3 | Establish relationships between classes (association, inheritance, etc.) | **Class Diagram** |
| 4 | Identify use cases and actors from requirements | **Use Case Diagram** |
| 5 | Realize each use case via interaction diagrams (sequence / communication) | **Sequence / Communication Diagram** |
| 6 | Model object lifecycle for non-trivial classes | **State Machine Diagram** |
| 7 | Model complex workflows and business processes | **Activity Diagram** |
| 8 | Map classes to physical components and nodes | **Component & Deployment Diagrams** |
| 9 | Refine using design principles (SOLID, GRASP) | Cleaner, robust model |
| 10 | Apply design patterns where appropriate | Pattern-compliant solution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When a 14-mark question asks to "design a system using OOD with UML", follow **steps 1 → 10** in order. Examiners reward *completeness of the pipeline* more than the artistic quality of one diagram.

### 2.2 UML 2.x Diagram Taxonomy (Complete Cheat Sheet)

| Category | Diagram | Purpose | KTU 2024 Importance |
|:--------:|:--------|:--------|:---------------------|
| **Structural** | Class Diagram | Static class structure: attributes, methods, relationships | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Most asked) |
| **Structural** | Object Diagram | Snapshot of objects and their links at runtime | ⭐⭐ |
| **Structural** | Component Diagram | Organization of physical code modules (.jar, .dll) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Structural** | Deployment Diagram | Physical hardware nodes and their connections | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Structural** | Package Diagram | Grouping of related UML elements into packages | ⭐⭐ |
| **Structural** | Composite Structure | Internal structure of a class (parts, ports) | ⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Use Case Diagram | Functional requirements; actors and goals | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Activity Diagram | Workflow, parallel processes, business logic | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | State Machine Diagram | Lifecycle states of an object | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Sequence Diagram | Time-ordered object interactions | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Communication Diagram | Object interactions emphasizing links | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Interaction Overview | High-level view of flow among interactions | ⭐⭐ |
| **Behavioral** | Timing Diagram | State vs. time chart for one object | ⭐ |

### 2.3 Class Diagram — Building Blocks

A **Class** is drawn as a rectangle with **three compartments**:

| Compartment | Contents | Example |
|:------------|:---------|:--------|
| 1. Name compartment | Class name in **bold**, capitalized | `Customer` |
| 2. Attribute compartment | Data members with visibility modifiers | `+ name : String` |
| 3. Operation compartment | Methods with signatures and return types | `+ withdraw(amount : Double) : Boolean` |

**Visibility Notation (standard UML):**

| Symbol | Visibility (Java/C++ equivalent) | Meaning |
|:------:|:-------------------------------:|:--------|
| `+` | `public` | Accessible to all classes |
| `-` | `private` | Accessible only within the class |
| `#` | `protected` | Accessible to subclasses |
| `~` | (default / package) | Accessible within same package |

**Multiplicity Notation (numbers at relationship ends):**

| Notation | Meaning |
|:--------:|:--------|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or more |
| `1..*` | At least one |
| `0..1` | Optional (zero or one) |
| `n..m` | Range from n to m |

### 2.4 Class Diagram — Relationship Types (HIGH YIELD)

This is the **most-tested subtopic** in KTU 2024. Memorize the visual distinction.

| Relationship | UML Notation | Semantics | Example | Strength |
|:-------------|:-------------|:----------|:--------|:---------|
| **Association** | Solid line | "uses-a" — loose structural link | Student — Course | Weak |
| **Directed Association** | Solid line with arrow | "uses-a" in one direction | Customer → Order | Weak |
| **Aggregation** | Hollow (empty) diamond ◇ | "has-a" whole–part, **part can exist independently** | Department ◇— Employee | Medium |
| **Composition** | Filled (black) diamond ◆ | "owns-a" whole–part, **part cannot exist without whole** | House ◆— Room | Strong |
| **Generalization (Inheritance)** | Hollow triangle △ | "is-a" — subclass extends superclass | Vehicle △— Car | Strong |
| **Realization (Interface)** | Dashed line + hollow triangle | Class implements interface | Circle - - △— Shape | Strong |
| **Dependency** | Dashed line with arrow | "depends-on" — temporary usage | Order - - → DatabaseConnection | Weakest |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students draw *aggregation* and *composition* with the diamond at the wrong end. **The diamond goes on the WHOLE (the container), not the part.** "Department has Employees" → diamond is on the *Department* end.

### 2.5 SOLID Design Principles

The **SOLID** acronym (by *Robert C. Martin / "Uncle Bob"*) is a **favourite KTU 14-marker**.

| Letter | Principle | Definition | Benefit |
|:------:|:----------|:-----------|:--------|
| **S** | Single Responsibility Principle | A class should have **one and only one** reason to change. | High cohesion |
| **O** | Open/Closed Principle | Classes should be **open for extension** but **closed for modification**. | Stable, extensible |
| **L** | Liskov Substitution Principle | Subclass objects must be substitutable for superclass objects without breaking correctness. | Behavioral consistency |
| **I** | Interface Segregation Principle | Prefer **many small specific interfaces** over one large general interface. | Decoupling |
| **D** | Dependency Inversion Principle | Depend on **abstractions (interfaces)**, not on concrete classes. | Loose coupling |

### 2.6 GRASP Principles (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)

These guide **how to assign responsibilities** to classes — a must-know for OOD.

| Pattern | Question Answered |
|:--------|:-----------------|
| **Information Expert** | Who has the information needed? → Assign responsibility to that class. |
| **Creator** | Who creates object A? → Assign to B if B aggregates, contains, or records A. |
| **Controller** | Who handles a system event? → A controller class representing the overall use case. |
| **Low Coupling** | Minimize dependencies between classes. |
| **High Cohesion** | Keep related responsibilities together. |
| **Polymorphism** | Use polymorphic operations to handle variations based on type. |
| **Pure Fabrication** | Invent a class not in problem domain to achieve low coupling/high cohesion. |
| **Indirection** | Introduce intermediate object to reduce direct coupling. |
| **Protected Variations** | Identify points of variation and protect them with stable interfaces. |

### 2.7 Design Patterns (Gang of Four)

| Category | Pattern | Intent | Example |
|:---------|:--------|:-------|:--------|
| **Creational** | Singleton | Ensure a class has only one instance. | `Runtime.getRuntime()` in Java |
| **Creational** | Factory Method | Create objects without specifying exact class. | `Calendar.getInstance()` |
| **Creational** | Abstract Factory | Family of related/dependent objects. | UI toolkit (Win vs Mac widgets) |
| **Creational** | Builder | Construct complex object step by step. | `StringBuilder` |
| **Creational** | Prototype | Clone existing object to create new. | `Object.clone()` |
| **Structural** | Adapter | Convert interface of one class to another. | Power adapter |
| **Structural** | Bridge | Decouple abstraction from implementation. | JDBC |
| **Structural** | Composite | Treat individual and group uniformly. | File system tree |
| **Structural** | Decorator | Add behavior dynamically. | Java I/O streams |
| **Structural** | Facade | Simplified interface to a complex subsystem. | `HomeTheaterFacade` |
| **Structural** | Proxy | Surrogate controlling access. | Lazy-loading images |
| **Behavioral** | Observer | Notify dependents of state changes. | Event listeners |
| **Behavioral** | Strategy | Encapsulate interchangeable algorithms. | Sorting strategies |
| **Behavioral** | Command | Encapsulate a request as an object. | Undo/Redo |
| **Behavioral** | State | Behavior changes with internal state. | Vending machine |
| **Behavioral** | Template Method | Define skeleton, defer steps to subclass. | `HttpServlet.service()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Depth:** Define, draw UML, give one real-world example, and discuss consequences/applicability. Don't write code unless the question specifically asks.

### 2.8 Key Design Metrics

- **Coupling** (C) → Degree of interdependence between modules. **Lower is better.**
- **Cohesion** (H) → Degree to which elements of a module belong together. **Higher is better.**

$$\text{Optimal Design} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \max(\text{Cohesion}) \;\land\; \min(\text{Coupling})$$

These are typically measured on a 7-point ordinal scale (1 = low, 7 = high).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This topic is design-oriented (not algebraic), so the "derivations" here are **step-by-step design workflows** and **worked examples** that are critical for KTU 14-mark answers.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing an **ATM System** Using OOD + UML

**Problem Statement:** *"Design the software for an ATM (Automated Teller Machine). The system must allow an authenticated customer to withdraw cash, check balance, transfer funds, and print a receipt. The bank maintains customer accounts. Each transaction is logged."*

#### Step 1 — Identify Actors and Use Cases

**Actors (external entities):**
1. **Customer** — primary user of the ATM.
2. **Bank Server** — remote system that holds accounts and validates PIN.
3. **Cash Dispenser** (hardware sub-system) — physically dispenses cash.
4. **Receipt Printer** (hardware sub-system) — prints receipts.
5. **Administrator** — refills cash, audits logs.

**Use Cases (functional goals):**
- `Authenticate Customer`
- `Withdraw Cash`
- `Check Balance`
- `Transfer Funds`
- `Print Receipt`
- `Maintain Cash (Admin)`
- `View Transaction Log (Admin)`

#### Step 2 — Noun Analysis → Candidate Classes

Scan the problem statement for **nouns** (excluding obvious non-classes like "system", "software"):

| Noun Found | Class? | Reason |
|:-----------|:------:|:-------|
| Customer | ✅ Yes | Core entity |
| ATM | ✅ Yes | The system controller |
| Account | ✅ Yes | Bank-side entity |
| Bank / BankServer | ✅ Yes | Required for validation |
| CashDispenser | ✅ Yes | Hardware sub-system |
| Receipt | ✅ Yes | Object produced |
| Transaction | ✅ Yes | All operations are transactions |
| TransactionLog | ✅ Yes | Maintains audit trail |
| Cash | ❌ No | Value/amount attribute of Withdrawal |
| PIN | ❌ No | Attribute of Customer/Account |
| Balance | ❌ No | Derived attribute of Account |
| Money / Amount | ❌ No | Primitive |

> **Refined Class List:** `Customer, ATM, Account, BankServer, CashDispenser, Receipt, Transaction, TransactionLog`

#### Step 3 — Identify Attributes and Methods per Class

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|:------|:-----------|:--------|
| `Customer` | -customerId : String, -name : String, -pin : int | +authenticate() : Boolean, +getAccount() : Account |
| `Account` | -accountNumber : String, -balance : double, -type : String | +debit(amt), +credit(amt), +getBalance() |
| `ATM` | -atmId : String, -location : String, -cashAvailable : double | +start(), +readCard(), +acceptPIN(), +selectTransaction() |
| `BankServer` | -serverUrl : String | +validatePIN(cust, pin) : Boolean, +getAccount(cust) |
| `CashDispenser` | -cashCount : int | +dispense(amount) : Boolean |
| `Receipt` | -receiptId : String, -date : Date, -amount : double | +print() : void |
| `Transaction` (abstract) | -txnId : String, -timestamp : Date, -amount : double | +execute() : Boolean (abstract) |
| `Withdrawal` (extends Transaction) | -cashDispensed : int | +execute() : Boolean |
| `Deposit` (extends Transaction) | -sourceAccount : Account | +execute() : Boolean |
| `Transfer` (extends Transaction) | -fromAccount, -toAccount : Account | +execute() : Boolean |
| `TransactionLog` | -entries : List<Transaction> | +addEntry(t), +getEntries() |

#### Step 4 — Identify Relationships

| Relationship | Type | Multiplicity | UML Notation |
|:-------------|:-----|:-------------|:-------------|
| `ATM` — `Customer` | Association | 1 ATM serves 0..* Customers | Solid line |
| `ATM` ◆— `CashDispenser` | **Composition** | 1 ATM contains 1 Dispenser | Filled diamond on ATM |
| `ATM` ◆— `ReceiptPrinter` | **Composition** | 1 ATM contains 1 Printer | Filled diamond on ATM |
| `Customer` ◇— `Account` | **Aggregation** | 1 Customer owns 1..* Accounts | Hollow diamond on Customer |
| `ATM` — `BankServer` | Association | 1 ATM connects to 1 Server | Solid line |
| `Withdrawal` △— `Transaction` | **Generalization** | Withdrawal is-a Transaction | Hollow triangle |
| `Deposit` △— `Transaction` | **Generalization** | Deposit is-a Transaction | Hollow triangle |
| `Transfer` △— `Transaction` | **Generalization** | Transfer is-a Transaction | Hollow triangle |
| `TransactionLog` — `Transaction` | Association | 1 log contains 0..* transactions | Solid line |

#### Step 5 — Map Use Cases to Classes (Realization)

For the **`Withdraw Cash`** use case:

1. Customer inserts card → `ATM.readCard()`
2. Customer enters PIN → `ATM.acceptPIN()` → calls `BankServer.validatePIN()`
3. If valid → `ATM.selectTransaction()` shows menu
4. Customer chooses "Withdraw" + amount → `ATM` creates a `Withdrawal` object
5. `Withdrawal.execute()` calls `Account.debit(amount)` via `BankServer`
6. On success, `ATM` calls `CashDispenser.dispense(amount)`
7. `ATM` calls `ReceiptPrinter.print(receipt)`
8. `TransactionLog.addEntry(withdrawal)` records the transaction
9. Card is ejected

#### Step 6 — Verify Against Design Principles

| Principle | Application in ATM Design |
|:----------|:-------------------------|
| **SRP** | `CashDispenser` only dispenses; `ReceiptPrinter` only prints; `Transaction` family only models transactions. |
| **OCP** | New transaction types (e.g., `BillPayment`) can be added by extending `Transaction` without modifying existing code. |
| **LSP** | Any `Transaction` subclass can be stored in `TransactionLog` and executed identically. |
| **ISP** | `BankServer` interface is split: `Authenticator`, `AccountAccessor`, `Ledger`. |
| **DIP** | `ATM` depends on an `IBankServer` interface, not a concrete server class. |

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Use Case Realization via Sequence Diagram (Withdraw Cash)

The following textual sequence is exactly what a sequence diagram captures (rendered in SECTION_4 as a Mermaid figure):

| Step | Time | Source | Destination | Message |
|:----:|:----:|:-------|:-----------|:--------|
| 1 | t1 | Customer | ATM | insertCard(cardNumber) |
| 2 | t2 | ATM | Customer | requestPIN() |
| 3 | t3 | Customer | ATM | enterPIN(pin) |
| 4 | t4 | ATM | BankServer | validatePIN(cardNumber, pin) |
| 5 | t5 | BankServer | ATM | true / false |
| 6 | t6 | ATM | Customer | showMenu() |
| 7 | t7 | Customer | ATM | selectWithdrawal(500) |
| 8 | t8 | ATM | BankServer | debitAccount(cardNumber, 500) |
| 9 | t9 | BankServer | ATM | success / failure |
| 10 | t10 | ATM | CashDispenser | dispense(500) |
| 11 | t11 | ATM | ReceiptPrinter | print(receiptDetails) |
| 12 | t12 | ATM | TransactionLog | log(withdrawalDetails) |
| 13 | t13 | ATM | Customer | ejectCard() |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Examiners ask *"How is the use case 'Withdraw Cash' realized?"* The answer is the **sequence diagram + collaboration of objects**. A class diagram alone is **insufficient** — you must show the *dynamic* view.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right UML Diagram

A common KTU 2-mark question is: *"Which UML diagram would you use to ...?"*

| Requirement / Question | Best UML Diagram | Why |
|:-----------------------|:-----------------|:----|
| Show the static class structure with relationships | **Class Diagram** | Captures structure, not time |
| Show step-by-step message passing between objects | **Sequence Diagram** | Time-ordered lifeline view |
| Show the lifecycle states of an order | **State Machine Diagram** | State transitions, events, guards |
| Show the workflow of approving a loan | **Activity Diagram** | Business process, parallel paths |
| Show which functional goals a system offers | **Use Case Diagram** | External view: actors + use cases |
| Show how software files are split across hardware | **Deployment Diagram** | Physical architecture |
| Show one snapshot of objects and their links | **Object Diagram** | Runtime instance view |
| Show a complex algorithm with branches and loops | **Activity Diagram** | Procedural flow control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Discriminator Rule:** *Time on the X-axis → Sequence.* *State as the unit → State Machine.* *Business workflow with decisions → Activity.* *Static structure → Class.*

### 3.4 Applying GRASP — Worked Mini-Case

> **Problem:** In a `Library` system, who should be responsible for calculating the *late fine* on a returned book?

**Applying GRASP "Information Expert":**

$$\text{Late Fine} = (\text{ReturnDate} - \text{DueDate}) \times \text{FineRatePerDay}$$

The classes holding the relevant data are:
- `Loan` (or `BorrowRecord`) → has `dueDate`, `returnDate`
- `Book` → has `category` (which maps to `fineRatePerDay`)

$$\Longrightarrow \text{The responsibility for } \texttt{calculateFine()} \text{ is assigned to the } \texttt{Loan} \text{ class.}$$

**Reasoning trace (write this in your exam answer):**
1. Late fine is computed from **due date** and **return date** — both stored in `Loan`.
2. By the **Information Expert** principle, the class that has the information is the one that gets the responsibility.
3. Alternative (wrong) assignments:
   - `Library` class? → Violates High Cohesion (Library would accumulate unrelated tasks).
   - `Member` class? → Violates Information Expert (Member doesn't have the dates).
4. Hence: `Loan.calculateFine() : double` is the correct design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — ATM System

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        -customerId : String
        -name : String
        -pin : int
        +authenticate() Boolean
        +getAccount() Account
    }

    class Account {
        -accountNumber : String
        -balance : double
        -type : String
        +debit(amt double) Boolean
        +credit(amt double) void
        +getBalance() double
    }

    class ATM {
        -atmId : String
        -location : String
        -cashAvailable : double
        +start() void
        +readCard() void
        +acceptPIN() Boolean
        +selectTransaction() void
    }

    class BankServer {
        <<interface>>
        +validatePIN(cust String, pin int) Boolean
        +getAccount(cust String) Account
        +debitAccount(acc String, amt double) Boolean
    }

    class CashDispenser {
        -cashCount : int
        +dispense(amount double) Boolean
    }

    class ReceiptPrinter {
        -paperLevel : int
        +print(r Receipt) void
    }

    class Transaction {
        <<abstract>>
        -txnId : String
        -timestamp : Date
        -amount : double
        +execute() Boolean
    }

    class Withdrawal {
        -cashDispensed : int
        +execute() Boolean
    }

    class Deposit {
        -sourceAccount : Account
        +execute() Boolean
    }

    class Transfer {
        -fromAccount : Account
        -toAccount : Account
        +execute() Boolean
    }

    class TransactionLog {
        -entries : List
        +addEntry(t Transaction) void
        +getEntries() List
    }

    class Receipt {
        -receiptId : String
        -date : Date
        -amount : double
    }

    Customer "1" --> "1..*" Account : owns
    ATM "1" --> "1" BankServer : connects
    ATM "1" *-- "1" CashDispenser : composition
    ATM "1" *-- "1" ReceiptPrinter : composition
    Transaction <|-- Withdrawal
    Transaction <|-- Deposit
    Transaction <|-- Transfer
    TransactionLog "1" o-- "0..*" Transaction : stores
    Withdrawal ..> CashDispenser : uses
    Withdrawal ..> Receipt : creates
    ATM ..> Receipt : prints
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Use Case Diagram — Library Management System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    actor1((Member))
    actor2((Librarian))
    actor3((External System))

    uc1([Search Catalog])
    uc2([Issue Book])
    uc3([Return Book])
    uc4([Reserve Book])
    uc5([Pay Fine])
    uc6([Add New Book])
    uc7([Remove Book])
    uc8([Manage Members])
    uc9([Send Notifications])

    actor1 --- uc1
    actor1 --- uc2
    actor1 --- uc3
    actor1 --- uc4
    actor1 --- uc5
    actor2 --- uc6
    actor2 --- uc7
    actor2 --- uc8
    uc2 -.includes.-> uc9
    uc3 -.extends.-> uc5

    uc9 --- actor3
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid `flowchart` is used here to express Use Case semantics because Mermaid has no native Use Case shape. Solid lines denote **association** (actor to use case), dashed arrows with `includes` (mandatory reuse) and `extends` (optional/conditional extension) follow UML 2.5 standards.

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — ATM Withdraw Cash

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Customer
    participant A as ATM
    participant B as BankServer
    participant D as CashDispenser
    participant P as ReceiptPrinter
    participant L as TransactionLog

    C->>A: insertCard(cardNumber)
    A-->>C: requestPIN()
    C->>A: enterPIN(pin)
    A->>B: validatePIN(cardNumber pin)
    B-->>A: true
    A-->>C: showMenu()
    C->>A: selectWithdrawal(500)
    A->>B: debitAccount(cardNumber 500)
    B-->>A: success
    A->>D: dispense(500)
    D-->>A: dispensed
    A->>P: print(receipt)
    P-->>A: printed
    A->>L: log(withdrawal)
    A-->>C: ejectCard()
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Machine Diagram — Order Processing

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending
    Pending --> Confirmed : paymentReceived
    Pending --> Cancelled : customerCancels
    Confirmed --> Shipped : goodsDispatched
    Confirmed --> Cancelled : outOfStock
    Shipped --> Delivered : customerAcknowledges
    Shipped --> Returned : deliveryFailed
    Delivered --> Returned : returnWindowExpires
    Delivered --> Closed : returnWindowCloses
    Returned --> Refunded : refundProcessed
    Cancelled --> [*]
    Refunded --> [*]
    Closed --> [*]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Activity Diagram — Loan Approval Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start((Start)) --> A[Receive Application]
    A --> B{Applicant Eligible}
    B -- No --> R1[Reject Application]
    B -- Yes --> C[Check Credit Score]
    C --> D{CreditScore > Threshold}
    D -- No --> R1
    D -- Yes --> E[Prepare Documents]
    E --> F[Manager Review]
    F --> G{Manager Approves}
    G -- No --> R1
    G -- Yes --> H[Disburse Amount]
    H --> I[Notify Applicant]
    I --> end1((End))
    R1 --> J[Notify Applicant of Rejection]
    J --> end1
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Component & Deployment Diagram (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientTier["Client Tier"]
        WebApp["Web Application\nBrowser / SPA"]
        MobileApp["Mobile App\nAndroid / iOS"]
    end

    subgraph ServerTier["Application Server Tier"]
        APIGateway["API Gateway\nSpring Boot / Express"]
        AuthService["Authentication\nMicroservice"]
        AccountService["Account\nMicroservice"]
        TxService["Transaction\nMicroservice"]
    end

    subgraph DataTier["Data Tier"]
        UserDB[("User DB\nPostgreSQL")]
        AccountDB[("Account DB\nOracle")]
        LogStore[("Log Store\nMongoDB")]
    end

    WebApp --> APIGateway
    MobileApp --> APIGateway
    APIGateway --> AuthService
    APIGateway --> AccountService
    APIGateway --> TxService
    AuthService --> UserDB
    AccountService --> AccountDB
    TxService --> AccountDB
    TxService --> LogStore
```

### 4.7 OOD Design Flowchart (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirements Specification] --> B[Use Case Modeling]
    B --> C[Identify Classes and Objects]
    C --> D[Define Class Attributes and Methods]
    D --> E[Establish Relationships]
    E --> F[Class Diagram]
    E --> G[Use Case Diagram]
    B --> G
    F --> H[Interaction Modeling]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Sequence Diagram]
    H --> J[Communication Diagram]
    I --> K[State Modeling for Complex Objects]
    K --> L[State Machine Diagram]
    F --> M[Component and Deployment Modeling]
    M --> N[Component Diagram]
    M --> O[Deployment Diagram]
    F --> P[Apply SOLID Principles]
    P --> Q[Apply Design Patterns]
    Q --> R[Refined Design Model]
    R --> S[Implementation in OOP Language]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** Per the KTU-PREMIER protocol, complex physical drawings (e.g., 3D system architecture blueprints) are replaced by **block-level functional flow diagrams** in Mermaid. The above diagrams faithfully represent UML semantics using supported Mermaid syntax.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Object-Oriented Design. Explain its advantages over traditional function-oriented design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
> **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** is a design methodology that decomposes a system into a collection of objects that collaborate to fulfill requirements. Each object bundles data (attributes) and behavior (operations) into a single unit called a *class*.

**Advantages over Function-Oriented Design (2 marks):**

1. **Encapsulation & Information Hiding** — Internal data is protected; clients access only public interfaces. Function-oriented designs expose global data, leading to maintenance nightmares.
2. **Reuse via Inheritance** — New classes inherit and extend existing ones. Function-oriented code lacks a natural reuse mechanism (relies on copy-paste).
3. **Closer Mapping to Real World** — Objects (Customer, Order, Invoice) mirror real entities, making analysis → design → implementation more natural.
4. **Easier Maintenance & Extension** — OOD satisfies **Open/Closed Principle**: new functionality is added by *new classes*, not by *modifying existing functions*.
5. **Polymorphism** — Same message (`draw()`) triggers different behavior per object type — impossible in function-oriented designs without long `if/else` or `switch` chains.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 1]**
*List and briefly explain any three relationships used in UML class diagrams.*

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1 mark each for any 3):**

| # | Relationship | Explanation |
|:-:|:-------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Association** | A general "uses-a" structural link between classes. Example: `Student` is associated with `Course` (a student takes courses). |
| 2 | **Aggregation** | A weak "has-a" whole–part relationship where the part can outlive the whole. Example: `Department ◇— Employee` — an employee may exist independently of any department. |
| 3 | **Composition** | A strong "owns-a" whole–part relationship where the part's lifetime is tied to the whole. Example: `House ◆— Room` — a room ceases to exist when the house is demolished. |
| 4 | **Generalization (Inheritance)** | An "is-a" relationship between a superclass and subclass. Example: `Vehicle △— Car` — Car is a specialized Vehicle. |
| 5 | **Dependency** | A weak "depends-on" link indicating one class temporarily uses another. Example: `Order - - → DatabaseConnection` — Order uses a DB connection during checkout. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **Design an Online Course Registration System for a university using Object-Oriented Design. Draw the complete Class Diagram, identify at least 5 use cases, draw the Use Case Diagram, and realize one use case ("Register for a Course") using a Sequence Diagram. Apply any two SOLID principles in your design.**

#### (a) Class Diagram and Use Case Identification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Class List (2 marks):**
After noun analysis, the principal classes are:
`Student, Course, Registration, CourseCatalog, Schedule, Professor, Department, Account, Notification, SystemController`.

**Step 2 — Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        -studentId : String
        -name : String
        -email : String
        -gpa : double
        +login() Boolean
        +registerCourse(c Course) Boolean
        +viewSchedule() Schedule
    }

    class Course {
        -courseCode : String
        -title : String
        -credits : int
        -capacity : int
        -enrolled : int
        +isFull() Boolean
        +getSchedule() Schedule
    }

    class Registration {
        -regId : String
        -date : Date
        -status : String
        +confirm() void
        +cancel() void
    }

    class CourseCatalog {
        -courses : List
        +searchByCode(c String) Course
        +searchByDept(d String) List
    }

    class Schedule {
        -slots : List
        +hasConflict(s Schedule) Boolean
    }

    class Professor {
        -profId : String
        -name : String
        -department : Department
        +teaches(c Course) void
    }

    class Department {
        -deptId : String
        -deptName : String
    }

    class SystemController {
        +login(u String p String) Boolean
        +processRegistration(s Student c Course) Registration
    }

    class Notification {
        -notifId : String
        -message : String
        -timestamp : Date
        +send() void
    }

    Student "1" --> "0..*" Registration : has
    Course "1" --> "0..*" Registration : has
    Registration "0..*" --> "1" Student : references
    Registration "0..*" --> "1" Course : references
    Student "1" --> "1" CourseCatalog : browses
    CourseCatalog "1" o-- "0..*" Course : contains
    Course "1" --> "1" Schedule : scheduledIn
    Professor "1" --> "0..*" Course : teaches
    Professor "1" --> "1" Department : belongsTo
    SystemController ..> Registration : creates
    Registration ..> Notification : triggers
```

**Step 3 — Use Cases (2 marks):**
1. `Login`
2. `Search Course`
3. `Register for Course`
4. `Drop Course`
5. `View Schedule`
6. `View Grade Report`

#### (b) Use Case Diagram + Sequence Diagram for "Register for a Course" + SOLID Application (7 marks)

**Use Case Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    student((Student))
    admin((Registrar))

    uc1([Login])
    uc2([Search Course])
    uc3([Register for Course])
    uc4([Drop Course])
    uc5([View Schedule])
    uc6([View Grade Report])
    uc7([Manage Course Catalog])

    student --- uc1
    student --- uc2
    student --- uc3
    student --- uc4
    student --- uc5
    student --- uc6
    admin --- uc7

    uc3 -.includes.-> uc1
    uc3 -.includes.-> uc2
```

**Sequence Diagram for "Register for a Course" (3 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Student
    participant SC as SystemController
    participant CC as CourseCatalog
    participant C as Course
    participant R as Registration
    participant N as Notification

    S->>SC: login(username password)
    SC-->>S: authToken
    S->>SC: requestRegisterCourse(courseCode)
    SC->>CC: searchByCode(courseCode)
    CC-->>SC: Course
    SC->>C: isFull()
    C-->>SC: false
    SC->>R: new Registration(student course)
    R-->>SC: regCreated
    SC->>C: incrementEnrolled()
    SC->>N: sendConfirmation(student course)
    N-->>S: registrationConfirmed
    SC-->>S: success
```

**SOLID Application (2 marks — pick any two):**

1. **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP):** The `Notification` class only handles sending messages; the `CourseCatalog` only handles search; the `Registration` only models the registration record. Each class has exactly one reason to change.
2. **Open/Closed Principle (OCP):** New notification channels (Email, SMS, Push) can be added by subclassing `Notification` or providing a new `INotificationChannel` implementation, without modifying the registration logic.
3. **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP):** `SystemController` depends on the abstraction `ICourseCatalog` (interface) rather than a concrete class, allowing the catalog implementation to be swapped (e.g., from in-memory to database-backed) without changing `SystemController`.

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

> **Explain the SOLID design principles in detail. For each principle, give (i) a one-line definition, (ii) a UML class diagram snippet showing a violation, (iii) a UML snippet showing the corrected design, and (iv) one real-world analogy.**

#### (a) SOLID Principles — Part 1: S, O, L (7 marks)

| Principle | Definition | Violation Snippet | Correction | Real-world Analogy |
|:----------|:-----------|:-------------------|:------------|:-------------------|
| **S** — Single Responsibility | A class has one, and only one, reason to change. | `Employee` class with `calculatePay()`, `saveToDB()`, `printPayslip()`, `sendEmail()`. | Split into `Employee`, `PayCalculator`, `EmployeeRepository`, `PayslipPrinter`, `EmailService`. | A chef cooks; a cashier takes money. One person doing both jobs in a small stall leads to chaos. |
| **O** — Open/Closed | Classes open for extension, closed for modification. | `Shape` with a `getArea()` method using `if (type == "Circle") ... else if (type == "Square") ...` | Abstract `Shape` with abstract `getArea()`; `Circle`, `Square` override. | A power strip with empty sockets — plug in new devices without rewiring. |
| **L** — Liskov Substitution | Subclass objects must be substitutable for superclass without breaking correctness. | `Square extends Rectangle` where `setWidth()` also changes height — breaks `Rectangle` invariants. | Prefer composition, or model `Square` and `Rectangle` as siblings of an abstract `Shape`. | A "Square" car is not substitutable for a "Rectangular" truck — they have different semantics, not a refinement relationship. |

**Mermaid Class Diagram — Violation vs Correction for OCP:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Shape_Bad {
        -type : String
        +getArea() double
    }
    class Shape_Good {
        <<abstract>>
        +getArea() double
    }
    class Circle {
        -radius : double
        +getArea() double
    }
    class Square {
        -side : double
        +getArea() double
    }
    Shape_Bad <.. Circle : would need modify
    Shape_Good <|-- Circle : extends
    Shape_Good <|-- Square : extends
```

#### (b) SOLID Principles — Part 2: I, D, plus a GRASP example (7 marks)

| Principle | Definition | Violation Snippet | Correction | Real-world Analogy |
|:----------|:-----------|:-------------------|:------------|:-------------------|
| **I** — Interface Segregation | Prefer many specific interfaces over one general-purpose interface. | `IMachine { print(), scan(), fax() }` — basic printer forced to implement scan/fax. | Split into `IPrinter`, `IScanner`, `IFax`. | A multi-tool Swiss Army knife with a corkscrew in every device vs. specialized tools. |
| **D** — Dependency Inversion | Depend on abstractions, not concretions. | `OrderService` directly instantiates `MySQLRepository`. | `OrderService` depends on `IOrderRepository`; MySQL implementation injected via constructor. | Plug-in power adapters: device depends on the socket standard (abstraction), not a specific wall outlet (concretion). |

**Mermaid Diagram — DIP Violation vs Correction:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class OrderService_Bad {
        -repo : MySQLOrderRepository
        +save(o Order) void
    }
    class MySQLOrderRepository {
        +save(o Order) void
    }
    OrderService_Bad --> MySQLOrderRepository

    class OrderService_Good {
        -repo : IOrderRepository
        +save(o Order) void
    }
    class IOrderRepository {
        <<interface>>
        +save(o Order) void
    }
    class MySQLOrderRepository2 {
        +save(o Order) void
    }
    class MongoOrderRepository {
        +save(o Order) void
    }
    OrderService_Good --> IOrderRepository
    IOrderRepository <|.. MySQLOrderRepository2
    IOrderRepository <|.. MongoOrderRepository
```

**Bonus: GRASP "Information Expert" example (1 mark):**
In a `Library` system, responsibility for computing a late fine belongs to the `Loan` class (which holds the dates), **not** to `Library` or `Member` — by Information Expert.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **No multiplicities on relationships** → loses 1 mark per diagram.
> 2. **Diamond on the wrong end** of aggregation/composition → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing includes vs extends** in use case diagrams → `includes` is mandatory, `extends` is optional/conditional.
> 4. **Confusing sequence vs communication diagrams** → sequence = time axis; communication = links emphasized.
> 5. **Forgetting return arrows** in sequence diagrams → marks deducted if not visible.
> 6. **Writing code in OOD questions** → unless explicitly asked, code is *not* required; UML diagrams are the answer.
> 7. **Mixing up "is-a" vs "has-a"** → wrong choice between inheritance and aggregation.
> 8. **Not stating design principles used** → even if your design is good, you must *articulate* the SOLID/GRASP application.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section. It is your one-page exam revision sheet.**

#### Core Concepts
- [ ] **OOD** = methodology; **UML** = notation. They are *not* synonyms.
- [ ] Three models of OOD: **Object (static)**, **Dynamic (time)**, **Functional (data transformation)**.
- [ ] UML 2.x has **14 diagrams** split into **Structural** (7) and **Behavioral** (7).
- [ ] Most-frequently-tested diagrams: **Class, Use Case, Sequence, Activity, State Machine**.

#### Class Diagram Essentials
- [ ] Three compartments: **Name / Attributes / Operations**.
- [ ] Visibility: `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- [ ] Multiplicities: `1`, `*`, `0..*`, `1..*`, `0..1`, `n..m`.
- [ ] **Diamond on the WHOLE side**, not the part.
- [ ] **Hollow diamond = Aggregation** (weak, part can exist alone).
- [ ] **Filled diamond = Composition** (strong, part dies with whole).
- [ ] **Hollow triangle = Generalization** (is-a) or **Realization** (implements interface, dashed line).

#### Use Case Diagram Essentials
- [ ] Actors (stick figures) — *external* entities only.
- [ ] Use cases (ellipses) — *goals* of the system.
- [ ] `<<include>>` — **mandatory** reuse, dashed arrow from base to included.
- [ ] `<<extend>>` — **optional/conditional** behavior, dashed arrow from extension to base.
- [ ] System boundary (rectangle) is optional but improves clarity.

#### Sequence Diagram Essentials
- [ ] Time flows **top to bottom** on the page.
- [ ] **Lifelines** = dashed vertical lines.
- [ ] **Activation bars** = thin rectangles on the lifeline when the object is busy.
- [ ] **Solid arrow** = method call; **dashed arrow** = return value.
- [ ] `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par` are **combined fragments** for conditions, optional paths, loops, and parallelism.

#### SOLID Principles (one-liners)
- [ ] **S** — One class, one responsibility.
- [ ] **O** — Open for extension, closed for modification.
- [ ] **L** — Subtypes must be substitutable for supertypes.
- [ ] **I** — Many specific interfaces > one general interface.
- [ ] **D** — Depend on abstractions, not concretions.

#### GRASP Principles (high-yield)
- [ ] **Information Expert** — Give responsibility to the class with the data.
- [ ] **Creator** — Class B creates A if B aggregates, contains, or records A.
- [ ] **Controller** — Use a dedicated controller class for system events.
- [ ] **Low Coupling**, **High Cohesion**, **Polymorphism**, **Pure Fabrication**, **Indirection**, **Protected Variations**.

#### Design Patterns (memorize intent only)
- [ ] **Creational** — Singleton, Factory, Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype.
- [ ] **Structural** — Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Proxy.
- [ ] **Behavioral** — Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Template Method.

#### Exam-Day Tips
- [ ] When asked to "design using OOD", always include: **Class list → Class diagram → Use case diagram → One sequence diagram → Note which SOLID/GRASP principles you used**.
- [ ] Use a **real-world problem** (ATM, Library, Hospital, E-commerce) — examiners reward domain clarity.
- [ ] Label **all** multiplicities and visibility — silent omissions cost easy marks.
- [ ] Always **explain why** you used a relationship, not just draw it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open-Source Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT503 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Open-Source Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Open-Source Development (OSD)** is a distributed, collaborative software engineering paradigm in which the **source code** of a software system is published under a license that grants every user the **freedoms to use, inspect, modify, enhance, and redistribute** the software — without requiring explicit permission from the original author.

The **Open Source Initiative (OSI)** formalises this concept through a canonical set of **ten criteria** that a valid open-source license MUST satisfy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OSI's 10 Canonical Criteria for Open-Source Software**
> 1. **Free Redistribution** — The license shall not restrict any party from selling or giving away the software.
> 2. **Source Code Availability** — The program must include source code, or a clear, written offer to obtain it.
> 3. **Derived Works** — The license must allow modifications and derived works, and must allow them to be distributed under the same terms.
> 4. **Integrity of The Author's Source Code** — The license may restrict source-code distribution only to ensure well-intended modifications.
